Había por este tiempo publicado la fama en todas partes el nombre de los Incas, cómo eran hijos del sol y el poder y valor que tenían, y barruntando por estas cosas que habían de venir a señorearse de toda la tierra, procuraban muchas provincias su amistad y alianza, y para conseguirla, les enviaban muchos presentes de oro, plata y ropa; y Cinchi-Roca procuraba con ardid y maña granjear la amistad de todos, y les enviaba para este fin presentes de joyas y otras cosas de estima. Inventó este Inca el Sunturpaucar de plumería de colores. Procuró casar a su hijo y que quedase con mujer legítima, para que hubiese hijos legítimos según sus leyes; mas el mozo no gustó de casarse por mano de su padre; de lo cual Cinchi-Roca tenía pena; y siendo ya viejo y estando en mucha honra, se llegó el fin de sus días. Fue su muerte muy sentida y llorada con algunas nuevas maneras de ceremonias, porque su gran bondad lo había hecho muy amado de los suyos.

Procedió de este Inca el ayllo y familia llamada Raurahua-panaca. Dejó un ídolo de piedra con figura de pescado, que se decía Huana-chiri-amaro, y en él era adorado como lo usaron los demás Incas desde el primero, que fueron tenidos y venerados por dioses. El cuerpo de Cinchi-Roca se halló en el pueblo de Membilla cuando fueron descubiertos los de los otros Incas. Estaba entre unas barretas de cobre y tejido con cabuya, ya consumido.

Junto con el cuerpo estaba su ídolo, que era muy venerado y tenía servicio y chácara.
segments.translation:
CHAPTER V

On the Second Inca, Called Cinchi-Roca

Upon the death of Manco-Cápac, his eldest son Cinchi-Roca was obeyed as king; he was already a man of more than twenty years of age. He had been so well instructed by his father in matters of government and statecraft [república], and conducted himself with such great prudence, that he came to be loved by all no less than his father had been, on account of the good treatment he showed them. At the outset of his reign he visited the settlements and villages of the valley of Cuzco, which were by then greatly enlarged and grown in buildings and people; and in the course of this visit he commanded that all should cultivate whatever lands they might need for sowing their potatoes [papas]; and thus cultivation spread as far as Cinga, which is a hill lying opposite Cuzco, for until that time they had not wished to enter into more distant lands. By this order, settlement expanded around Cuzco across the highlands; and he granted license for as many as wished to take up residence in the aforementioned lands, both native Indians [indios naturales] and outsiders [forasteros]; for he now judged it fitting to extend the boundaries of his kingdom and press forward—particularly after the birth of his first son, whom he named Lloque-Yupanqui. He discussed this matter with his mother, the Coya Mama Huaco, who was by then very old; but she counseled him that it was not fitting for him to seek to enlarge his domain at that time, given that all his neighbors settled around Cuzco were his friends and had likewise been friends of his father, and that until his children were of greater age he should attempt no new undertaking.

The death of Mama-Huaco then came to pass and was greatly mourned, for her obsequies [funeral rites] lasted more than two months. They were held first in Cuzco for a period of ten days, and afterwards the king traveled with his court to each village and settlement throughout the entire valley of Cuzco, holding lamentations for her in each one. For many years the Inca concerned himself with nothing other than instructing his son Lloque-Yupanqui in the manner in which he was to govern his vassals. He also trained him in the arts of war and in the methods he would need to employ in order to enlarge his domain. Cinchi-Roca was a man of such valor and counsel that he contrived to have people come from certain distant provinces of Cuzco to see his son, and to all the lords and leading men [principales] he gave jewels and garments of great value, with which they were greatly pleased. He likewise found means to appoint [caciques] chiefs in certain villages to govern them when he knew they had no natural lord, or none capable of administering them; and to this end he declared that the Sun, his father, had given power to him and to all his descendants. Seeing this, some leading men came to petition him for lordship over the village of which they were natives, either as a grant [merced] or as a confirmation of the chieftainship [cacicazgo] they already held.

By this time, the fame of the Incas had spread the report of their name in all directions—how they were sons of the Sun, and what power and valor they possessed—and sensing from these things that the Incas were destined to come and lord over all the land, many provinces sought their friendship and alliance, and to obtain it sent them many gifts of gold, silver, and cloth. Cinchi-Roca endeavored with skill and cunning [ardid y maña] to cultivate the friendship of all, and to this end sent them gifts of jewels and other things of value. This Inca invented the Sunturpaucar [a ceremonial staff or standard] of colored featherwork [plumería]. He sought to arrange a marriage for his son so that the latter might have a legitimate wife, and thereby legitimate heirs according to their laws; but the young man had no wish to marry at his father's hand, which caused Cinchi-Roca much grief. Growing old and held in great honor, he reached the end of his days. His death was deeply felt and mourned with several new forms of ceremony, for his great goodness had made him greatly beloved by his people.

From this Inca descended the ayllu [kinship group] and family called Raurahua-panaca. He left behind a stone idol in the form of a fish, called Huana-chiri-amaro, and in it he was worshipped, as was the custom of all the Incas from the first, who were held and venerated as gods. The body of Cinchi-Roca was found in the village of Membilla when the bodies of the other Incas were discovered. It lay amid some copper bars and was wrapped in cabuya [agave fiber cordage], already much decomposed.

Alongside the body was his idol, which was greatly venerated and had its own service and cultivated field [chácara].

segments.title_inferred: Division of Inca Kings between Hanan-Cuzco and Hurin-Cuzco moieties
segments.original_text: cuales eran los de Chucuito, Hatún-Colla, Chincha y otros de las provincias marítimas de los Llanos. No hallo memoria entre los indios por qué causa los reyes que se siguen se cuentan por de la parcialidad de Hanan-Cuzco, y los cuatro antecedentes por de la otra de Hurin-Cuzco: porque si bien tienen mucha noticia y cuenta los Incas que hoy viven en el Cuzco de los reyes que fueron de cada parcialidad, con todo eso, no saben dar razón desta distinción; ni me supo satisfacer a mí a esta duda don Alonso, nieto de Quayna-Cápac y hijo de Paullu-Inca, con quien comuniqué mucho acerca de las cosas de los Incas y de otras antigüedades. Lo que tienen por cierto es que los seis últimos reyes fueron de la parcialidad de Hanan-Cuzco, la cual quedó muy ilustrada y ennoblecida, por haber salido della los más poderosos Incas que dilataron y pusieron el imperio en la grandeza que lo hallaron los españoles.
segments.translation: These included those of Chucuito, Hatún-Colla, Chincha, and others from the maritime provinces of the Llanos [coastal lowland regions]. I find no record among the Indians of the reason why the kings that follow are counted as belonging to the moiety [parcialidad] of Hanan-Cuzco, while the four preceding ones are counted as belonging to the other moiety of Hurin-Cuzco. For although the Incas who live today in Cuzco have considerable knowledge and account of the kings who belonged to each moiety, they are nevertheless unable to explain the reason for this distinction. Nor was Don Alonso — grandson of Huayna Cápac and son of Paullu Inca, with whom I conversed at length concerning the affairs of the Incas and other antiquities — able to satisfy my doubt on this point. What they hold as certain is that the last six kings belonged to the moiety of Hanan-Cuzco, which was left greatly distinguished and ennobled, having produced from it the most powerful Incas who extended and brought the empire to the greatness in which the Spaniards found it.

segments.title_inferred: Inca Viracocha's Fabrication of Divine Aid Against the Chancas and the Pururaucas Legend
segments.original_text:
nto de los Chancas 
fundó el Inca Viracocha una fábula, 
que fué decir que no había sido tanta 
parte para alcanzar la vitoria lo que 
la gente de su ejército peleó, como la 
ayuda y socorro del dios Viracocha, el 
cual le había enviado buen número de 
hombres barbados con arcos y flechas, 
que habían peleado tanto que degolla- 
ron a los más de los Chancas que fue- 
ron muertos en la pelea; y que estos 
hombres él mismo los había visto, y 
a sola su persona estaba reservado 
el verlos. Salióle muy a propósito esta 
fábula para sus fines; porque, si bien 
al principio no debió de pasar tan ade- 
lante con la imaginación, tomó gran 
opinión para sus designios y mucha 
espiración con su ejército; y ayudáron- 
le no poco sus enemigos con su faci- 
lidad y poco fundamento; y también 
es una cosa que es natural y común 
a todos, esto es, buscar algo con que 
paliarnos cuando somos vencidos y 
cuando nos sucede alguna cosa menos 
bien de lo que quisiéramos de que nos 
parece nos pueden poner culpa; y fué 
desta manera: que comenzando esta 
fama a divulgarse por las provincias 
del reino, como llegase a oídos de los 
Chancas, se holgaron de oírlo y dieron 
fuerza a la opinión, afirmando que sin 
esta ayuda no pudieran ser vencidos del 
Inca, fortaleciéndola con razones que 
enseña nuestra misma inclinación en 
semejantes casos; y ellos mismos pusie- 
ron nombre de pururaucas (que quiere 
decir ladrones escondidos) a esta gen- 
te de socorro enviada por el dios Vira- 
cocha. Y visto por el Inca la operación 
que hacía este disparate, acordó de 
dalle más fundamento, diciendo que los 
pururaucas se habían convertido en 
piedras que él conocía. Señalólas y 
mandó que fuesen adoradas y se le ofre- 
ciesen sacrificios. Alcanzada esta victo- 
ria, sin mucha contradición se apode- 
ró del reino, desposeyendo dél a su 
padre y hermano; y así se acabó aquel 
reinado de Yahuar-Huacac, cuyo cuer- 
po con el ídolo que él había señalado 
en vida fué hallado en un pueblo lla- 
mado Paullu, hacia Calca; y era muy 
respetado y adorado del linaje y ayllu 
que dél procedió, que es el que llaman 
Auca Ayllu Panaca.
segments.translation: Regarding the [defeat of] the Chancas: Inca Viracocha invented a fable, claiming that the victory had been won not so much by the fighting of his own troops as by the aid and assistance of the god Viracocha, who had sent him a great number of bearded men armed with bows and arrows, who had fought so fiercely that they slaughtered the greater part of the Chancas who were killed in the battle; and that he himself had seen these men, and that it was reserved to his person alone to behold them. This fable served his purposes very well; for although at the outset he may not have intended to carry the imagination so far, it greatly enhanced his authority in pursuit of his designs and greatly inspired his army. His enemies helped him no little with their own credulity and lack of discernment. It is also a thing natural and common to all people — that is, to seek something with which to excuse ourselves when we are defeated, or when something turns out less well than we would have wished and for which we think we may be held to blame. And so it came about that as this report began to spread through the provinces of the realm, when it reached the ears of the Chancas, they were pleased to hear it and lent weight to the account, affirming that without such divine assistance they could not have been defeated by the Inca, strengthening the story with arguments that our very own inclinations teach us in similar circumstances. And they themselves gave the name pururaucas — meaning 'hidden thieves' [or 'ambush warriors'] — to this force of relief sent by the god Viracocha. Seeing the effect that this absurdity was producing, the Inca resolved to give it still more foundation by saying that the pururaucas had turned into stones which he could identify. He pointed them out and ordered that they be worshipped and that sacrifices be offered to them. Having achieved this victory, he seized the kingdom with little opposition, dispossessing his father and brother of it; and so ended the reign of Yahuar-Huacac, whose body, together with the idol he had designated during his lifetime, was found in a village called Paullu, near Calca. He was greatly revered and worshipped by the lineage and ayllu [clan] descended from him, which is called Auca Ayllu Panaca.

segments.title_inferred: Viracocha-Inca, Eighth King of Cuzco: Early Reign, Conquests, and Marriage
segments.original_text:
CAPITULO XI
De Viracocha-Inca, octavo rey

El Inca Viracocha, octavo rey del Cuzco, en poniéndose la borla, que era lo mismo que coronarse por rey, dio grandes muestras de valeroso príncipe, así en la paz como en la guerra; por lo cual fué muy amado y respetado de sus vasallos y temido de sus enemigos; porque la fama de su saber y valor llegó en breve a las provincias más apartadas. Como su padre no había sido guerrero ni tratado de conquistar nuevas tierras, halló muy caída y desestimada la milicia; y así, lo primero que procuró fué levantarla, ofreciendo grandes honras y premios a los que la quisiesen seguir, y como es ordinario en todas las naciones que aquello abrazan los súbditos a que ven inclinado a su príncipe, en comenzando Viracocha a levantar gente y nombrar capitanes y oficiales de guerra, era de ver el contento y alborozo con que todos tomaban las armas; por donde muy en breve se juntó en la ciudad del Cuzco un lucido ejército. Viéndole los grandes y señores de su corte tan animado y resuelto a emprender nuevas conquistas, y que publicaba que quería ir en persona a ellas, le aconsejaron que antes de comenzarlas tuviese por bien de casarse, para asegurar la sucesión del reino. Respondió el Inca a esta proposición, que no gustaba de hacerlo, dando por razón que ningún hombre de reputación había de tratar de tomar mujer hasta haber alcanzado en la guerra ilustres hechos; porque echaba de ver que los que se daban a mujeres se hacían cobardes y afeminados y no hacían estima de cosas de honra. Mostróse Viracocha-Inca desde sus tiernos años tan belicoso, que cuando estaba desterrado y en desgracia de su padre, solía platicar a menudo de las cosas de la guerra, mostrando con palabras de desestima el sentimiento que tenía de ver a su padre tan poco soldado que no tratase de emprender cosas dignas de memoria; y añadía en estas pláticas, que no recibiera pesar de que faltara su padre, porque tenía esperanzas que, viéndose con el cetro del reino, había de conquistar medio mundo.

Los señores y caciques de los pueblos vecinos al Cuzco no estaban sujetos a los Incas, pero tenían paz y confederación con ellos de tiempos muy antiguos; y a esta causa, los predecesores de Viracocha, por no faltar a la lealtad y fe con que estaban unidos, no se habían atrevido a moverles guerra para sojuzgarlos: mayormente por no dar ellos ocasión para ello. Por donde, puesto caso que el señorío de los Incas se extendía ya a provincias distantes del Cuzco muchas leguas, todavía no les reconocían vasallaje los sobredichos caciques sus vecinos. Mas agora, Viracocha-Inca, o porque ellos hubiesen dado alguna causa de romper la paz, o porque la buscase el mismo Inca, llevado de la ambición de sujetarlos él, les hizo tan cruda guerra, que a todos los metió debajo de su obediencia. Entre los indios hay memoria que Viracocha guerreó a estos señores, porque no sentían bien de lo que él con su padre había hecho en quitarle el reino, y de que intentase alterar las cosas de la religión, mandando que el dios Viracocha fuese preferido al sol y a los demás dioses, y que murmuraban dello. Comoquiera que haya sido, lo cierto es que el Inca los sujetó por armas, comenzando por el señor del valle de Calca, cuatro leguas del Cuzco, y los otros sus comarcanos, que habitaban las riberas del río de Yucay y todo lo que hoy es el Marquesado.

Habidas estas victorias, volvieron los grandes a suplicarle que se casase, y él condecendió con su voluntad. Buscaron en todo el reino una doncella en quien concurriesen las calidades y dotes de nobleza, honestidad y hermosura que la hiciesen digna de la honra y título de coya, o reina. Cupo esta suerte a una hija del señor de Anta, pueblo distante tres leguas del Cuzco, llamada Mama-Rancay, la cual fué llevada en andas a la corte con grande acompañamiento de los mayores señores della y aparatos de arcos que pusieron por todo el camino cubiertos de flores y paños finos, danzas, cantares y todas muestras de alegría.

Contento Viracocha con haber puesto en sujeción los caciques referidos, trató, en casándose, de continuar la guerra, cosa que grandemente deseaban los soldados, por lo mucho que medraban en ella con los sacos y despojos de los pueblos. Apercibió su ejército, y sin descubrir a los capitanes la jornada que emprendía (que fue estilo que guardó este Inca, mandando salir el ejército a una o dos leguas de la ciudad y allí publicaba...
segments.translation:
CHAPTER XI
Of Viracocha-Inca, Eighth King

The Inca Viracocha, eighth king of Cuzco, upon taking the [borla]—the royal fringe, equivalent to being crowned king—gave great demonstrations of a valiant prince, both in peace and in war; for which reason he was greatly loved and respected by his subjects and feared by his enemies, as the fame of his wisdom and valor quickly reached the most distant provinces. Since his father had not been a warrior, nor had he sought to conquer new lands, Viracocha found the military arts in a greatly diminished and disregarded state; and thus the first thing he set about doing was to restore them, offering great honors and rewards to those who wished to follow that calling. As it is common in all nations that subjects embrace whatever they see their prince inclined toward, when Viracocha began to raise troops and to appoint captains and officers of war, it was something to behold—the joy and excitement with which everyone took up arms; and so in a very short time a brilliant army assembled in the city of Cuzco. Seeing him so spirited and resolved to undertake new conquests, and that he was publicly declaring his intention to lead them in person, the great lords and nobles of his court advised him that before beginning those campaigns he should agree to marry, in order to secure the succession of the kingdom. The Inca replied to this proposition that he had no wish to do so, giving as his reason that no man of reputation should seek to take a wife until he had achieved distinguished deeds in war; for he could see that those who gave themselves over to women became cowards and effeminate, and placed no value on matters of honor. Viracocha-Inca showed himself from his earliest years to be so warlike that even when he was exiled and in disgrace with his father, he would frequently speak of matters of war, expressing with words of contempt the sorrow he felt at seeing his father so little a soldier that he did not seek to undertake deeds worthy of remembrance; and he would add in these conversations that he would not grieve if his father were gone, for he had hopes that, once he held the scepter of the kingdom, he would conquer half the world.

The lords and [caciques] of the towns neighboring Cuzco were not subject to the Incas, but had maintained peace and confederation with them from very ancient times; and for this reason, Viracocha's predecessors, not wishing to break the loyalty and good faith that bound them together, had not dared to make war upon them to subjugate them—particularly since those lords had given no occasion for it. Thus, even though the Inca dominion already extended to provinces many leagues distant from Cuzco, the aforementioned neighboring [caciques] still rendered no vassalage to it. But now, Viracocha-Inca—whether because they had given some cause for breaking the peace, or because the Inca himself sought one, driven by ambition to subjugate them—made such fierce war upon them that he brought them all under his obedience. Among the Indians there is a tradition that Viracocha waged war on these lords because they did not look favorably upon what he had done with his father in taking the kingdom from him, nor upon his attempt to alter religious matters by ordering that the god Viracocha be preferred above the sun and all the other gods, and because they murmured about it. Whatever the case may have been, what is certain is that the Inca subjugated them by force of arms, beginning with the lord of the valley of Calca, four leagues from Cuzco, and the other neighboring lords who inhabited the banks of the Yucay River and all of what is today the Marquisate [of Oropesa].

Having achieved these victories, the great lords again implored him to marry, and he yielded to their wishes. They searched throughout the entire kingdom for a maiden in whom there should be combined the qualities and endowments of nobility, virtue, and beauty that would make her worthy of the honor and title of [coya], or queen. This lot fell to a daughter of the lord of Anta, a town three leagues from Cuzco, named Mama-Rancay, who was carried on a [litter] to the court with a great retinue of the highest lords of the realm and with ceremonial arches erected all along the road, covered with flowers and fine cloths, along with dances, songs, and every demonstration of joy.

Satisfied at having brought the aforementioned [caciques] into subjection, Viracocha, upon marrying, turned his attention to continuing the war—something greatly desired by the soldiers, who profited greatly from it through the sacking and plunder of towns. He assembled his army, and without revealing to his captains the campaign he was undertaking (for it was the custom of this Inca to order the army to march one or two leagues from the city and there to make public...

segments.title_inferred: Inca Viracocha's conquests, temple at Cacha, and fate of his mummy
segments.original_text:
la empresa). Apartió él mismo en persona, como lo había hecho en las pasadas, y se encaminó para las provincias de los Canas y Canchas [Cánchis]; los cuales, aunque al principio se defendieron bien, al fin quedaron vencidos y puestos debajo de su obediencia. Fueron siempre estas dos naciones de Canas y Canchas tenidas en mucha estimación del Inca Viracocha y de sus sucesores, que les concedieron particulares insignias de honra; porque desde que dieron la obediencia a este Inca, ayudaron y sirvieron con notable esfuerzo y fidelidad en todas las guerras y conquistas que hicieron los Incas. En la provincia de los Canas, cabe el pueblo Cacha, distante diez y ocho leguas del Cuzco, edificó el Inca Viracocha un suntuoso templo, y colocó en él una estatua del Ticci-Viracocha, cuyas ruinas y paredones se ven hoy día. Es una obra de extraordinaria grandeza, con las paredes altísimas, hechas de muy grandes adobes, porque en tiempo deste rey aún no se edificaba de piedra.

Corría la fama de las hazañas de Viracocha por todas partes, y movidas por ella muchas provincias, y lo principal de temor de ser asoladas si las guerrease el Inca, le enviaban sus embajadores y presentes pidiéndole su amistad y ofreciéndole obediencia, con que vino a dilatar grandemente su reino y a ser mucho más rico que sus mayores. Tuvo grandes vajillas de oro y plata; y desde su tiempo comenzó el nombre de los Incas a ser mucho más famoso y estimado por las provincias extrañas que antes. Fundó el ayllo y parcialidad llamada Socsoc-Panaca, y dejó un ídolo que se decía Inca-Amaro, al cual señaló por hermano suyo y era muy reverenciado de su parcialidad. Estuvo depositado el cuerpo deste rey en Jaquijaguana, y teniendo noticia y rastro dél Gonzalo Pizarro, anduvo mucho tiempo buscándolo, por haber el gran tesoro que había fama estaba enterrado con él; y por descubrirle, quemó algunos indios, hombres y mujeres. Al cabo lo halló y gran suma de hacienda suya que le dieron los que lo guardaban. Hizo el dicho Pizarro quemar su cuerpo, mas los indios de su ayllo recogieron las cenizas, y con cierta confección las metieron en una tinajuela pequeña junto con el ídolo, que, como era de piedra, se lo dejaron los de Gonzalo Pizarro sin reparar en él. Después, al tiempo que el licenciado Polo andaba descubriendo los cuerpos e ídolos de los Incas, en teniendo noticia de las cenizas e ídolo déste, lo mudaron los indios de donde antes estaba, escondiéndolo en muchas partes; porque, después que lo quemó Gonzalo Pizarro, le tuvieron en mayor veneración que antes.

Ultimamente se puso tan buena diligencia, que fue hallado y sacado de poder de sus descendientes.
segments.translation:
the enterprise). He set out in person, as he had done on previous occasions, and made his way toward the provinces of the Canas and Canchas [Cánchis]; who, although they defended themselves well at first, were in the end conquered and brought under his authority. These two nations of the Canas and Canchas were always held in great esteem by Inca Viracocha and his successors, who granted them particular insignia of honor; for from the time they gave their obedience to this Inca, they aided and served with notable valor and fidelity in all the wars and conquests undertaken by the Incas. In the province of the Canas, near the town of Cacha, eighteen leagues distant from Cuzco, Inca Viracocha built a sumptuous temple and placed within it a statue of Ticci-Viracocha, whose ruins and standing walls are visible to this day. It is a work of extraordinary grandeur, with very tall walls built of very large adobe bricks, for during this king's time construction in stone had not yet begun.

The fame of Viracocha's deeds spread in all directions, and many provinces, moved partly by that fame and chiefly by fear of being laid waste should the Inca make war upon them, sent him their ambassadors and gifts, seeking his friendship and offering him obedience, whereby he came to greatly extend his kingdom and to be far wealthier than his forebears. He possessed great services of gold and silver; and from his time onward the name of the Incas began to be far more famous and esteemed among foreign provinces than before. He founded the ayllo [kin group] and faction called Socsoc-Panaca, and left behind an idol called Inca-Amaro, which he designated as his brother and was greatly venerated by his faction. The body of this king was kept [deposited] at Jaquijaguana, and Gonzalo Pizarro, having heard word of it and followed its trail, spent a long time searching for it in hope of the great treasure that was reputed to be buried with it; and in order to discover it, he had several Indians, men and women, burned. In the end he found it, together with a great sum of the king's possessions which were handed over to him by those who had been guarding it. The said Pizarro had the body burned, but the Indians of his ayllo collected the ashes and, with a certain preparation [confección], placed them in a small earthen jar together with the idol, which, being of stone, Gonzalo Pizarro's men left behind without taking notice of it. Afterward, when the Licenciado Polo was going about discovering the bodies and idols of the Incas, upon receiving word of this one's ashes and idol, the Indians moved them from where they had previously been, hiding them in many different places; for after Gonzalo Pizarro burned him, they held him in greater veneration than before.

In the end, such diligent efforts were made that it was found and taken from the possession of his descendants.

segments.title_inferred: Eastward disappearance into an unnamed province
segments.original_text: en el oriente de aquella provincia, sin que se supiese más dellos.
segments.translation: in the east of that province, without anything more being known of them.

segments.title_inferred: The Inca Armies' Campaign Against the Araucanians and the Establishment of the Maule River as the Southern Border of the Inca Empire
segments.original_text: unos y los otros, y en lo más recio della les llegó socorro a los del Inca, que fue causa desmayasen los Chilenos y que los del Inca quedasen vitoriosos. Pusiéronse en huida los Araucanos, y el ejército peruano los fué siguiendo y dando alcance y degollando a muchos dellos. Entrando por su tierra, los Araucanos se fortificaron en una angostura, y siguiéndolos los capitanes del Inca, como no sabían la tierra, se entraron sin advertir por el peligro. Hallándose ya con ventaja los Araucanos, revolvieron contra sus enemigos y encendióse la más reñida y brava batalla que jamás los Peruanos habían tenido, los cuales fueron vencidos en ella con muerte de su capitán general y la mayor parte del ejército. Los demás se retiraron destotra parte del río Maulé, que dista cuarenta leguas de la ciudad de Santiago y valle de Mapocho hacia el Mediodía. Intentaron otras veces los capitanes del Inca plantar sus banderas de esotra parte del dicho río; más los valerosos Araucanos, unidos con sus vecinos los de Tucapel y Purén, se lo estorbaron y no dieron lugar a que poseyesen los Incas un palmo de tierra de la otra parte de Maulé. Sabido por el Inca lo que pasaba, y la multitud de indios que habitaban aquellas provincias que caen al sur del río Maulé, y cuán valientemente se defendían, envió mandar a sus capitanes fortificasen la ribera septentrional del río Maulé, y que por entonces fuese frontera contra los Araucanos y la raya de su Imperio; de la cual ni entonces ni después pasó el señorío de los Incas.
segments.translation: both sides fought on, and at the height of the battle reinforcements arrived for the Inca's forces, which caused the Chileans to lose heart and left the Inca's men victorious. The Araucanians took flight, and the Peruvian army pursued them, overtaking and killing many of them. As the Peruvians entered Araucanian territory, the Araucanians fortified themselves in a narrow pass, and the Inca's captains, following in pursuit and being unfamiliar with the land, advanced without recognizing the danger. Finding themselves now at an advantage, the Araucanians turned against their enemies, and there broke out the most fiercely contested and fierce battle the Peruvians had ever fought, in which they were defeated, with the death of their captain-general and the greater part of the army. The survivors withdrew to the far side of the Maule River [río Maulé], which lies forty leagues from the city of Santiago and the valley of Mapocho toward the south. The Inca's captains attempted on other occasions to plant their banners on the far side of that river, but the valiant Araucanians, joined by their neighbors of Tucapel and Purén, blocked them and gave the Incas no opportunity to possess so much as a handsbreadth of land beyond the Maule. When the Inca learned what had transpired—the multitude of Indians inhabiting those provinces lying south of the Maule River and how valiantly they defended themselves—he sent orders to his captains to fortify the northern bank of the Maule River, which was henceforth to serve as the frontier against the Araucanians and the boundary of his Empire; and beyond that boundary the dominion of the Incas never passed, either then or afterward.

segments.title_inferred: Tupa Inca's inspection tour, conquest of the coast, death, and fate of his mummy
segments.original_text:
consentir que el Inca señorease su estado. Dio cuenta el visitador al Inca de lo que pasaba, preguntándole lo que había de hacer en aquel caso. El Inca, recebida esta nueva, se rió y dijo que las mujeres le seguían. Preguntóle la Coya que qué mujeres; y él le respondió: "Tú y esta viuda, que si no fuera por ti, yo le hiciera que no tuviera tantos bríos." La Coya entonces pidió al Inca que le diese licencia, que ella se profería de sujetarle aquella mujer sin que le costase un soldado; respondióle el Inca, que en hora buena, que hiciese lo que quisiese. Tomó a su cargo la Coya este negocio y despachó al visitador, dándole parte del camino donde pensaba guiarlo, y mandándole que dijesen a aquella cacica, cómo él tenía aviso del Inca y de la Coya que querían reservar toda aquélla provincia para ella, y que en albricias le pidiese le mandase hacer una fiesta solemne en la mar.

La viuda, creyendo ser verdad la nueva que le dio el visitador, concedió lo que le pedía y mandó para cierto día que le señaló el mismo visitador, que todos los del pueblo saliesen a la mar en sus balsas a festejarle; lo cual todo se efectuó; y estando los indios en la mar con sus instrumentos músicos y mucho regocijo bien seguros de la cautela y engaño del visitador, entraron en el pueblo dos capitanes del Inca y se apoderaron dél; lo cual visto desde la mar por la cacica y sus vasallos, no tuvieron otro medio que rendirse. Prendieron los Capitanes a la cacica y lleváronsela a presentar a la Coya.

Gastó el rey en esta visita cuatro años. Mandó acabar de edificar las fortalezas y palacios que en muchas partes estaban comenzadas y en otras hizo labrar muchas de nuevo. Llegó hasta la provincia de Quito, más de cuatrocientas leguas del Cuzco, desde donde se volvió a su corte, dejando acrecentado su reino por aquella parte del septentrión más de veinte leguas, y por la costa y por la mar, conquistando todos los valles hasta Túmbez. Con tan ilustres victorias alcanzó este Inca nombre de grande, llamándole los suyos el Gran Tupa Inca y en hecho de verdad lo fue. Murió en la ciudad del Cuzco, y su cuerpo embalsamado y bien curado se conservó entero hasta la prisión de Huascar-Inca, su nieto. Entonces lo quemaron los capitanes de Atahuallpa, Quízquiz y Chalcochima [sic]. Tuvo un ídolo que se llamó Cuxichuri, al cual hacían los indios mucha honra, juntamente con sus cenizas, que tenían guardadas en un cántaro. Fue muy rico este rey, y los sobredichos capitanes de Atahuallpa le tomaron mucha hacienda, y la demás quedó en poder de sus yanaconas.
segments.translation:
allow the Inca to lord over her domain. The inspector [visitador] reported to the Inca what was happening, asking him what he should do in that situation. The Inca, upon receiving this news, laughed and said that the women were following his lead. The Coya [queen consort] asked him what women he meant; and he replied: "You and this widow — were it not for you, I would have seen to it that she had not so much spirit." The Coya then asked the Inca for leave, offering to bring that woman to submission without it costing him a single soldier. The Inca replied, very well, let her do as she wished. The Coya took charge of the matter and dispatched the inspector, directing him along the route she intended him to follow, and instructing him to tell that chieftainess [cacica] that he had word from the Inca and the Coya that they wished to reserve that entire province for her, and that as a reward [albricias] he should ask her to command a solemn celebration to be held at sea.

The widow, believing the news the inspector brought her to be true, granted what he asked and ordered that on a certain day designated by the inspector himself, all the people of the town should go out to sea on their rafts [balsas] to celebrate; and all of this came to pass. While the Indians were at sea with their musical instruments and great rejoicing, wholly unsuspecting of the inspector's cunning and deceit, two of the Inca's captains entered the town and seized it. When the chieftainess and her subjects saw this from the sea, they had no recourse but to surrender. The captains arrested the chieftainess and brought her to present before the Coya.

The king spent four years on this inspection tour [visita]. He ordered the completion of the fortresses and palaces that had been begun in many places, and in other places he had many new ones built. He reached the province of Quito, more than four hundred leagues from Cuzco, from where he returned to his court, having extended his kingdom to the north by more than twenty leagues, and along the coast and by sea, conquering all the valleys as far as Túmbez. With such illustrious victories this Inca earned the name of the Great, his people calling him the Great Tupa Inca — and in truth he was. He died in the city of Cuzco, and his embalmed and well-preserved body remained intact until the imprisonment of Huascar Inca, his grandson. At that time it was burned by the captains of Atahuallpa, Quízquiz and Chalcochima [sic]. He had an idol called Cuxichuri, which the Indians held in great honor, together with his ashes, which were kept in a jar [cántaro]. This king was very wealthy, and the aforementioned captains of Atahuallpa seized much of his estate, and the remainder passed into the possession of his yanaconas [personal retainers/servants].

segments.title_inferred: Discovery of the mummified body of the Inca Huayna Cápac and his veneration among the Indians
segments.original_text:
s indios deste rey, que era muy amigo de los pobres y que mandaba que se tuviese muy gran cuidado dellos en todo su reino, que era muy afable con los suyos y bebía más que tres indios juntos, sin que jamás lo viesen fuera de su acuerdo; y que, preguntado de sus privados ¿cómo bebiendo tanto no se emborrachaba?, decía que porque bebía por los pobres, de cuyo sustento él mucho cuidaba.

Entrados los españoles en esta tierra, hicieron grandes diligencias para descubrir su cuerpo, y aun no pocas violencias, por la fama de que tenía gran tesoro y que había de estar enterrado con su cuerpo o en los lugares que en vida más frecuentaba, porque ésta era costumbre antigua entre ellos. Al fin, por gran solicitud que se puso, y no con poco trabajo, fué hallado al tiempo que los cuerpos de los otros Incas. Hállose en el camino de la fortaleza, en una casa donde pareció haberle llevado la noche antes; que como los españoles iban ya por el rastro dándole alcance, los indios que lo guardaban lo mudaron a muchas partes, y con traerlo con tanta priesa y sobresaltos de unos lugares a otros, siempre lo mudaban con cinco o seis ídolos en su compañía, a quienes hacían gran veneración, porque estaban persuadidos que entendían en la guarda del cuerpo del Inca.

El ídolo principal que tuvo en vida se decía Guaraquinga; era grande y de oro puro, mas no estaba con su cuerpo ni pareció. Hicieron los indios grandes llantos en el descubrimiento, el cual se halló envuelto en muchas mantas de las muy finas y mucho algodón. Estaba entero y bien curado y conservado. Trújose a esta ciudad de Lima con los otros, y particularmente con el de su madre, que fué descubierto al mismo tiempo; y era tenido de los indios en grande estima, porque su hijo lo había introducido en mucha veneración y por su mandado se le hacían frecuentes sacrificios y tenía mucha gente de servicio. Estaba su cuerpo más bien curado que todos, porque no parecía estar muerto, y solos los ojos tenía postizos, tan bien hechos, que parecían naturales. Cuéntase comúnmente Huayna Cápac por el último rey de los Incas; lo uno por haber sido el postrero que poseyó el reino entero y pacífico; y lo otro porque muy poco después de su muerte entraron los españoles conquistando esta tierra y apoderándose della, de que los Incas, que por algún tiempo sustentaron el título de reyes, lo fueron más que de sólo nombre.
segments.translation:
The Indians of this king [reported] that he was a great friend of the poor and ordered that very great care be taken of them throughout his kingdom; that he was very affable with his people and drank more than three Indians together, yet was never seen out of his senses. When asked by his intimates how he could drink so much without becoming drunk, he replied that it was because he drank on behalf of the poor, for whose sustenance he cared greatly.

Once the Spaniards had entered this land, they made great efforts to discover his body, and even resorted to no small violence, on account of the rumor that he possessed great treasure that would be buried with his body or in the places he most frequently visited during his lifetime, for this was an ancient custom among them. In the end, through great diligence and no little labor, it was found at the same time as the bodies of the other Incas. It was found on the road to the fortress, in a house to which it appeared to have been brought the night before; for as the Spaniards were already on the trail closing in, the Indians who guarded it moved it to many different places, and in carrying it in such haste and alarm from one place to another, they always moved it in the company of five or six idols, to whom they showed great veneration, being persuaded that these idols were responsible for guarding the body of the Inca.

The principal idol he had possessed in life was called Guaraquinga; it was large and of pure gold, but it was not found with his body, nor did it ever appear. The Indians made great lamentations at the discovery. The body was found wrapped in many of the finest [woven] cloaks [mantas] and much cotton. It was intact, well treated and preserved. It was brought to this city of Lima together with the others, and particularly with that of his mother, which was discovered at the same time; it was held in great esteem by the Indians, because his son had introduced it to great veneration and, by his orders, frequent sacrifices were made to it and it had many people in service. His body was better preserved than all the others, for it did not appear to be dead, and only his eyes were artificial [postizos], so well made that they appeared natural. Huayna Cápac is commonly counted as the last king of the Incas: first, because he was the last to possess the kingdom whole and at peace; and second, because very shortly after his death the Spaniards entered, conquering this land and taking possession of it, so that those Incas who for some time maintained the title of kings were kings in little more than name alone.

segments.title_inferred: Burial of Atahualpa and His Descendants
segments.original_text:
Hualpa en el pueblo de Cajamarca, y después lo enterraron con mucha honra y le pusieron una cruz encima, por haberse hecho cristiano antes de morir. Pero sus indios le desenterraron secretamente y llevaron a enterrar a sus huacas. Dejó Ataúhualpa sucesión, cuyos nietos, llamados don Diego Hilaquita, don Francisco Hilaquita y don Juan Ninancoro, vivían ahora cuarenta años estando yo en la ciudad del Cuzco.

(24) Choque-lluipay o Chuqui-Huipay.
segments.translation:
Hualpa in the town of Cajamarca, and afterward they buried him with great honor and placed a cross upon [his grave], because he had become a Christian before dying. But his Indians secretly disinterred him and took him to be buried at their huacas [sacred shrines]. Atahualpa left descendants; his grandsons, called don Diego Hilaquita, don Francisco Hilaquita, and don Juan Ninancoro, were living some forty years ago when I was in the city of Cuzco.

(24) Choque-lluipay or Chuqui-Huipay.

segments.title_inferred: Manco Inca's Successors and the Elevation of Paullu Inca as Spanish-Sponsored Ruler
segments.original_text:
bamba, lo pusieron en el templo del sol, adonde fué hallado por los españoles en el tiempo del virrey don Francisco de Toledo, conquistada aquella provincia. Dejó Manco-Inca tres hijos: Sayr-Titu, Cusi-Tito-Yupanqui y Tupac-Amara, y una hija que se llamó Cusi-Huarcay.

Después que Manco-Inca se retiró a Vilcabamba, dieron los españoles acá fuera la borla y título de Inca a su hermano Paullu, hijo menor de Huayna-Capac, que haciéndose cristiano en tiempo del gobernador Vaca de Castro, y por consejo y persuasión suya, tomó el nombre del gobernador y se llamó don Cristóbal Paullu-Tupa. Fue gran servidor de Su Majestad, y favoreció siempre al partido de los españoles contra su hermano Manco-Inca. Confirmó esta elección el Rey y concedió al nuevo Inca escudo de armas con el águila imperial, y en un cuartel del escudo la borla que usaban los reyes Incas por insignia y corona real, y en otro un árbol con dos dragones o serpientes coronadas, que eran las armas y divisa de sus mayores. Tuvo dos hijos legítimos, llamados don Carlos Inca y don Felipe Manco-Tupa. Don Carlos casó con una señora española (27), y de ella tuvo a don Melchor Inca, que murió en España. Dejó también don Cristóbal Paullu-Tupa dos hijos naturales dichos don Fernando Puma-Capi y don Alonso Tupa-Atan; a este último conocí yo en el Cuzco y le comuniqué mucho; el cual, tratando de los descendientes que han quedado del linaje de los Incas, me certificó que había dentro del Cuzco como cuatrocientos varones. Aunque Paullu-Inca murió cristiano y como tal fue enterrado en la iglesia, con todo eso, los indios le hicieron una estatua pequeña y le pusieron algunas uñas y cabellos que secretamente le quitaron; la cual estatua hallé tan venerada de ellos como cualquiera de los otros cuerpos de los reyes Incas.
segments.translation:
...bamba, they placed it [the body or effigy] in the Temple of the Sun, where it was found by the Spaniards at the time of the Viceroy Don Francisco de Toledo, after that province had been conquered. Manco Inca left three sons: Sayr-Titu, Cusi-Tito-Yupanqui, and Tupac Amaru, and a daughter named Cusi-Huarcay.

After Manco Inca withdrew to Vilcabamba, the Spaniards on this side [i.e., in Spanish-controlled territory] bestowed the [royal] fringe [borla] and the title of Inca upon his brother Paullu, a younger son of Huayna Capac, who, having converted to Christianity at the time of Governor Vaca de Castro, and by his counsel and persuasion, took the governor's name and called himself Don Cristóbal Paullu-Tupa. He was a great servant of His Majesty and always favored the Spanish side against his brother Manco Inca. The King confirmed this appointment and granted the new Inca a coat of arms bearing the imperial eagle, and in one quarter of the shield the fringe [borla] that the Inca kings used as their insignia and royal crown, and in another quarter a tree with two crowned dragons or serpents, which were the arms and device of his ancestors. He had two legitimate sons, named Don Carlos Inca and Don Felipe Manco-Tupa. Don Carlos married a Spanish noblewoman (27), and by her had Don Melchor Inca, who died in Spain. Don Cristóbal Paullu-Tupa also left two natural [illegitimate] sons called Don Fernando Puma-Capi and Don Alonso Tupa-Atan; this last one I knew personally in Cuzco and had considerable dealings with him. He, speaking of the descendants who remained of the lineage of the Incas, assured me that there were some four hundred males within Cuzco itself. Although Paullu Inca died a Christian and was buried as such in the church, the Indians nonetheless made a small statue of him and placed upon it some nails and hair that they had secretly removed from him; which statue I found venerated by them as much as any of the other bodies [mummies] of the Inca kings.

segments.title_inferred: Inca Religious and Political Justifications for Conquest
segments.original_text:
dellos se pobló el mundo; sobre lo cual traían mil fábulas y disparates; finalmente, comoquiera que sea, ellos afirman que todas las gentes proceden dellos y de su generación, y que por esto les debían veneración y servicio, y que lo habían de dar.

De otra fábula semejante a ésta tomaron también ocasión para apoyar sus intentos, diciendo que eran hijos del sol enviados por él al mundo para que enseñasen a los hombres cómo le habían de servir y honrar. En efecto, el título de religión fué uno de los principales con que guerreaban las gentes, y conforme a esto, a todos los que metían debajo de su dominio, no solamente pretendían tenerlos en sujeción y por súbditos, sino también los compelían a admitir sus ídolos y tener enteramente sus opiniones y desprenderse de sus ritos y ceremonias, y a guardar en todo la misma orden en las huacas y adoratorios que se tenía en el Cuzco. Demás desto, quitaban a los vencidos las huacas principales de sus provincias y las traían al Cuzco, creyendo las tendrían con esto favorables para mantener aquellas tierras de donde eran; ni les faltaba buena diligencia para poner en ejecución todo lo dicho, ni castigo para los que no lo guardaban. Y es averiguado, que cuando algunas provincias se rebelaban, no sólo negaban la obediencia a los Incas, pero también contradecían la religión: y éste era el principal título que después llevaban los Incas para tornarlos a conquistar, y la ocasión con que hacían castigos tan rigurosos que los mismos indios cuentan.
segments.translation:
From them [the Incas] the world was peopled; concerning which they told a thousand fables and absurdities. In short, whatever the case may be, they affirm that all peoples descend from them and from their lineage, and that for this reason all owed them veneration and service, and were bound to render it.

From another fable similar to this one they likewise took occasion to support their aims, declaring that they were children of the Sun, sent by him into the world to teach men how they were to serve and honor him. Indeed, the title of religion was one of the principal pretexts with which they waged war upon peoples; and in accordance with this, of all those whom they brought under their dominion, they not only sought to hold in subjection as subjects, but also compelled them to accept their idols, to adopt their beliefs entirely, to abandon their own rites and ceremonies, and to observe in every respect the same order in their huacas [sacred shrines] and places of worship as was maintained in Cuzco. Beyond this, they removed from the conquered peoples the principal huacas of their provinces and brought them to Cuzco, believing that by this means they would keep those sacred objects favorably disposed toward maintaining their hold over the lands from which they came. Nor did they lack diligence in carrying out all of the foregoing, nor punishment for those who failed to comply. And it is established that when certain provinces rebelled, they not only denied obedience to the Incas but also rejected the religion; and this was the principal title the Incas subsequently invoked for reconquering them, and the occasion upon which they inflicted punishments so severe as those which the Indians themselves recount.

segments.title_inferred: Inca Policy of Resettlement: The Mitimae System
segments.original_text:
CAPITULO XXIII

Cómo disponían los Incas las tierras que de nuevo adquirían, plantando en ellos indios extraños, que llamaban mitimaes, y las diferencias que había dellos

Todo el imperio de los Incas, aunque tan extendido y compuesto de tantas y tan diversas naciones, era una sola república, regida por unas mismas leyes, fueros y costumbres, y observantes de una misma religión, ritos y ceremonias, puesto caso que cada una de esas provincias, antes de ser reducidas a su obediencia, tenía sus fueros y modo de vivir y gobernarse diferente; y por esta unión y uniformidad que en todas partes se guardaba, se ha de entender que lo que aquí dijéremos que introducían en las naciones que sujetaban, ese mismo modo de gobierno era el que se guardaba en su corte y en lo que de antes señoreaban.

Lo primero que hacían estos reyes en ganando una provincia, era sacar della seis o siete mil familias (más o menos, como les parecía, atento a la capacidad y disposición que hallaban) y llevarlas a otras partes de las provincias quietas y pacíficas, repartiéndolas por diversos pueblos; y en trueque dellas, metían otra tanta gente, que hacían salir de los lugares adonde asentaban los primeros, o de la parte que les parecía, y entre ellos muchos orejones de la sangre real. Estos tales que se avecindaban en las nuevas tierras, eran llamados mitimaes, que es tanto como decir hombres advenedizos y extraños a diferencia de los naturales; el cual nombre comprehendía no menos a los vasallos nuevos que a los antiguos que con ellos se trocaban, pues los unos y los otros pasaban de sus propias tierras a las extrañas; y el día de hoy lo entendemos desta suerte, llamando mitimaes a todos los advenedizos que están poblados en todas las provincias deste reino. Atendíase en esta transmigración a que los que se trasladaban, así de los recién conquistados como de los otros, no se mudasen a cualesquier tierras, así a poco más o menos, sino a las que fuesen del mismo temple y calidades o muy conformes a las que dejaban y en que se habían criado. Por manera que a los que eran naturales de tierra fría, los pasaban a tierras frías, y a los de tierras calientes, a tierras calientes; para que desta suerte sintiesen menos la mudanza de su naturaleza y se hallasen más sanos en las nuevas tierras, sin ser probados dellas, como lo fueran si los llevaran a las de contrario temperamento que las suyas. A los que así mudaba el Inca, los sacaba de la obediencia de sus caciques antiguos, mandándoles estuviesen sujetos a los de las tierras donde los plantaba; en las cuales mandaba dar a los unos y a los otros mitimaes sitios para labrar sus casas y tierras en que hiciesen sus chácaras y sementeras, y que quedasen por moradores perpetuos de los pueblos en que los ponía, guardando los usos y modo de vivir de los naturales dellos, salvo que se quedaban con el traje, divisas y señales de los de su nación y provincia; lo cual han conservado hasta el tiempo presente, en que por las cosas dichas conocemos en cada pueblo los que son naturales dél o mitimaes.

Introdujo el Inca esta mudanza de vecindad, para tener con más facilidad quieto y seguro su señorío; porque, considerando que la ciudad del Cuzco, cabeza dél, donde tenía su corte y residencia, estaba tan distante de las últimas provincias, en que había muchas naciones de gentes bárbaras y belicosas, juzgó que no podría de otro modo mantenerlas en paz y obediencia; y como era éste el fin principal a que se ordenaba este medio, mandaba que la mayor parte de los mitimaes que hacía ir a los pueblos recién sujetados, asentasen en las cabeceras de provincias, para que fuesen como guarnición y presidio, no por vía de sueldo ni por tiempo limitado, sino perpetuamente ellos y sus descendientes; y como a gente de guerra les daba algunos privilegios con que pareciesen más nobles, y les ordenaba que estuviesen siempre muy obedientes a cuanto sus capitanes y gobernadores les mandasen. Con esta traza e industria, si los naturales se rebelaban, estando estos mitimaes a devoción de los gobernadores, eran luego reducidos a la obediencia del Inca; y si los mitimaes tumultuaban y levantaban algún motín, eran reprimidos y castigados por los naturales; y así, mediante esta disposición de hacer estuviese avecindada la mayor parte de su gente los unos en tierras de los otros, tenía el Rey seguros sus estados que no se le rebelasen, y el trato y comercio de unas provincias con otras era más frecuente y toda la tierra más bien proveída de lo que era necesario. Otrosí, con esta traslación de sus vasallos de unas partes a otras, tiraban los Incas a que hubiese en todo su imperio similitud y conformidad en las cosas de la religión y gobierno político, y todas las naciones dél aprendiesen y hablasen la lengua del Cuzco, que por esta vía vino a ser general en todo el Perú; porque, con esta mudanza de domicilios, los nuevamente conquistados, que eran trasladados adentro del reino, aprendían todo esto con brevedad y sin pesadumbre ni apremio, y los vasallos antiguos que se avecindaban por mitimaes en lo que de nuevo se pacificaba, la enseñaban a los naturales, en que se ponía gran cuidado y eran compelidos a ello; porque a todos obligaban los Incas a recebir su lengua, leyes y religión, con todas las opiniones tocantes a estas cosas que estaban establecidas en el Cuzco, quitándoles los usos y ritos que antes tenían, o del todo o en parte, y haciéndoles recebir los suyos; y para con más efecto introducir y asentar estas cosas, ultra de la trasmutación de hombres dicha, hacían quitar a la provincia que conquistaban el ídolo principal que te[nían...]
segments.translation:
CHAPTER XXIII

How the Incas Organized Newly Acquired Lands by Settling Them with Foreign Indians Called Mitimaes, and the Distinctions Among Them

The entire Inca empire, extensive as it was and composed of so many diverse nations, constituted a single commonwealth [república], governed by the same laws, statutes [fueros], and customs, and observing the same religion, rites, and ceremonies — notwithstanding that each of its provinces, before being brought under Inca obedience, had its own statutes and its own distinct manner of living and governing itself. By reason of this unity and uniformity maintained everywhere, it should be understood that whatever we here describe as having been introduced among the nations they subjected was the same mode of government that was observed at their court and in the territories they had ruled from earlier times.

The first thing these kings did upon conquering a province was to remove from it six or seven thousand families — more or fewer, as they saw fit, having regard to the capacity and condition they found there — and transfer them to other parts of the peaceful and pacified provinces, distributing them among various towns. In exchange for these, they brought in an equal number of people drawn from the places where the first group had settled, or from whatever part seemed appropriate, and among them many orejones ["big-ears," members] of the royal blood. Those who thus took up residence in the new lands were called mitimaes [mitimaes], a term meaning, in effect, men who are newcomers and strangers, as distinguished from the native inhabitants [naturales]. This name applied no less to the newly conquered subjects than to the old vassals who were exchanged with them, since both groups were moving from their own lands to foreign ones. To this day we understand the term in this sense, calling mitimaes all newcomers who are settled throughout the provinces of this kingdom. Care was taken in this resettlement that those who were transferred — whether recently conquered or otherwise — should not be moved to just any lands indiscriminately, but specifically to lands of the same climate [temple] and character, or very similar to those they were leaving and in which they had been raised. Thus those who were natives of cold lands were moved to cold lands, and those from warm lands to warm lands, so that they might feel the change from their native conditions as little as possible and remain in better health in the new lands, without being harmed by them, as they would have been had they been taken to regions of a contrary temperament to their own. Those whom the Inca thus relocated were removed from the authority of their former caciques [local lords] and ordered to be subject to the lords of the lands where they were planted. In these lands, he commanded that plots be given to both groups of mitimaes on which to build their houses and fields [chácaras and sementeras] in which to grow their crops, and that they should remain as permanent inhabitants [moradores perpetuos] of the towns where they were placed, observing the customs and way of life of the local inhabitants — except that they retained their own dress, insignia [divisas], and identifying marks of their own nation and province. This they have preserved to the present day, by which signs we can identify in each town those who are native to it and those who are mitimaes.

The Inca introduced this resettlement policy in order to maintain his dominion more easily in peace and security. Recognizing that the city of Cuzco, the capital, where he held his court and resided, was so distant from the outermost provinces — which contained many nations of barbarous and warlike peoples — he judged that he could not otherwise keep them in peace and obedience. Since this was the principal end to which this measure was directed, he ordered that the greater part of the mitimaes sent to newly subjected towns should settle in the provincial capitals [cabeceras de provincias], so that they would serve as a garrison [guarnición] and garrison force [presidio] — not for pay, nor for a fixed term, but perpetually, they and their descendants. As soldiers [gente de guerra], he granted them certain privileges that made them appear more distinguished, and he ordered that they should at all times be fully obedient to whatever their captains and governors commanded. By this scheme and contrivance, if the native inhabitants rebelled, these mitimaes, being devoted to the governors, would promptly reduce them to obedience to the Inca; and if the mitimaes rioted or stirred up some uprising, they were suppressed and punished by the native inhabitants. Thus, through this arrangement of having the greater part of his people settled in one another's lands, the King kept his dominions secure against rebellion, and the trade and commerce between provinces became more frequent, and all the land was better supplied with what was necessary.

Furthermore, by thus transferring his vassals from place to place, the Incas aimed to bring about throughout their empire a similarity and uniformity in matters of religion and political government, and to have all the nations within it learn and speak the language of Cuzco, which by this means came to be universal throughout Peru. For through this change of domiciles, the newly conquered — who were transferred into the interior of the kingdom — learned all these things quickly and without hardship or compulsion; while the old vassals who settled as mitimaes in the newly pacified regions taught it to the native inhabitants, and great care was taken in this and they were compelled to it. For the Incas obliged all to receive their language, laws, and religion, together with all the beliefs pertaining to these matters as established in Cuzco, removing from them the customs and rites they had previously held, either entirely or in part, and compelling them to adopt their own. And in order to introduce and establish these things more effectively, in addition to the aforementioned transfer of peoples, they caused the principal idol [ídolo principal] of each conquered province to be removed… [text breaks off]

segments.title_inferred: The Inca System of Mitimaes: Resettlement Policies, Colonial Redefinition, and Agricultural Colonies
segments.original_text:
nían, y ponerlo en el Cuzco con el mismo aparato y culto que solía tener en ella, y que de todo eso le proveyesen sus naturales, ni más ni menos que cuando tenían en su provincia el dicho ídolo y huaca; y por razón desto, residían siempre en aquella ciudad y corte indios de todas las provincias del reino, ocupados en la guarda y ministerio de sus ídolos, adonde aprendían los usos y costumbres de los cortesanos; y como se mudaban por sus mitas y turnos, vueltos a su patria, guardaban y enseñaban a los suyos cuanto habían visto y aprendido en la corte.

No se reparaba, en estas mudanzas de mitimaes, en la distancia que había de sus tierras adonde los mandaban ir, aunque fuese muy grande; antes sucedía no pocas veces trasplantarlos de un extremo a otro del reino, otras trescientas y a cuatrocientas leguas más o menos, como al Príncipe se le antojaba; por donde vemos hoy en las provincias del Collao mitimaes naturales y originarios de las de Chinchaysuyu, y en éstas muchos indios de aquélla. Ello es cosa averiguada que estaban tan mezclados y revueltos los de distintas provincias, que apenas hay valle o pueblo en todo el Perú adonde no haya algún aylla y parcialidad de mitimaes.

A dos cosas principalmente tenía consideración el Inca en el mudar a sus súbditos: la primera (como queda dicho), a que no pasasen a temple contrario a el de su naturaleza, y la otra, a que las provincias todas de su Imperio estuviesen bien pobladas y bastecidas de mantenimientos y de todo lo necesario para la vida humana; y a este fin, metía gente de fuera en las poco pobladas, y de las que tenían más de la que se podía sustentar cómodamente, sacaba colonias que poblasen aquéllas; y éstos que por mandado del rey dejaban su patria y sujeción de sus caciques y se avecindaban en tierras extrañas, dando juntamente la obediencia a los caciques déllas, son los que propiamente se decían mitimaes en tiempo de los Incas. Pero después que los españoles ocuparon esta tierra, se ha extendido este nombre a otros que antiguamente no eran mitimaes propiamente, conviene a saber, a los indios que por orden de sus caciques y con licencia suya o del Inca, estaban de asiento fuera de sus pueblos y provincias, en distritos de otros caciques, aunque no sujetos a ellos, sino a los de la provincia de donde habían salido o eran oriundos. Para declaración desto, se ha de presuponer una costumbre antigua desta gente, y es que cuando alguna provincia era estéril de comidas, señaladamente de su pan, que era el maíz, y aparejada para otros aprovechamientos, cuales son todas las del Collao, que por su gran frialdad no llevan maíz ni otras semillas ni frutas de tierra templada, pero son abundantísimas de pastos y muy a propósito para criar ganados, y producen papas, de que hacen chuño, que les sirve de pan, y otras algunas raíces, a los habitantes, pues, de las tales provincias tenía el Inca señaladas tierras en los valles calientes de la costa de la mar, que les caen a un lado, y en esotra bajada de la serranía hacia los Andes, en los valles templados que por allí hay, en que sembrasen las cosas de que se carecía en sus pueblos; y por estar estos valles distantes de su tierra a veinte, a treinta más leguas, y no poder acudir a sembrarlos de comunidad, como lo hacían en lo restante del reino, tenían cuidado los caciques de dar a sus tiempos gente que lo hiciese, los cuales, cogida su cosecha, se tornaban con ella a sus pueblos. Fuera déstos, había, por orden del Inca, salida de cada pueblo, cierta cantidad de indios con sus mujeres y casas, para residir de asiento con sus hijos y descendientes, como residían, en los dichos valles, para entender en la guarda y beneficio de las chácaras de sus caciques y comunidades. Estos, aunque vivían en tierra ajena, eran sujetos a sus caciques y no a los de la tierra donde residían; pero entrados los españoles en este reino, al tiempo que la primera vez se visitó la tierra para repartirla y encomendarla en los pobladores, a estos indios que se hallaron en los valles dichos puestos en ellos por sus caciques para el efecto dicho, los contaron y repartieron con los naturales del distrito en que estaban, sacándolos de la obediencia de sus caciques antiguos y sujetándolos a aquellos en cuya tierra y jurisdicción habitaban; y consiguientemente los dieron en encomienda al mismo que repartieron el tal distrito y no al encomendero del cacicazgo de donde ellos eran naturales. A todos éstos, pues, que por el modo dicho se habían quedado en las tierras en que los hallamos, damos también agora nombre de mitimaes, sin diferencia de los primeros que sólo lo solían ser en tiempo de los Incas, y no esotros.
segments.translation:
they had [the idol brought], and placed it in Cuzco with the same ceremony and worship it had customarily received there, and that their own native people should provide for all of this in no different manner than when they had kept the said idol and huaca [sacred object or place] in their own province. For this reason, there always resided in that city and court Indians from every province of the kingdom, occupied in the keeping and ministry of their idols, where they learned the customs and usages of the court; and as they rotated through their mitas [obligatory labor turns] and shifts, upon returning to their homeland they preserved and taught their own people all that they had seen and learned at court.

In these transfers of mitimaes [resettled colonists], no account was taken of the distance between their own lands and the place to which they were ordered to go, however great it might be; on the contrary, it happened not infrequently that they were transplanted from one end of the kingdom to the other, sometimes three or four hundred leagues more or less, as it pleased the Prince. This is why we see today in the provinces of the Collao mitimaes who are natives and originals of those of Chinchaysuyu, and in those provinces many Indians from the Collao. It is an established fact that the people of different provinces were so intermingled and intermixed that there is scarcely a valley or town in all of Peru where there is not some aylla [kin group or community unit] and faction of mitimaes.

The Inca had two principal considerations in mind when relocating his subjects: the first (as has already been said) was that they should not be moved to a climate contrary to their native one, and the other was that all the provinces of his Empire should be well populated and supplied with provisions and everything necessary for human life. To this end, he would bring people from outside into sparsely populated areas, and from those regions that had more people than could comfortably be sustained, he would draw colonies to populate those others. And these people who, by the king's command, left their homeland and submission to their caciques [chiefs] and settled in foreign lands, giving their obedience to the caciques of those places, are those who in the time of the Incas were properly called mitimaes. But since the Spaniards occupied this land, this name has been extended to others who in former times were not properly mitimaes — namely, to those Indians who, by order of their caciques and with their permission or that of the Inca, resided permanently outside their own towns and provinces, in the districts of other caciques, though not subject to those caciques but rather to those of the province from which they had come or were native. To explain this, one must presuppose an ancient custom of these people: when a province was barren with respect to foodstuffs, most notably with respect to their bread, which was maize — and yet well suited to other uses, as are all those of the Collao, which on account of their great cold do not bear maize, nor other seeds or fruits of temperate land, but are exceedingly rich in pastures and very suitable for raising livestock, and produce potatoes [papas] from which they make chuño [freeze-dried potato], which serves them as bread, along with certain other roots — to the inhabitants of such provinces, the Inca had designated lands in the warm valleys of the seacoast that lie on one side of their territory, and on the other slope of the mountain range toward the Andes, in the temperate valleys found there, where they might sow the things that were lacking in their own towns. And because these valleys lay twenty to thirty or more leagues from their lands, and they could not attend to sowing them communally as they did in the rest of the kingdom, the caciques took care to send people at the appropriate times to do so; and these, once they had gathered their harvest, returned with it to their towns. Beyond these, there were, by order of the Inca, a certain number of Indians sent out from each town with their wives and households to reside permanently with their children and descendants — as they indeed did — in the said valleys, in order to attend to the maintenance and improvement of the chácaras [agricultural plots or fields] of their caciques and communities. These people, although they lived in foreign land, were subject to their own caciques and not to those of the land where they resided. But once the Spaniards had entered this kingdom, at the time the land was first surveyed in order to be divided and granted in encomienda [labor and tribute grant] to the settlers, those Indians who were found in the said valleys, having been placed there by their caciques for the aforementioned purpose, were counted and distributed together with the native inhabitants of the district in which they were found, being removed from the obedience of their former caciques and subjected to those in whose land and jurisdiction they dwelt; and consequently they were given in encomienda to the same grantee to whom the said district was assigned, and not to the encomendero of the chiefdom from which they were native. All of these, therefore, who in the manner described had remained in the lands where we find them, are now also called mitimaes, without distinction from the former, who alone were properly so called in the time of the Incas, and not the latter.

segments.title_inferred: Distinguishing Marks and Headdresses of Andean Nations Under Inca Rule
segments.original_text:
en sus pueblos sin poder salir déllos ni andar vagueando o hacer viajes por tierras extrañas sin licencia de sus caciques.

Tenían los de cada nación y provincia hombres y mujeres, sus señales y divisas por donde eran conocidos, y no podían andar sin ellas ni trocarlas con las de otra nación, so graves penas. Estas traían en el vestido con diferentes listas y colores; y los hombres otra en la cabeza más señalada, diferenciándose cada nación en el tocado; porque, así como todos andaban con cabello largo, unos lo traían cortado por bajo de las orejas y otros muy largo; unos trenzado y otros suelto, y los más vendado o ceñido con diversas suertes de ligaduras. Los Cañares, que eran los naturales de Tumibamba, se ponían en las cabezas una corona redonda de palo a manera de aro de cedazo o ruedo de cajeta de conserva. Los de Cajamarca traían cogido el cabello con una honda; y sus vecinos, con unos cordones delgados de lana colorada con muchas vueltas. Los de Guaylas unos rodetes en las cabezas, que ellos llamaban pillos, y con hondas muy blancas alrededor. Los de Bombón, unas toquillas pintadas de amarillo y colorado alrededor de las cabezas. Los de Jauja, unas fajas coloradas del ancho de una mano. Los de Huaylas liaban la cabeza con unas cuerdas de lana que les venían a caer por debajo de la barba. Los del Cuzco y todos los del linaje de los Incas, usaban de cierta ligadura llamada llanto, que era una cinta de lana del ancho de un dedo con algunas vueltas. Los Collas traían unos bonetes de lana justos pero ahusados, porque así se amoldaban las cabezas; y sus mujeres unos bonetes puntiagudos al talle de capillas de frailes; sacando estos Collas, el tocado de todas las otras naciones era en forma de guirnalda, y los más de cordones y ramales con muchas vueltas formados como una madeja de hilo del tamaño de la cabeza; y se diferenciaban en que unos eran de lana, otros de cabuya (que es su cáñamo), unos más delgados que otros, y unos de un color y otros de otro; con otras diversidades que hacían se diferenciasen los de cada provincia; las cuales divisas son pocos los que ya las usan, porque todos van entrando en el uso de nuestros sombreros.

Eran tan conocidos por estas señales, que en viendo cualquiera indio, o viniendo a la presencia del Inca, echaba de ver de qué nación y provincia era; y no hay duda sino que fue ingeniosa invención ésta para distinguirse unos de otros, porque, siendo casi innumerables las naciones diversas que se congregaban a cualquiera llamamiento general del rey, y siendo, como son, todos los indios desbarbados, de un color, aspecto y facciones, y usando de una misma lengua y traje, fuera imposible poderse distinguir de otra suerte los de cada nación. Dejado aparte, que, cuando iban a la guerra era muy de ver un ejército numeroso compuesto de tanta variedad de gentes como marchaba, llevando su lugar distinto los de cada nación, repartidos en varios tercios y escuadrones, que con estas divisas campeaba de lejos la variedad; y eran fácilmente conocidos de su general y de los demás oficiales del campo; y en la batalla no se podía ocultar la nación que más valor mostraba.
segments.translation:
in their towns without being able to leave them or wander about or make journeys through foreign lands without the permission of their [caciques] [local lords].

The men and women of each nation and province had their own marks and [divisas] [insignia] by which they were known, and they were not permitted to go about without them nor to exchange them for those of another nation, under severe penalties. These they wore on their clothing in different stripes and colors; and the men wore another mark more prominently on the head, with each nation distinguished by its headdress [tocado]. For although all of them went about with long hair, some wore it cut below the ears and others very long; some braided and others loose, and most bound or wrapped with various kinds of ties [ligaduras]. The Cañares, who were the native people of Tumibamba, placed on their heads a round wooden crown in the manner of a sieve-hoop or the rim of a jar of preserves. Those of Cajamarca gathered their hair with a sling [honda]; and their neighbors with thin cords of red wool wound many times around. Those of Guaylas wore circular pads [rodetes] on their heads, which they called pillos, with very white slings wrapped around them. Those of Bombón wore head-cloths [toquillas] painted in yellow and red around their heads. Those of Jauja wore red bands [fajas] the width of a hand. Those of Huaylas bound their heads with wool cords that came down beneath their chins. Those of Cuzco and all of the lineage of the Incas used a certain binding called llanto [mourning-band], which was a wool ribbon the width of a finger wound several times around. The Collas wore tight but tapering wool bonnets [bonetes], because they molded their heads to that shape from infancy; and their women wore pointed bonnets resembling the hoods of friars. Setting aside these Collas, the headdress of all the other nations was in the form of a garland, most of them made of cords and strands wound many times and formed like a skein of thread fitted to the size of the head; and they differed in that some were of wool, others of cabuya (which is their hemp [cáñamo]), some thinner than others, and some of one color and others of another; with other variations that distinguished those of each province from one another. Very few people still use these [divisas] today, for everyone is gradually adopting the use of our [Spanish] hats.

They were so well identified by these marks that upon seeing any Indian, or when one came into the presence of the Inca, it was immediately apparent from which nation and province he came. There is no doubt that this was an ingenious invention for distinguishing one from another, because, given that the diverse nations that gathered at any general summons of the king were almost innumerable, and given that all Indians are beardless, of one color, appearance, and features, and use the same language and dress, it would have been impossible to distinguish those of each nation by any other means. Beyond this, when they went to war it was a remarkable sight to see a great army composed of such a variety of peoples marching together, each nation keeping its distinct place, divided into various tercios [infantry regiments] and squadrons, so that from a distance the variety of insignia displayed the diversity of the force; and they were easily recognized by their general and the other officers of the field; and in battle it was impossible to conceal which nation showed the greatest valor.

segments.title_inferred: Inca System of Cacique Succession and Administrative Hierarchy
segments.original_text:
