It is a matter very worthy of note that, while all other things of the Indians differ so greatly from ours, in the kinds of numbers and manner of counting, these Peruvians conform so closely to the Mexicans—and both to us—that there is no difference whatsoever between them and us. This I cannot attribute to anything other than the fact that the peoples who came to settle this New World must have preserved the method of counting they learned in the Old World, from which they departed, and that it was the very same method that we ourselves have retained.

BOOK THE THIRTEENTH

segments.title_inferred: On the False Religion of the Peruvians and Their Devotion to It
segments.original_text:
CAPITULO PRIMERO

De la religión falsa que tenían los del Perú, y cuán dados eran a ella

Eran los indios del Perú tan grandes idólatras que adoraban por dioses casi cuantas especies hay de criaturas. Porque, como no tuvieron lumbre sobrenatural, decendieron en los mismos errores y desatinos que las otras naciones de gentiles, por concurrir en éstos las mismas causas que en aquéllos para no atinar con el verdadero Dios; conforme a saber, el abismo grande de vicios y pecados en que estaban sumergidos, con que se habían hecho inhábiles e indignos de que se les comunicase la luz pura del conocimiento de su Criador; y la malicia y astucia del demonio enemigo del linaje humano, que por su antigua soberbia e invidia de nuestro bien, procuró usurpar en estas ciegas gentes la adoración que debían a sólo el que los crió, para tenerlos aprisionados en su duro cautiverio y servidumbre y privarlos de la bienaventuranza de que él no fue merecedor: y hallando para conseguir sus designios bastante aparejo y disposición en la simpleza e ignorancia destos bárbaros, tuvo por tantos siglos puesto su trono entre ellos, hasta que la virtud de la Cruz le va desposeyendo y echando así desta, como de las otras naciones deste Nuevo Mundo.

Y como las naciones désta, por ser tan extendida, eran muchas, lo eran también las maneras de religión e idolatrías que seguían, no sólo antes que fuesen sujetadas y reducidas a un imperio, sino también después. Porque si bien los reyes peruanos compelían a todos los que sojuzgaban a recibir su religión, no era de suerte que les hiciesen dejar del todo la que tenían antes, más de aquello que parecía contradecir a la suya; y así, no sólo se quedaban con sus dioses antiguos, sino que los mismos Incas los admitían y hacían traer al Cuzco y colocarlos entre los suyos propios; a los cuales hacían alguna veneración, aunque mucho menor que la que daban a sus dioses naturales; y echábase bien de ver el poco caso que destos dioses extraños hacían y la poca estimación en que los tenían, por lo que aquí diré: y es, que cuando se les rebelaba alguna provincia, mandaban sacar y poner en público a los dioses naturales y protectores della, y que los azotasen afrentosamente cada día hasta reducir a su servicio la dicha provincia; y en reduciéndola, los hacían restituir a sus lugares y festejarlos con sacrificios, diciendo que en virtud dellos y por no ser afrentados, se había reducido la tal provincia; y aun cuentan que los más de los rebelados se reducían sólo por oír que sus ídolos estaban en afrenta pública.

Por ser, pues, como queda dicho, tantas las diferencias de idolatrías que tenían las gentes deste imperio peruano, que si de todas se hubiera de tratar en particular, fuera cosa infinita, escribiré aquí no más que la que guardaba la nación de los Incas, por haber venido a ser general en todo el reino, haciéndola recibir los reyes dél a todos sus vasallos; los cuales reyes se dieron tal maña en introducirla, que no sólo acabaron que la recibiesen todos los que ponían debajo de su obediencia, sino también que la viniesen a estimar en tanto, que se preciasen y tuviesen por honra el profesarla, haciendo más caso della que de la suya propia. Esto consiguieron con encarecerles la señalada merced que les hacían en admitirlos a la veneración de sus dioses; y para dárselo a entender, puesto caso que su pretensión y deseo era que los vencidos abrazasen sus opiniones en materia de religión, no permitían que desde luego gozasen todos deste favor, ni que sus fiestas fuesen comunes, sino que en todo dejaban para sí reservadas algunas cosas que nadie más que los dichos Incas y los de su linaje las podían usar; y en otras les prohibían el hacerlas con la solemnidad y ceremonias que ellos acostumbraban; y con el tiempo y servicios que hacían, los reyes iban dispensando con algunas provincias para que las pudiesen hacer; y desta suerte los vasallos tenían esto por gran premio de sus servicios; y la dificultad con que se alcanzaba hacía poner mayor cuidado y diligencia en la observancia dello; y el no ser general a todos ni poder estar presentes los que no tenían privilegio, hacía crecer la estima y devoción que con estos ritos y supersticiones tenían; y los súbditos extranjeros estimaban estas dispensaciones y privilegios en más que ninguna otra cosa de cuantas el Inca podía darles; porque tenían por cierto que ellas eran parte para alcanzar lo que pedían en sus necesidades y tribulaciones: y de todo esto se tenían por remediados cuando tenían licencia para usar de los sacrificios y ceremonias de los Incas; porque de todo los certificaban y aseguraban los mismos Incas cuando les concedían esta merced. Todo lo cual fundaban ellos por sus imaginaciones, sueños, revelaciones y mandatos que fingían tener de sus dioses, a fin de hacer entender al pueblo rudo, que a ellos solos y a quien ellos quisiesen era permitida la veneración del Viracocha, que era su principal dios, y de los otros sus ídolos con los ritos que estaban estatuidos para ello. Lo cual todo era artificio de los Incas para que fuese estimada su religión y mediante ella tener a sus súbditos más sujetos y obedientes.

Verdad es que no siempre los Incas desde que comenzó su imperio estuvieron firmes e invariables en su religión ni tuvieron unas mismas opiniones ni adoraron unos mismos dioses, sino que en varios tiempos fueron añadiendo y quitando muchas cosas y perpetuamente cargando a los pueblos de más cultos y ceremonias; moviéndose para variar en esta materia por echar de ver que por este camino se afirmaban mejor en el reino y lo tenían más en obediencia. Porque como todo el fundamento de su gobierno político estribaba en medios ordena[dos...]
segments.translation:
CHAPTER ONE

On the False Religion of the Peruvians and How Devoted They Were to It

The Indians of Peru were such great idolaters that they worshipped as gods nearly every species of creature in existence. For, lacking supernatural enlightenment, they fell into the same errors and follies as the other heathen nations, the same causes being at work in them as in those others that prevented them from finding the true God: namely, the great abyss of vices and sins in which they were submerged, which had rendered them incapable and unworthy of receiving the pure light of knowledge of their Creator; and the malice and cunning of the demon, enemy of the human race, who, out of his ancient pride and envy of our good, sought to usurp among these blind peoples the adoration owed to Him alone who created them, in order to hold them imprisoned in his harsh captivity and servitude and to deprive them of the blessedness of which he himself was found unworthy. Finding in the simplicity and ignorance of these barbarians sufficient opening and disposition to accomplish his designs, he maintained his throne among them for so many centuries, until the power of the Cross has been gradually dispossessing and expelling him from this land as from the other nations of this New World.

And since the nations of this land were many, owing to its great extent, so too were the varieties of religion and idolatry they followed — not only before they were subjected and brought under a single empire, but also afterward. For although the Peruvian kings compelled all those they subjugated to receive their religion, this was not done in such a manner as to make them abandon entirely the religion they had previously held, beyond what appeared to contradict their own; and so not only did the conquered peoples retain their ancient gods, but the Incas themselves admitted those gods and had them brought to Cuzco and installed among their own deities. These foreign gods were given some veneration, though far less than that accorded to the Incas' own native gods. The low regard and scant esteem in which these foreign gods were held could be clearly seen from what I will recount here: when a province rose in rebellion, the Incas would order the native and tutelary gods of that province to be brought out and publicly displayed, and to be flogged shamefully each day until the said province was reduced to obedience; and once it was reduced, they would have those gods restored to their places and celebrated with sacrifices, saying that it was by virtue of those gods — and in order to spare them further public shame — that the province had submitted. It is even reported that most of the rebels submitted upon merely hearing that their idols were being publicly dishonored.

Because, as has been said, the varieties of idolatry practiced by the peoples of this Peruvian empire were so many that to treat all of them individually would be an endless undertaking, I shall here write only of that religion maintained by the nation of the Incas, since it came to be universal throughout the entire kingdom, its kings having caused all their vassals to receive it. Those kings were so skillful in introducing it that not only did they succeed in having all peoples brought under their authority adopt it, but also in making them come to esteem it so highly that they took pride and honor in professing it, holding it in greater regard than their own native religion. This they achieved by impressing upon their subjects the signal favor done to them in admitting them to the veneration of the Incas' gods. To make this understood, and although their aim and desire was that the conquered peoples embrace their religious opinions, they did not permit everyone to enjoy this favor at once, nor did they make their festivals open to all. Rather, they always reserved for themselves certain things that no one other than the Incas themselves and those of their lineage could perform; and in other matters they prohibited the subject peoples from conducting ceremonies with the solemnity and rites to which the Incas themselves were accustomed. Over time, and in recognition of services rendered, the kings would grant dispensations to certain provinces allowing them to perform such ceremonies. In this way their vassals came to regard this as a great reward for their service, and the difficulty of obtaining it led to greater care and diligence in its observance. The fact that this privilege was not universal and that those without it could not be present at such ceremonies caused the esteem and devotion accorded to these rites and superstitions to grow. Foreign subjects valued these dispensations and privileges above anything else the Inca could grant them, being firmly convinced that through them they could obtain what they sought in their needs and tribulations; and they considered themselves fully provided for in all things when they had license to make use of the sacrifices and ceremonies of the Incas, since the Incas themselves would certify and assure them of this when granting the favor. All of this they grounded in their imaginings, dreams, revelations, and commands they claimed to receive from their gods, in order to make the ignorant common people understand that only the Incas, and those whom the Incas chose, were permitted to venerate [Viracocha] — who was their principal god — and their other idols, with the rites prescribed for that purpose. All of which was a contrivance of the Incas to make their religion esteemed and, through it, to hold their subjects in greater subjection and obedience.

It is true that the Incas, from the very beginning of their empire, were not always steadfast and unchanging in their religion, nor did they hold the same opinions or worship the same gods at all times. Rather, at various periods they added and removed many things, perpetually burdening the peoples with additional cults and ceremonies, moved to make changes in this matter by perceiving that in this way they consolidated their hold on the kingdom more effectively and kept it in greater obedience. For since the entire foundation of their political governance rested on [the means they ordain... text breaks off]

segments.title_inferred: On Inca Religion, Idolatry, and the Rationality of Indigenous Peoples
segments.original_text:
dos a tener los suyos más sujetos y que perdiesen el brío de levantarse contra ellos, a este mismo fin iban también enderezadas las opiniones que fundaban entre ellos en lo tocante a su religión, cuyo título llevaban por delante en sus conquistas, con pretexto que diesen la honra y obediencia debida al Viracocha como a criador del mundo, y al sol, trueno y demás dioses suyos, que juzgaban ser causa de la conservación de lo criado, mediante el poder que les estaba dado para ello; especialmente el parentesco que fingían tener con el sol y las ayudas particulares que recebían de su mano en la guerra, y otras ficciones. Que como eran señores tiranos, el tiempo y las ocasiones les enseñaron todos los medios necesarios para sustentar su tiranía y señorear con más seguridad. Y así, por esta diversidad de culto y ritos que en diferentes edades tuvieron, conviene advertir que no se escribe aquí más de lo que observaban al tiempo que los españoles entraron en esta tierra y ellos comenzaron a recebir la fe de la doctrina del cielo; que son las opiniones en que se habían venido a resumir y había más general conformidad entre ellos.

Aunque esta forma de idolatría y falsa religión de los Incas era la más concertada y menos apartada de razón que los disparates y errores de las otras naciones destas Indias, con todo eso estaba tan llena de ficciones, imposturas y desvarios, que pone admiración cómo hombres de entendimiento se hubiesen persuadido a tenerla por verdadera; por la infinidad de cosas que adoraban por dioses, hasta dar reverencia a las más viles y asquerosas que produce la tierra, las boberías que de ellas creían y el caso que hacían de su culto y sacrificios, como constará por este libro: si bien es verdad que esto no ha de ser motivo para que tengamos por bestias y faltos de discurso a los que tales cosas creían y observaban; porque quien tuviere noticia de las vanidades y desatinos que tuvieron las más nobles y sabias naciones de Europa y de las otras dos partes del Mundo, como fueron los egipcios, caldeos, griegos y romanos, de cuya sabiduría tanta cuenta se ha hecho en todas edades, no tendrá por cosa nueva y extraña leer las fábulas y desvarios destos indios, antes echará de ver que muchos de aquellos antiguos filósofos tuvieron algunas opiniones más fáciles y de menos fundamento que éstos. Ni nos es argumento de la inhabilidad destos indios adorar ídolos y cosas perecederas, antes arguye en ellos ingenio, careciendo de lumbre verdadera de fe, buscar algo a que tener respeto y en que tener confianza.

Y no es poco que ellos mismos se diesen a buscar la causa de cada cosa, como los gentiles, y si erraron en su investigación, en el mismo error y por el mismo camino dieron los antiguos por ser tan dados a las ciencias y especulación de las cosas; los cuales, sin dar con quién fuese el verdadero artífice de lo criado, vinieron a adorar piedras, palos, figuras de animales y cosas hechas de mano de hombre, como éstos. Antes alcanzaron estos indios algunas cosas guiados por razón natural, en que pasaron adelante a muchos de los otros gentiles, como es en haber alcanzado a conocer que el verdadero Dios y primera causa era uno solo, al cual, aunque confusamente, adoraban como a Criador de todo.

Y no parando aquí, se dieron a buscar las razones que había para cada cosa y las causas de donde procedían, con que vinieron en conocimiento de algunas de las segundas causas que se podían ver por los efectos; y puesto que erraron notablemente en que, creyendo que era uno solo el Criador universal de todo y siempre dirigían a él sus rogativas y sacrificios, adoraban con igual reverencia y con unos mismos actos de culto y sumisión a las segundas causas como al sol, al agua, a la tierra y a otras muchas cosas que tenían por divinas, por entender tenían virtud para hacer o conservar lo necesario para la vida humana: a lo cual tuvieron siempre su principal intento y que por esto se les debía adoración como al Criador, de donde resultaron infinitas idolatrías y supersticiones; con todo eso, el haber querido especular es argumento de ser gente de razón y menos bárbara que las otras de este Nuevo Mundo. Adonde hallamos que cuanto menos tiene una nación de religión, tanto más tiene de rudeza y barbaridad; y es mucho de estimar que, aunque erraron estos peruanos, al fin buscaron la primera y segunda causa de las cosas; y si se engañaron en dar culto a las causas segundas, era por el poder que creían les estaba dado para que fuesen parte en la conservación del universo. Lo que yo siento, considerando atentamente los ritos y opiniones destos indios, es que en las más tuvieron las mismas costumbres e invenciones que los romanos, y no es de maravillar, pues los unos y los otros tuvieron un mismo maestro. Porque en la estimación y autoridad que pretendieron se tuviese la ciudad del Cuzco después de sus victorias por todas las gentes que señorearon, y en traer a ella los dioses principales de todas las provincias, los cuales pusieron al principio en el templo del sol, como los romanos en aquel suntuoso edificio que llaman Panteón, conformaron grandemente con ellos.

La gran facilidad destos indios fue la principal causa de haberse introducido en ellos tan grandes desatinos y errores; porque son tan fáciles, que después de haber admitido un yerro, creían luego cualquiera cosa que sobre él quisiesen inventar los sacerdotes o los Incas, para que su opinión recibiese fuerza y corroboración; y aprovechándose desta facilidad los sacerdotes y ministros de las guacas, por ser tan interesados en las ofrendas y sacrificios que ofrecía el pueblo, inventaban y fingían cada día mil novelas y misterios de visiones y milagros que sucedían.
segments.translation:
to keep their subjects more subdued and to make them lose the spirit to rise against them—toward this same end were also directed the opinions they established among them concerning their religion, which they placed at the forefront of their conquests, under the pretext that the people should render the honor and obedience owed to Viracocha as creator of the world, and to the sun, thunder, and their other gods, whom they judged to be the cause of the preservation of all created things, by virtue of the power granted to them for that purpose; and especially the kinship they pretended to have with the sun, and the particular assistance they claimed to receive from his hand in war, and other such fictions. For, being tyrannical lords, time and circumstance taught them all the means necessary to sustain their tyranny and to rule with greater security. And so, on account of this diversity of worship and rites that they observed in different ages, it is worth noting that what is written here covers only what they practiced at the time the Spaniards entered this land and they began to receive the faith of the heavenly doctrine—that is, the beliefs into which they had come to consolidate, and which enjoyed the most general agreement among them.

Although this form of idolatry and false religion of the Incas was the most orderly and least removed from reason of all the absurdities and errors of the other nations of these Indies, it was nevertheless so full of fictions, impostures, and follies that it is astonishing how men of understanding could have been persuaded to hold it as true; given the infinite number of things they worshipped as gods, going so far as to venerate the most vile and disgusting things the earth produces, the foolishness they believed about them, and the weight they placed on their worship and sacrifices, as will be evident from this book. It is true, however, that this should not be grounds for us to regard as beasts and lacking in reason those who believed and observed such things; for anyone acquainted with the vanities and follies held by the most noble and learned nations of Europe and of the other two parts of the World—such as the Egyptians, Chaldeans, Greeks, and Romans, whose wisdom has been so highly regarded in every age—will find nothing new or strange in reading the fables and follies of these Indians; indeed, he will observe that many of those ancient philosophers held opinions more far-fetched and less well-founded than these. Nor does the worship of idols and perishable things argue the incapacity of these Indians; rather it argues ingenuity in them, for lacking the true light of faith, they sought something toward which to feel reverence and in which to place their trust.

And it is no small thing that they themselves set out to seek the cause of each thing, as the Gentiles did; and if they erred in that investigation, the ancients fell into the same error and by the same path, being so devoted to the sciences and the speculation of things—who, without finding who was the true maker of created things, came to worship stones, sticks, figures of animals, and things made by human hands, just as these people did. Indeed, these Indians attained certain things guided by natural reason, in which they surpassed many other Gentiles; as, for example, in having come to know that the true God and first cause was one alone, whom, albeit confusedly, they worshipped as the Creator of all things.

And not stopping there, they set about seeking the reasons behind each thing and the causes from which they proceeded, whereby they came to knowledge of some of the secondary causes that could be seen through their effects. And yet they erred notably in that, believing there was but one universal Creator of all things—to whom they always directed their prayers and sacrifices—they worshipped with equal reverence and with the same acts of worship and submission the secondary causes, such as the sun, water, the earth, and many other things they held to be divine, believing these had the power to produce or preserve what is necessary for human life; to which end they always directed their principal attention, and for this reason they believed these causes were owed adoration alongside the Creator—whence arose infinite idolatries and superstitions. Nonetheless, their very desire to speculate is an argument that they were a people of reason, and less barbarous than the other peoples of this New World. We find that the less religion a nation has, the more rudeness and barbarity it possesses; and it is greatly to be esteemed that, though these Peruvians erred, they ultimately sought the first and second cause of things; and if they went astray in rendering worship to secondary causes, it was because of the power they believed had been granted to these causes to have a part in the preservation of the universe. What I myself perceive, upon careful consideration of the rites and beliefs of these Indians, is that in most respects they had the same customs and inventions as the Romans—and this is not to be wondered at, since both had the same teacher. For in the esteem and authority they sought to have accorded to the city of Cuzco after their victories by all the peoples they ruled, and in bringing to it the principal gods of all the provinces—which they placed at first in the Temple of the Sun, just as the Romans placed them in that sumptuous edifice called the Pantheon—they conformed greatly with them.

The great credulity [lit. 'easiness'] of these Indians was the principal cause of the introduction among them of such great follies and errors; for they are so credulous that, once they had accepted one error, they would immediately believe anything the priests or the Incas wished to invent upon it, so that their belief might gain strength and corroboration. Taking advantage of this credulity, the priests and ministers of the huacas [guacas]—being so interested in the offerings and sacrifices that the people brought—invented and fabricated new stories and mysteries of visions and miracles every single day.

segments.title_inferred: Inca Religion, Sacrifice, and Oral Preservation of Sacred Memory
segments.original_text:
clían en sus guacas, y las hacían creer al simple vulgo, para que, creciendo la reputación de sus dioses, se acrecentasen las ofrendas; y estaban recibidas y asentadas algunas cosas tan aparentes y sin fundamento, que no es posible que algunos dejasen de mirar en ellas y conociesen su falsedad y engaño, siendo tan claras y demostrativas, sino que, cuando una cosa está introducida, aunque sea notoriamente mala, ninguno, aunque lo alcance, osa ser el primero que lo contradiga. Porque claro está que muchos filósofos alcanzaron ser burla la multitud de los dioses, y con todo eso no osaban hablar en ello, por no contradecir a la multitud de la gente. Lo cual especialmente ha más lugar entre estos indios, en quien la libertad era poca, los castigos grandes y mucha la gente que se mantenía destos oficios.

Tenía el demonio a esta ciega gente tan edificada y habituada en su errada seta, particularmente a los Incas (que era la nación más dada a la religión que debe haber habido en el mundo), que hacían sus ritos y sacrificios con tanta determinación y en tanta cantidad, que casi cuanto cogían y cuanto trabajaban y hasta sus propios hijos consumían en ello; y así, de tan profunda devoción como mostraban y cuidado que cada uno ponía en venerar y sacrificar lo que estaba a su cargo de una religión tan arraigada y universalmente recibida y de una determinación que pone espanto de ofrecer y sacrificar a sus propios hijos y haciendas, matándolos y quemándolos, conforme a sus costumbres, no se puede presumir que sus actos fuesen solamente exteriores, pues no permite la naturaleza que uno mate a su mismo hijo y aventure su hacienda con ánimo y voluntad tan alegre, si no tuviese fines y pretensiones de galardón por lo que hace, o pensase y tuviese creído enviaba su hijo a mejor lugar que el que acá tenía; y para dar muestras exteriores de contento cuando lo hace, necesario es que interiormente lo crea y que tenga por cierto que no se pierde el sacrificio. Y así no hay duda sino que las obras déstos pendían de alguna esperanza, pues fuera bestialidad matar nadie sus hijos y consumir su hacienda si no creyese que le aprovechaba para algo.

Puesto caso que todas las naciones deste reino del Perú tuvieron fines y cuenta con sus dioses y adoratorios, ninguna hubo que llegase con mucho a preciarse de tan religiosa como los Incas; y así eran ellos los más cargados de ceremonias, supersticiones, ídolos y sacrificios, y guardaban con tanta observancia lo estatuido en orden a esto, que eran leyes y opiniones inviolables entre ellos; y aunque el delito que contra ellas se cometía, y aun el descuido en la veneración de lo estatuido, fuera castigado ásperamente, fue tanta su religión, que por falta della dicen haberse castigado muy pocos, aunque con gran diligencia se tuvo con ello cuenta; que cierto no es de pasar por esto fácilmente, pues al que le cabía sacrificar el hijo, aunque no tuviese otro, era delito notable el mostrar alguna tristeza, sino que lo hacían con muestras de alegría y contentamiento, como si los llevaran a dar un cargo de grande importancia.

Lo que en esta materia de religión más admira, es la cuenta tan extraordinaria que tenían estos indios en conservar la memoria de las cosas tocantes a ella. Porque dado que no las tenían por escrito para saberlas y guardarlas, suplían esta falta con aprenderlas y guardarlas por tradición tan exactamente, que parece las tenían esculpidas en los huesos. Porque para sólo este efecto tenían los Incas puestos en la ciudad del Cuzco más de mil hombres que no entendían en otra cosa más que en la conservación desta memoria; y con éstos se criaban otros desde mancebos, que eran instruidos dellos, para que no se pudiese perder; que bien creo yo que semejante cuidado no se halló en los gentiles antiguos ni en otra ninguna gente para conservar su religión y memoria de sus opiniones y adoratorios. Estos que entendían en este ministerio eran comúnmente viejos y sacerdotes o ministros de sus templos, y hacíanlo con tanto cuidado, que cuando eran preguntados, uno por sí daba razón y declaraba la virtud que decía tener la guaca que estaba a su cargo, las solemnidades y palabras con que se le había de hacer el sacrificio, y la ofrenda que se le había de dar, prometiendo gran esperanza del buen suceso con la relación que hacía de otros semejantes que para acreditar su adoratorio fingía. Y si bien es verdad que todas las razones que apartan del camino verdadero de Dios son débiles y de ningún fundamento, todavía en muchas cosas las tenían estos indios más coloreadas para sus yerros que los gentiles filósofos y poetas. Verdad es que no se les podía pedir entera razón de todo, porque no la alcanzaban, ni sabían los fundamentos en que estribaban para sus opiniones; pues de lo más tenían por principal causa la costumbre de sus antepasados, la cual tuvieron por ley inviolable en muchas cosas en tanto grado, que de algunas no sabían dar otra razón, y si daban alguna, puesto caso que en la forma, solemnidad y supersticiones estaban conformes, en ella diferenciaban mucho; porque, como no tenían escrituras, perdíase la razón y motivo de los pasados, y después tenían las cosas en la opinión que las hallaron, y cada uno inventaba lo que le parecía; en lo cual los que reparaban eran los menos y gente estimada y noble, que la común no se ponía a particularizar razones, sino que seguía en general a éstos y hacía lo que le mandaban y veía que se usaba. Y así aun los mismos que no trataban de otra cosa que de lo tocante a su religión, no tenían memoria del principio de las más de sus opiniones y devaneos. Y sin duda esto viene de ser muy de atrás, porque tantas cosas no se pueden [ILLEGIBLE: text breaks off]
segments.translation:
they would proclaim in their guacas [shrines], and made the simple common people believe them, so that, as the reputation of their gods grew, the offerings would increase. And certain things had been received and established that were so specious and lacking in foundation that it is impossible some did not see through them and recognize their falseness and deceit—being so clear and self-evident—but when something has become established, even if it is notoriously bad, no one, even if he perceives it, dares to be the first to contradict it. For it is plain that many philosophers recognized the multitude of gods to be a mockery, and yet for all that they dared not speak of it, lest they contradict the multitude of the people. This holds especially true among these Indians, among whom freedom was scarce, punishments severe, and great numbers of people sustained their livelihoods through these offices.

The Devil had this blind people so thoroughly built up and habituated in their erring sect—particularly the Incas, who were the nation most given to religion that has perhaps ever existed in the world—that they performed their rites and sacrifices with such resolve and in such quantity that they consumed in them nearly all they harvested, all they labored for, and even their own children. And so, from such profound devotion as they displayed, and the care that each one took to venerate and sacrifice what was under his charge, from a religion so deeply rooted and universally received, and from a resolve that strikes one with awe—to offer and sacrifice their own children and estates, killing and burning them according to their customs—one cannot presume that their acts were merely outward, for nature does not permit a man to kill his own child and risk his estate with so cheerful a spirit and will unless he had ends and aims of reward for what he does, or believed and was convinced that he was sending his child to a better place than the one he had here. And in order to show outward signs of contentment when doing so, it is necessary that he inwardly believe it and hold it as certain that the sacrifice is not in vain. Thus there is no doubt that the actions of these people depended upon some hope, for it would be sheer bestiality for anyone to kill his children and consume his estate if he did not believe it served him for something.

Although all the nations of this kingdom of Peru had regard for their gods and their shrines [adoratorios], none came anywhere close to priding itself on being as religious as the Incas. And so they were the most burdened with ceremonies, superstitions, idols, and sacrifices, and they observed what was ordained in this regard with such strictness that these were inviolable laws and opinions among them. And although the offense committed against them—and even carelessness in venerating what was ordained—was punished harshly, their religion was so great that they say very few were punished for failing in it, even though it was tracked with great diligence. This is certainly not a trivial matter, for the one whose duty it was to sacrifice his son—even if he had no other—committed a notable offense by showing any sadness; rather, they did it with outward signs of joy and contentment, as if they were sending him off to be given a position of great importance.

What is most astonishing in this matter of religion is the extraordinarily careful account these Indians kept in preserving the memory of things pertaining to it. For although they did not have them in writing in order to know and keep them, they supplied this deficiency by learning and preserving them through oral tradition [tradición] so exactly that it seems they had them engraved in their very bones. For this purpose alone, the Incas had stationed in the city of Cuzco more than a thousand men who occupied themselves with nothing other than the preservation of this memory; and alongside these, others were raised from youth, instructed by them, so that it could never be lost. I truly believe that such care was not found among the ancient gentiles or among any other people to preserve their religion and the memory of their opinions and shrines. Those who attended to this ministry were commonly old men and priests or ministers of their temples, and they carried it out with such diligence that when questioned, each one would individually account for and declare the virtue he claimed the guaca [shrine] under his charge possessed, the solemnities and words with which sacrifice was to be offered to it, and the offering that was to be given to it, promising great hope of a good outcome with the account he gave of similar cases he fabricated in order to lend credit to his shrine. And although it is true that all the arguments that lead one away from the true path of God are weak and without foundation, in many things these Indians had more plausible coloring for their errors than the pagan philosophers and poets. It is true that one could not demand a complete account of everything from them, because they did not attain it, nor did they know the foundations upon which their opinions rested; for in most things they held the custom of their ancestors to be the principal cause, which they regarded as an inviolable law in so many things to such a degree that for some of them they could give no other reason, and if they did give one, although they agreed in form, solemnity, and superstitions, they differed greatly in the substance; because, as they had no writing, the reason and motive of those of the past was lost, and afterward they held things in the opinion in which they found them, each one inventing what seemed right to him. In this, those who took pains to reflect were the fewest—and men of standing and nobility—for the common people did not trouble themselves with particular reasons, but followed these leaders in general, doing what they were told and what they saw practiced. And so even those who occupied themselves with nothing other than matters pertaining to their religion had no memory of the origin of most of their opinions and fancies. And without doubt this stems from being of very ancient date, for so many things cannot [ILLEGIBLE: text breaks off mid-sentence]

segments.title_inferred: On the religious knowledge of common people and the Inca elite; the terms Vilca and Guaca
segments.original_text:
odían inventar en poco tiempo, ni juntas, ni carga tan pesada se podía admitir por el pueblo sino en muchas edades, añadiendo en cada una un poco.

Fuera, pues, desta gente dedicada a este vano culto y a conservar los ritos y ceremonias dél, de lo restante del pueblo eran muy pocos los que tenían noticias destas cosas, ni entendían las razones y motivos por donde se establecieron; sólo hacían lo que les estaba mandado y acudían con los sacrificios a las guacas y adoratorios, sin pasar adelante; salvo los principales del linaje de los Incas, que alcanzaban más razón de sus opiniones y la daban por más orden que otros ningunos; y con todo eso no se podía tratar dello sino con los menos.

Con dos nombres llamaban estos indios a sus dioses, que son Vilca el uno y el otro Guaca, y cualquiera dellos significa en común no sólo cualquiera dios e ídolo, sino también todo lugar de adoración, como templos, sepulturas y cualquiera otro de los que veneraban y en que ofrecían sacrificios. Por lo cual usaré dellos en este tratado, particularmente del de guaca, que era el más universal y usado de los indios, en la misma significación y con toda la ampliación que lo usaban ellos.
segments.translation:
They could not have invented [these practices] in a short time, nor could such a heavy burden have been taken on by the people all at once, but only over the course of many ages, adding a little in each one.

Beyond the people dedicated to this vain cult and to preserving its rites and ceremonies, of the rest of the populace very few had any knowledge of these things, nor did they understand the reasons and motives by which they had been established; they merely did what they were commanded, and came with their sacrifices to the guacas [shrines] and adoratorios [places of worship], without going any further—except for the principales [leading nobles] of the lineage of the Incas, who had a greater grasp of the reasoning behind their beliefs and could explain it in a more orderly fashion than any others; and even so, it could only be discussed with very few of them.

These Indians called their gods by two names: one is Vilca and the other Guaca, and either of them signifies in general usage not only any god or idol, but also any place of worship, such as temples, burial sites, and any other places they venerated and in which they offered sacrifices. For this reason I will use these terms in this treatise—particularly that of guaca, which was the most universal and widely used among the Indians—in the same sense and with all the breadth with which they themselves employed it.

segments.title_inferred: Cañari Origin Myths: The Flood, Tiahuanaco Creation, and the Guacamaya Women
segments.original_text: ontorno a buscar de comer, dejando sola su morada, que era una pequeña choza que habían hecho en que albergarse, y que, sustentándose de raíces y yerbas, pasaron por algún tiempo grandes necesidades y hambre; mas que, volviendo un día a su choza de buscar de comer quebrantados de cansancio, la hallaron muy abastecida de diversos manjares y abundancia de chicha, sin saber de dónde ni quién les hubiese hecho tan notable beneficio y regalo. Quedando muy admirados desto, buscaron con diligencia si parecía por allí alguien, con deseo de saber de cuya mano eran socorridos en tiempo de tanta apretura, y no hallando rastro de gente, se asentaron a comer y mataron la hambre por entonces; y que desta manera pasaron diez o doce días, hallando siempre proveído de comidas el rancho como el primer día. A cabo deste tiempo, curiosos de ver y conocer a quien les hacía tanto bien, acordaron que el uno se quedase escondido en casa, y para esto, hicieron un hoyo en la parte más oscura della, en que se metió el uno, y el otro se fue a su ejercicio del campo. En este tiempo vió el que estaba en centinela entrar por la puerta dos guacamayas (son aves de género de papagayos), y que luego que estuvieron dentro se transformaron en dos hermosas mujeres pallas, que es tanto como nobles de sangre real, ricamente vestidas del traje que usan hoy las mujeres cañares, con el cabello largo y tendido y ceñida por la frente una hermosa cinta; y que, quitándose las llicllas, que son sus mantos, empezaron a aderezar de comer de lo que traían. El mancebo salió en esto de su escondrijo, y saludándolas cortésmente, comenzó a trabar conversación con ellas; las cuales, entonces, alborotadas y turbadas de que las hubiesen visto, sin responderle palabra se salieron a prisa de la casa, y vueltas en su primera forma de guacamayas, se fueron volando sin hacer ni dejar este día qué comiesen. El mozo, cuando se halló solo, viendo que no le había salido el lance como deseaba, se comenzó a afligir y lamentar, maldiciendo su fortuna. Estando en esta congoja, llegó del campo el otro hermano, y sabido el suceso, le riñó con enojo y cólera, motejándolo de cobarde y hombre sin brío ni valor, pues había perdido tan grande ocasión; en fin, se determinaron de quedarse ambos escondidos en casa, para ver si volvían las guacamayas. Ellas, a cabo de tres días, volvieron como solían, y entrando por la puerta, tomaron forma humana, apareciendo dos bellas doncellas, y empezaron a poner en orden la comida. Los mozos, que estaban en acechanza, habiéndolas dejado asegurar un rato, salieron de improviso, y cerrándoles la puerta sin preceder cortesía alguna, se abrazaron con ellas, a las cuales no dió lugar la turbación a tomar su figura de aves. Comenzaron con enojo y despecho a dar gritos y hacer fuerzas para soltarse, pero los mancebos, al fin, con halagos y palabras amorosas las aplacaron y quietaron; y cuando las vieron sosegadas, les rogaron ahincadamente les contasen su progenie y linaje y la causa de venirles a hacer aquel beneficio. Ellas, ya pacíficas y tratables, les respondieron que el Ticciviracocha les había mandado hacer aquel ministerio, socorriéndoles en aquel conflicto, porque no pereciesen de hambre. En conclusión, ellas se quedaron por esposas de los dos mancebos, y de la sucesión que dejaron afirman haberse poblado aquella provincia de los Cañares; y así tenía esta nación por guaca y adoratorio célebre al dicho cerro de Huacayñan, y por diosas principales a las guacamayas, con cuyas plumas se suelen engalanar en sus fiestas y regocijos, y adoraban ídolos en figura destas aves. Y yo vi no ha muchos años en esta ciudad de Lima traída de la dicha provincia de Cajamarca, una columna pequeña de cobre con dos guacamayas en su cumbre, obradas del mismo metal, a la [manera de ídolo].
segments.translation:
...going out in search of food, leaving their dwelling unattended — which was a small hut they had built for shelter — and that, sustaining themselves on roots and herbs, they suffered great want and hunger for some time. But one day, returning to their hut from the search for food, exhausted and worn out with fatigue, they found it very well stocked with diverse dishes and an abundance of chicha [maize beer], without knowing from where or from whom such a notable benefit and gift had come. Greatly astonished by this, they searched diligently to see whether anyone was in the vicinity, wishing to know by whose hand they had been aided in such a time of great distress; and finding no trace of any person, they sat down to eat and relieved their hunger for the time being. In this manner ten or twelve days passed, with the hut always found provisioned with food just as on the first day. After this time had elapsed, being curious to see and know who was doing them so much good, they agreed that one of them should remain hidden in the house; and for this purpose they dug a hole in the darkest part of it, into which one of them entered, while the other went out to his labors in the field. During this time, the one who stood watch saw two guacamayas [macaws — birds of the parrot family] enter through the door, and that as soon as they were inside they transformed themselves into two beautiful women pallas [i.e., noblewomen of royal blood], richly dressed in the garments worn today by Cañari women, with long hair hanging loose and a beautiful band bound across their foreheads; and that, removing their llicllas [shawls or mantles], they began to prepare food from what they had brought. At this the young man emerged from his hiding place, and greeting them courteously, began to engage them in conversation; whereupon they, startled and flustered at having been seen, left the house in haste without answering a word, and, having returned to their original form as macaws, flew away without providing anything for that day's meal. The young man, finding himself alone and seeing that the affair had not turned out as he had wished, began to grieve and lament, cursing his fortune. While he was in this distress, the other brother arrived from the field, and upon hearing what had happened, scolded him angrily and in a fury, mocking him as a coward and a man of no spirit or valor, since he had let slip so great an opportunity. In the end, they resolved that both should remain hidden in the house, to see whether the macaws would return. After three days, the birds returned as they had been accustomed to do, and entering through the door, took human form, appearing as two beautiful maidens, and began to set out the food in order. The young men, who were lying in wait, having let them settle for a while, came out suddenly, and shutting the door without any prior courtesy, seized hold of them; the women's alarm gave them no chance to resume their bird form. They began in anger and vexation to cry out and struggle to free themselves, but the young men at last soothed and calmed them with caresses and loving words; and when they saw them at rest, they earnestly besought them to tell them their lineage and ancestry and the reason for coming to do them such a service. The women, now peaceful and tractable, replied that Ticciviracocha [the creator deity] had commanded them to perform that office, succoring them in their hardship so that they should not perish from hunger. In the end, the women remained as wives of the two young men, and from the progeny they left behind, it is said that the province of the Cañares was peopled. And so this nation held the said hill of Huacayñan as a celebrated guaca [sacred shrine] and place of worship, and the macaws as their principal goddesses; with the feathers of these birds they customarily adorned themselves at their festivals and celebrations, and they worshipped idols in the likeness of these birds. And not many years ago I myself saw, here in this city of Lima, brought from the said province of Cajamarca, a small copper column with two macaws at its summit, fashioned from the same metal, in the manner of an idol.

[Interpolated text from the same chapter, appearing in the left-hand column of the original printed page:] They had all things created, because the highest mountains of the world were covered by it [the flood], so that no living creature remained, except one man and one woman who placed themselves inside a box of timber [ark], which floated upon the water without sinking; and that, as the waters receded, it descended and came to rest at Tiahuanaco. Others say that after the flood had ceased, in which all perished, the Creator formed from clay at Tiahuanaco all the nations that exist in this land, painting on each one the dress and clothing it was to wear; and that he gave to each nation the language it was to speak, the songs it was to sing, and the foods, seeds, and vegetables with which it was to sustain itself; and that, having done this, he commanded them to go beneath the earth, each nation by itself, so that from there they should emerge in the parts and places he directed them to; and that some came out from flat ground, others from hills, others from springs, from lakes, from tree trunks, and others from various other places. [These peoples] began to venerate [those places], each province its own, as principal guacas [sacred shrines], for their lineage and ancestry had issued and begun from there, and they came to regard those first progenitors as gods, placing their images and statues in the said places; and so each nation dressed in the garb that its own [founding] ancestor wore. They further recount that those first men in those very places, after having reproduced, were transformed, some into stones, others into falcons, condors, and other birds and animals; and so the guacas and idols they worshipped are of different forms.

Other nations held the belief that with the waters of the flood all men perished, save for a few who managed to escape in caves, trees, and hills, and that these were very few, from whom the world was repopulated; and that because [certain places] had served as refuge in those times, they constituted them as objects of worship and placed in them idols of stone, silver, and other metals in memory and in the name of those who had sheltered there, giving each idol the name...

segments.title_inferred: Indigenous Flood Myths of the Andes: Cañares, Anemarca, and the Incas
segments.original_text:
en su gentilidad adoraban por dios, los cañares, en memoria de la fábula referida.

Los indios de la provincia de Anemarca, distrito del Cuzco, tenían la fábula siguiente: Refieren que cuando quiso venir el diluvio, un mes antes las llamas o carneros de la tierra mostraron tan gran tristeza, que de día no comían y las noches se les pasaban mirando a las estrellas, hasta tanto que, reparando en ello un pastor, les preguntó la causa de su tristeza; al cual respondieron, que mirase cierta junta de estrellas que le mostraron, las cuales estaban en ayuntamiento y consulta sobre que se había de acabar el mundo con agua; lo cual oído por el pastor, lo trató con sus hijos y hijas, que eran seis, y acordó con ellos que recogiesen comida y ganado lo más que pudiesen; y que, hecha esta provisión, se subieron en un cerro muy alto llamado Ancasmarca. Y cuentan, que como las aguas iban creciendo y anegando la tierra, iba levantándose el dicho cerro de tal manera, que jamás fue cubierto dellas; y después, al paso que iba el agua menguando y recogiéndose, se iba el cerro bajando hasta quedar asentado en su lugar; y que destos hijos de aquel pastor se volvió a poblar su provincia.

Otros cuentan, que antes que comenzase el diluvio, como tuviese el Criador intento de tornar a reparar el mundo, proveyó que en cada provincia se avisasen tres o cuatro personas de lo que había de suceder, y que éstos fuesen de los más buenos y más principales, para que tomasen lugares a propósito en que salvarse y no pereciesen. Y lo tienen por opinión que se hizo, y que de aquéllos procede la gente que hay agora.

La historia que los Incas tenían de su origen y prosapia y cómo escaparon del diluvio, conté ya en el libro antecedente.
segments.translation:
…whom the Cañares [people] worshipped as a god in their paganism [gentilidad], in memory of the aforementioned legend.

The Indians of the province of Anemarca, in the district of Cuzco, held the following legend: They relate that one month before the Flood came, the llamas or sheep of the land [carneros de la tierra, i.e., llamas] displayed such great sadness that during the day they would not eat, and their nights were spent gazing at the stars. A shepherd, taking notice of this, asked them the cause of their sorrow. They replied that he should look upon a certain grouping of stars they pointed out to him, which were in assembly and deliberation over the fact that the world was to be destroyed by water. Upon hearing this, the shepherd discussed it with his sons and daughters, who numbered six, and agreed with them that they should gather as much food and livestock as they possibly could. Having made this provision, they climbed a very high hill called Ancasmarca. It is told that as the waters rose and flooded the land, the said hill rose in such a manner that it was never covered by the waters; and afterwards, as the water receded and withdrew, the hill descended until it settled back in its place. And from the children of that shepherd, the province was repopulated.

Others relate that before the Flood began, the Creator, intending to restore the world, arranged for three or four persons in each province to be warned of what was to come, and that these should be among the best and most prominent people, so that they might take to suitable places to save themselves and not perish. And it is their belief that this indeed took place, and that from those persons descends the people who exist today.

The history that the Incas held concerning their origin and lineage, and how they escaped the Flood, I have already recounted in the preceding book.

segments.title_inferred: Inca and Andean beliefs on origins, ancestor worship, and the afterlife (Book IV, Chapter III, part 1)
segments.original_text:
capítulo III, los cuales veneraban como adoratorio insigne la cueva de Pacaritampu, por haberse escapado allí de la destrucción del mundo sus progenitores; de los cuales tenían creído que se había poblado la tierra y que todas las gentes descendían dellos. Aunque también discrepaban, porque unos dicen que los Incas que salieron de aquella cueva los puso Dios allí; otros que no, sino que, cuando vino el diluvio, se metieron en ella y taparon bien la puerta, con que escaparon de las aguas. Estos y otros desatinos semejantes contaban, así los Incas como las otras naciones deste reino, que por evitar prolijidad no los pongo aquí, pues para el intento bastan los referidos. Lo cual todo causó en estas ciegas gentes el no haber conocido al verdadero Dios y haberse dado sin rienda a toda suerte de vicios; a que ayudó su parte el haber carecido de letras, que si las hubieran tenido, pudiera ser no tuvieran tan ciegos y tan torpes errores.

Lo que destas sus ficciones y novelas yo saco a propósito deste discurso es que dellas tuvieron origen muchísimos adoratorios y guacas, cada provincia el suyo, que fué el lugar donde entendían haberse salvado aquellos que tenían por principio y cepa de cada nación; y eran estos lugares en cada provincia muy conocidos y venerados con toda suerte de sacrificios. El fin que en la adoración dellos tenían, era la conservación y propagación de la gente de la dicha provincia. Asimismo desta opinión tuvieron principio los dioses principales y patrones de las provincias, que eran aquellos que concebían haber escapado del diluvio en los lugares sobredichos; y aunque es verdad que en ninguna parte tuvieron los cuerpos dellos, mas como fueron imaginaciones, halló también el demonio camino para hacerles entender que, cumplido el tiempo de su vida, que dicen haber sido muy larga, se habían convertido en piedras, y a éstas en lugar de sus cuerpos tenían en veneración y les ofrecían sacrificios.

El fundamento de tantos disparates que vinieron a creer todas estas naciones del Perú acerca de su origen fué causado por la ambición de los Incas, que fueron los primeros que hicieron veneración a la cueva de Pacaritampu como a principio de su generación; y como afirmasen que della habían procedido todas las gentes, y que por esta causa les debían sujeción y vasallaje y se lo debían dar, y llevasen por delante este título en sus conquistas, vino a resultar de aquí esta muchedumbre de adoratorios y dioses, en cada provincia el suyo, de la misma dedicación que los Incas habían dado al de Pacaritampu; y no negando el diluvio, se procuraban excusar de sujetarse a los Incas, defendiéndose con decir que no los debían reconocer por esa razón, de haber procedido dellos. Porque después del diluvio había habido gente nueva en cada provincia, de donde se tornó a poblar la tierra, en la cual opinión les pusieron sus viejos y hechiceros; y para darle fuerza y establecimiento, les señalaron los lugares donde y como se habían salvado sus progenitores. Y como los Incas respetaban la cueva de Pacaritampu, también los otros hicieron veneración al lugar que señalaron para el efecto dicho, aunque no con tanta orden ni con sacrificios tan principales; y así, en cualquiera provincia se halla este adoratorio, cada uno con su imaginación, contando el caso cada nación diferentemente.

CAPÍTULO III

De las opiniones que tuvieron estos indios en lo que toca a las ánimas y otra vida después désta

Acerca deste punto tuvieron muchas opiniones diferentes unas de otras; en lo que concordaban todos (a lo que se puede alcanzar) sin discrepar ninguno, es en dos cosas sustanciales: la primera, en conocer la inmortalidad del alma y que en el hombre hay más de lo que se ve con los ojos; y la otra, en que el bueno tiene gloria y el malo pena después desta vida. Mostraban lo primero en el cuidado grande que tenían de prevenir y adornar sus sepulturas; en meter en ellas con el difunto todos sus haberes, mujeres y servicio y cantidad de comida y bebida; y lo segundo, en la enseñanza, exhortaciones y reprehensiones que los sacerdotes y maestros suyos daban al común del pueblo, para apartarlos de lo malo e inclinarlos a lo bueno, unas veces amenazándolos con el castigo y otras convidándolos con el premio de la otra vida; dado caso que en la forma que esta gloria y pena se da y recibe difieren y desatinan, en lo cual era imposible acertasen sin lumbre de fe.

Algunos creían que, salida el alma del cuerpo, si había vivido bien, se hacía estrella, y que de allí procedían todas las del cielo, y que allí gozaban de gloria. Y si la vida había sido mala, iba a cierto lugar donde tenía pena perpetua; la cual dónde y cómo se la daban también discrepaban, y cada uno fingía lo que quería: porque no tenían en esto cosa fija ni asentada ni obligatoria, sino que, como gente sin lumbre, andaban vacilando e inventando cada día cosas nuevas, conforme a la flaqueza humana. Otros tenían que las ánimas que salían de los cuerpos de unas partes, venían a nacer en otras, y que cuando del todo saliesen de esta vida (que fin afirmaban que ha de tener el mundo) recibirían gloria o pena, según sus méritos. Otras naciones pensaban que las ánimas de los difuntos se quedaban en este mundo, y que unas veces tenían gozo, y otras eran afligidas, y andaban vagas y solitarias, padeciendo hambre, sed, frío, calor y cansancio; y que ellas o sus fantasmas solían visitar a sus parientes y a otras personas, en señal que habían de morir o les había de venir algún mal: y por esta razón de creer que las ánimas tenían hambre, sed y otros trabajos, ofrecían en las sepulturas comidas, bebidas, ropa y otras cosas, para que aprovechasen a los difuntos; y po[níanlas]
segments.translation:
Chapter III — [continued] — which they venerated as a renowned shrine [adoratorio insigne], namely the cave of Pacaritampu, because their ancestors had escaped there from the destruction of the world; and they believed that from those ancestors the earth had been populated, and that all peoples descended from them. They disagreed among themselves, however: some said that the Incas who came forth from that cave had been placed there by God; others held the contrary, saying that when the flood came, those ancestors had entered the cave and sealed the door tightly, and thus escaped the waters. Such nonsense, and other follies like it, were related both by the Incas and by the other nations of this realm; I do not set them all down here to avoid tedium, since those already recounted are sufficient for the purpose. All of this was brought about in these benighted peoples by their never having known the true God, and by their having given themselves without restraint to every kind of vice — to which their lack of letters also contributed, for had they possessed writing, it is possible they would not have held such blind and crude errors.

What I draw from these fictions and tales [novelas] for the purpose of this discourse is that from them arose a great multitude of shrines [adoratorios] and huacas [guacas], each province having its own — namely, the place where those regarded as the founding stock [principio y cepa] of each nation were believed to have been saved. These places were well known and greatly venerated in each province with every manner of sacrifice. The purpose of their veneration was the preservation and propagation of the people of the said province. Likewise from this belief arose the principal gods and patron deities of the provinces, who were those conceived to have escaped the flood in the places mentioned above. And although in truth the bodies of these ancestors were nowhere to be found, the Devil — since these were mere imaginings — also found a way to make them believe that, when the span of their lives (said to have been very long) was completed, those ancestors had been turned into stones; and these stones, in place of their bodies, they held in veneration and offered sacrifices to.

The foundation of so many absurdities come to be believed by all these nations of Peru regarding their origins was caused by the ambition of the Incas, who were the first to venerate the cave of Pacaritampu as the origin of their lineage. Because they asserted that from it all peoples had sprung, and that for this reason those peoples owed them subjection and vassalage and were obliged to render it — and because they carried this claim before them in their conquests — there resulted from this the great multitude of shrines and gods, each province having its own, dedicated in the same manner as the Incas had dedicated that of Pacaritampu. The other nations, not denying the flood, tried to excuse themselves from submitting to the Incas, defending themselves by saying they owed them no such recognition on the grounds of descent from them, because after the flood there had been new peoples in each province from whom the land had been repopulated — an opinion instilled in them by their elders and sorcerers [hechiceros]. To give it force and permanence, these elders designated the places where and how their ancestors had been saved. And just as the Incas venerated the cave of Pacaritampu, so the others paid veneration to the places designated for the said purpose, though not with as much order nor with sacrifices as important; and thus in every province one finds this type of shrine, each shaped by its own imagination, with each nation recounting the matter differently.

CHAPTER III

On the opinions held by these Indians concerning souls and the afterlife

On this point they held many opinions, differing one from another. In what all agreed (as far as can be determined), without any dissent, there were two essential things: the first, in recognizing the immortality of the soul and that in man there is more than what the eyes can see; and the second, that the good person enjoys glory and the wicked person suffers punishment after this life. They demonstrated the first point by the great care they took in preparing and adorning their tombs, and in placing within them alongside the deceased all of their possessions, wives, servants, and quantities of food and drink. They demonstrated the second in the teachings, exhortations, and reprimands that their priests and teachers gave to the common people, to draw them away from evil and incline them toward good — sometimes threatening them with punishment and other times enticing them with the reward of the next life — even though in the manner in which this glory and punishment are given and received they went astray and spoke nonsense, a matter in which it was impossible to arrive at the truth without the light of faith.

Some believed that when the soul left the body, if it had lived well, it became a star, and that all the stars of heaven had arisen in this way, and that there they enjoyed glory. And if the life had been wicked, the soul went to a certain place where it suffered perpetual punishment; on the question of where and how this punishment was administered they also disagreed, each inventing whatever he pleased — for they had nothing fixed, settled, or binding on this matter, but like people without the light [of faith] they wavered and invented new things every day, in keeping with human weakness. Others held that the souls leaving the bodies of persons in one place came to be born in other places, and that when this life finally ended altogether (for they affirmed that the world is destined to have an end), they would receive glory or punishment according to their merits. Still other nations thought that the souls of the dead remained in this world, sometimes experiencing joy and at other times suffering affliction, wandering vague and solitary, enduring hunger, thirst, cold, heat, and weariness; and that these souls, or their phantoms [fantasmas], were wont to visit relatives and other persons as a sign that they were about to die or that some evil was about to befall them. For this reason — believing that souls suffered hunger, thirst, and other hardships — they offered food, drink, clothing, and other things at the tombs, so that the dead might benefit from them; and they placed [text breaks off]

segments.title_inferred: Inca beliefs concerning the afterlife, heaven, hell, and the fate of souls
segments.original_text:
Y esto tenían tan especial cuidado de hacer sus aniversarios.

Los Incas afirmaban que las ánimas de los que han sido buenos van al cielo y tienen perpetua gloria, y ésta dicen que es estar con el sol en partes de gran deleite que tiene aparejadas el Viracocha para este fin. Unos creían que en la gloria no comen, ni beben, ni duermen, ni tienen mujeres ni necesidad de lo uno ni de lo otro; pero los más tenían lo contrario, creyendo que los que iban al cielo comen y beben espléndidamente muy buenas y regaladas comidas que el Criador les tiene aparejadas y las que acá les ofrecen y queman en sus honras sus deudos y amigos; y así tenían tanto cuidado de ofrecer de comer y beber a sus muertos, mayormente a los cuerpos embalsamados de los señores, hablando con ellos como si estuvieran vivos y diciéndoles: "Cuando eras vivo solías comer y beber destos manjares y bebida: recíbalo ahora tu ánima y cómalo doquiera que estuviere." Y tenían por cierto que así pasaba, que dondequiera que las ánimas estaban recibían y comían los manjares que les ofrecían, como hacían antes que muriesen; y para que más se confirmasen en este error, a veces, y cuando por voluntad de Dios le era permitido, tomaba el demonio la figura de algún hombre principal de los ya muertos, y aparecía con su propio traje y forma, como cuando era vivo, con su atavío y acompañamiento, a sus deudos y conocidos, y les hacía entender que estaba en otro reino alegre y deleitoso de la manera que allí lo vían. Por cuyo dicho e ilusiones, teniendo los deudos por ciertas aquellas apariencias falsas, ponían más cuidado en el servicio y regalo de sus difuntos: y esperando los vivos ir en compañía dellos a gozar de aquella gloria después desta vida, templaban la tristeza que les causaba el morir; y estando para ello, encomendaban a sus parientes sus casas y familias, pidiéndoles que hiciesen y cumpliesen lo que les dejaban encargado, que ellos los volverían a ver desdel cielo.

Asimismo estaban persuadidos a que hay infierno para los malos, y que allí los atormentan los demonios, a quienes pintaban muy feos y espantables. El lugar del infierno decían estar debajo de tierra, y que es muy oscuro y apretado; y que los que allá van padecen mucha hambre y sed; y que les hacen comer carbones, culebras, sapos y otras sabandijas asquerosas, y beber agua turbia y hedionda: y que de sólo esto se mantienen las ánimas de los condenados, cuya pena dicen ser perpetua.

Acerca de los méritos que han de tener para alcanzar la gloria, y las culpas por donde son condenados, también disparan; porque, dado que convenían en que los malos se condenaban y los buenos se salvaban, no acertaban a señalar y distinguir los buenos de los malos. Porque les persuadió el demonio que los nobles y gente de calidad, aunque fuesen de costumbres depravadas, eran siempre buenos y no era posible poderse condenar; porque el infierno sólo era para los hombres bajos y sin calidad, como ladrones y gente pobre, para los hechiceros que mataban con yerbas y para otros deste género, y señaladamente para los que iban contra los mandatos del rey o decían mal del sol y de las guacas; en los cuales pecados no incurrían los del linaje de los Incas, que eran los nobles; no haciendo caso de las otras especies de pecados.

No se halló entre todos estos indios noción que tratase de la resurrección de la carne, ni por alguna vía creyese que los cuerpos han de venir a ser algo más. Y no es de maravillar, siendo artículo que no se puede comprender por razón natural; y como no entendían que hubiese más que cielo e infierno, no hacían sacrificios y sufragios por las ánimas de los difuntos, dando por razón, que si estaban en el cielo, no lo habían menester, y si en el infierno, no les aprovechaba.
segments.translation:
And for this reason they took such special care to observe their anniversary rites.

The Incas affirmed that the souls of those who had been good go to heaven and enjoy perpetual glory, which they described as dwelling with the Sun in places of great delight that Viracocha [the Creator deity] had prepared for this purpose. Some believed that in glory souls neither eat, nor drink, nor sleep, nor have women, nor feel need of any such things; but most held the contrary, believing that those who went to heaven eat and drink splendidly of very fine and choice foods that the Creator has prepared for them, as well as those which their relatives and friends offer and burn in their honor here below. And so they took great care to offer food and drink to their dead, especially to the embalmed bodies of the lords [señores], speaking to them as though they were still alive, saying: "When you were alive you used to eat and drink of these foods and drink: may your soul now receive them and consume them wherever it may be." And they held it as certain that this is what happened—that wherever the souls were, they received and ate the offerings made to them, as they had done before death. And so that they might be further confirmed in this error, sometimes, when God in His will permitted it, the devil would take on the likeness of some prominent man already dead, and would appear to his relatives and acquaintances in his own dress and form, just as he had appeared in life, with his adornments and retinue, and would make them understand that he was dwelling in another realm, joyful and delightful, in the manner in which they saw him there. On account of these words and illusions, his relatives taking those false appearances as true, they redoubled their care in the service and comfort of their dead; and the living, hoping to go in the company of the departed to enjoy that glory after this life, moderated the sorrow that death caused them; and when they were at the point of death, they commended their houses and families to their relatives, asking them to do and fulfill what they charged them with, saying that they would see them again from heaven.

Likewise they were persuaded that there is a hell for the wicked, and that there the demons torment them—demons whom they depicted as very ugly and frightful. They said the place of hell was below the earth, and that it is very dark and confined; and that those who go there suffer great hunger and thirst; and that they are made to eat coals, serpents, toads, and other loathsome vermin, and to drink muddy and foul-smelling water; and that on these alone are the souls of the damned sustained, whose punishment they said is everlasting.

Regarding the merits required to attain glory, and the offenses for which one is condemned, they also went astray; for although they agreed that the wicked were condemned and the good were saved, they could not rightly identify and distinguish the good from the wicked. For the devil persuaded them that the nobles and people of quality, even if they were of depraved morals, were always good and could not possibly be condemned; because hell was only for men of low station and without quality—such as thieves and poor people—for sorcerers [hechiceros] who killed with herbs [yerbas], and for others of that kind, and especially for those who acted against the commands of the king or who spoke ill of the Sun and of the huacas [guacas; sacred shrines or idols]; which sins those of the lineage of the Incas, who were the nobles, did not commit—paying no heed to other categories of sin.

No notion was found among all these Indians concerning the resurrection of the flesh, nor did they in any way believe that bodies were to become anything more than they are. And this is not surprising, it being a point of doctrine that cannot be grasped by natural reason; and since they understood there to be nothing beyond heaven and hell, they made no sacrifices or suffrages [votive offerings] for the souls of the dead, giving as their reason that if the dead were in heaven they had no need of them, and if they were in hell, such offerings would do them no good.

segments.title_inferred: The God Viracocha: Supreme Lord and Creator in Andean Religion
segments.original_text:
CAPITULO IV

Del dios Viracocha, que era tenido de los indios por supremo señor y hacedor de todo

En medio de las tinieblas espesas de la ignorancia, no dejó la luz de la razón de obrar algo en estos indios; porque, alumbrados della, vinieron a alcanzar y creer que había un Dios Criador universal de todas las cosas y Soberano Señor, y gobernador dellas; aunque fue tan corta y oscura esta noticia, que ni aun vocablo propio con que nombrarlo tuvieron; porque todos los que le daban eran metafóricos, como luego diré; por lo cual, en los Catecismos que están hechos para instruirlos en la doctrina cristiana, se pone nuestro nombre Dios y dél usamos siempre que hablamos con ellos, a causa de no haber en su lengua palabra que con propiedad le corresponda: y los mismos indios cristianos lo tienen y usan ya por tan propio como nosotros, dado que en la pronunciación difieran algo los que no son ladinos y españolados; porque como no tuvieron en su lengua D, sino que en lugar della usaban desta letra, T, así, en lugar de decir Dios, suelen pronunciar Tíos.

Item, mezclaban y envolvían en esta flaca noticia que de Dios tenían una infinidad de vanidades y errores, imaginando y atribuyéndole cosas muy ajenas e indignas de su nobilísima naturaleza. Admitían asimismo con la adoración del Supremo Señor la de otras innumerables cosas, que veneraban con igual respeto y reverencia, si bien confesaban ser los otros dioses criados y ministros del Hacedor e intercesores para con él. Y así, cuando hablaban con el Criador, diferían en las palabras, atribuyéndole el poder y mando de todo: teniendo a los otros dioses solamente por señores particulares cada uno en su jurisdicción, conforme a la advocación y patrocinio que tenían. Daban a la primera causa títulos y nombres de gran excelencia: los más honrosos y usados eran dos, ambos translativos y de grande énfasis: Viracocha el uno, y el otro, Pachayacháchic; al primero solían anteponer o posponer algunas palabras, diciendo unas veces Ticciviracocha, y otras Viracocháyacháchic. El de Ticciviracocha era tenido por misterioso, el cual, interpretado, significa "fundamento divino"; el nombre de Pachayacháchic quiere decir "Criador del mundo"; y la misma significación tiene el de Viracocháyacháchic.

Desde que se acuerdan estos indios viene la memoria del Viracocha de mano en mano, y tuvieron siempre gran veneración a este nombre, en tanto grado, que su padre de Incayupanqui se llamó Viracocha-Inca, y ningún rey antes ni después usó ponerse tal nombre: y a él (según dicen) no se lo tuvieron a bien que se lo hubiese puesto, aunque dió por razón que el mismo Viracocha se lo había mandado. Pero dado caso que este dios Viracocha fue tenido en mucha veneración antes del tiempo del dicho Inca, y le solían hacer sacrificios, con todo eso, no le tenían en más que al sol hasta su tiempo. Y la causa de haber ordenado el dicho rey que el dios Viracocha fuese preferido a todos los demás y adorado como Dios universal y Supremo Señor, fue la victoria que alcanzó este Inca de los Chancas, como queda dicho en el libro precedente.
segments.translation:
CHAPTER IV

On the god Viracocha, who was regarded by the Indians as supreme lord and maker of all things

Amidst the thick darkness of ignorance, the light of reason did not cease to produce some effect in these Indians; for, illumined by it, they came to grasp and believe that there was a universal God, Creator of all things, Sovereign Lord and Governor thereof; though this knowledge was so limited and obscure that they did not even possess a proper word with which to name him. All the names they gave him were metaphorical [translaticios], as I shall presently explain. For this reason, in the catechisms [Catecismos] composed to instruct them in Christian doctrine, our word 'God' [Dios] is used, and we employ it always when speaking with them, since their language contains no word that properly corresponds to it. The Christian Indians themselves now regard and use it as their own word just as we do, although those who are not fluent [ladinos] in Spanish and accustomed to Spaniards differ somewhat in pronunciation — for since their language had no letter D, using T in its place, they tend to say 'Tios' instead of 'Dios.'

Furthermore, they mixed and wrapped within this weak notion they had of God an infinity of vanities and errors, imagining and attributing to him things utterly foreign to and unworthy of his most noble nature. They likewise admitted, alongside the worship of the Supreme Lord, the adoration of countless other things, which they venerated with equal respect and reverence — though they did confess that these other gods were created beings, ministers of the Maker and intercessors before him. Thus, when they addressed the Creator, their words differed from those used for lesser gods, attributing to him the power and dominion over all things, while regarding the other gods merely as particular lords, each within his own jurisdiction, according to his sphere of patronage and advocacy [advocación y patrocinio]. They gave to this First Cause titles and names of great excellence; the most honored and commonly used were two, both metaphorical [translativos] and of great weight: one was Viracocha, and the other Pachayacháchic. To the former they would sometimes prepend or append other words, saying at times Ticciviracocha and at other times Viracocháyacháchic. The name Ticciviracocha was considered mysterious, and when interpreted it signifies 'divine foundation'; the name Pachayacháchic means 'Creator of the world'; and Viracocháyacháchic carries the same meaning.
