The college is obliged to attend the cathedral on feast days to serve at the divine offices and to be present in the choir. The governance and administration of the college is vested in a cleric appointed by the archbishop, who bears the title of rector; care is always taken that this person be of exemplary life, someone to whom may be entrusted the formation—in piety and good conduct—of the youth reared in this place.

segments.title_inferred: Foundation and Description of the Hospital of San Diego, run by the Brothers of Saint John of God, Lima
segments.original_text:
CAPITULO XXX

Del hospital de San Diego de los hermanos de San Juan de Dios

El año de mil quinientos noventa y cuatro fundaron este hospital con advocación de San Diego, y le dejaron su hacienda dos personas ricas, marido y mujer, llamados don Cristóbal Sánchez Bilbao, y doña María de Esquivel, vecinos de esta ciudad: los cuales por no haber tenido hijos instituyeron por sus herederos a los pobres de Cristo, y el año de mil seiscientos seis que entraron en este reino y ciudad a fundar su orden los hermanos de San Juan de Dios, se encargaron de él para curar y servir a los pobres enfermos como su regla profesa; fue su primer superior el hermano Francisco López, que al presente es vicario general de su orden en este reino y es el primero que gozó de este título.

Fundóse este hospital para recoger sólo los convalecientes que salen de curarse de San Andrés: y así para ser admitidos en él han de traer firma del mayordomo de San Andrés y son aquí sustentados con el regalo posible hasta que convalecen, lo cual es tan importante a causa de ser esta tierra muy dejativa que los que salen de enfermedad no volvieran en sí y recobraran sus fuerzas en mucho tiempo si no se hubiera dado esta traza. También recogen en esta casa los religiosos de ella, después que la tienen a su cargo, algunos enfermos viejos, como son tullidos e impedidos de otras dolencias, de quien no se espera alcanzaran salud para más ejercitar su acostumbrada caridad; susténtanse aquí de ordinario de treinta a cuarenta enfermos, y los hermanos que atienden a servirlos son diez o doce. Tiene de gasto al año este hospital seis mil pesos, los dos mil son de renta suya y los demás se juntan de limosna. La casa tiene suficiente sitio, con una sala grande que sirve de enfermería y una iglesia mediana bien adornada y otros aposentos. Hay fundada en este hospital una capellanía de cuatrocientos pesos de renta que sirve un clérigo.
segments.translation:
CHAPTER XXX

On the Hospital of San Diego of the Brothers of Saint John of God

In the year fifteen hundred and ninety-four, this hospital was founded under the dedication of Saint Diego [San Diego de Alcalá], and two wealthy persons, husband and wife, named Don Cristóbal Sánchez Bilbao and Doña María de Esquivel, residents of this city, endowed it with their estate. Having had no children, they appointed the poor of Christ as their heirs. In the year sixteen hundred and six, the Brothers of Saint John of God entered this kingdom and city to establish their order, and they took charge of the hospital in order to cure and serve the poor sick, as their rule professes. Their first superior was Brother Francisco López, who at present is Vicar General of his order in this kingdom and is the first person to have held that title.

This hospital was founded solely to receive convalescents who are discharged from treatment at [the Hospital of] San Andrés. Accordingly, in order to be admitted, they must bring a certificate signed by the majordomo [steward] of San Andrés, and they are here maintained with every possible comfort until they recover. This arrangement is of the greatest importance, because this land is so enervating [dejativa] that those who emerge from illness would not have recovered their senses or regained their strength for a very long time had this provision not been made. The friars of the house also receive, after taking charge of it, certain elderly sick persons—such as the crippled and those incapacitated by other ailments—from whom no recovery is expected, doing so to exercise further their customary charity. Between thirty and forty patients are ordinarily maintained here, and the brothers who attend to their service number ten or twelve. The hospital's annual expenditure is six thousand pesos: two thousand come from its own income [renta] and the remainder is gathered from alms. The building has sufficient space, with a large hall that serves as the infirmary, a medium-sized, well-adorned church, and other rooms. A chaplaincy [capellanía] of four hundred pesos annual income has been established in this hospital, served by a cleric.

segments.title_inferred: Foundation and Description of the Hospital of San Pedro for Poor Clergy
segments.original_text:
CAPITULO XXXI
Del hospital de San Pedro

En el hospital de San Pedro no se curan más pobres que clérigos de orden sacro, el cual tuvo este principio: saliéndose un día a pasear tres o cuatro clérigos juntos por el barrio de San Lázaro, hallaron un sacerdote enfermo en un rancho de un indio, tan desamparado, que no tenía más cama que una barbacoa o zarzo de cañas, ni otro regalo que un poco de maíz que le daba el indio: moviéronse a compasión de él y lleváronle a curar al hospital de San Andrés, donde cuidaron de él todo el tiempo que estuvo enfermo. Con este motivo comenzaron a platicar entre sí que sería de gran servicio de Dios instituir alguna obra pía en beneficio de los clérigos pobres, y habiendo comunicado con otros este su deseo, fundaron una cofradía de veinticuatro clérigos, con nombre de la Cátedra de San Pedro, dedicada para curar clérigos pobres, cuya institución se hizo por el mes de septiembre del año de mil quinientos noventa y cuatro. Al preste dieron el nombre de abad mayor y fué el primero Pedro de Escobar, y los demás oficiales [fueron] los siguientes: mayordomo, Miguel de Bobadilla, que murió racionero de la catedral de esta ciudad; diputados, el bachiller Luis de Betanzos y el bachiller Luis López de Alarcón; procurador, el bachiller Pedro Romero; vicario, Gaspar de Montalvo, que murió religioso de la Compañía de Jesús.

Curaban sus enfermos al principio en el hospital de San Andrés, y los visitaban a menudo, hasta que el año de mil quinientos noventa y nueve compraron sitio y edificaron su hospital. Dieron la primera renta y limosna para esta obra dos sacerdotes, llamados el canónigo León y don Gabriel Solano. Tiene de capellanías esta casa doscientas setenta misas y de renta quinientos pesos y se gastan tres mil en cada un año, porque lo demás de la renta se junta de limosnas. Acompañan los de esta cofradía por vía de hermandad o todos o la mitad los entierros para que son llamados, y la limosna que se da por este acompañamiento es para el hospital: cuando va toda la hermandad se dan de limosna cien pesos, y cuando la mitad cincuenta. Cúranse aquí los clérigos enfermos con todo regalo y hay ordinariamente desde cuatro hasta ocho enfermos. La casa es mediana, con los aposentos suficientes, un patio moderado, un pedazo de huerta y un cuarto aparte donde vive el mayordomo. La iglesia es muy grande, con la capilla mayor de bóveda bien edificada y su cementerio que sale a una esquina de la cuadra. Los clérigos de esta hermandad entierran los difuntos de ella con mucha pompa, y se hacen muchos sacrificios por ellos.
segments.translation:
CHAPTER XXXI
On the Hospital of San Pedro

At the Hospital of San Pedro, none but poor clergymen of holy orders [clérigos de orden sacro] are treated. Its origin was as follows: one day, three or four clergymen went out together for a walk through the neighborhood [barrio] of San Lázaro, where they found a sick priest in the hut of an Indian, in so wretched a condition that he had no bed other than a barbacoa or zarzo [a raised platform or frame of canes], nor any other comfort than a little maize that the Indian gave him. Moved to compassion, they took him to be treated at the Hospital of San Andrés, where they cared for him throughout his illness. This event prompted them to discuss among themselves that it would be a great service to God to establish some pious work [obra pía] for the benefit of poor clergymen. Having shared this desire with others, they founded a confraternity [cofradía] of twenty-four clergymen, under the name of the Chair of Saint Peter [Cátedra de San Pedro], dedicated to the care of poor clergy. Its foundation took place in the month of September in the year fifteen ninety-four. To the presiding priest [preste] they gave the title of abbot-major [abad mayor]; the first to hold this office was Pedro de Escobar. The remaining officers [were] as follows: steward [mayordomo], Miguel de Bobadilla, who died as a prebendary [racionero] of the cathedral of this city; deputies [diputados], Bachiller Luis de Betanzos and Bachiller Luis López de Alarcón; attorney [procurador], Bachiller Pedro Romero; vicar [vicario], Gaspar de Montalvo, who died as a member of the Society of Jesus [Compañía de Jesús].

At first they treated their sick in the Hospital of San Andrés and visited them frequently, until in the year fifteen ninety-nine they purchased a site and built their own hospital. Two priests, known as the Canon León and Don Gabriel Solano, provided the first income and alms for this undertaking. This establishment has two hundred and seventy chaplaincy masses [capellanías] and an income of five hundred pesos, though three thousand are spent each year, the remainder of the revenue being gathered from alms. The members of this confraternity, acting as a brotherhood, accompany—either all together or half of them—the funeral processions to which they are called, and the alms given for this service go to the hospital: when the whole brotherhood attends, one hundred pesos are given as alms, and when only half attend, fifty. Sick clergymen are treated here with every comfort [regalo], and there are ordinarily between four and eight patients. The building is of moderate size, with sufficient rooms, a modest courtyard, a small garden, and a separate quarter where the steward resides. The church is very large, with a well-built vaulted main chapel [capilla mayor de bóveda] and a cemetery opening onto a corner of the block. The clergymen of this brotherhood bury their deceased members with great pomp, and many masses [sacrificios] are offered for them.

segments.title_inferred: Foundation of the Hospital of Our Lady of Atocha for Orphaned Children, Lima
segments.original_text:
CAPITULO XXXII

Del hospital de Nuestra Señora de Atocha de los niños huérfanos

Para que ninguna suerte de pobres de todos sexos y edades dejase de participar de la gran piedad de esta ciudad, después de haberse fundado en ella tantos hospitales de hombres y mujeres como habemos visto, se instituyó casa y albergue donde se recogiesen y criasen los niños huérfanos expuestos, que es una de las obras pías de mayor utilidad que tiene esta república, cuyo principio fue de esta manera. En el año de mil quinientos noventa y seis vino a esta ciudad con el virrey don Luis de Velasco un siervo de Dios, llamado por su humildad Luis Pecador, el cual vestido de un grosero saco de sayal dio gran ejemplo de virtud y penitencia el tiempo que vivió: en vida fue tenido y reputado de todos por santo, y después de su muerte creció mucho más la veneración en los que alcanzamos a conocer su santa vida. Movido, pues, este varón de Dios con celo de hacerle en sus pobres algún servicio que le fuese agradable, trató de fundar un hospital para curar negros horros, y para este efecto compró con dinero que juntó de limosnas el sitio en que ahora está edificada esta casa. Tenía por confesor un religioso de San Francisco llamado fray Juan de la Roca, el cual saliendo dos noches de su convento a confesiones de enfermos en diferentes tiempos, halló la una que unos perros se estaban comiendo una criatura en la pescadería, y la otra otros perros comiéndose otra en el cementerio de la Merced; lastimado el religioso de estos dos casos, persuadió a Luis Pecador que el hospital que edificaba para negros lo conmutase para criar en él estos niños expuestos. Abrazó el consejo de su confesor el siervo de Dios, y con limosnas que comenzó a juntar hizo el retablo que hoy tiene la iglesia de esta casa, que es de pincel, de Nuestra Señora de Atocha, y fue recogiendo niños y dándolos a criar a amas, que pagaba de las limosnas que juntaba. Pero como viese que las limosnas se acortaban y crecía el número de las criaturas que le echaban al torno, acudió al virrey don Luis de Velasco y le suplicó que hablase a algunas personas ricas, que acudiesen a socorrerle, porque ya no podía ir adelante con esta obra; el virrey llamó algunos mercaderes y les pidió se encargasen de esta obra de tan grande misericordia; mas ellos se excusaron, diciendo que no podrían acudir a ella por andar muy ocupados en sus mercancías.

Quedó muy desconsolado Luis Pecador porque no hallaba dinero para pagar unas amas que le habían dejado por esto los niños que criaban. Él entonces, cargándose con dos en la capa o capilla que siempre traía al hombro, y con ellos en los brazos, se fue a la plaza y a grandes voces dijo: «Ayudadme, hermanos, a criar estos niños y otros que me han echado.» Oyéndolo uno de los escribanos que a la sazón se halló cerca debajo de los portales, acudió a otros seis que estaban allí y les persuadió que tomasen a su cargo esta obra, que Dios Nuestro Señor les ayudaría para ello. Juntáronse luego dentro de media hora, sin llamarlos nadie, como de ochenta escribanos reales y relatores, y convenidos en esto se fueron al virrey y se ofrecieron de encargarse de este cuidado. Agradecióselo mucho el virrey y exhortólos a la perseverancia; ellos juntaron su cabildo y eligieron mayordomo, con que desde entonces quedó instituida esta obra pía y hermandad de los escribanos que cuidan de ella.

Viendo Luis Pecador el ofrecimiento tan grande de los escribanos y cumplidos sus deseos, lleno de gozo del cielo dijo: «Ahora que ven mis ojos aquesto, deja, Señor, a tu siervo en paz»; y parece haberle oído Nuestro Señor, porque murió dentro de tres días. Se le hizo un solemnísimo entierro, como hombre santo, acudiendo a él todas las religiones y clerecía y gran concurso de pueblo.

La habitación de esta casa es capaz aunque de humilde fábrica, con una iglesia mediana que ahora se ha renovado y agrandado y héchosele buena portada. El sacerdote que sirve una capellanía que aquí hay fundada es coadjutor de los curas de la iglesia mayor como queda dicho en su lugar.

Los niños que echan al torno de esta casa se dan a amas que están salariadas fuera de ella, para que les den el pecho y en destetándolos los vuelven, y se crían aquí, para cuya enseñanza está asalariado un maestro de escuela, que los enseña a leer y escribir. Serán los niños que cada año traen a criar a esta casa de cuarenta a cincuenta, y hay ordinariamente de pecho ochenta fuera de la casa, en poder de las amas, y de puertas adentro hasta cuarenta; con que vienen a ser ciento veinte los que ordinariamente aquí se crían. Para cuyo sustento tiene de renta este hospital mil quinientos pesos en cada un año; y gástanse seis mil con lo que se junta de limosna; los niños mayorcitos van en comunidad con sus sobrepellices sobre sus ropas pardas, y su pendón negro a acompañar los entierros, por la limosna que por ello les dan. Tiene la administración la hermandad de los escribanos, los cuales con limosnas que juntan sustentan esta obra de tan gran servicio de Dios y beneficio de la república.
segments.translation:
CHAPTER XXXII

On the Hospital of Our Lady of Atocha for Orphaned Children

So that no class of poor persons of either sex or any age should fail to partake of the great charity of this city—after so many hospitals for men and women had already been founded here, as we have seen—a house and shelter was established where abandoned orphaned children [niños expuestos, i.e., foundlings] could be gathered and raised. This is one of the most beneficial pious works [obras pías] that this commonwealth possesses, and its origin was as follows. In the year fifteen hundred and ninety-six, there came to this city in the company of the Viceroy Don Luis de Velasco a servant of God who, out of humility, called himself Luis Pecador [Luis the Sinner]. Clothed in a coarse sack of sackcloth [sayal], he gave a great example of virtue and penitence throughout his life; while living he was universally regarded and reputed as a saint, and after his death the veneration of those of us who were privileged to know his holy life grew considerably. Moved, then, by zeal to render some service pleasing to God among His poor, this man of God set about founding a hospital to treat freed black slaves [negros horros], and to this end purchased with money he had gathered from alms the site upon which this house now stands. His confessor was a Franciscan friar named Fray Juan de la Roca, who, going out from his convent on two separate nights to hear the confessions of the sick, found on one occasion that some dogs were eating an infant in the fish market, and on another that other dogs were devouring another infant in the cemetery of La Merced. Deeply grieved by these two incidents, the friar persuaded Luis Pecador to convert the hospital he was building for black slaves into a house for raising these abandoned children. The servant of God embraced his confessor's counsel, and with alms he began to collect he commissioned the painted altarpiece [retablo de pincel] of Our Lady of Atocha, which the church of this house still possesses today; and he proceeded to gather children and place them with wet nurses [amas], whom he paid from the alms he collected. But seeing that the alms were diminishing while the number of infants deposited in the turning-cradle [torno] continued to grow, he appealed to Viceroy Don Luis de Velasco and begged him to speak to certain wealthy persons who might come to his aid, since he could no longer sustain the work on his own. The viceroy summoned some merchants and asked them to take charge of this work of such great mercy; but they excused themselves, saying they could not attend to it because they were too occupied with their trade.

Luis Pecador was left deeply disconsolate because he could find no money to pay the wet nurses who, for this reason, had abandoned the children they were nursing. He then, loading two infants into the hood or capelet [capa o capilla] he always wore over his shoulder and carrying them in his arms, went to the plaza and cried out in a loud voice: «Help me, brothers, to raise these children and others who have been left with me.» Upon hearing him, one of the notaries [escribanos] who happened to be nearby under the arcades went to six others who were there and persuaded them to take this work upon themselves, for God Our Lord would assist them in it. Within half an hour, with no one summoning them, some eighty royal notaries and court reporters [escribanos reales y relatores] assembled; and having agreed upon the matter, they went to the viceroy and offered to assume this responsibility. The viceroy thanked them heartily and urged them to persevere; they formed their council [cabildo] and elected a steward [mayordomo], so that from that time forward this pious work and the brotherhood [hermandad] of the notaries who oversee it was formally established.

When Luis Pecador saw the great commitment of the notaries and his desires fulfilled, he said, filled with heavenly joy: «Now that my eyes have seen this, Lord, let Thy servant depart in peace»; and Our Lord appears to have heard him, for he died within three days. He was given a most solemn funeral, befitting a holy man, attended by all the religious orders, the clergy, and a great multitude of people.

The building of this house is of considerable capacity, though of modest construction, with a medium-sized church that has recently been renovated and enlarged and given a fine doorway. The priest who serves the chaplaincy [capellanía] founded here acts as curate's assistant [coadjutor] to the priests of the principal church [iglesia mayor], as has been noted in its proper place.

The children deposited in the turning-cradle of this house are given to salaried wet nurses who live outside the establishment, so that they may nurse them; once the children are weaned they are returned, and are raised here, for which purpose a schoolmaster is employed and paid to teach them to read and write. Between forty and fifty children are brought each year to be raised in this house; ordinarily there are eighty at the breast outside the house in the care of wet nurses, and up to forty within the walls, making some one hundred and twenty children ordinarily raised here in total. For their sustenance, this hospital has a fixed income of fifteen hundred pesos per year, and six thousand are spent in total counting what is gathered in alms. The older boys go out as a group, wearing their surplices [sobrepellices] over their plain brown garments and carrying their black banner [pendón negro], to accompany funeral processions, in exchange for the alms given to them for this. Administration is held by the brotherhood of the notaries, who with the alms they gather sustain this work of such great service to God and benefit to the commonwealth.

segments.title_inferred: Chapel of the City Jail and Its Brotherhood
segments.original_text:
CAPITULO XXXIII

De las capillas de las cárceles, y hermandad que está fundada en ellas

En el mismo sitio de las casas de Cabildo está la cárcel de la ciudad: la cual tiene una buena capilla e iglesia de la advocación de San Pedro y San Pablo, cuya puerta principal sale a la plaza por debajo de los portales. Y otra que tiene a un lado a la calle de Santo Domingo. Está bien edificada y tiene dos altares, sacristía y los ornamentos necesarios, con dos sacerdotes que la sirven, el uno con nombre de capellán y de sacristán el otro, ambos con salario competente; en esta capilla está fundada una hermandad de treinta personas honradas, capilla y hermandad; son muy grandes las gracias e indulgencias y jubileos que han alcanzado de los Sumos Pontífices, de que tienen veintidós bulas. Basta decir, en suma, que en esta capilla se ganan todas las gracias e indulgencias que en la iglesia de San Juan de Letrán, en Roma.

Servía esta capilla antes para las dos cárceles, la de la ciudad y la de la corte, y para entrambas tenía reja; pero después que la de la corte se pasó adonde ahora está, se le edificó capilla muy buena, la cual goza de las mismas gracias que ésta, y la hermandad que se fundó en esta de San Pedro y San Pablo acude a socorrer los pobres presos de entrambas, para cuyo beneficio fue instituida y usa con ellos de muy grande piedad; porque con limosnas que pide les da de comer todos los días, y por su turno se eligen dos hermanos de los treinta, que solicitan y procuran sus negocios, y tienen letrado y procurador para ello.
segments.translation:
CHAPTER XXXIII

On the Chapels of the Jails, and the Brotherhood Founded in Them

In the same location as the houses of the Cabildo [town council] stands the city jail, which has a fine chapel and church dedicated to Saint Peter and Saint Paul, whose main door opens onto the plaza beneath the arcades [portales], and another door on one side facing the street of Santo Domingo. It is well constructed and has two altars, a sacristy, and the necessary ornaments [vestments and liturgical furnishings], with two priests who serve it—one bearing the title of chaplain and the other of sacristan—both with a fitting salary. In this chapel a brotherhood of thirty honorable persons has been established. The graces, indulgences, and jubilees that this chapel and brotherhood have obtained from the Supreme Pontiffs are very great, as evidenced by the twenty-two papal bulls they hold. It suffices to say, in sum, that in this chapel one may gain all the graces and indulgences that are granted at the church of San Giovanni in Laterano in Rome.

Formerly this chapel served both jails—the city jail and the court jail—and had a grating [reja] giving access to both; but after the court jail was moved to its present location, a very fine chapel was built for it as well, which enjoys the same graces as this one. The brotherhood founded in this chapel of Saint Peter and Saint Paul undertakes to aid the poor prisoners of both jails, for whose benefit it was instituted, and it deals with them with very great piety: for through alms that it solicits it provides them with food every day, and two brothers from among the thirty are chosen in turn to attend to and manage their affairs, and they retain a lawyer [letrado] and a legal representative [procurador] for that purpose.

segments.title_inferred: Table of Contents: Book XIII (chapters on festivals and priests) and Book XIV (chapters on Inca culture and customs)

LIBRO DECIMOCUARTO

I.—De la lengua quichua, que es la general del Perú . 235

II.—Del traje y vestidos destos indios . 237

IV.—Del ajuar y alhajas que tenían en sus casas . 242

segments.title_inferred: Description of Scotland and the British Isles — principal towns, castles, rivers, bridges, and islands
segments.original_text: Item se cuentan en él pueblos principales con sus ferias y mercados, seiscientos y cuarenta y uno; castillos, ciento y ochenta y seis; ríos, quinientos y cincuenta y cuatro, y novecientas y cincuenta y seis puentes. El reino de Escocia tiene dos arzobispados y trece obispados; es de tierra más doblada, fría y menos poblada que el de Inglaterra. En torno desta isla hay otras muchas pequeñas; ciento y cuarenta y cinco dellas están pobladas, y las demás, son de rocas y peñascos.
segments.translation: Likewise, counted within it [England] are principal towns with their fairs and markets: six hundred and forty-one; castles: one hundred and eighty-six; rivers: five hundred and fifty-four; and nine hundred and fifty-six bridges. The kingdom of Scotland has two archbishoprics and thirteen bishoprics; it is a land more rugged, cold, and less populous than that of England. Around this island there are many other smaller ones; one hundred and forty-five of them are inhabited, and the rest are bare rocks and crags.

segments.title_inferred: On Salt: Its Abundance in Peru and Its Use in Silver Processing
segments.original_text:
CAPITULO IV
De la sal

Así como este reino del Perú es el más abundante de metales, mayormente de plata, de cuantos sabemos hoy en el mundo, así también lo es de salinas naturales; que parece proveyó Dios de ellas con tanta abundancia donde tan necesaria es la sal para beneficiar los metales de plata, en que se consume una cantidad increíble de sal; y no hay provincia ni ciudad que padezca falta della; porque de la mucha que se saca en unas tierras, se reparte a otras y ninguna
segments.translation:
CHAPTER IV
On Salt

Just as this kingdom of Peru is the most abundant in metals — above all in silver — of any known to us in the world today, so too is it in natural salt deposits [salinas]; for it seems God provided them in such abundance in a place where salt is so necessary for the processing [beneficio] of silver ores, in which an incredible quantity of salt is consumed. And there is no province or city that suffers any lack of it, because of the great amounts extracted in some lands, which are then distributed to others, and none [the passage breaks off here]

segments.title_inferred: On Various Clays and Earths Used in Pottery
segments.original_text:
CAPITULO VI

De algunos barros y greda de que se hace loza

De la varia mixtura de los elementos e influencias de los cuerpos celestes se forman tan varias tierras y tan diferentes entre sí en colores y cualidades como vemos, de que se hallan tantas diferencias en este Nuevo Mundo, como
segments.translation:
CHAPTER VI

On certain clays and fuller's earth [greda] from which earthenware is made

From the varied mixture of the elements and the influences of the celestial bodies there are formed soils so various and so different from one another in colors and qualities as we observe, of which so many varieties are found in this New World, as

segments.title_inferred: Building Stones: Quarried and Volcanic Varieties
segments.original_text:
CAPITULO XIV
De las piedras para fábricas

Toda suerte de piedra franca y berroqueña que es suave de labrar es la que decimos ser a propósito para edificios de cantería, de la cual hay escogidas canteras en todas estas Indias; unas piedras se hallan muy livianas y cavernosas, a modo de un panal o piedra pómez, que no se hunde en el agua, la cual es maravillosa para fábricas, señaladamente para murallas y fortalezas; semejantes a ellas hay otros muchos géneros de piedras.
segments.translation:
CHAPTER XIV
On Building Stones

Every sort of freestone [piedra franca] and granite [berroqueña] that is easy to work is what we call suitable for ashlar [cantería] construction, of which there are choice quarries throughout these Indies. Some stones are found to be very light and porous, in the manner of a honeycomb or pumice stone [piedra pómez], which does not sink in water; this is marvellous for construction, particularly for walls and fortifications. Similar to these there are many other kinds of stones.

segments.title_inferred: On the Bezoar Stone
segments.original_text:
CAPITULO XXIX
De la piedra bezar

La piedra bezar está compuesta de unas escamas o costras como cáscaras de bellotas, unas sobre otras, de las cuales la primera es muy lisa y lustrosa. Hállanse estas piedras de diferente tamaño, desde tan pequeñas como garbanzos hasta de la grandeza de un huevo de gallina; y algunas (aunque raras) tan grandes como el puño y de una libra y más de peso (20). La figura que tienen es muy varia; porque unas son redondas; otras, ovadas y chatas, y de tan di[versa forma...]
segments.translation:
CHAPTER XXIX
On the Bezoar Stone

The bezoar stone is composed of layers or crusts resembling acorn shells, one upon another, of which the outermost is very smooth and lustrous. These stones are found in different sizes, from as small as chickpeas up to the size of a hen's egg; and some (though rare) as large as a fist and weighing one pound or more (20). Their shape is highly varied: some are round; others, oval and flat, and of such diverse [forms...]

segments.title_inferred: Formation of Bezoar Stones from an Antidotal Herb

segments.title_inferred: On Pearls: Early Discovery in the Americas
segments.original_text:
CAPITULO XXXII
De las perlas

Los dos primeros descubridores de los mares y costas de la América fueron los que primero hallaron las perlas que en entrambos mares se crían: el almirante don Cristóbal Colón, en la costa de la mar del Norte, y el capitán Vasco Núñez de Balboa, en la de la mar del Sur. Porque la tercera vez que vino el almirante a Indias, el año de 1498, viniendo costeando la tierra firme que en aquel viaje descubrió, tomó tierra en la isla de Cubagua, ec
segments.translation:
CHAPTER XXXII
On Pearls

The first two explorers of the seas and coasts of America were also those who first discovered the pearls that are found in both seas: Admiral Don Cristóbal Colón, on the coast of the North Sea [Caribbean], and Captain Vasco Núñez de Balboa, on that of the South Sea [Pacific]. For on the third occasion that the Admiral came to the Indies, in the year 1498, sailing along the mainland coast that he discovered on that voyage, he made landfall on the island of Cubagua, ec [text breaks off]

segments.title_inferred: On Precious Stones of Various Colors and Virtues Found in the Indies
segments.original_text:
CAPITULO XXXIII

De otras piedras preciosas y de varios colores y virtudes que se hallan en estas Indias

Por piedras preciosas entiendo todas aquellas que son claras y relucientes, aunque muchas de ellas no sean tenidas de los hombres en tanto precio, como son el cristal, el topacio, el granate y otras deste género. Algunos tienen por opinión que el cristal se engendra de sola agua congelada con excesivo frío, sin mixtión de tierra; lo cual, si fuera verdad, se h[ILLEGIBLE: text truncated mid-sentence]
segments.translation:
CHAPTER XXXIII

On Other Precious Stones of Various Colors and Virtues Found in These Indies

By precious stones I understand all those that are clear and lustrous, even though many of them are not held in such high esteem by men, such as crystal, topaz, garnet, and others of this kind. Some hold the opinion that crystal is generated from water alone, congealed by excessive cold, without any admixture of earth; which, if it were true, [ILLEGIBLE: sentence breaks off mid-clause, likely continuing to discuss implications of the theory of crystal's origin]

segments.title_inferred: Obsidian and Gold-Purple Stone: Indigenous Stone Tools of Peru and New Spain
segments.original_text:
Otra piedra hay muy parecida a ésta, de color de oro tirante a purpúreo, que también se parte en hojas.

Las navajas, cuchillas, lancetas y todo género de herramientas que nosotros hacemos de hierro, solían hacer los indios, así del Perú como de la Nueva España, de cierta piedra que los mexicanos llaman itzli, y los del Perú, chillca lisa; la cual es transparente, como vidrio y se halla de tres colores: blanca, negra y azul. Hay muchas canteras della, así en el Perú como en la Nueva España.
segments.translation:
There is another stone very similar to this one [i.e., the white laminar stone preceding this passage], of a golden color tending toward purple, which likewise splits into sheets [leaves/laminae].

The razors, blades, lancets, and all manner of tools that we make of iron, the Indians—both of Peru and of New Spain—were accustomed to make from a certain stone which the Mexicans call itzli [obsidian], and those of Peru call chillca lisa; this stone is transparent, like glass, and is found in three colors: white, black, and blue. There are many quarries of it, both in Peru and in New Spain.

segments.title_inferred: On Types of Ore Deposits: Loose Metals and Fixed Veins
segments.original_text:

Las minas de metales continuados y seguidos están siempre entre dos peñas, que llaman la caja de la mina, entre las cuales corren largo espacio, y se llaman vetas fijas, porque son permanecientes y de dura. Unas son más anchas que otras, y una misma veta por partes se ensancha mucho y
segments.translation:
of very low grade [lit. 'little assay value'], and these are called quemazones [lit. 'burnings' or 'scorchings']; and others (which is the more common case) occur in the pockets [senos] of the earth. However, these mines of loose metals are not held in great regard, on account of the uncertainty inherent in them.

The mines of continuous and uninterrupted metals are always found between two rock formations, which are called the caja [lit. 'box' or 'case'] of the mine, between which they run for a considerable distance, and are called vetas fijas [fixed veins], because they are permanent and enduring. Some are wider than others, and one and the same vein may in places widen considerably and [text breaks off]

segments.original_text: de noche; alúmbranse con velas de sebo, nunca cesan en su labor, remudándose unos y otros, para que los unos reposen mientras trabajan los otros. Pasan increíble trabajo y afán; porque además de no gozar del aire puro y fresco que baña la superficie de la tierra, sino de uno avahado y espeso con el humo de las candelas y diversos vapores que exhalan los minerales (que suelen ser dañosos y pestilenciales, por proceder algunos de piedra azufre y caparrosa), es muy grande la fat[iga]
segments.translation: at night; they light themselves with tallow candles, never ceasing in their labor, relieving one another in turns so that some may rest while others work. They endure incredible toil and hardship; for beyond being deprived of the pure, fresh air that bathes the surface of the earth, they breathe only a steamy and thick air fouled by the smoke of the candles and the various vapors exhaled by the mineral ores (which are commonly harmful and pestilential, as some proceed from sulfur stone [piedra azufre] and copperas [caparrosa]), and very great is the fati[gue]

segments.title_inferred: Mercury Amalgamation Process for Silver Refining
segments.original_text:
CAPITULO XXXIX
Del beneficio con azogue

El beneficio de azogue es de mucha más riqueza que el de fundición, porque es más copioso y general y se saca con él todo la plata de los metales, por bajos y pobres que sean. Cuando el metal acude de dos onzas para arriba por quintal, se puede beneficiar con ganancia, y de aquí para abajo, es con muy poca o ninguna, particularmente en los asientos de minas que no tienen indios de cédula y repartimiento, o de mita, como llaman en el Perú.
segments.translation:
CHAPTER XXXIX
On the Mercury Amalgamation Process

The mercury amalgamation process [beneficio de azogue] is far more productive than smelting [fundición], because it is more abundant in yield and more universally applicable, and by means of it all the silver is extracted from the ores, however low-grade and poor they may be. When the ore yields two ounces or more per hundredweight [quintal], it can be processed profitably; below this threshold, profit is very little or none at all, particularly in mining settlements that do not have Indians assigned by royal warrant and allotment [cédula y repartimiento], or by the mita, as it is called in Peru.

segments.title_inferred: On the Pigs and Bars of Silver: Washing and Separating Mercury from Silver
segments.original_text:
CAPITULO XL
De las piños y barras de plata

Lavado el metal y apartado el azogue abrazado con la plata, que llaman pella, de la tierra y escoria, lo echan en unos lienzos bastos, y apretando la pella, le sacan a golpes parte del azogue, porque ya no queda otro beneficio que hacerle a la plata más de apartarla de su tan íntimo compañero el azogue. Exprimida de esta manera la pella queda suelta y blanda y muy semejante en el tacto a la cernada o nieve condensada. Della se hacen
segments.translation:
CHAPTER XL
On the Pigs and Bars of Silver

The ore having been washed and the mercury [azogue] that was bonded with the silver — which they call the pella [amalgam mass] — separated from the earth and slag, it is placed into coarse linen cloths, and by pressing and beating the pella, part of the mercury is expelled, since no further treatment remains to be done to the silver beyond separating it from its most intimate companion, the mercury. Pressed out in this manner, the pella becomes loose and soft, very similar to the touch to lye-ash residue [cernada] or compacted snow. From it are made [text continues]

segments.title_inferred: On Copper: Mining and Indigenous Use in the New World
segments.original_text:
CAPITULO XLII
Del cobre

Hallase mayor copia de minas de cobre en este reino y en otras muchas partes de Indias, que de plata ni de otros metales. Sacábanlo antiguamente los indios en mucha cantidad, a cuya causa se ven hoy muchas minas labradas de tiempo antiguo. Porque, como carecían de hierro, forjaban deste metal las armas, herramientas e instrumentos para la agricultura y algunos otros oficios; mas no se aprovechaban del en algún uso de medicina. Al presente labran
segments.translation:
CHAPTER XLII
On Copper

Greater quantities of copper mines are found in this kingdom and in many other parts of the Indies than of silver or any other metals. The Indians formerly extracted it in great amounts, on account of which many mines worked in ancient times are still visible today. Because they lacked iron, they forged from this metal their weapons, tools, and instruments for agriculture and various other trades; however, they made no use of it in any application of medicine. At present [they work]

segments.title_inferred: American Nettles, Celery, Houseleeks, Plantain, Sorrel, and Sage: Medicinal Plants Used by Indigenous Peoples
segments.original_text:
un verde claro, hojas pequeñas, crespas y casi redondas, cuyo vello es más espinoso. Demás déstas, se hallan las ortigas muertas, que no pican al que las toca. Aprovéchanse los indios de las ortigas para muchas curas.

Item, se hallan apio; siempre-viva de dos o tres maneras; llantén, romaza, salvia; la una especie della, de dos que pone el doctor Laguna sobre Dioscórides, que es la mayor, de hojas anchas y sin aquellas orejillas que tienen las hojas de la otra; escorzo
segments.translation:
a light green [color], with small, curly and nearly round leaves, whose hairs are more spiny. Beyond these, one also finds dead nettles [ortigas muertas], which do not sting those who touch them. The Indians make use of nettles for many cures.

Likewise, one finds celery [apio]; houseleek [siempre-viva] of two or three varieties; plantain [llantén]; sorrel [romaza]; sage [salvia]—one species of it, of the two that Doctor Laguna describes in his commentary on Dioscorides, namely the larger one, with broad leaves and lacking those little ear-like lobes [orejillas] that the leaves of the other species bear; [escorzo—text breaks off]

segments.title_inferred: Chicha: Indigenous Fermented Beverages of the New World
segments.original_text:
CAPITULO IV
De la chicha del maíz

Debajo de este nombre de chicha se comprehenden todas las bebidas que usaban los naturales de este Nuevo Mundo en lugar de vino, y con que frecuentemente se embriagan; al cual vicio son tan inclinados, que ni ha aprovechado haberse convertido a nuestra Santa Fe, ni el trato y comunicación con los españoles, ni los castigos hacen en ellos sus curas y las [ordenanzas] para que se aparten dél, dado que en algunas provincias se ve alguna enmienda y en general [el texto continúa].
segments.translation:
CHAPTER IV
On the chicha of maize

Under this name of chicha are comprehended all the beverages that the natives of this New World used in place of wine, and with which they frequently make themselves drunk; to which vice they are so inclined that neither their conversion to our Holy Faith has availed, nor their dealings and intercourse with the Spaniards, nor do the punishments [administered by] their curates and [the ordinances enacted] to turn them from it have effect upon them—though in some provinces a degree of improvement is seen, and in general [the text continues].

segments.title_inferred: On Ají (Chili Pepper)
segments.original_text:
CAPITULO XXV
Del ají

Entre las legumbres que producen el fruto en sus ramas, tiene el ají, después del maíz, el primer lugar, como la planta más general y de mayor estima entre los indios de cuantas se hallaron en esta tierra; porque entre las especias que dió Dios a los naturales de ella, es tan recibida de todas las naciones deste Nuevo Mundo, que no se ha hallado ninguna que no tuviese el uso della y en mucha estimación; y no sólo
segments.translation:
CHAPTER XXV
On Ají

Among the vegetables that bear their fruit on their branches, ají holds, after maize, the foremost place, as the most widespread and highly esteemed plant among the Indians of all those found in this land; for among the spices that God gave to its native peoples, it is so universally received by all the nations of this New World that none has been found that did not make use of it and hold it in great esteem; and not only

segments.title_inferred: The Paraguay Gourd
segments.original_text:
CAPITULO XXX
De la calabaza del Paraguay

A cierta especie de calabaza propia de esta tierra llaman los españoles en este reino del Perú calabazas del Paraguay, por ser naturales de aquella provincia. La planta es voluble, del género de las que se asen y encaraman sobre árboles, y no teniendo arrimo se extienden sobre la tierra; el vástago es delgado como un dedo, con cinco esquinas a lo largo que lo hacen acanalado; la hoja es redonda, de media tercia de diámetro, con siet[e...]
segments.translation:
CHAPTER XXX
On the Paraguay Gourd

A certain species of gourd native to this land is called by the Spaniards in this kingdom of Peru the 'Paraguay gourd' [calabaza del Paraguay], on account of its being native to that province. The plant is a climbing vine [voluble], of the kind that grips and scrambles up over trees, and when lacking support spreads itself along the ground. The stem is slender as a finger, with five lengthwise ridges that give it a channeled [grooved] appearance. The leaf is round, half a third [of a vara, roughly 14 cm] in diameter, with sev[en... — text ends abruptly]

segments.title_inferred: On the Amancaes flower
segments.original_text:
CAPITULO XLII

De los amancaes

De innumerables géneros de flores que producen estas Indias se hallan muy pocas que rindan suave olor, y en ésas esperimentamos una cosa bien particular, y es que, en maltratándolas en las manos, pierden su fragancia y se convierte en desabrido olor, con que se verifica no ser a propósito para sacar agua dellas por alquitara, como en efecto no se saca, sino que todas las que se destilan son de nuestras flores
segments.translation:
CHAPTER XLII

On the Amancaes

Among the innumerable kinds of flowers produced in these Indies, very few are found that yield a gentle fragrance, and in those we observe a quite particular thing: that when they are roughly handled in the hands, they lose their fragrance and it turns into an unpleasant smell, by which it is confirmed that they are not suitable for drawing water from them by alembic [alquitara], as indeed none is extracted from them — rather, all the waters that are distilled come from our [i.e., Old World] flowers

segments.title_inferred: The Maguey of Wine (New Spain Agave)
segments.original_text:
CAPITULO XXI
Del maguey del vino

El maguey del capítulo pasado es el que nace generalmente en este reino del Perú, y el de este capítulo es natural de la Nueva España, y difieren los dos en muchas cosas; y una es que todas las castas de magüeyes de la Nueva España tienen en la punta de las hojas una espina negra muy dura y aguda semejante a una lesna; de las cuales espinas carece el magüey deste reino. El magüey del vino es muy general en la Nueva España, del cual algunos mo[...]
segments.translation:
CHAPTER XXI
On the Maguey of Wine

The maguey of the preceding chapter is that which grows commonly in this kingdom of Peru, while the one treated in this chapter is native to New Spain, and the two differ in many respects; one of which is that all varieties [castas] of magueys of New Spain bear at the tip of their leaves a very hard and sharp black spine resembling an awl [lesna], which spines are entirely absent in the maguey of this kingdom. The maguey of wine is very widespread in New Spain, of which some mo[...]

segments.title_inferred: On Coca: Description of the Plant and Its Trade Value
segments.original_text:
CAPITULO XXIX
De la coca

En este reino del Perú no hay cosa más conocida que la coca, cuyo trato es de los gruesos y de mayor ganancia que hay en las Indias y con que no pocos españoles se han hecho ricos. Es la coca una mata no mayor que los manzanos enanos de España, de hasta un estado en alto; su hoja, que es la que tanto precian y estiman los indios, es del tamaño y talle de la del limoncillo, y a veces menor. Da una frutilla colorada, seca y sin jugo, tamaña como pequeños escaramujos, que...
segments.translation:
CHAPTER XXIX
On Coca

In this kingdom of Peru there is no commodity [cosa] more widely known than coca, whose trade is among the largest and most profitable to be found in the Indies, and by which no few Spaniards have made themselves rich. Coca is a shrub [mata] no larger than the dwarf apple trees of Spain, reaching up to one estado [approximately one fathom, roughly 1.7 metres] in height. Its leaf — which is the part so prized and esteemed by the Indians — is the size and shape of that of the lime [limoncillo], and sometimes smaller. It produces a small red fruit, dry and without juice, about the size of small rose-hips [escaramujos], which...

segments.title_inferred: The Canglla shrub of the Peruvian highlands
segments.original_text:
CAPITULO LXXI
De la canglla

En los páramos y sierras frías del Perú, principalmente en las dilatadas provincias del Collao, donde no nacen árboles para leña, suplió su falta el Criador con algunas especies de matas pequeñas, entre las cuales, la más general es la canglla, la cual nace en gran cantidad y es una mata aparrada en la tierra, que no se levanta della más que de uno a dos pies. Echa muchos ramos llenos de unas espinillas blancas muy delgadas y agudas. La hoja no es mayo
segments.translation:
CHAPTER LXXI
On the Canglla

In the high moorlands [páramos] and cold mountain ranges of Peru, principally in the vast provinces of the Collao, where no trees grow for use as firewood, the Creator supplied their lack with certain species of small shrubs, among which the most widespread is the canglla, which grows in great abundance and is a low, spreading shrub that rises no more than one to two feet above the ground. It puts forth many branches covered with very slender, sharp, white little thorns. The leaf is no larger [text breaks off here]

segments.title_inferred: On the Trees of the Indies in General
segments.original_text:
CAPITULO PRIMERO

De los árboles de las Indias en común

Son tantos los géneros y especies de árboles, así frutales como infructíferos y silvestres que se hallan en este Nuevo Mundo, que faltan nombres con que significarlos; porque, demás de muchos que son generales y nacen en todas partes, en cada provincia se hallan nuevas plantas no vistas ni sabidas antes; y lo que pone gran admiración, es ver que siendo tierra ésta de tantos bosques y selvas, sean tan pocas las plantas que se
segments.translation:
CHAPTER THE FIRST

On the Trees of the Indies in General

So numerous are the genera and species of trees — both fruit-bearing and unfruitful [infructíferos] or wild — found in this New World, that names are wanting to signify them all; for, beyond the many that are widespread and grow in every region, in each province new plants are encountered that were never before seen or known. And what causes great wonder is to observe that, this being a land of so many forests and jungles [bosques y selvas], so few of the plants are — [TEXT BREAKS OFF]

segments.title_inferred: Trees Found in the Indies of the Same Genera as Those of Spain
segments.original_text:
CAPITULO II

De los árboles que se hallaron en estas Indias de los mismos géneros que los de España

Muy pocos son los árboles que cuando vinieron a esta tierra los españoles hallaron en ella semejantes en especie a los de España; y ésos los más son infructíferos y silvestres. El árbol más general deste género que se halla en todas las provincias del Perú es el aliso, el cual nace en los valles templados de la Sierra, de cuya madera se gasta gran cantidad en los edificios de la
segments.translation:
CHAPTER II

On the trees found in these Indies of the same genera as those of Spain

Very few are the trees that, when the Spaniards came to this land, they found to be similar in species to those of Spain; and most of those are unfruitful [infructíferos] and wild [silvestres]. The most widespread tree of this kind found throughout all the provinces of Peru is the alder [aliso], which grows in the temperate valleys of the Sierra [highland mountain range], the timber of which is consumed in great quantities in the buildings of the

segments.title_inferred: The Mamón: Description of the Tree and Fruit
segments.original_text:
CAPITULO VI

Del mamón

En la isla Española llaman mamón a otro árbol y fruta parecida a la anona. Es el mamón árbol mediano, del grandor de un durazno, de muy buen parecer; su hoja es semejante a la del aliso, algo mayor y más acanalada y de un verde muy claro, que hermosea mucho el árbol; la fruta tiene el mismo nombre, la cual, cuando está pequeña y verde, parece alcachofa, por la cáscara muy desigual en la superficie, como compuesta de escamas verdes unas sobre otras.
segments.translation:
CHAPTER VI

On the Mamón

On the island of Hispaniola they call mamón another tree and fruit resembling the anona [custard apple]. The mamón is a medium-sized tree, about the size of a peach tree [durazno], and of very pleasing appearance; its leaf resembles that of the alder [aliso], being somewhat larger and more channeled, and of a very light green that greatly beautifies the tree. The fruit bears the same name; when it is small and unripe, it resembles an artichoke [alcachofa] on account of its very uneven surface, as though composed of green scales layered one upon another.

segments.title_inferred: On Zapotes: Varieties, Tree, and Fruit Description
segments.original_text:
CAPITULO XII
De los zapotes.

Los zapotes son propios de la Nueva España, adonde se hallan cinco diferencias dellos; los mejores son los colorados. El árbol que los lleva es grande y copado como el mamey, con los cogollos muy poblados de hoja; ésta es larga un palmo, lisa, tiesa, de un verde oscuro y más ancha al cabo que en medio. La fruta es del tamaño de un membrillo y aun como una grande cidra, ahusada, con la cáscara delgada y tierna; la carne, muy colorada y blanda
segments.translation:
CHAPTER XII
On Zapotes.

The zapotes are native to New Spain, where five varieties of them are found; the best are the red ones [colorados]. The tree that bears them is large and spreading [copado], like the mamey, with the young shoots [cogollos] very densely leafed; the leaf is a palm's length [approx. 8 inches] long, smooth, stiff, of a dark green, and wider at the tip than in the middle. The fruit is the size of a quince and even as large as a big citron [cidra], spindle-shaped [ahusada], with a thin and tender skin; the flesh is very red and soft [text breaks off].

segments.title_inferred: Additional Palms of the First Genus, Province of Santa Cruz de la Sierra
segments.original_text:
CAPITULO LXVII

De las demás palmas de este primer género

En la sobredicha provincia de Santa Cruz de la Sierra llaman a otra palma muqui; es muy alta y lleva cierta fruta odorífera del tamaño y hechura de un albaricoque, la cual comen los indios.

Otra palma, llamada metaquigi, echa unos racimos grandes de cierta fruta morada del tamaño de aceitunas; casi todo es hueso, y lo que tiene de carne es muy sabroso y oloroso.

Otra, llamada siriha, da la fruta en racimos, la cual se co[ntinúa...]
segments.translation:
CHAPTER LXVII

On the remaining palms of this first genus

In the aforementioned province of Santa Cruz de la Sierra, another palm is called muqui; it is very tall and bears a certain fragrant fruit the size and shape of an apricot, which the Indians eat.

Another palm, called metaquigi, puts forth large clusters of a certain purple fruit the size of olives; it is almost entirely stone [pit], and what flesh it has is very flavorful and fragrant.

Another, called siriha, bears its fruit in clusters, which [text breaks off mid-sentence]

segments.title_inferred: Trees of Peru: The Hoque and the Canacaspi
segments.original_text:
CAPITULO CXX

Del Hoque

El Hoque es un árbol que nace en la [sierra] del Perú, de madera recia y [dura]. Echa sus ramas largas y derechas, de las cuales se hacen bordones, que salen muy pesados, nudosos y recios, y varas para otros usos para que se requiere madera fuerte.

CAPITULO CXXI

Del canacaspi

El canacaspi nace en tierras yuncas. Es árbol muy grande; produce las ramas y hojas muy semejantes a las del guarango-espino, salvo que no tiene espinas. De este género de árboles hac[e...]
segments.translation:
CHAPTER CXX

On the Hoque

The Hoque is a tree that grows in the [sierra, i.e., highlands] of Peru, with hard and [tough] wood. It puts forth long, straight branches, from which walking staffs [bordones] are made, which turn out very heavy, knotted, and strong, as well as rods for other uses that require strong timber.

CHAPTER CXXI

On the Canacaspi

The canacaspi grows in the yunca [lowland/coastal valley] lands. It is a very large tree; it produces branches and leaves very similar to those of the guarango-espino [thorny guarango], except that it has no thorns. From this kind of tree [the text is cut off here...]

segments.title_inferred: On the Enduring Memory of Spanish Presence Among the Native Peoples
segments.original_text:
CAPITULO XIX
Prosigue la materia del pasado

Todo esto he traído a propósito de probar que está la memoria de nuestra nación tan arraigada en esta tierra, que cuando ahora la desamparáramos, no era posible borrarse de los ánimos de los naturales della que en los tiempos venideros sucedieren a los que hoy viven, ni que de tanta multitud de vocablos dejase de haber perpetuamente rastros muy notorios; y la memoria de los nombres susodichos tengo por imposible se extinguiese; y qu
segments.translation:
CHAPTER XIX
Continuing the Subject of the Previous Chapter

All of this I have brought forward with the purpose of proving that the memory of our nation is so deeply rooted in this land, that were we now to abandon it, it would be impossible for such memory to be erased from the minds of the native peoples thereof who in times to come shall succeed those who live today; nor could it be that, from so great a multitude of words, there should fail to remain perpetually very notable traces; and I hold it to be impossible that the memory of the aforementioned names should ever be extinguished; and [TEXT BREAKS OFF]

segments.title_inferred: Rebuttal of opposing arguments on the location of Ophir
segments.original_text:
CAPITULO XX

En que se responde a los argumentos de la opinión contraria y se declara dónde caía Ophir

Bien miradas las razones y argumentos en que fundan su opinión los contrarios, juzgo ser de muy poca sustancia para determinar por ellos cosas tan grandes. Porque, al primero que traen del dicho del almirante don Cristóbal Colón, digo que no se debe hacer mucho caso de los nombres con que publican y celebran las nuevas tierras sus descubridores, para sacar dellos el h[ILLEGIBLE: text truncated mid-sentence]
segments.translation:
CHAPTER XX

In which the arguments of the opposing opinion are answered and it is declared where Ophir was located

Having carefully examined the reasons and arguments upon which the opposing side bases its opinion, I judge them to be of very little substance for determining matters so weighty by their means. For, regarding the first argument they bring — namely, the statement of Admiral Don Christopher Columbus — I say that one ought not to place much weight upon the names with which the discoverers of new lands proclaim and celebrate those lands, in order to draw from them the [ILLEGIBLE: text truncated mid-sentence]

segments.title_inferred: On the credibility of sources regarding the Incas and indigenous memory
segments.original_text: los Incas, pues la autoridad de los que averiguaron el uno y el otro es una misma, y no es justo les demos menos crédito en lo uno que en lo otro. Cuanto más que, cuando nada hubieran escrito de ello, es negocio que está en la memoria de los mismos indios más fresco de lo
segments.translation: the Incas, since the authority of those who investigated the one and the other is the same, and it is not right that we give them less credit in one matter than in the other. All the more so because, even had they written nothing of it, this is a matter that remains fresher in the memory of the Indians themselves than

segments.title_inferred: Inca Creation Myth: Viracocha, Tiahuanaco, and the Island of Titicaca
segments.original_text:
su habitación en aquel asiento; y que este fué el principio de la ciudad del Cuzco.

Otro desvarío es que cuando el Criador del mundo (que en su lengua llaman de dos maneras, conviene a saber, Ticciviracocha y Pachayachachi) formó todas las cosas en Tiahuanaco, fingen que residía, mandó al sol, luna y estrellas irse a la isla de Titicaca, que está en la laguna deste nombre, y que desde allí se subiesen al cielo; y que al tiempo que se quería partir e[l sol...]
segments.translation:
their dwelling in that place; and that this was the origin of the city of Cuzco.

Another absurdity [desvarío] is the claim that when the Creator of the world—whom they call by two names in their language, namely Ticciviracocha and Pachayachachi—formed all things at Tiahuanaco, where they pretend he resided, he commanded the sun, moon, and stars to go to the Island of Titicaca, which lies in the lake of that name, and from there to ascend into the heavens; and that at the moment he was about to depart, the sun [text breaks off]

segments.title_inferred: Manco Cápac, First King of the Incas — Character and Early Rule
segments.original_text:
CAPÍTULO IV

De Manco-Cápac, primer rey de los Incas

Apoderado el Inca Manco-Cápac del mando de aquella corta comunidad y república de hombres bárbaros, se portó con ellos más con humanidad y llaneza de hermano, que con autoridad de superior. Empleaba toda su industria en procurar el bien y acrecentamiento de los suyos.

Ante todas cosas, dividió la nueva población del Cuzco en las dos parcialidades de Hanan-Cuzco y Hurin-Cuzco arriba dichas; ordenó las cosas de la...
segments.translation:
CHAPTER IV

Of Manco Cápac, First King of the Incas

Having taken command of that small community and republic of barbarous men, the Inca Manco Cápac conducted himself among them with more humanity and the familiarity of a brother than with the authority of a superior. He devoted all his industry to seeking the welfare and advancement of his people.

First and foremost, he divided the new settlement of Cuzco into the two factions [parcialidades] of Hanan-Cuzco and Hurin-Cuzco, mentioned above; he ordered the affairs of the...

segments.title_inferred: Reign of Lluqui Yupanqui, Third Inca
segments.original_text:
CAPITULO VI

De Lluqui-Yupanqui, tercero Inca

Habiendo el Inca Lluqui-Yupanqui tercero rey del Perú, luego que entró a gobernar, puesto en plática la manera como había de ser señor de todos los pueblos del contorno del Cuzco, y señalado algunos hombres principales para que en esto le ayudasen como capitanes y oficiales de los ejércitos que tuviese, cuentan los indios en sus fábulas, que estando en sus sacrificios y oraciones, como lo acostumbraban los Incas sus padres, se le
segments.translation:
CHAPTER VI

Of Lluqui Yupanqui, the Third Inca

The Inca Lluqui Yupanqui, third king of Peru, having entered into government, set about deliberating [puesto en plática] the manner by which he was to become lord of all the towns surrounding Cuzco, and having designated certain principal men to assist him in this as captains and officers of whatever armies he might command. The Indians recount in their fables [fábulas] that while he was engaged in his sacrifices and prayers, as the Incas his forebears were accustomed to do, there appeared to him [the text breaks off here]

segments.title_inferred: Mayta Cápac, Fourth Inca King: Upbringing and Character
segments.original_text:
CAPITULO VII

De Mayta Cápac, cuarto rey de los Incas

Criaron los gobernadores al príncipe con más regalo y libertad que solían criarse los otros hijos de los reyes antes dél; por lo cual salió Mayta Cápac mozo travieso y atrevido, demás de que de su natural era valiente y animoso. Antes que saliese de tutela, estando un día jugando con otros mozos de su edad, no faltó quien le dijese que mirase por sí y se enmendase, porque, si no lo hacía, habían sus travesuras de ser causa
segments.translation:
CHAPTER VII

Concerning Mayta Cápac, Fourth King of the Incas

The governors raised the prince with more indulgence and freedom than the other sons of kings before him had customarily been raised; on account of which Mayta Cápac grew into a mischievous and bold youth, beyond the fact that by his natural disposition he was brave and spirited. Before he had come out of guardianship [tutela], while playing one day with other young men of his age, there was no shortage of those who told him to look to himself and mend his ways, because, if he did not do so, his mischiefs were bound to be the cause [of—text breaks off]

segments.title_inferred: Chapter IX: Inca Roca, Sixth King of Peru
segments.original_text:
CAPITULO IX

Del sexto rey del Perú, llamado Inca-Roca

Del sexto rey desta tierra fué Inca-Roca, de quien dicen los indios haber comenzado la parcialidad de Hanan-Cusco. Mas comoquiera que desde Manco-Cápac fué hecha aquella división de la ciudad del Cuzco en las dos parcialidades referidas de Hanan-Cuzco y Hurin-Cuzco, no pudo tener principio en este Inca la de Hanan-Cuzco. La razón de hacerlo cabeza y origen della, quizá fué por haber sido el primero de los Incas reyes [text ends abruptly]
segments.translation:
CHAPTER IX

Concerning the sixth king of Peru, called Inca Roca

The sixth king of this land was Inca Roca, of whom the Indians say he began the faction [parcialidad] of Hanan-Cusco. However, inasmuch as the division of the city of Cuzco into the two factions [parcialidades] known as Hanan-Cuzco and Hurin-Cuzco had already been established since the time of Manco Cápac, the origin of Hanan-Cuzco cannot properly be attributed to this Inca. The reason for designating him as the head and founder of it was perhaps that he had been the first of the Inca kings [text ends abruptly; remainder of passage not supplied]

segments.title_inferred: Yahuar-Huacac Inca Yupanqui, Seventh King of the Incas
segments.original_text:
CAPITULO X

De Yahuar-Huacac-Inca-Yupanqui, séptimo rey

A Inca-Roca sucedió en el reino su hijo mayor, Yahuar-Huacac-Inca-Yupanqui. Quiere decir Yahuar-Huacac "el que llora sangre"; y cuentan haberle sido dado este nombre porque una vez, siendo vencido y preso de sus enemigos, de puro dolor y pena de verse en tan miserable estado, lloró sangre. No tenían sus vasallos mucha opinión dél, por estar en reputación de cobarde, y se confirmaban en su opinión con los pronó[sticos]
segments.translation:
CHAPTER X

Concerning Yahuar-Huacac Inca Yupanqui, the seventh king

Inca Roca was succeeded in the kingdom by his eldest son, Yahuar-Huacac Inca Yupanqui. The name Yahuar-Huacac means "he who weeps blood" [lit. 'blood-weeper']; and it is recounted that this name was given to him because on one occasion, having been defeated and taken prisoner by his enemies, out of sheer grief and anguish at finding himself in so wretched a state, he wept blood. His vassals held no great opinion of him, as he had a reputation for cowardice, and they were confirmed in this view by the prognos[tics — text breaks off]

segments.title_inferred: Tupac Inca Yupanqui: Tenth King of the Incas
segments.original_text:
CAPITULO XIV

De Tupac-Inca-Yupanqui, décimo rey

A Pachacútic sucedió su hijo mayor, Tupac-Inca-Yupanqui, el cual comenzó a gobernar en vida de su padre: porque hallándose muy viejo Pachacútic e impedido para administrar tan grande reino, con consentimiento de sus vasallos lo renunció en su hijo Tupac-Inca-Yupanqui, de quien hay memoria tuvo dos hermanos, dichos Amaru-Tupa-Inca y Tupa-Inca, y que fueron señores ricos y estimados. Este rey quebrantó la costumbre
segments.translation:
CHAPTER XIV

On Tupac Inca Yupanqui, the Tenth King

Pachacútic was succeeded by his eldest son, Tupac Inca Yupanqui, who began to govern during his father's lifetime; for Pachacútic, finding himself very old and unable to administer so great a kingdom, renounced it in favor of his son Tupac Inca Yupanqui with the consent of his vassals. It is recorded that this king had two brothers, known as Amaru Tupa Inca and Tupa Inca, who were wealthy and esteemed lords. This king broke with the custom [text continues]

segments.title_inferred: Inca Campaign to Collasuyu: Capture of Chiefs and Preparatory Sacrifices
segments.original_text:
le era al Inca favorable la fortuna. Prendió a los caciques y capitanes de aquella tierra y los mandó llevar con los demás prisioneros, para entrar, como entró, triunfando dellos en el Cuzco.

Pasados algunos años que gastó en ordenar las cosas del gobierno, se dispuso a hacer jornada a Collasuyu. Antes de partir hizo sus acostumbrados sacrificios al sol, su padre, y a los demás dioses, prometiéndoles que, si le favorecían, no había de llegar a su corte antes de llegar a l[...]
segments.translation:
Fortune was favorable to the Inca. He seized the chieftains [caciques] and captains of that land and ordered them taken along with the other prisoners, so that he might enter Cuzco in triumph over them, as indeed he did.

After several years spent ordering the affairs of government, he prepared to undertake a campaign to Collasuyu. Before departing, he performed his customary sacrifices to the sun, his father, and to the other gods, promising them that, if they favored him, he would not return to his court before reaching [ILLEGIBLE: text cuts off mid-sentence]

segments.title_inferred: Other Events of Tupa Inca Yupanqui: The Favored Concubine
segments.original_text:
CAPITULO XV

De los demás sucesos de Tupa Inca Yupanqui

Entre las mujeres deste Inca había una de nación Guayro, de extremada hermosura, a la cual él amaba y favorecía más que a su legítima mujer, y tenía en ella un hijo igualmente amado que su madre; y no había fiestas y regocijos adonde no llevase consigo a madre y hijo. Viendo, pues, sus vasallos que esta mujer era la que más preciaba y más cabida tenía con el rey, la procuraban todos agradar y servir, por el contento y
segments.translation:
CHAPTER XV

On the Other Events of Tupa Inca Yupanqui

Among the wives of this Inca there was one, a woman of the Guayro nation, of extraordinary beauty, whom he loved and favored more than his legitimate wife. He had a son by her whom he loved equally as much as the mother herself; and there was no feast or celebration to which he did not bring both mother and son along with him. His subjects, seeing that this woman was the one he most valued and who held the greatest sway with the king, all sought to please and serve her, on account of the favor and [text continues]

segments.title_inferred: Huayna Capac, Last King of the Incas
segments.original_text:
CAPITULO XVI

De Guayna-Cápac, último rey de los Incas

A Inca-Yupanqui sucedió su hijo primogénito Guayna-Cápac, nombre que alcanzó ilustres hazañas, el cual significa "mancebo rico y excelente", porque desde su mocedad tuvo lo uno y lo otro con más ventaja que ninguno de sus predecesores. Fué muy amado de sus vasallos y tenido por valiente y determinado. Alcanzó muchas e insignes victorias; dilató los límites de su Imperio con muchas provincias que agregó a él. Most[ró...]
segments.translation:
CHAPTER XVI

On Huayna Capac, last king of the Incas

Inca Yupanqui was succeeded by his firstborn son Huayna Capac, a name he lived up to through illustrious deeds, which signifies "wealthy and excellent youth" [mancebo rico y excelente], for from his youth he possessed both qualities to a greater degree than any of his predecessors. He was greatly loved by his subjects and held to be brave and resolute. He achieved many notable victories and expanded the limits of his Empire by adding many provinces to it. He show[ed...]

segments.title_inferred: Inca Pacification of Cochabamba and Resettlement of Mitimae Colonists
segments.original_text:
cercanías y cordilleras, que, fiados en la aspereza de la tierra, llevaban mal la servidumbre del Inca. Salió de los Andes por Cochabamba, y viendo cuán fértiles y abundantes de mantenimiento eran los valles de aquella provincia y que la gente que los habitaba era muy poca, mandó que de los pueblos del Collao fuesen algunas familias a avecindarse en ellos; y a esta causa son ahora casi todos sus habitadores mitimaes.

De Cochabamba pasó a Pocona, a visitar aquella fron[tera]
segments.translation:
...surrounding areas and mountain ranges [cordilleras], who, trusting in the ruggedness of the terrain, bore the Inca's subjugation poorly. He departed from the Andes by way of Cochabamba, and seeing how fertile and abundantly provisioned were the valleys of that province, and that the people inhabiting them were very few in number, he ordered that some families from the towns of the Collao [the altiplano region around Lake Titicaca] should come to settle there; and for this reason nearly all of its inhabitants today are mitimaes [mitimaq: transplanted colonist-settlers under the Inca resettlement system].

From Cochabamba he proceeded to Pocona, to visit that frontier [passage breaks off]...

segments.title_inferred: Inca Huayna Capac's Military Campaigns and the Resistance of the Cayambes
segments.original_text: enviaba el Inca capitanes a diversas jornadas, y a otras iba él mismo, en que pasó muchas dificultades, porque los indios de aquellas provincias eran valientes y guerreros, y muchas veces vencieron y desbarataron los escuadrones del Inca, y no pocas pusieron en huida al mismo Inca; particularmente los Cayambes, por ser hombres de valor y ánimo, dieron en qué entender al Inca Guayna-Capac y a sus capitanes, que les costó mucha sangre y tiempo el conquistarlos. Emprendió el Inca en per[sona...]
segments.translation: The Inca sent captains on various campaigns, and to others he went himself, in which he encountered many difficulties, because the Indians of those provinces were brave and warlike, and many times they defeated and routed the squadrons of the Inca, and not infrequently put the Inca himself to flight; particularly the Cayambes, being men of valor and spirit, gave much trouble to the Inca Huayna Capac and his captains, costing them much blood and time to conquer them. The Inca undertook in per[son...] [text breaks off]

segments.title_inferred: Inca Campaign Against the Island of Puná and the Guayaquil Region
segments.original_text:
Túmbala, y a la tierra firme, frontera, es la provincia de Guayaquil, y fué muy reñida y porfiada; pero la multitud de los del Inca oprimió a los isleños por entonces, si bien no mucho después les costó bien caro; porque, en volviéndose a Túmbez el Inca, dieron los de la Puná sobre el presidio que les había dejado, y los mataron a todos.

Enojado el Inca del caso, volvió con extraña furia a la isla y hizo en toda ella crueles castigos; mas, considerando que no ganaba nada por aquel camino...
segments.translation:
Túmbala, and on the mainland opposite lies the province of Guayaquil. The campaign was very fierce and stubborn; yet the great numbers of the Inca's forces overpowered the islanders for the time being, although not long afterward this victory cost them dearly. For upon the Inca's return to Túmbez, the people of Puná fell upon the garrison he had left behind and slaughtered them all.

Enraged by this turn of events, the Inca returned to the island with extraordinary fury and carried out cruel punishments throughout it; but, considering that he was gaining nothing by that course of action...

segments.title_inferred: Battle of Ambato: Atahualpa defeats Huáscar's forces
segments.original_text:
vieron a ira y enojo, proponiendo de vengar las injurias hechas a su rey.

Juntó Atau-Hualpa un grueso ejército y salió en busca del enemigo. El capitán Atoc, que regía el de Huáscar, no rehusó la batalla. Embistiéronse los dos ejércitos en Ambato, y quedó roto y deshecho el de Huáscar, con muerte de su capitán general y gran parte de su gente. Partió Atau-Hualpa de los términos de Quito muy acrecentado en fuerzas y reputación, y entrando por las tierras que obedecían
segments.translation:
they were moved to anger and fury, resolving to avenge the wrongs done to their king.

Atahualpa assembled a large army and set out in search of the enemy. The captain Atoc, who commanded Huáscar's forces, did not refuse battle. The two armies clashed at Ambato, and Huáscar's force was broken and destroyed, with the death of its captain-general and a great part of his men. Atahualpa departed the borders of Quito greatly increased in strength and reputation, and entering into the lands that obeyed [him] —

segments.title_inferred: Raids and pursuit of hostile forces near Guamanga and Limatambo
segments.original_text: fuesen a buscarlo. Con todo eso, no dejaban sus gentes de infestar la tierra, saliendo en cuadrilla a los caminos. Adonde hacían más frecuentes correrías era a Limatambo, a Andaguaylas y a los términos de Guamanga. Llegaron una noche a esta ciudad estando sus moradores descuidados, y habiendo de presto robado lo que pudieron, se volvieron sin recebir daño; salieron los nuestros en su seguimiento y fueron dándoles alcance y picándoles hasta meterlos en sus montañas que
segments.translation: they went out to look for him. Even so, his people did not cease to trouble the land, going out in armed bands [cuadrillas] onto the roads. The places where they made their most frequent raids were Limatambo, Andaguaylas, and the territories of Guamanga. One night they arrived at this city while its inhabitants were off their guard, and having quickly plundered what they could, they withdrew without suffering any harm. Our men set out in pursuit of them, overtaking them and harrying them until they drove them back into their mountains, where [text breaks off]

segments.title_inferred: Sons of Manco Inca who maintained the title of king in Vilcabamba
segments.original_text:
CAPITULO XXI

De los hijos de Manco-Inca que en Vilcabamba mantuvieron el titulo de reyes

Quedó en Vilcabamba con la borla y título de rey Sayri-Tupa-Inca; y como acá fuera se siguieron las guerras civiles de Gonzalo Pizarro, no se atendió ni a reducirlo ni hacer la guerra, hasta que, sosegada la tierra con la Victoria que alcanzaron en Jaquijaguana los leales el año de 1548, el presidente Pedro de la Gasca, deseando dejar la tie-
segments.translation:
CHAPTER XXI

Concerning the sons of Manco Inca who maintained the title of kings in Vilcabamba

Sayri Tupac Inca remained in Vilcabamba with the royal fringe [borla] and the title of king; and since, out here in the rest of the land, the civil wars of Gonzalo Pizarro continued, no effort was made either to bring him to submission or to make war upon him, until, with the land pacified by the victory that the loyalists achieved at Jaquijaguana in the year 1548, President Pedro de la Gasca, wishing to leave the land [in order]—

segments.title_inferred: Reception of the Inca and Cusi-Huarcay in Lima: Festivities and Homage
segments.original_text:
Cusi-Huarcay, a quien también hizo el virrey mucha honra y asentar en el estrado donde la estaban esperando las señoras de Lima muy aderezadas de sus mayores galas y joyas. Mandó que se les diesen buenos aposentos y los sirviesen criados españoles.

Estuviéronse muchos días en Lima con fiestas y regocijos que se les hicieron. Holgábanse todos los indios de ver su Inca, y venían los caciques de todas partes a hacerle reverencia, y traían muchos presentes; con lo cual...
segments.translation:
Cusi-Huarcay, upon whom the viceroy likewise bestowed great honor, was invited to take her seat upon the dais [estrado] where the ladies of Lima awaited her, attired in their finest garments and jewels. He ordered that they be given comfortable lodgings and attended by Spanish servants.

They remained many days in Lima amid the festivities and celebrations held in their honor. All the Indians rejoiced to see their Inca, and the caciques [local lords] came from all parts to pay him reverence and brought many gifts; with which...

segments.title_inferred: Capture of the Inca by Martín García de Loyola
segments.original_text: batalla llevaban los suyos lo peor, se huyó por lugares ocultos y fragosos.
