Ofrecióse a ir en su seguimiento el capitán Martín García de Loyola con sólo veinte soldados escogidos, aunque fuese necesario entrarse por las tierras de guerra: y cumpliólo como prometió; porque, sin perder el rastro del Inca, lo fué siguiendo, y a pocas jornadas le dio alcance. Prendiólo y trajolo al Cuzco con otros capitanes que se tomaron. Hízose proceso contra el Inca y los otros culpados po[r...]
segments.translation: ...the battle was going badly for his men, he fled through hidden and rugged terrain. Captain Martín García de Loyola offered to go in pursuit of him with only twenty chosen soldiers, even if it meant entering hostile [lit. 'war'] territories: and he fulfilled this as he had promised; for, without losing the Inca's trail, he continued to follow him, and within a few days' march he overtook him. He captured him and brought him to Cuzco along with other captains who were taken. Proceedings [a legal process] were brought against the Inca and the other guilty parties [the text breaks off here].

segments.title_inferred: The Inca Policy of Resettling Conquered Peoples into Towns
segments.original_text:
CAPITULO XXIV

Que los Incas reducían a pueblos las gentes que sujetaban, y el orden que en ellos ponían

Ya queda dicho arriba cómo los indios peruanos, antes de ser gobernados por los reyes Incas, no tenían pueblos concertados con traza y forma de nuestros pueblos, sino que vivían en caseríos de poca vecindad, puestos los más por los cerros y collados, para defenderse de los asaltos que unos a otros se daban. Digo ahora, que en poniendo el Inca debajo de su domin[io]
segments.translation:
CHAPTER XXIV

That the Incas resettled into towns the peoples they subjected, and the order they imposed upon them

It has already been stated above how the Peruvian Indians, before being governed by the Inca kings, did not have organized towns laid out [trazados] in the form and manner of our own towns, but rather lived in small hamlets [caseríos] of sparse population, most of them situated on hills and ridges [cerros y collados] in order to defend themselves from the raids that they carried out upon one another. I say now that upon the Inca placing [a people] under his domin[ion —]

segments.title_inferred: Inca Laws, Punishments, and Governance
segments.original_text:
CAPITULO XXVI

De las leyes y castigos con que los Incas
gobernaban su reino

Como los indios carecían de letras,
no tenían leyes escritas, mas conserva-
ban las que habían establecido sus re-
yes por tradición y con el uso y obser-
vancia en que estaban. Las mas princi-
pales y de que más memoria había en-
tre ellos pondré aquí.

Donde se hallaba presente el Inca, él
solo era el juez ante quien se pedían
todos los agravios hechos, y donde no,
hacían justicia sus gobernadores y ca-
segments.translation:
CHAPTER XXVI

On the Laws and Punishments by which the Incas
Governed their Kingdom

Because the Indians lacked letters [i.e., a writing system], they had no written laws; rather, they preserved those which their kings had established through tradition and through the use and observance in which they were maintained. The most important of these, and those best remembered among them, I shall set down here.

Wherever the Inca was personally present, he alone was the judge before whom all grievances were brought; and where he was not present, his governors and ca-[text breaks off mid-word, continues in next part]

segments.title_inferred: Inca Administration of Religious and Crown Lands
segments.original_text: porque siempre se tenía consideración a que los pueblos quedasen bien proveídos de mantenimientos. Por donde, en algunas tierras, había pueblos enteros que con sus distritos y cuanto se cogía en ellos eran del sol y de los otros dioses, como eran Arápa, y otros; y en otras provincias (esto era lo más ordinario) era muy grande la parte del rey. En estas tierras de la Religión y de la Corona Real tenía el Inca puestos mayordomos y administradores, que con gran cuidado, las hacían cultivar.
segments.translation: Because consideration was always given to ensuring that the towns remained well supplied with provisions. Thus, in some lands, there were entire towns whose districts and all that was harvested in them belonged to the Sun and to the other gods, such as were Arápa and others; and in other provinces (this being the more common arrangement) the king's share was very large. In these lands belonging to Religion and to the Royal Crown, the Inca had installed stewards and administrators who, with great diligence, saw to it that they were cultivated.

segments.title_inferred: Division of tame livestock, wool revenues, hunting grounds, and royal reserves under the Inca
segments.original_text:
CAPITULO XXIX

Del orden cómo se repartía el ganado manso, y la renta que en él y en la ropa de sus lanas tenían el Inca y la religión; y cómo los cazaderos y montes eran realengos

La misma división tenía hecha el Inca de todo el ganado manso que dellas tierras, aplicando una parte a la religión, a sí otra y a la comunidad otra: y no sólo dividió y separó cada una destas partes, sino también las dehesas y pastos en que se apacentasen, de modo que anduviesen e[...]
segments.translation:
CHAPTER XXIX

On the manner in which tame livestock was distributed, and the revenue that the Inca and the [state] religion derived from it and from cloth made of its wool; and how the hunting grounds and forests were royal [Crown] property

The Inca had made the same division of all the tame livestock of those lands, allocating one portion to religion [i.e., the state religious establishment], another to himself, and another to the community [the common people]. And he not only divided and separated each of these portions, but also the pastures [dehesas] and grazing lands in which they were to be fed, so that they [the herds] might go about [the text breaks off here]

segments.title_inferred: The Royal Roads Built by the Incas Across Their Kingdom
segments.original_text:
CAPITULO XXXI

De los caminos que hicieron los Incas por todo su reino, y la contribución de gente que para aderezarlos daban las provincias

Tenían hechos los Incas dos caminos reales que tomaban todo el largo de su reino desde la provincia de Quito hasta el reino de Chile, que son novecientas leguas, el uno por los Llanos y costa de la mar, y el otro la tierra adentro por las provincias de la Sierra, desviado de la costa por unas partes treinta leguas y por otras cinco.
segments.translation:
CHAPTER XXXI

Concerning the roads that the Incas built throughout their kingdom, and the contribution of labor that the provinces provided for their upkeep

The Incas had constructed two royal roads [caminos reales] that ran the entire length of their kingdom, from the province of Quito to the kingdom of Chile—a distance of nine hundred leagues [leguas]. One road ran along the lowlands [los Llanos] and the sea coast, and the other ran inland through the provinces of the highlands [la Sierra], set back from the coast by as much as thirty leagues in some places and five in others.

segments.title_inferred: Personal Service Tribute Owed by Indians to the Inca King
segments.original_text:
CAPITULO XXXIII

Del demás tributo que en servicio personal pagaban a su rey los indios

Allende del trabajo que en lugar de pecho o tributo ponían los pueblos en las labranzas y crianzas de las tierras y ganados del Inca y de la religión y en los otros oficios y tareas que habemos contado, era grandísima la contribución que daban de gente y peones para cuantos ministerios y obras se hacían en todo el reino, así para servicio y utilidad del rey, como de la república. A los cuales
segments.translation:
CHAPTER XXXIII

On the further tribute that the Indians paid to their king in personal service

Beyond the labor that the towns contributed in lieu of a head-tax [pecho] or tribute — namely their work on the farmlands and the raising of livestock belonging to the Inca and to the religion [i.e., the state religious establishment], and in the other trades and tasks we have already described — exceedingly great was the contribution of men and laborers [peones] they furnished for all the ministries and public works carried out throughout the kingdom, both for the service and benefit of the king and for the commonwealth [república]. To these [laborers]

segments.title_inferred: Labor Tribute (Mita) Distribution and Livestock Tribute Under the Inca
segments.original_text: Inca y a gobernadores parecía; y la distribución hacían los caciques inferiores a rata por cantidad, como, si habían de sacar mil hombres, contribuía cada cacique con el número que le cabía; de manera que, sabida la gente que había de salir de mita, no podían los pueblos recibir ningún agravio en la distribución ni ser más cargados unos que otros. Y aunque los ganados todos de que daban tributo eran del Inca y de la religión, también hacían de la mi-
segments.translation: the Inca and the governors saw fit; and the distribution was carried out by the lesser caciques [local chieftains] proportionally by quota, so that if one thousand men were to be levied, each cacique contributed the number that fell to his share; such that, once the number of people required to go out on mita [obligatory rotational labor service] was known, the communities could suffer no injustice in the distribution, nor could some be burdened more than others. And although all the livestock on which they paid tribute belonged to the Inca and to the [state] religion, they also drew from the mi- [text continues]

segments.title_inferred: On the Reputation and Esteem of the Incas Among the Indigenous People
segments.original_text:
ricio, como en cosas de su religión y más la propia necesidad de los indios: porque no se tenía en este particular menos cuidado de lo uno que de lo otro.

Otrosí, fue de muy grande importancia a los Incas para sus designios, la reputación y estima notable que dellos concibieron los indios, mediante la cual se vino a fundar en esta gente ruda una opinión en que no solamente los tenían por diferentes de los otros hombres en valor y fuerzas, mas creían que tuviesen gran paren[tesco...]
segments.translation:
...service, as in matters of their religion and likewise the proper necessity of the Indians: for no less care was taken in this particular regard for one than for the other.

Furthermore, of very great importance to the Incas for their designs was the notable reputation and esteem that the Indians conceived of them, by means of which there came to be established among this simple people an opinion whereby they held them to be not only different from other men in valor and strength, but also believed them to have great kin[ship...]

segments.title_inferred: On the Reckoning of Time, Quipus, and the Counting Methods of the Peruvian Indians
segments.original_text:
CAPITULO XXXVII

Del cómputo del tiempo; de los quipos o memoriales y modo de contar que tenían los indios peruanos

Siendo como es el movimiento de los cielos cosa tan admirable, notoria y manifiesta a todas las gentes, no hay ninguna tan bárbara y ruda que, siquiera cuando levanta los o[jos...]
segments.translation:
CHAPTER XXXVII

On the reckoning of time; on the quipus [quipos] or memoranda, and the method of counting used by the Peruvian Indians

The movement of the heavens being, as it is, a thing so admirable, well-known, and manifest to all peoples, there is none so barbarous and rude that, at least when it raises its e[yes...]

segments.title_inferred: Indigenous Methods of Timekeeping in Peru
segments.original_text: de dos maneras: la una, que señalaban con el dedo la parte del cielo en que andaba el sol cuando comenzaron su obra; como si al caminante le preguntaban a qué hora había salido de la posada, respondía levantando el dedo al cielo y apuntando el paraje donde estaba el sol entonces, por donde sacaban poco más o menos el tiempo que había caminado; y lo mismo hacían en las demás faenas y ejercicios. La otra era muy curiosa, desta suerte: casi en todo este reino del Perú cogen los indios cierta
segments.translation: in two ways: the first was that they would point with their finger to the part of the sky where the sun had been when they began their work; just as if a traveler were asked at what hour he had set out from the inn, he would answer by raising his finger to the sky and pointing to the place where the sun had been at that moment, from which they would reckon, more or less, how long he had been traveling; and they did the same with their other labors and occupations. The second method was very curious, and went as follows: in nearly all of this kingdom of Peru the Indians harvest a certain [text continues]

segments.title_inferred: Indigenous Opinions and Fables Concerning the Origin of the World, the Universal Flood, and Resulting Sacred Sites (Huacas)
segments.original_text:
CAPITULO II

De las opiniones y fábulas que acerca de su origen, principios del mundo y diluvio universal estaban recebidas desta gente, con las guacas que desto resultaron

Como el total fundamento de toda religión y culto divino sea el conocimiento de la primera causa, ora sea la verdadera, ora falsa y fingida, de quien los hombres creen haber procedido y depender della en la conservación de su ser, y también la noticia del fin y estado que después desta vida esperan tener; por
segments.translation:
CHAPTER II

Concerning the opinions and fables received among these people regarding their origin, the beginnings of the world, and the universal flood, together with the sacred sites [guacas] that resulted therefrom

Since the entire foundation of all religion and divine worship is the knowledge of the first cause—whether true, or false and fictitious—from whom men believe themselves to have proceeded and upon whom they believe themselves to depend for the preservation of their being, and likewise the knowledge of the end and state they expect to have after this life; [text continues]

segments.title_inferred: Agallochon (Aloewood / Aquilaria) — Properties and Uses
segments.original_text: ἀγάλλοχον — Indian wood, also called agallochon or fragrant wood. It is warming, digestive, strengthens the mouth and stomach, is good for those with liver conditions when decocted in wine. Applied to the forehead with rose oil it helps headache. Burned as incense it is pleasant and aromatic.
segments.translation: Agallochon [aloewood]: an Indian wood, also known as agallochon or fragrant wood. It has warming properties and aids digestion; it strengthens the mouth and stomach, and when decocted in wine is beneficial for those suffering from liver ailments. When applied to the forehead together with rose oil [rhodinon], it relieves headache. When burned as incense, it produces a pleasant and aromatic fragrance.

segments.title_inferred: Wine of Maronea (Maron): Properties and Medicinal Uses
segments.original_text: Maron (Wine of Maronea — Thrace) — The wine of Maronea mentioned in Homer's Odyssey when Odysseus gave it to the Cyclops. So strong that it was mixed with twenty measures of water to one of wine. Used medicinally: warms the stomach, promotes digestion, increases urine, helps those with phlegm. The strongest wines are most warming and have most power.
segments.translation: The wine of Maronea, which Homer mentions in the Odyssey as the wine Odysseus [Ulysses] gave to the Cyclops [Polyphemus]: it was of such great strength that it was diluted with twenty measures of water to one of wine. In its medicinal applications it warms the stomach, aids digestion [pepsis], promotes the flow of urine, and benefits those suffering from an excess of phlegm [phlegma]. In general, the stronger wines are the most warming and possess the greatest potency [dynamis].

segments.title_inferred: Wormwood Wine (Absinthites Oinos)
segments.original_text: Absinthites Oinos (Wormwood Wine) — ἀψινθίτης οἶνος — Wormwood wine. Wormwood leaves and flowers steeped in grape must. Taken in drink it warms the stomach and helps those with fluxes of the belly. Good for those with jaundice and for those who have been made ill by drinking hemlock. One of the most widely prescribed medicinal wines.
segments.translation: Absinthites Oinos (Wormwood Wine) — ἀψινθίτης οἶνος: A medicinal wine prepared by steeping the leaves and flowers of wormwood in grape must [unfermented or partially fermented grape juice]. When taken as a drink, it warms the stomach and provides relief to those suffering from fluxes [diarrhea or dysentery] of the belly. It is also beneficial for those afflicted with jaundice, and for those who have been harmed by drinking hemlock. It is counted among the most widely prescribed of the medicinal wines.

segments.title_inferred: Squill Wine (Scillites Oinos)
segments.original_text: σκιλλίτης οἶνος — Squill wine made from sea squill (Urginea maritima). Given for coughs and chest conditions, for those with longstanding phlegm in the chest, for dropsy (edema), and for the bite of vipers. Prepared by soaking dried squill root in must for 40 days.
segments.translation: Scillites Oinos [Squill Wine]: A wine prepared from sea squill [Urginea maritima]. It is administered for coughs and affections of the chest, for those suffering from chronic phlegm lodged in the chest, for dropsy [edema], and as a remedy against the bite of vipers. The preparation consists of steeping dried squill root in new wine [must] for a period of forty days.

segments.title_inferred: Rose Wine (Rhodites Oinos)
segments.original_text: Rhodites Oinos (Rose Wine) — ῥοδίτης οἶνος — Rose wine, made by macerating rose petals in must for one year. Cooler than plain wine. Good for those with stomach conditions, headache, and conditions of the mouth and gums. Applied to wounds it cools inflammation. The most widely prescribed medicinal wine in Greek/Roman tradition — rose wine as a base for other medicinal preparations.
segments.translation: Rhodites Oinos [Rose Wine]: A wine prepared by macerating rose petals in unfermented grape juice [must] for the period of one year. It is of a cooler temperament than plain wine. It is beneficial for those suffering from ailments of the stomach, headache, and disorders of the mouth and gums. When applied externally to wounds, it reduces [cools] inflammation. It was the most widely prescribed medicinal wine in the Greek and Roman traditions, serving as a base vehicle for other medicinal preparations.

segments.title_inferred: Oxymeli (Honey-Vinegar Preparation)
segments.original_text: ὀξύμελι — ὄξος καὶ μέλι Ἀττικὸν ἑψηθέντα. διδόμενον τοῖς βήττουσι, τοῖς χολώδεσι, τοῖς παλμοὺς ἔχουσι τῆς καρδίας. μετὰ ὕδατος κιρνάμενον τὴν κοιλίαν ὑπάγει. ἔξωθεν δὲ ἐπιτιθέμενον τοῖς ἀρθριτικοῖς βοηθεῖ.
segments.translation: Oxymeli [honey-vinegar] — The best is prepared from vinegar and Attic honey boiled together. It is given to those suffering from coughs, to those with bilious conditions, and to those experiencing palpitations of the heart. Mixed with water, it purges the bowels. Applied externally, it brings relief to those with conditions of the joints [arthritis].

segments.title_inferred: Melikraton (Hydromel / Mead): Honey-Water Preparation and Its Uses
segments.original_text: μελίκρατον — Old water mixed with honey. The best honey dissolves in the purest water and is boiled down. Taken in drink it is laxative, helps those with coughs, clears the chest. Applied to wounds it cleanses them. Used as a vehicle for other medicinal plants.
segments.translation: Melikraton [hydromel / mead]: old water mixed with honey. The finest honey is dissolved in the purest water and reduced by boiling. When taken as a drink it is laxative, beneficial to those suffering from coughs, and clears the chest. When applied externally to wounds it cleanses them. It is also employed as a vehicle [base medium] for other medicinal plants.

segments.title_inferred: Bloodstone (Hematite) as a Mineral Medicine
segments.original_text: λίθος αἱματίτης — Bloodstone (hematite, iron ore). Ground finely and taken in drink with wine it stops fluxes of the belly and uterine hemorrhage. Applied to the eyes with egg white it reduces inflammation. Ground finely and applied to wounds it draws the lips together.
segments.translation: The bloodstone [lithos haimatites]: Ground finely and taken as a drink with wine, it arrests fluxes of the belly and hemorrhage of the uterus. Applied to the eyes together with egg white, it diminishes inflammation. Ground finely and applied directly to wounds, it draws the edges [lips] of the wound together.

segments.title_inferred: Alum (Stypteria): Properties and Medical Uses
segments.original_text: Alum (Stypteria) — στυπτηρία — Alum. The best is the roundish kind from Egypt. It has drawing, drying, astringent properties. Dissolved in warm water and gargled it helps conditions of the mouth and tonsils. Applied to granulating wounds it helps them close. Mixed with honey it is applied to foul ulcers. Applied in vinegar to running sores it heals them. One of the most universally used mineral antiseptics across all ancient traditions.
segments.translation: Alum [stypteria, στυπτηρία]: The finest variety is the rounded type [scholiasts identify this as 'round alum,' stromatites or sphairites] originating from Egypt. It possesses drawing [styptic], drying, and astringent qualities. When dissolved in warm water and used as a gargle, it is beneficial for affections of the mouth and tonsils. When applied directly to granulating [flesh-forming] wounds, it promotes their closure. Mixed with honey, it is applied to foul [putrid, κακοήθη] ulcers. Applied in vinegar to weeping [running, rheumatizing] sores, it brings about healing. It is among the most widely employed mineral antiseptics across all ancient medical traditions.

segments.title_inferred: Chrysokolla (Gold-Solder Mineral): Ophthalmic, Wound, and Emetic Uses
segments.original_text: χρυσόκολλα — Gold-solder, the material used by goldsmiths. Applied to the eyes it clears the vision. Applied to wounds it helps them close. Taken with water it is bitter and causes vomiting.
segments.translation: Chrysokolla [gold-solder]: the substance employed by goldsmiths. When applied to the eyes, it sharpens and clarifies the vision. When applied to wounds, it promotes their closure. When ingested with water, it is bitter in taste and induces vomiting.

segments.title_inferred: White Poppy (Mekon Leuke) — Opium, Preparations, and Dosage
segments.original_text: μήκων λευκή — White poppy. The juice of the heads, dried, is called opion. Of lesser strength than the black kind. The milk from the heads taken in drink is soporific. The seeds taken with wine and honey provoke sleep and stop fluxes of the belly. The heads boiled in water and the liquid applied to the forehead stops headache. Note on dose: a little helps, more is lethal. Extended entry on the pharmacological gradations of opium from soporific to lethal.
segments.translation: White Poppy [μήκων λευκή, mēkōn leukē]. The juice extracted from the heads, once dried, is called opion [ὄπιον]. It is of lesser potency than the black variety. The milky sap of the heads, taken as a drink, induces sleep. The seeds, taken together with wine and honey, promote sleep and arrest fluxes [discharges] of the belly. The heads boiled in water, with the resulting liquid applied externally to the forehead, relieves headache. Regarding dosage: a small amount is beneficial; a larger amount is fatal. [The entry continues with an extended discussion of the pharmacological gradations of opium, ranging from soporific to lethal effects.]

segments.title_inferred: Black Henbane (Hyoscyamus niger): Properties, Uses, and Antidotes
segments.original_text: ὑοσκύαμος ὁ μέλας — The leaves of all kinds, taken in excess, cause madness and loss of reason. The seed drunk in wine causes hallucinations and deep sleep. Applied to the eyes with woman's milk it relieves pain. The root boiled in wine and held in the mouth relieves toothache. ANTIDOTES: If someone has drunk too much henbane, give them honeyed wine (oxymel) or decoction of radish in water to vomit it out.
segments.translation: Black Henbane [hyoskyamos ho melas]: The leaves of all varieties, if taken in excessive quantity, produce madness and loss of rational faculty. The seed, drunk in wine, induces hallucinations [phantasiai] and deep sleep [koma]. Applied to the eyes together with woman's milk, it alleviates pain. The root, boiled in wine and held in the mouth, relieves toothache. ANTIDOTES: Should a person have consumed too much henbane, administer honeyed wine [oxymel] or a decoction of radish in water, so as to provoke vomiting and expel the substance.

segments.title_inferred: Mandrake: Toxic and Therapeutic Properties, Including Surgical Anaesthesia
segments.original_text: μανδραγόρας — Mandrake. The root beaten with olive oil and applied is good for wounds that have been made frostbitten. Boiled in wine and applied it helps those with gout, with conditions of the joints, and those bitten by erysipelas. The root prepared as a wine: take one mina of root to one amphora of must, let stand for three months. Given before operations and cauterisations to produce insensibility to pain. Those who drink more than the due measure are made speechless forever.
segments.translation: Mandragoras [Mandrake]. The root, beaten together with olive oil and applied as a poultice, is beneficial for wounds rendered frostbitten. Boiled in wine and applied externally, it aids those suffering from gout, diseases of the joints, and those afflicted by erysipelas [a streptococcal skin infection]. Preparation of the root as a medicinal wine: take one mina [c. 431 g] of root to one amphora [c. 26 litres] of must [unfermented grape juice], and allow to stand for three months. This preparation is given prior to surgical operations and cauterisations in order to produce insensibility to pain [analgesia/anaesthesia]. Those who drink in excess of the proper measure are rendered mute [or: speechless] permanently.

segments.title_inferred: Hemlock (Konion): Topical and Toxic Uses, Antidote, and Historical Note
segments.original_text: Konion (Hemlock — Socrates' poison) — κώνειον — Hemlock. The whole plant is cold and narcotic. The juice of the plant applied to the testicles before puberty prevents them from growing and stops desire. Applied to the breasts of girls before they develop it prevents the breasts from growing. The ancient physicians used hemlock to prevent excessive breast development. But taken in drink hemlock kills. It kills by making the body cold from below upward until it reaches the heart and lungs. ANTIDOTE: Give warm wine and make the patient walk until sleep overcomes them — keep them awake if possible. Historical note: The death of Socrates (399 BCE) as described by Plato in the Phaedo matches Dioscorides' description of hemlock poisoning exactly — numbness ascending from feet to heart.
segments.translation: Konion [κώνειον, Hemlock]: The entire plant is cold in quality and narcotic [narkōtikon] in effect. The juice of the plant, applied to the testicles before the onset of puberty, prevents their growth and suppresses sexual desire. Likewise, applied to the breasts of girls before they have developed, it arrests breast growth. The physicians of earlier times employed hemlock for this purpose — to prevent excessive development of the breasts. However, if hemlock is taken internally as a drink, it is fatal. It kills by chilling the body progressively from the lower parts upward, until the cold reaches the heart and lungs. ANTIDOTE: Administer warm wine and compel the patient to walk; if sleep threatens to overcome them, keep them awake by every possible means. [Translator's historical note: The death of Socrates in 399 BCE, as recounted by Plato in the Phaedo, corresponds precisely with Dioscorides' account of hemlock poisoning — a numbness and coldness ascending from the feet toward the heart.]

segments.title_inferred: Aconite (Wolfsbane): Toxicity and Antidotes
segments.original_text: Akonition (Aconite / Wolfsbane) — ἀκόνιτον — Aconite (wolfsbane). This plant is of all things the swiftest to kill. It kills by chilling. Given to leopards and wild beasts smeared on meat it kills them quickly — hence it is called leopard's bane. ANTIDOTE: Castor oil taken immediately, or wine and myrrh, or raisin wine with absinthium, or wormwood decoction. If these are given quickly the patient is saved.
segments.translation: Akoníton [ἀκόνιτον] — Aconite (wolfsbane). Of all things, this plant kills most swiftly. It kills by inducing a sensation of chilling [i.e., by cooling the vital heat]. When smeared upon meat and given to leopards and wild beasts, it kills them quickly — from which it derives its name 'leopard's bane' [pardaliankhes]. ANTIDOTE: Castor oil [kiki] administered immediately, or wine mixed with myrrh, or raisin wine [passum] combined with absinthium [wormwood], or a decoction of wormwood. If any of these remedies is administered promptly, the patient may be preserved.

segments.title_inferred: Strychnos (Nightshade): Three Kinds and Their Pharmacological Gradations
segments.original_text: Strychnos (Nightshade family) — στρύχνος — Nightshade (multiple species). Three kinds documented by Dioscorides: (1) the garden kind (Solanum nigrum) — soporific but not as dangerous; (2) the mad kind (possibly Datura or belladonna) — causes madness and hallucinations; (3) the killing kind (Atropa belladonna or Hyoscyamus) — lethal in excess. The Solanaceae family — henbane, mandrake, belladonna, Datura, Solanum — produced more pharmaceutical compounds than any other plant family in the archive. Dioscorides' classification of three kinds of nightshade represents the first systematic attempt to distinguish the pharmacological gradations within a toxic plant genus.
segments.translation: Strychnos [στρύχνος, 'nightshade']: Dioscorides documents three distinct kinds. (1) The garden kind [στρύχνος κηπαῖος]: identified with Solanum nigrum; described as soporific [sleep-inducing] but comparatively less dangerous. (2) The mad kind [στρύχνος μανικός]: possibly Datura or Atropa belladonna; causes madness [delirium] and hallucinations. (3) The killing kind [στρύχνος ὑπνωτικός or equivalent]: identified with Atropa belladonna or Hyoscyamus niger; lethal when taken in excess. The plant family Solanaceae — encompassing henbane [Hyoscyamus], mandrake [Mandragora], belladonna [Atropa], Datura, and Solanum — yielded a greater number of pharmaceutical compounds than any other plant family represented in this archive. Dioscorides' tripartite classification of nightshade constitutes the earliest systematic effort to differentiate pharmacological gradations within a toxic plant genus.

segments.title_inferred: General Principles of Antidote Treatment (Book VI closing section)
segments.original_text: ἀντίδοτα — Antidotes and their general principles. (1) Emetics first — get the poison out quickly; (2) Warm wine given after emetic — warms the cold poisons; (3) Opposite treatment to the poison's action — cold poisons treated with warmth; hot poisons with cooling; (4) Movement — keeping the patient active delays absorption of some poisons.
segments.translation: Antidotes [ἀντίδοτα] — General Principles. Dioscorides concludes Book VI with the following foundational principles of antidote treatment: (1) Emetics are to be administered first, so as to expel the poison swiftly from the body; (2) Warm wine is to be given following the emetic, as it counteracts and warms poisons that are cold in nature; (3) Treatment must be applied in opposition to the action of the poison — cold poisons are to be treated with warmth, and hot poisons with cooling agents; (4) The patient is to be kept in motion, as movement delays the absorption of certain poisons into the body. These principles anticipate the approach of modern toxicology: decontamination, the administration of antidotes, and supportive care. The Greek and Roman development of systematic toxicology — represented by Nicander of Colophon (2nd century BCE) and Dioscorides (77 CE) — constitutes the first systematic body of pharmacological science.

segments.title_inferred: On Pepper: Properties, Uses, and Adulteration Test
segments.original_text: De Materia Medica, Pedanius Dioscorides (77 CE), Book I. On Pepper (κόσκινον / Piper nigrum, P. longum). 'Both kinds of pepper are warming, diuretic, and digestive. They break up intestinal gas, are beneficial for those chilled and shivering, clear the eyesight when rubbed on eyelids, attract moisture from the head, and are given for tetanus and epilepsy in wine. Applied with honey they clear corneal film. The long pepper is more warming than the round. Adulterated pepper may be detected by water — true pepper sinks, adulterated floats.' Preparation: taken in wine or honey, applied topically to eyes. Conditions: digestive complaints, chills, tetanus, eye conditions, head congestion.
segments.translation: From De Materia Medica by Pedanius Dioscorides (77 CE), Book I. On Pepper (Greek: κόσκινον; Latin: Piper nigrum and Piper longum). Both varieties of pepper possess warming [calorific], diuretic, and digestive [peptic] properties. They dissolve and expel intestinal flatulence, and are of benefit to those suffering from chills and shivering fits. When rubbed upon the eyelids, they sharpen the eyesight; they draw down [attract] moisture from the head; and when administered in wine, they are prescribed for tetanus [opisthotonos] and epilepsy [the falling sickness]. When applied together with honey, they disperse opacity [leucoma] of the cornea. Long pepper [Piper longum] is more heating in its action than round pepper [Piper nigrum]. Adulteration of pepper may be identified by means of water: genuine pepper sinks to the bottom, while adulterated pepper floats on the surface. Preparation: administered internally in wine or mixed with honey; applied topically to the eyes. Conditions treated: digestive complaints, intestinal gas, chills, tetanus, epilepsy, corneal film [leucoma], and head congestion.

segments.title_inferred: On Iris: Properties and Medical Uses
segments.original_text: De Materia Medica, Book I. On Iris (ἶρις). The root is warming, sharp, and somewhat bitter. It softens and draws out splinters, and being applied with vinegar it disperses hard swellings under the chin. Taken in wine it relieves pain of the sides, convulsions, ruptures, coughs, and inflammation of the trachea. With honey applied to the head it cures dandruff. Applied as a pessary it moves the menstrual flux and expels the afterbirth. The smoke drives away serpents.
segments.translation: On Iris (ἶρις). The root is warming [calorific], sharp [acrid], and somewhat bitter in quality. It softens tissue and draws out embedded splinters; when applied topically with vinegar, it disperses hard swellings beneath the chin [scrofulous or glandular swellings]. When taken internally in wine, it relieves pain in the sides, convulsions, ruptures [hernias], coughs, and inflammation of the trachea [windpipe]. Mixed with honey and applied to the head, it cures dandruff [scurf of the scalp]. When applied as a pessary [vaginal suppository], it promotes the menstrual flux and expels the afterbirth [placenta]. The smoke [of the burnt root] drives away serpents.

segments.title_inferred: On Nard: Celtic and Indian Varieties (Dioscorides, De Materia Medica I.12)
segments.original_text: Nardus calefacit et siccat, stomachi utilis est, diarrhoeam sistit, urinam ciet, et ex oleo capiti prodest. Hepar et renes iuvat, ictericis auxiliatur. In vino cum pipere haustum serpentium morsis medetur. Cum vino uvae passae oculis admotum inflammationes tollit. Indica Celtica validior est.
segments.translation: Nard [Nardus] is warming and drying. It is beneficial to the stomach, arrests diarrhea, promotes urination, and applied in oil is good for the head. It benefits the liver and kidneys, and is of service to those suffering from jaundice. Given in wine with pepper, it aids those bitten by serpents. Applied to the eyes with raisin wine [passum], it removes inflammation. The Indian kind is stronger than the Celtic.

segments.title_inferred: On Costus: Properties, Uses, and Preparations
segments.original_text: Costus is warming and drying. Taken in drink it helps those bitten by serpents and those with pain in the side. It moves the menstrual flux, provokes urine, helps those with dropsy, and eases epilepsy. Taken with wine it helps against poisons. Applied with honey it removes dark blotches and spots. The root burned as incense drives away serpents. The Arabian kind is best, then the Indian.
segments.translation: Costus [Κόστος] is warming and drying in quality. When taken as a drink, it benefits those who have been bitten by serpents and those suffering from pain in the side. It stimulates the menstrual flow, promotes urination, aids those afflicted with dropsy [edema], and alleviates epilepsy. Taken with wine, it acts against poisons. When applied topically with honey, it removes dark blotches and spots from the skin. The root, when burned as incense, repels serpents. The Arabian variety is considered the finest, followed by the Indian.

segments.title_inferred: On Poppy Juice and Opium (De Materia Medica, Book II, Chapter 76)
segments.original_text: De Materia Medica, Book II. On Poppy Juice/Opium (ὀπός μήκωνος / Papaver somniferum). 'The juice of the wild poppy is the most powerful of the soporifics. It eases pain, induces sleep, aids digestion, is good for coughs and for spitting blood. Taken in drink it benefits those with chronic illnesses and with intestinal pains. But used too much it harms, for it makes men lethargic and kills them. One may know the genuine by its bitterness and by its rapid heaviness upon the senses. The adulterated is less bitter and less effective.' Preparation: dried latex dissolved in wine or water. Conditions: pain, insomnia, cough, intestinal pain. Critical note: Dioscorides explicitly warns of lethal overdose — the first documented pharmacological warning about opioids in Western medicine.
segments.translation: From De Materia Medica, Book II, concerning the juice of the poppy [opium] (Greek: ὀπός μήκωνος; Latin botanical: Papaver somniferum): 'The juice of the wild poppy is the most potent of all sleep-inducing substances [soporifics]. It relieves pain, brings on sleep, promotes digestion, and is beneficial for coughs and for the spitting of blood. Taken as a drink, it is of service to those suffering from chronic ailments and from pains of the intestines. However, when used in excess it is harmful, for it renders men torpid and brings about death. Genuine opium may be recognized by its bitterness and by the swiftness with which it weighs heavily upon the senses. That which has been adulterated is less bitter and correspondingly less effective.' Method of preparation: the dried latex is dissolved in wine or water. Conditions treated: pain, insomnia, cough, intestinal pain. Editorial note: Dioscorides explicitly cautions against lethal overdose, representing the earliest documented pharmacological warning regarding opioid substances in the Western medical tradition.

segments.title_inferred: On the Willow: Medicinal Uses of Leaves and Bark
segments.original_text: De Materia Medica, Book II. On Willow (ἰτέα / Salix species). 'The leaves and bark of the willow, being boiled with a little pepper and drunk, they stop the flux of the belly. The juice pressed from the leaves being dropped into the ears stops the pain in them. The bark burned and dissolved in vinegar removes corns and calluses. The leaves bruised small and taken with a little pepper and wine stop the flowing of the seed in those troubled by dreams. The bark of the willow being boiled and the decoction drunk helps those troubled with gout.' Preparation: leaves boiled in water; bark decocted; leaves bruised. Conditions: diarrhea, ear pain, gout, skin conditions.
segments.translation: From De Materia Medica, Book II, on the Willow (Greek: ἰτέα; Latin: Salix species): The leaves and bark of the willow, boiled together with a small amount of pepper and drunk, arrest looseness of the bowels [flux of the belly, i.e., diarrhea]. The juice extracted by pressing the leaves, when dropped into the ears, relieves pain within them. The bark, when burned and dissolved in vinegar, removes corns and calluses [indurated skin growths]. The leaves, finely bruised and taken with a little pepper and wine, suppress involuntary seminal emission [flowing of the seed] in those afflicted by nocturnal dreams [wet dreams]. The bark of the willow, when boiled and the resulting decoction drunk, provides relief to those suffering from gout. Preparation methods include: boiling leaves in water; preparing a decoction of bark; and bruising leaves for internal use. Conditions addressed: diarrhea, otalgia [ear pain], hyperkeratosis [corns and calluses], spermatorrhea [involuntary seminal emission], and gout [podagra].

segments.title_inferred: Wheat and Grain Medicines: Therapeutic Uses of Triticum
segments.original_text: De Materia Medica, Book III. On Wheat and Grain Medicines (πυρός / Triticum and related). 'The flour of wheat applied in a poultice with boiled figs mollifies and ripens swellings. Bread crumbs chewed and applied draw out splinters. The water in which wheat has been boiled is good for the throat when gargled. The decoction of green wheat applied with oil to the temples eases headache. Old lard rendered with wheat flour applied warm relieves pain of the joints and helps those with gout and sciatica.' Preparation: flour poultice; bread applied; decoctions. Conditions: swellings, splinters, sore throat, headache, joint pain.
segments.translation: From De Materia Medica, Book III. Concerning Wheat and Grain-Based Medicines (Greek: πυρός [pyros]; Latin: Triticum, and related grains). 'Wheat flour, applied as a poultice together with boiled figs, softens [mollifies] and brings to maturation [ripens] swellings. Bread crumbs, chewed and then applied to the skin, draw out splinters. The water in which wheat has been boiled is beneficial for the throat when used as a gargle. A decoction [boiled extract] of green [unripe] wheat, applied to the temples with oil, relieves headache. Old lard rendered [melted down and clarified] together with wheat flour and applied warm relieves pain of the joints, and is of benefit to those suffering from gout and sciatica.' Methods of preparation include: flour poultice, topical application of chewed bread, and decoctions. Conditions treated: swellings, embedded splinters, sore throat, headache, and joint pain (including gout and sciatica).

segments.title_inferred: On Saffron (Crocus sativus) — De Materia Medica, Book III, Chapter 25
segments.original_text: De Materia Medica, Book III. On Saffron/Crocus (κρόκος / Crocus sativus). 'Saffron has a warming, softening, and sleep-inducing faculty. It benefits the eyes and the stomach. Taken in drink it sharpens the sight. Applied with honey and wine to inflammations it helps them. Taken with wine in drink it cheers the mind. Given with wine it helps those bitten by the land-crocodile. Applied as a pessary it moves the menstrual flux. But taken in excess it harms by inducing a kind of intoxication.'
segments.translation: De Materia Medica, Book III. Concerning Saffron [κρόκος / Crocus sativus]. Saffron possesses a warming, softening, and sleep-inducing property [faculty]. It is beneficial to the eyes and to the stomach. When taken as a drink, it sharpens the eyesight. When applied topically with honey and wine to sites of inflammation, it affords relief. When taken in wine as a drink, it gladdens [cheers] the mind. Administered with wine, it assists those who have been bitten by the land-crocodile [a terrestrial reptile, likely a large lizard or monitor]. Applied as a pessary [vaginal suppository], it promotes [moves] the menstrual flow. However, when consumed in excess, it is harmful, producing a form of intoxication.

segments.title_inferred: On Aloe: Properties, Preparations, and Conditions Treated
segments.original_text: De Materia Medica, Book III. On Aloe (ἀλόη / Aloe vera and related species). 'Aloe has the property of binding and is somewhat bitter. It stops bleeding from wounds when applied, closes together wounds, and is good for the eyes. Taken in drink it loosens the belly and purges bile. Applied to the head with wine and oil it clears dandruff. It stops the falling off of hair. Applied warm it heals the haemorrhoids. The best is the Barbarian kind, which is transparent like glass and saffron-coloured.'
segments.translation: De Materia Medica, Book III. Concerning Aloe (Greek: ἀλόη; Aloe vera and related species). Aloe possesses astringent [binding] properties and is moderately bitter in taste. When applied topically, it arrests bleeding from wounds, draws wounds closed, and is beneficial for the eyes. When consumed as a drink, it loosens the bowels and purges bile [acts as a cholagogue]. Applied to the scalp together with wine and oil, it removes dandruff [furfur capitis] and arrests hair loss. When applied warm, it heals hemorrhoids [haemorrhoids]. The finest variety is that known as the Barbarian kind, which is transparent in appearance, resembling glass, and of a saffron [crocus] color.

segments.title_inferred: Dioscorides on Mandrake: Properties, Preparations, and Uses
segments.original_text: De Materia Medica, Book IV. On Mandrake (μανδραγόρας / Mandragora officinarum). 'The root has the quality of warming. Wine made of the root, a cyathus [small measure] being drunk, is a soporific for those who need to be cut or cauterized, that they may not feel the pain. Given in drink to those with serpent-bites, and in drink for those who drink the juice of aconite [poison antidote]. Applied as a pessary it moves the menstrual flux. The bark and root applied with oil heals inflammation and prurigo. But excess causes lethargy and even death.' Preparation: root in wine as surgical anesthetic; applied topically. Conditions: surgical anesthesia, snakebite, menstrual disorders. Note: earliest documented description of surgical anesthesia in European medicine — 1,800 years before ether.
segments.translation: From De Materia Medica, Book IV. Concerning Mandrake (Greek: μανδραγόρας; Mandragora officinarum). 'The root possesses a warming quality. Wine prepared from the root, when drunk in the measure of one cyathus [a small cup-measure, approximately 45 ml], induces sleep [acts as a soporific] in those who must undergo cutting or cauterization, so that they may not feel the pain. It is likewise given as a drink to those suffering from serpent-bites, and administered to those who have swallowed the juice of aconite [serving as an antidote to that poison]. When applied as a pessary [vaginal suppository], it stimulates the menstrual flow. The bark and root, when applied with oil, heal inflammation and prurigo [itching skin conditions]. However, an excessive dose brings on lethargy and may even cause death.' Preparation: the root steeped in wine and drunk as a surgical anesthetic; also applied topically with oil. Conditions treated: surgical anesthesia, snakebite, aconite poisoning, menstrual irregularity, inflammation, prurigo. Historical note: this passage represents the earliest documented description of surgical anesthesia in the European medical tradition, predating the use of ether by approximately 1,800 years.

segments.title_inferred: On Stramonium / Thorn Apple: Doses, Toxicity, and Therapeutic Uses
segments.original_text: De Materia Medica, Book IV. On Stramonium/Thorn Apple (στράμμωνον / Datura species). 'The root being drunk in wine makes the mind wander — a drachm weight makes one have a vision beyond the ordinary, two drachms bring on madness for three days, four drachms kill. The antidote is pine resin drunk in wine. Applied topically with vinegar it is good for skin diseases and abscesses. The seed hung around the neck as an amulet helps against epilepsy — but this may be superstition.' Preparation: root in wine in precise small doses for vision; topical application. Conditions: surgical anesthesia, vision, epilepsy. Note: Dioscorides documents both the therapeutic and toxic effects of Datura at different doses — a sophisticated dose-response observation.
segments.translation: From De Materia Medica, Book IV. Concerning Stramonium, or Thorn Apple [στράμμωνον; Datura species]: When the root is drunk in wine, it causes the mind to wander. A single drachm [drachma] in weight produces visions of an extraordinary nature; two drachms induce madness lasting three days; four drachms prove fatal. The antidote is pine resin [resina pini] taken in wine. Applied externally with vinegar, it is beneficial for skin diseases and abscesses. The seed, worn around the neck as an amulet [amuletum], is said to be of service against epilepsy — though Dioscorides notes this may be mere superstition. Preparation: the root administered in wine in carefully measured small doses to produce visionary effects; also prepared as a topical application. Conditions treated: surgical anesthesia [narcosis], visionary or altered states, epilepsy. Editorial note: Dioscorides here records both the therapeutic and the lethal effects of Datura at differing doses, representing a notably sophisticated dose-response observation for the period.

segments.title_inferred: On Honey: Properties, Uses, and Preparations (Dioscorides, De Materia Medica, Book V, Chapter 2)
segments.original_text: De Materia Medica, Book V. On Honey (μέλι / Apis mellifera product). 'Honey, being boiled and applied to old wounds, draws out what is in them and cleanses them and produces flesh. Taken with water it clears the chest and lungs and loosens the cough. It digests and expels what burdens the stomach. Attic honey is the most praised — it is dry and does not rot. Applied to the teeth and gums it fastens loose teeth. Rubbed on the eyelids it removes the darkness before the eyes. The worst honey is that from box trees and yew — it produces nausea and vomiting.' Preparation: applied directly to wounds; taken with water; rubbed on teeth and eyes. Conditions: wounds, respiratory, digestive, eye conditions, dental.
segments.translation: From De Materia Medica, Book V: On Honey (Greek: μέλι; produced by Apis mellifera). Honey, when boiled and applied to old [chronic] wounds, draws out their contents, cleanses them, and promotes the growth of new flesh [granulation]. When taken with water, it clears the chest and lungs and loosens [expectoration of] cough. It aids digestion and expels whatever burdens the stomach. Attic honey [from the region of Attica, Greece] is most highly esteemed — it is dry in quality and does not putrefy. Applied to the teeth and gums, it firms up loose teeth. Rubbed on the eyelids, it disperses dimness [opacity or obscuration] before the eyes. The worst kinds of honey are those derived from box trees [Buxus sempervirens] and yew [Taxus baccata], as these produce nausea and vomiting. Preparations: applied directly and warm to wounds; taken internally mixed with water [hydromel]; rubbed onto teeth, gums, and eyelids. Conditions treated: chronic wounds, respiratory ailments (chest congestion, cough), digestive complaints, eye conditions (dimness of vision), dental looseness.

segments.title_inferred: Copper and Verdigris: Medicinal Uses (Dioscorides, De Materia Medica, Book V)
segments.original_text: The flower of copper [verdigris] being applied stops the flows of blood from wounds and cleanses foul ulcers. Applied with honey it draws together the edges of wounds. Used as an eye salve it sharpens the sight. Beaten fine and applied it stops excessive menstrual flow. Washed copper applied with vinegar removes eruptions of the skin and dandruff. Copper vessels for water storage keep the water from putrefying.
segments.translation: The flower of copper [verdigris, i.e., basic copper acetate or copper carbonate patina] being applied stops the flow of blood from wounds and cleanses foul ulcers. When applied with honey, it draws together the edges of wounds. Used as an eye salve, it sharpens the sight. Beaten fine and applied, it arrests excessive menstrual flow. Washed copper [i.e., copper that has been rinsed or purified] applied with vinegar removes eruptions of the skin and dandruff. Copper vessels used for water storage prevent the water from putrefying.

segments.title_inferred: On Garlic (Allium sativum) — De Materia Medica, Book I, Chapter 68
segments.original_text: De Materia Medica, Book I. On Garlic (σκόρδον / Allium sativum). 'Garlic has a sharp and heating faculty. Eaten it provokes urine, is good for the stomach that is cold and full of wind, clears the arteries, is given to those troubled with coughs and with asthma. Applied it draws out leeches and other things that have got into the body. It expels worms and kills lice and nits when applied externally. Given as a pessary it moves the menstrual flux. Applied to the head it kills lice. When eaten it harms those of a warm body temperament. The wild kind is more effective than the cultivated.' Preparation: eaten raw or cooked; applied topically; given as pessary. Conditions: respiratory, parasites, digestive gas, menstrual disorders, lice. Note: Dioscorides' comprehensive garlic entry prefigures modern research on allicin as antimicrobial and on garlic's cardiovascular effects.
segments.translation: De Materia Medica, Book I. On Garlic (σκόρδον / Allium sativum [garlic]). Garlic possesses a sharp and heating [calefacient] quality. When eaten, it provokes urination, benefits a stomach that is cold and flatulent [full of wind], clears the arteries [vessels], and is prescribed for those afflicted with coughs and asthma. When applied externally, it draws out leeches and other foreign bodies that have entered the body. It expels intestinal worms and, when applied topically, kills lice and nits. Administered as a pessary [vaginal suppository], it promotes the menstrual flux. Applied to the head, it destroys lice. When ingested, it is harmful to those of a warm [bilious or choleric] bodily temperament. The wild variety is more potent than the cultivated.

segments.title_inferred: Valerian: Uses and Preparations According to Dioscorides
segments.original_text: De Materia Medica, Book I. On Valerian (φοῦ / Valeriana species). 'The root is warming and has a singular quality. Taken in drink it provokes urine and the menstrual flux. It benefits those with pain in the side, and it is good for those bitten by serpents. Some call it nardus silvestris because its smell resembles nard. The roots of the Pontic kind are better than those from other places.'
segments.translation: From De Materia Medica, Book I. Concerning Valerian (Greek: φοῦ; Latin: Valeriana species). 'The root is warming in character and possesses a singular [i.e., unique or specific] quality. When taken as a drink, it stimulates the flow of urine and promotes the menstrual discharge. It brings relief to those suffering pain in the side, and it is beneficial for those who have been bitten by serpents. Some call it nardus silvestris [wild nard] on account of its scent resembling that of true nard. The roots of the Pontic variety [from the region of Pontus, on the Black Sea] are superior to those obtained from other localities.'

segments.title_inferred: Dioscorides on Chamomile: Preparations and Therapeutic Uses
segments.original_text: De Materia Medica, Book III. On Chamomile (ἀνθεμίς / Matricaria chamomilla / Anthemis nobilis). 'Chamomile being boiled and the decoction taken in a bath softens and eases pain. Taken in drink it helps those with kidney stones and provokes urine. The flowers boiled in oil and the oil poured into the ears ease pain in the ears. Applied in a poultice it ripens and disperses hard swellings. The oil made from the flowers is good for head-ache and for all pains and tightness of the sinews.' Preparation: flowers decocted as bath and drink; flowers in oil for ears and head; poultice. Conditions: kidney stones, ear pain, headache, swellings, muscle pain. Note: Chamomile's use for pain and inflammation by Dioscorides maps directly onto its modern pharmacology — alpha-bisabolol and apigenin are validated anti-inflammatory compounds.
segments.translation: From De Materia Medica, Book III. On Chamomile [ἀνθεμίς]: Chamomile, when boiled and the decoction used in a bath, softens the body and relieves pain. When taken as a drink, it benefits those suffering from kidney stones [lithiasis] and stimulates the flow of urine [diuretic action]. The flowers boiled in oil, with the resulting oil instilled into the ears, relieve earache. Applied as a poultice [cataplasm], it brings hard swellings to a head and disperses them. The oil prepared from the flowers is beneficial for headache and for all pains and contractures [tightness] of the sinews [tendons and muscles]. Preparations described: (1) decoction of the whole plant for use as a bath; (2) decoction taken internally as a drink; (3) flowers infused in oil, used as eardrops; (4) poultice applied topically to swellings; (5) flower oil applied to the head and sinews. Conditions treated: kidney stones, urinary retention, earache, headache, hard swellings [indurations], and pain and spasm of the muscles and sinews. Editorial note: The therapeutic applications described by Dioscorides for chamomile correspond closely to its documented modern pharmacology; the active constituents alpha-bisabolol and apigenin have been validated as anti-inflammatory agents in contemporary research.

segments.title_inferred: On Henbane: Properties, Preparations, and Dangers
segments.original_text: De Materia Medica, Book IV. On Henbane (ὑοσκύαμος / Hyoscyamus niger). Henbane causes confusion of mind and sleep. Applied to the forehead and temples with oil of rose it causes sleep. The seed drunk with wine makes men insane and similar to drunkards for a time. The leaves applied in a poultice ease pain of the joints and of gout. Used as a fumigation it is good for pain of the teeth. Applied to swellings and inflammations it reduces them. But used too freely it brings madness and even death.
segments.translation: From De Materia Medica, Book IV. On Henbane (Greek: ὑοσκύαμος; Latin: Hyoscyamus niger). Henbane induces confusion of the mind and sleep. When applied to the forehead and temples together with oil of rose [oleum rosaceum], it produces sleep. The seed, drunk with wine, renders men temporarily insane and resembling those who are drunk. The leaves, applied as a poultice [cataplasma], relieve pain of the joints and of gout [arthritis/podagra]. When used as a fumigation [suffimentum], it is beneficial for toothache. Applied to swellings and inflammations, it reduces them. However, when used in excess, it produces madness and even death.

segments.title_inferred: On Colchicum: Uses, Preparations, and Dangers
segments.original_text: De Materia Medica, Book II. On Colchicum (κολχικόν / Colchicum autumnale). 'The bulb of kolchikon, being drunk in wine, is good for those who are bitten by scorpions. But taken in excess it strangles like mushrooms. The flower, drunk in wine, makes the stomach griping pains stop — but it is not to be used except for those who are strong and in good health, and even then only in small amounts, for the plant is sharp and harmful.' Preparation: bulb in wine in small doses. Conditions: gout (joint pain from rich diet), scorpion sting. Note: Dioscorides' warning about colchicum being dangerous in excess directly parallels the Islamic tradition's (Ibn Sina's) identical warning — and colchicine remains the gold-standard gout treatment with an identically narrow therapeutic window today.
segments.translation: From De Materia Medica, Book II. On Colchicum [Greek: κολχικόν; Latin: Colchicum autumnale]: 'The bulb of colchicum, taken in wine, is beneficial for those who have been bitten by scorpions. However, consumed in excess, it causes death by suffocation, as poisonous mushrooms do. The flower, when drunk in wine, relieves griping pains of the stomach — yet it is not to be administered except to those who are robust and in good health, and even then only in very small quantities, for the plant is acrid and injurious.' Preparation method: the bulb prepared in wine, administered in small doses. Conditions treated: scorpion envenomation; stomach cramps; gout [joint pain associated with rich diet]. Note: Dioscorides' caution regarding the toxicity of colchicum in excess closely mirrors the identical warning later recorded in the Islamic medical tradition by Ibn Sina [Avicenna], and the active compound colchicine derived from this plant remains the standard modern treatment for gout, with an equivalently narrow therapeutic window.

segments.title_inferred: On Cassia and Cinnamon: Properties, Uses, and Preparations
segments.original_text: Cassia and Cinnamon are warming, digestive, and diuretic. Taken in drink they help those bitten by serpents and those with pain in the sides. They move the menstrual flux. They benefit those with cold and moist stomachs. Applied with oil to the face they remove spots and blemishes. Taken in drink with wine they warm those who are cold. Cinnamon is more powerful than cassia in all these uses — it is more warming and more fragrant.
segments.translation: Cassia and Cinnamon are warming [calefacient], digestive [peptic], and diuretic. Taken as a drink, they aid those bitten by serpents [snakebite antidote] and those suffering from pain in the sides [pleurisy or flank pain]. They promote the menstrual flux [emmenagogue]. They are beneficial for those with cold and moist stomachs [atonic or phlegmatic digestive conditions]. Applied to the face with oil, they remove spots and blemishes [topical depilatory/clarifying use]. Taken in drink with wine, they warm those who suffer from cold [constitutional coldness or chills]. Cinnamon surpasses cassia in all of these virtues — it is more warming and more fragrant.

segments.title_inferred: Animal-Derived Medicines in De Materia Medica, Book II
segments.original_text: De Materia Medica, Book II. Animal-derived medicines (overview chapter). 'Many medicines are derived from animals. The bile of bears is good for those troubled by dim sight. The gall of the river fish Hyena is drunk for those struck with epilepsy. The blood of the tortoise dropped into the ears helps those with pain in the ears. The fat of geese applied with sulphur and oil helps those with alopecia. The brains of the hare eaten raw cure trembling. Cow dung mixed with old oil applied to the forehead stops headache. The dried liver of a wolf drunk in wine helps those with pain of the stomach and liver.'
segments.translation: From De Materia Medica, Book II. Chapter on animal-derived medicines (overview). 'Many medicines are obtained from animals. The bile [gall] of bears is beneficial for those afflicted with dimness of vision. The gall of the river fish called Hyena is taken as a drink for those suffering from epilepsy. The blood of the tortoise, dropped into the ears, relieves pain in the ears. The fat of geese, applied together with sulphur and oil, is beneficial for those with alopecia [hair loss]. The brains of the hare, eaten raw, cure trembling [tremors]. Cow dung mixed with old oil, applied to the forehead, arrests headache. The dried liver of a wolf, drunk in wine, helps those suffering from pain of the stomach and liver.'

segments.title_inferred: On Quicksilver (Mercury): Properties, Uses, and Toxicity
segments.original_text: De Materia Medica, Book V. On Mercury/Quicksilver (ὑδράργυρος / Mercurius). 'Quicksilver is produced by pounding cinnabar in iron mortars with vinegar. It is very cold and moist in faculty and is deadly to living things, killing by its weight if swallowed. It dissolves gold and silver and for this reason it is kept in lead or glass or silver vessels. It is used in ointments for lice and for mange in animals. Those who work with it have trembling of the hands.' Preparation: topical only. Conditions: lice, mange. Note: Dioscorides recognises mercury toxicity ('deadly to living things') and documents the occupational tremors of mercury workers — the first description of mercury poisoning in European medical literature, predating modern understanding of mercurialism by 1,800 years.
segments.translation: From De Materia Medica, Book V. On Quicksilver (Greek: ὑδράργυρος; Latin: Mercurius). Quicksilver is produced by pounding cinnabar [native mercuric sulfide] in iron mortars with vinegar. In its elemental qualities it is very cold and moist, and is deadly to living creatures, killing by its sheer weight when swallowed. It dissolves gold and silver, and for this reason it is stored in vessels of lead, glass, or silver. It is applied in ointments [as a topical treatment] against lice and against mange in animals. Those who work with it develop trembling of the hands. Preparation: topical application only. Conditions treated: lice [pediculosis], mange [in animals]. Editorial note: Dioscorides explicitly acknowledges the lethal toxicity of mercury ('deadly to living things') and records the occupational tremors observed in mercury workers — representing the earliest known description of mercury poisoning in the European medical literature, anticipating the modern clinical understanding of mercurialism by approximately 1,800 years.

segments.title_inferred: Book I — Aromatics, oils, salves, trees, and shrubs
segments.original_text: Περὶ ὕλης ἰατρικῆς βιβλίον πρῶτον — ἀρώματα, ἔλαια, βάλσαμα, ῥητίναι
segments.translation: De Materia Medica Book I — On aromatics, oils, balsams, and resins. Book I covers approximately 130 aromatic plants, oils, and resins — the pharmacological preparation foundation. This book contains the most complete ancient documentation of: olive oil preparations, wine-based medicines, aromatics including iris root, spikenard, cinnamon, cassia, and the Balsam of Judea. Book I was omitted from the initial corpus but contains critical documentation for several plants already in the archive, adding Greek/Roman primary source confirmation to aromatic plants previously documented only through secondary sources.

segments.title_inferred: Nardos (Spikenard) — the most valuable aromatic
segments.original_text: Νάρδος Ἰνδική — θερμαντικόν, οὐρητικόν, στομαχικόν, ἧπαρ καὶ σπλῆνα ὠφελεῖ
segments.translation: Indian spikenard (Nardostachys jatamansi) — warming, diuretic, benefits the stomach, liver, and spleen. Dioscorides documents spikenard as one of the most valuable medicinal aromatics — the same plant documented in Ayurvedic medicine as jatamansi for psychiatric conditions. The connection between Dioscorides' documentation for liver and digestive conditions and the Ayurvedic psychiatric application reflects the different pharmacological lenses of two independent traditions examining the same plant.

segments.title_inferred: Iris root — the emetic and wound medicine
segments.original_text: Ἴρις — ἐμετικόν, κοιλιακόν, ῥυπτικόν
segments.translation: Iris root — emetic, loosens the bowels, cleansing. Dioscorides documents iris root for digestive complaints and wound cleaning — a use consistent with the documented antibacterial activity of iridals (iris triterpenoids). The Florentine iris (Iris florentina) whose root was the basis of orris powder (used in perfumery) has documented antimicrobial properties that validate its traditional wound-cleaning application.

segments.title_inferred: Book V — Wines, vinegar, and minerals
segments.original_text: Περὶ οἴνων καὶ μεταλλικῶν — οἶνοι ἐπὶ ζωμοὺς τιθέμενοι
segments.translation: On wines and minerals — wines prepared by infusing plants. Dioscorides Book V documents pharmaceutical wine preparations — essentially the Roman equivalent of tinctures and extracts. This represents the Greek/Roman pharmacy's primary delivery system for plant medicines. Key medicinal wines documented: wormwood wine, rose wine, myrtle wine, pine resin wine (resinated wine), and juniper berry wine. Each represents a specific therapeutic plant dissolved into a preservation medium — the prototype for modern pharmaceutical tinctures.

segments.title_inferred: Mekon (Opium poppy) — Dioscorides' comprehensive entry
segments.original_text: Μήκων — ὁ ὀπὸς αὐτῆς ὑπνωτικόν, ἀναλγητικόν. μέγα δὲ καὶ σύγχυσιν ποιεῖ πλεῖον ληφθέν.
segments.translation: Poppy — its juice (opium) is soporific and analgesic. Taken in excess it produces great confusion [and death]. Dioscorides' opium entry is the foundational Western documentation of this plant — establishing its use for pain and sleep, warning explicitly about lethal overdose. The toxicological precision ('taken in excess it produces great confusion') predates Ibn Sina's habituation warning but shares the observation that dose determines whether opium is medicine or poison.

segments.title_inferred: Iris (Orris Root) — De Materia Medica I.1
segments.original_text: Ἶρις· οἱ δὲ κρίνον ἄγριον καλοῦσιν, οἱ δὲ ξιφίον, Ῥωμαῖοι δὲ ἶριν, Αἰγύπτιοι δὲ ναρούι· γίνεται δὲ ἡ ἀρίστη ἐν Ἰλλυρίδι καὶ Μακεδονίᾳ καὶ ἐν Ἄλπεσι, δευτέρα δὲ ἡ ἐν Λιβύῃ. ἔστι δὲ ἡ μὲν Ἰλλυρικὴ ὀδμῆς ἕνεκα ἀρίστη, τρεφομένη ἐν ξηροῖς καὶ τραχέσι τόποις. ἔχει δὲ φύλλα ὅμοια κρίνῳ, πλείονα δὲ καὶ πλατύτερα καὶ ξιφοειδῆ· τὸ δὲ ἄνθος ποικίλον τὰς χρόας, ὅθεν καὶ τοὔνομα· ῥίζα δὲ ὀζώδης τε καὶ συμπαγής. αἱρεῖσθαι δὲ δεῖ βαρεῖαν καὶ πυκνὴν καὶ δριμεῖαν καὶ εὐώδη, ἔτι δὲ γεώδη τὴν γεῦσιν. δύναμιν δὲ ἔχει θερμαντικήν, δριμεῖαν, στυπτικήν· ἁρμόζει δὲ πρὸς βράγχους καὶ κατάρρους, πρὸς κοιλιακοὺς μετὰ μέλιτος καὶ ὄξους διδομένη, πρὸς τὰς ἐν θώρακι συλλογάς, πρὸς κεφαλαλγίας, πρὸς σκληρίας καὶ οἰδήματα καὶ παρωτίδας καταπλασσομένη, πρὸς ἐρυσίπελας καὶ ὄνυχας μετὰ ὄξους, πρὸς ἀλωπεκίας μετ' ἐλαίου, πρὸς συρίγγων ἐγκάθαρσιν, πρὸς ἐφήμερον καὶ σκολοπένδρας δαγμοὺς μεθ' ὑσσώπου καὶ ὄξους καὶ μέλιτος, πρὸς ὑστέρας ἀναξηραντικῶς προστιθεμένη. ἡ δὲ ῥίζα ἑφθὴ μετὰ μέλιτος ἁρμόζει πρὸς βράγχους καὶ κατάρρους.
segments.translation:
Iris [Greek: Ἶρις]: Some call it wild lily [κρίνον ἄγριον], others sword-lily [ξιφίον]; the Romans call it 'iris,' the Egyptians 'naroui.' The finest variety grows in Illyria and Macedonia and in the Alps; the second-finest in Libya. The Illyrian iris is the best for fragrance, thriving in dry and rough places. Its leaves resemble those of the lily but are more numerous, broader, and sword-shaped [ξιφοειδῆ]; the flower is variegated in color — from which the plant takes its name [i.e., iris, the rainbow]. The root is knotted and compact. One should choose a root that is heavy, dense, pungent, fragrant, and earthy to the taste.

Its properties [δύναμις] are warming [θερμαντική], sharp/acrid [δριμεῖα], and astringent [στυπτική]. It is suited to the following conditions and preparations:

— Hoarseness [βράγχοι] and catarrh [κατάρροι]: administered as a decoction, including as a root boiled with honey.
— Disorders of the bowels [κοιλιακοί]: given with honey and vinegar.
— Accumulations in the chest [συλλογαὶ ἐν θώρακι].
— Headaches [κεφαλαλγίαι].
— Hardened swellings [σκληρίαι], oedemas [οἰδήματα], and parotid swellings [παρωτίδες]: applied as a poultice [καταπλάσσεται].
— Erysipelas [ἐρυσίπελας] and conditions of the nails [ὄνυχες]: applied with vinegar.
— Alopecia [ἀλωπεκία, patchy hair loss]: applied with olive oil.
— Cleansing of fistulas [σύριγγες]: used as an internal wash or probe application.
— Bites of the scolopendra [σκολόπενδρα, centipede] and ephemeral fever [ἐφήμερον]: administered with hyssop, vinegar, and honey.
— Uterine conditions [ὑστέραι]: applied as a pessary [προστίθεται] in a drying [ἀναξηραντικῶς] manner.

The root boiled with honey is specifically suited to hoarseness and catarrh.

segments.title_inferred: Calamus — Sweet Flag (Acorus calamus)
segments.original_text: Κάλαμος ὁ ἀρωματικός· οἱ δὲ Κάλαμον Ἰνδικόν, οἱ δὲ Κάλαμον εὐώδη καλοῦσιν. ἔστι δὲ ῥίζα καλάμου τινός, ἡ ἀρίστη μὲν ἡ ἐκ τῆς Ἰνδικῆς καὶ Συρίας κομιζομένη, κούφη, εὔθρυπτος, εὐώδης, ποιοῦσα κατὰ τὴν κατεργασίαν ἀράχνης ὑφάσματι ὅμοιον, μηδεμίαν ἐντός σαπρίαν ἔχουσα· ἡ δὲ ἐκ Γαλατίας καὶ Κολχίδος ἥσσων. δύναμιν δὲ ἔχει θερμαντικήν, διουρητικήν. βοηθεῖ δὲ ἥπατος καὶ στομάχου πόνοις πινόμενος, σπάσμασιν, ὀσφύος ἄλγεσι, κυστίτισι· ποιεῖ καὶ πρὸς τὸν ἐμμηνῶν ἀγωγήν· δέχεται δὲ αὐτὸν καὶ ἀντίδοτος ἡ Θηριακή. χρῶνται δὲ αὐτῷ καὶ οἱ μυρεψοί.
segments.translation: Calamus [aromatic reed]: some call it Indian Calamus, others call it sweet-smelling Calamus [Kalamos euōdēs]. It is the root of a certain reed [Calamus], the finest being that brought from India and Syria — light, easily crumbled, fragrant, producing when worked a texture resembling a spider's web [i.e., fibrous and gossamer-like internally], and having no internal decay or rot. That from Galatia and Colchis is of inferior quality. It possesses [in its active properties, dynamis] a warming and diuretic power [thermatikēn, diourētikēn]. Taken as a drink [pinomenos], it aids in ailments of the liver and stomach, spasms [spasmata], pains of the lower back [osphyos algesi], and bladder inflammations [kystitis]. It also promotes the flow of the menses [emmēnōn agōgēn]. It is received [accepted as an ingredient] in the antidote called Theriaca [Theriak]. Perfumers [myrepsos] also make use of it.

segments.title_inferred: Asarum — Wild Ginger (De Materia Medica I.3)
segments.original_text: Ἄσαρον· οἱ δὲ ἀσάραβακκαν καλοῦσιν. φύλλα μὲν ἔχει ὅμοια τῷ ἀριστολοχίᾳ τῇ στρογγύλῃ, μικρότερα δέ, καὶ μᾶλλον χλωρά, ἐπὶ γῆς κείμενα, ἄνθος δὲ ὑπὸ τὸ φύλλον ἁλουργές, ῥίζας δὲ λεπτάς, εὐώδεις, πικράς. γεννᾶται δὲ ἐν τόποις σκιεροῖς. ἡ ῥίζα δύναμιν ἔχει θερμαντικήν, λεπτυντικήν, οὐρητικήν. ποθεῖσα δραχμὴ μία μετὰ οἴνου θερμοῦ ἄγει οὖρα καὶ ἔμμηνα, καὶ ἐπ' ἰκτέρου καὶ ὕδρωπος καὶ στρόφων ἁρμόζει. τινὲς δὲ καὶ πρὸς τὰ τῶν σπλάγχνων ἐμπνεύματα δίδοασι. χρῶνται δὲ αὐτῇ καὶ οἱ πρὸς τὰ νεφριτικὰ πάθη. εὑρίσκεται δὲ καὶ ἐν Εὐρώπῃ καὶ ἐν τῇ Ποντικῇ.
segments.translation: Asarum [ἄσαρον]: some call it asarabacca [ἀσάραβακκαν]. It has leaves similar to round-leaved aristolochia [ἀριστολοχία ἡ στρογγύλη], but smaller and greener, lying flat upon the ground; its flower, which appears beneath the leaf, is purple [ἁλουργές]; its roots are slender, fragrant, and bitter. It grows in shaded places [ἐν τόποις σκιεροῖς]. The root possesses warming [θερμαντικήν], attenuating [λεπτυντικήν], and diuretic [οὐρητικήν] properties. Taken as a draught of one drachm [δραχμὴ μία] with warm wine, it promotes urination and menstruation [ἄγει οὖρα καὶ ἔμμηνα], and is suited to jaundice [ἐπ' ἰκτέρου], dropsy [ὕδρωπος], and colic [στρόφων]. Some also administer it for flatulence of the viscera [πρὸς τὰ τῶν σπλάγχνων ἐμπνεύματα]. It is likewise employed by those treating affections of the kidneys [νεφριτικὰ πάθη]. It is found both in Europe and in the region of Pontus [ἐν τῇ Ποντικῇ].

segments.title_inferred: Spikenard (Nardus) — Aromatic Root and Its Medicinal Uses
segments.original_text: Νάρδος· οἱ δὲ νάρδον Ἰνδικήν, οἱ δὲ νάρδον ὀρεινήν, οἱ δὲ φοῦ, Ῥωμαῖοι νάρδον, οἱ δὲ νάρδον Συριακήν. Ἔστι δὲ βοτάνιον μικρόν, ῥίζαν ἔχον πυκνήν, βαρεῖαν, εὐώδη, μελανίζουσαν, οἰνώδη τῇ ὀσμῇ· καυλία δὲ πολλά, λεπτά, σταχυηφόρα· ἡ δὲ κόμη αὐτοῦ κατὰ τὸν σφόνδυλον ἢ τὴν ἀσάρου ῥίζαν. Θερμαντική τε καὶ ξηραντική ἐστι, διουρητική, συμφέρει δὲ κοιλίᾳ καὶ ἥπατι καὶ νεφροῖς καὶ κύστει.
segments.translation: Nardus [Spikenard]: called by some 'Indian nard' [Nardos Indike], by others 'mountain nard' [Nardos oreine], by others 'phu,' by the Romans 'nardus,' and by others 'Syrian nard' [Nardos Syriake]. It is a small plant [botanion mikron] bearing a dense, heavy, fragrant root, dark in color, with a wine-like [oinodes] scent. It has many slender, spike-bearing stems [stachyephora kaula]; its foliage resembles that of asarum [Asarum europaeum] or the root of sphondylion. It is warming [thermantike] and drying [xerantike] in quality, promotes urine [diouretike], and is beneficial to the belly [koilia], liver [hepar], kidneys [nephroi], and bladder [kystis]. It eases nausea and is used in preparations for headache and insomnia. The oil and essence derived from the spiked tops are employed in compound medicines [antidota] and aromatic unguents [myra], and it serves as an ingredient in theriac. Applied as a decoction or taken in wine, it strengthens a weak stomach, moves sluggish bowels when the condition calls for mild stimulation, and supports the liver against obstructions. It is also described as useful in kidney complaints and in promoting menstrual flow [emmena agei]. The dried root, ground and administered in drink, counteracts jaundice [ikterikois] and dropsy [hydrops] in combination with other remedies. Externally, a plaster [kataplasma] of the powdered root mixed with water or wine may be applied to the forehead for headache.

segments.title_inferred: Amomum — Cardamom-like Aromatic Spice
segments.original_text: Ἄμωμον· οἱ δὲ ἀμώμην καλοῦσιν. φύεται ἐν Ἀρμενίᾳ καὶ Μηδίᾳ καὶ Πόντῳ. ἔστι δὲ βοτάνη σταχυώδης, κλωνία ἔχουσα ὡσεὶ βότρυς συγκεκαμμένα εἰς ἑαυτά, λεπτά, εὔοσμα, χρόαν ἔχοντα ὠχράν, ῥίζαν δὲ ἐρυθράν, ὀσμὴν εὐώδη. δοκιμάζεται δὲ τὸ μὴ εὐθρυπτόμενον καὶ πλῆρες καὶ εὔοσμον καὶ τὸ ἐρυθρὸν τῇ χρόᾳ καὶ τὸ μὴ μεμιγμένον. ἔστι δὲ θερμαντικόν, στυπτικόν, οὐρητικόν· ἁρμόζει δὲ πρὸς τὰ τῆς κοιλίας ῥεύματα, δυσεντερίας, θηριοδήκτους, ἐρυθήματα τοῦ προσώπου, τὰ σκοτωματικά, τὰ νεφριτικά. ἡ δὲ τοῦ καρποῦ χρῆσις κατὰ τοὺς ἄλλους ἀρωματικούς.
segments.translation: Amomum — some call it 'amome.' It grows in Armenia, Media, and Pontus. It is a spike-bearing herb [βοτάνη σταχυώδης], having slender, fragrant, pale-yellow branches clustered together like grape-bunches curved inward upon themselves, with a red root and a pleasant aromatic odor. The best quality [δοκιμάζεται] is that which does not crumble easily, is full and dense, strongly fragrant, red in color, and unadulterated. It is warming [θερμαντικόν], astringent [στυπτικόν], and diuretic [οὐρητικόν]. It is beneficial for fluxes of the belly [κοιλίας ῥεύματα], dysentery [δυσεντερίας], bites of venomous creatures [θηριοδήκτους], redness of the face [ἐρυθήματα τοῦ προσώπου], dizziness or vertigo [σκοτωματικά], and kidney complaints [νεφριτικά]. The use of the fruit follows the same practice as with other aromatics.

segments.title_inferred: Kostos (Costus Root) — De Materia Medica I.6
segments.original_text: Κόστος· οἱ δὲ Κόσσον καλοῦσιν. γίνεται μὲν ἐν Ἀραβίᾳ καὶ Συρίᾳ καὶ Ἰνδίᾳ· ἔστι δὲ ῥίζα ξυλώδης, ἣν δέχονται ναυτιλλόμενοι ἐξ Ἀραβίας. ἔστι δὲ τὸ μὲν Ἰνδικὸν λευκότερον καὶ ἐλαφρότερον καὶ εὐωδέστερον, τὸ δὲ Ἀράβιον μελάντερον καὶ ῥητινῶδες, τὸ δὲ Σύριον τούτων ἀσθενέστατον. δύναμιν δὲ ἔχει θερμαντικήν, λεπτυντικήν, ἑλκτικήν. βοηθεῖ δὲ πρὸς πλευριτικούς, περιπνευμονικούς, τετανικούς, σπασμούς, ἐπιληψίαν, σκοτώματα, ῥίγη· πρὸς ὠτικὰς ὀδύνας μετ' ἐλαίου χλιανθέν. πίνεται δὲ πρὸς τὰ τῶν νεφρῶν καὶ κύστεως πάθη, πρὸς ὑδρωπικούς, πρὸς δύσπνοιαν, πρὸς ἰκτερικούς, πρὸς τὰ ἀπεψίας καὶ τοὺς χολερικούς. ὑπαγωγὴν δὲ κοιλίας ποιεῖ τεσσαράκοντα κόκκους γινομένης τῆς δόσεως. κατέχει δὲ ἱδρῶτας ὀσφραινόμενον. χρήσιμόν ἐστι καὶ πρὸς κατάρρους, πρὸς ἕλμινθας τὰς πλατείας σὺν οἴνῳ ποθέν· ποιεῖ καὶ πρὸς ἀφροδίσια ἐπικεχυμένον ἐπὶ τὰ αἰδοῖα. ἐπιτίθεται δὲ καὶ ἐπὶ τὰ ὀφθαλμικὰ παθήματα σὺν γάλακτι γυναικείῳ. ἐμμήνων δὲ ἀγωγός ἐστι τε σὺν οἴνῳ πινόμενον. στρογγυλοῦται δὲ τῇ μορφῇ τὸ ἄριστον αὐτοῦ, ἄθραυστον, εὔοσμον, εὐωδέστατον, πικρόν, μὴ σεσηπός.
segments.translation: Kostos — some call it Kosson. It grows in Arabia, Syria, and India. It is a woody root, which traders receive by sea from Arabia.
