It grows in Cappadocia and Galatia. It is a plant with leaves resembling fennel [márathon], and a root that is sharp [acrid], warming to the tongue, and productive of saliva [sialagogue]. The root, taken as a drink with vinegar, relieves toothache [odontalgia]; held in the mouth, it keeps the teeth free from pain. Warmed in olive oil and instilled into the ears, it stops earache [algémata]. It is also suited to the treatment of nerve contractions [sinew-spasms] and to quartan fevers [tetartaíous], being applied before the onset of the paroxysm. Some use wild stavesacre [agrían staphída] as a substitute for pyrethrum.

segments.title_inferred: Feverfew for Uterine Conditions (Parthenion / Matricaria parthenium)
segments.original_text: Παρθένιον· οἱ δὲ μητρωνία καλοῦσιν, οἱ δὲ ἀμάρακον ἄγριον. φύεται ἐν τόποις τραχέσι καὶ λιθώδεσι. φύλλα ἔχει ὅμοια σελίνῳ, πλατύτερα δέ, δυσώδη· ἄνθη λευκά, μικρά, κέντρον ξανθὸν ἔχοντα. ποιεῖ δὲ πρὸς τὰ ἔμμηνα καὶ τὰ ἐκτρωτικά· λαμβάνεται δὲ ἡ κόμη ἑψηθεῖσα ἐν οἴνῳ. θερμαίνει δὲ καὶ ξηραίνει, καὶ ἐμπλάσσεται ἐπὶ τῶν σκληρῶν καὶ χρονίων φλεγμονῶν.
segments.translation: Parthenion [Feverfew]: some call it mēthrōnia [lit. 'of the womb/mother'], others call it wild amaracus [marjoram]. It grows in rough and stony places. It has leaves similar to celery [selinon] but broader, and of an unpleasant odour; its flowers are white and small, with a yellow centre [kentron]. It is effective for [promoting] the menses [emmena] and for inducing abortion [ektrōtika]. The leafy tops [komē] are taken, boiled in wine. It heats and dries [in the Galenic sense of thermal qualities], and may be applied as a poultice [emplassesthai] upon hard and long-standing inflammations [phlegmonai].
segments._label: Dioscorides IV.29 — Feverfew for uterine conditions

segments.title_inferred: Black Cumin (Nigella sativa) — De Materia Medica IV.30
segments.original_text: Μελάνθιον· οἱ δὲ μελασπέρμιον καλοῦσιν, οἱ δὲ νίγελλα· Ῥωμαῖοι git. Φύεται ἐν τοῖς σίτοις· φύλλα δὲ ἔχει μάραθρον ἐοικότα, μικρά, λεπτά· ἄνθος λευκόν, σπέρμα μέλαν, τρίγωνον, εὔοσμον, ἔν τισι κελύφεσι περιεχόμενον. Δύναμιν ἔχει θερμαντικήν, διαφορητικήν. Βοηθεῖ δὲ πινόμενον, μεθ' ὕδατος ἢ ὀξυμέλιτος, δυσπνοίᾳ καὶ κεφαλαλγίᾳ· τὰς ἐν τῇ κεφαλῇ ψύξεις διαφορεῖ ἐγκαταδεθέν τε ἐν ὀθονίῳ καὶ ὀσφραινόμενον· ἀναγωγὸν δὲ πτυέλου παχέος ἐστί· κίνησιν δὲ οὖρον ποιεῖ καὶ ἔμμηνα. Ἀποκτείνει δὲ τοὺς μύας, σὺν ὄξει λεαινόμενον, καταχριόμενον· σκώληκας δὲ τοὺς ἐν ῥισὶν καὶ ὠσίν, ἐν ἐλαίῳ ἀφεψηθέν, ἐγχεόμενον. Πρὸς δὲ τοὺς ὄνυχας τοὺς ὑποστρεφομένους, σὺν ὄξει ἑψηθέν, καλῶς ποιεῖ. Σὺν ἐλαίῳ δὲ καταχριόμενον ἀναιρεῖ τοὺς ἐν τῷ σώματι βδέλλας.
segments.translation: Black Cumin [melanthion]: some call it melaspermaion ['dark-seeded one'], others nigella; the Romans call it git. It grows among grain crops. Its leaves resemble those of fennel — small and slender. The flower is white; the seed is black, triangular, fragrant, and enclosed in certain pods [kelyphesi]. It has a warming [thermantikē] and dispersive [diaphorētikē] faculty. Taken as a drink with water or oxymel [a preparation of honey and vinegar], it benefits [those suffering from] difficult breathing [dyspnoea] and headache. Bound in a linen cloth and used as a sniff, it disperses cold humours [psychxeis] in the head. It promotes the bringing up [anagōgon] of thick phlegm [ptuelos]. It stimulates urination and promotes menstruation [emmēna]. Crushed with vinegar and applied as an ointment, it kills mice. Boiled in oil and poured in, it kills worms [skōlēkes] in the nostrils and ears. Boiled with vinegar, it is effective against ingrowing nails [onychas tous hypostrephoutas]. Applied with oil as an ointment, it destroys leeches on the body [bdellas en tō sōmati].

segments.title_inferred: Seseli (Meadow Saxifrage / Seseli)
segments.original_text: Σέσελι — τὸ μὲν Μασσαλιωτικόν, τὸ δὲ Αἰθιοπικόν, τὸ δὲ Πελοποννησιακόν. τὸ Μασσαλιωτικὸν φύλλα μὲν ἔχει ὅμοια μαράθῳ, πλατύτερα δέ· καυλὸν δὲ ἀδρόν, σπέρμα πλατύ, εὐῶδες, ὀξεῖαν ἔχον τὴν δύναμιν· τὸ δὲ Αἰθιοπικὸν σπέρμα ἔχει μεῖζον καὶ εὐωδέστερον. τῆς ῥίζης τε καὶ τοῦ σπέρματος ἡ χρῆσις· θερμαίνει γάρ, οὐρητικόν ἐστι, καταμήνια ἄγει, σπάσματα καὶ στρόφους λύει, πνεύματα φυσώδη διαφορεῖ, ἐπιληψίαν θεραπεύει.
segments.translation: Seseli — there is the Massaliote [from Massalia/Marseille] variety, the Ethiopian variety, and the Peloponnesian variety. The Massaliote variety has leaves resembling fennel [marathron], but broader; a thick stem; a flat seed that is fragrant and has a sharp [pungent] potency. The Ethiopian variety has a larger and more fragrant seed. The root and seed are used medicinally: they are warming [thermic], diuretic [ourētikon], promote menstruation [katamēnia agei], relieve spasms [spásmata] and colic [strόphous], disperse flatulent winds [pneumata physōdē], and treat epilepsy [epilēpsian].

segments.title_inferred: Stone Parsley (Sison)
segments.original_text: Σίσων· οἱ δὲ ἄμωμον καλοῦσιν, οἱ δὲ σέσελι Αἰθιοπικόν, Ῥωμαῖοι ἄμμιον· φύεται δὲ ἐν τραχέσι καὶ πετρώδεσι τόποις. φύλλα ἔχει σελίνῳ παρόμοια, μικρότερα δέ, σπέρμα λεπτότερον σελίνου καὶ εὐωδέστερον, ῥίζαν λεπτήν. δύναμιν δὲ ἔχει θερμαντικήν, οὐρητικήν. σπέρμα αὐτοῦ πινόμενον κοιλίαν ῥήγνυσι καὶ οὖρα κινεῖ καὶ καταμήνια καὶ ἐμμήνων ἐποχὴν λύει καὶ στρόφους παύει. ἁρμόζει δὲ καὶ πρὸς τοὺς ἀνελκωμένους τὰς νεφρούς, καὶ πρὸς τοὺς ἐν κύστει λίθους, καὶ πρὸς τὸ ψύχος τοῦ στομάχου· ῥίζα δὲ αὐτοῦ ὡσαύτως ἐνεργεῖ.
segments.translation: Sison [stone parsley]: some call it amomon, others call it Aethiopian seseli [Sison amomum], and the Romans call it ammion. It grows in rough and rocky places. It has leaves resembling celery [selinon] but smaller, seeds finer and more fragrant than celery, and a slender root. Its property [dynamis] is warming [thermantike] and diuretic [ouretike]. The seed, taken as a drink, loosens the bowels, promotes urination, stimulates the menses [katamenia], resolves suppression of the monthly flow [emmenon epoche], and stops colic [strophous]. It is also beneficial for those with ulceration of the kidneys [anelkomenos tas nephrous], for stones in the bladder [en kyste lithous], and for coldness of the stomach [psychos tou stomachou]. The root acts in the same manner.

segments.title_inferred: Hartwort (Tordylium) — De Materia Medica IV.33
segments.original_text: Τορδύλιον· οἱ δὲ σέσελι Καισαρέων καλοῦσιν, οἱ δὲ σκανδίκιον, Ῥωμαῖοι δὲ τορδύλιον. φύεται ἐν Καππαδοκίᾳ καὶ τῇ Συρίᾳ. φύλλα ἔχει παραπλήσια τῷ κορίῳ, σπέρμα δὲ στρογγύλον, πλατύ, λευκόν, εὐῶδες, θερμαντικῆς καὶ τμητικῆς δυνάμεως. βοηθεῖ δὲ σπληνικοῖς σὺν ὄξει πινόμενον, καὶ δυσουροῦσι, καὶ εἰλεοῖς, καὶ ἐμμήνων ἀγωγόν ἐστι· τὰ δὲ σπέρματα εὐστόμαχα καὶ πεπτικά, καὶ ὀδόντων ἀλγήμασι βοηθεῖ ἐγχεόμενον εἰς τὸ οὖς τὸ ἐκ τοῦ σπέρματος ἀφέψημα μετ' ὄξους.
segments.translation: Tordylion [Hartwort]: some call it 'seseli of Caesarea,' others call it 'skandikion,' and the Romans call it 'tordylium.' It grows in Cappadocia and Syria. It has leaves resembling those of coriander, and a seed that is round, flat, white, and fragrant, with a warming [thermatikē] and incising [tmētikē] faculty [dynamis]. Drunk with vinegar, it benefits those with disorders of the spleen [splēnikois], those suffering from difficult urination [dysourousi], and those with intestinal obstructions [eileoís]; it is also an emmenagogue [promotes menstruation]. The seeds are beneficial to the stomach [eustomacha] and aid digestion [peptika]. A decoction [aphepsēma] of the seed in vinegar, poured into the ear, relieves toothache [odontōn algēmasi].

segments.title_inferred: Masterwort (Imperatoria / Peucedanum ostruthium)
segments.original_text: Ὀστρούθιον· οἱ δὲ Ἰμπερατόριαν καλοῦσιν, οἱ δὲ Ἀστραγαλίαν, Ῥωμαῖοι δὲ Σερπεντάριαν. φύλλα ἔχει ὅμοια σελίνῳ, μείζονα δέ, καυλὸν δὲ ὑψηλόν, σπέρμα πλατύ, ῥίζαν παχεῖαν καὶ εὐώδη. βοτάνη δέ ἐστι δυνάμεως θερμαντικῆς καὶ τμητικῆς. ἡ ῥίζα αὐτῆς σὺν μέλιτι λειωθεῖσα ὀδόντων ἄλγη παύει. σὺν ὄξει δὲ πινομένη κωλικοὺς ὠφελεῖ καὶ ῥῖγος προσβολὰς ἀποκρούεται. θερμαίνει δὲ καὶ σπλῆνα. τοὺς δὲ ὑπὸ θηρίων δεδηγμένους ἐπιτιθεμένη ὠφελεῖ.
segments.translation: Ostrouthion [Masterwort]: some call it Imperatoria, others Astragalia, and the Romans call it Serpentaria. It has leaves resembling celery [selinon] but larger, a tall stem, a broad seed, and a thick, fragrant root. It is a plant of heating [thermantikē] and cutting [tmētikē] power. Its root, ground with honey, relieves toothache [odontōn algē]. Taken in vinegar [oxos], it benefits those suffering from colic [kōlikous] and repels the attacks of fever-chills [rigos prosbolai]. It also warms the spleen [splēna]. Applied externally as a poultice, it benefits those bitten by wild beasts [thēria].

segments.title_inferred: Hog's Fennel (Peucedanum)
segments.original_text: Περὶ Πευκεδάνου. Πευκέδανον· οἱ δὲ σίλφιον Μηδικόν, οἱ δὲ θαψίαν, Ῥωμαῖοι δὲ Ferulam sylvestrem καλοῦσιν. Φύεται ἐν ὄρεσι καὶ τραχέσι τόποις. Ῥίζα αὐτοῦ ἐστι παχεῖα, ὀσμὴν ἔχουσα ἰσχυράν· ἐξ ἧς ὀπὸς ῥεῖ τεμνομένης, ᾧ χρώμεθα πρὸς τὰς ἰατρικὰς χρείας. Ὁ δὲ ὀπὸς ξηραντικός ἐστι, θερμαντικός, λεπτυντικός, ῥυπτικός.
segments.translation: Concerning Peucedanum [Hog's Fennel]. Peucedanum — called by some 'Median silphium' [σίλφιον Μηδικόν], by others 'thapsia' [θαψία], and by the Romans 'Ferula sylvestris' [wild fennel-giant]. It grows on mountains and rough terrain. Its root is thick and possesses a powerful odour; from this root, when cut, flows a juice [ὀπός, resinous exudate] which we employ for medicinal purposes. This juice is drying [ξηραντικός], warming [θερμαντικός], thinning [λεπτυντικός], and cleansing [ῥυπτικός]. It is beneficial when drunk with vinegar-water [ὀξύμελι] for those suffering from sciatica [ἰσχιαδικοί], cramps [σπάσματα], convulsions [ἐπιληψία], and difficulty of breathing [δύσπνοια]. Taken with wine it provokes menstruation [ἐμμήνων ἀγωγός] and expels the afterbirth [ἀποκύημα]. Applied externally with honey [μέλι] or resin [ῥητίνη] it draws out splinters and thorns embedded in the flesh. It is used as a pessary [πεσσός] to promote uterine discharge. Mixed with Cimolian earth [Κιμωλία γῆ] or with rose oil [ῥόδινον] it is applied to the head for headaches [κεφαλαλγία]. Instilled into the ears it relieves chronic ear pain [ὠτὸς ἀλγήματα χρόνια] and ringing [ἦχοι]. Combined with wax ointment [κηρωτή] it is rubbed on for shivering fits [ῥίγη] and scrofulous swellings [χοιράδες]. The root itself, dried and powdered, is applied to foul ulcers [κακοήθη ἕλκη] and to wounds. The juice [ὀπός], smelled or held near the nostrils, revives those who have fainted [λειποθυμία] and those gripped by epilepsy [ἐπίληπτοι].

segments.title_inferred: Sulfurwort (Peucedanum)
segments.original_text: Πευκέδανον· οἱ δὲ πευκέδαμον καλοῦσιν, οἱ δὲ θεόφραστον, Ῥωμαῖοι δὲ φερούλαν σιλβατικάν, οἱ δὲ σέρπουλλον, Αἰγύπτιοι δὲ σεσέλι, Δάκοι δὲ κοθιάτα. γεννᾶται μὲν ἐν τοῖς ὄρεσιν, ἔχει δὲ φύλλα μαράθῳ παραπλήσια, πλὴν λεπτότερα καὶ πυκνότερα, καυλὸν δὲ κοῖλον καὶ μακρόν, ῥίζαν παχεῖαν, ἣ ὀπὸν ἀνίησι δριμύν, τεμνομένη ἢ φλοιζομένη. ὁ δὲ ὀπὸς λαμβάνεται οὕτως· ἐγχαράττεται ἡ ῥίζα, καὶ ὑπό τι σκεῦος ὑποτίθεται, καὶ ὁ ὀπὸς ἐκρεῖ, ξηραίνεταί τε καὶ ἀποτίθεται. ἔστι δὲ ὁ ἄριστος ὁ ἀκαθαρσίας ἐκτός, εὐώδης, ὑπόπικρος, δακνώδης, μέλας τε καὶ ῥᾳδίως κεχυμένος. δύναμιν δὲ ἔχει θερμαντικὴν καὶ διαφορητικήν.
segments.translation:
Peucedanum [sulfurwort]: some call it peukedamon, others theophraston; the Romans call it ferula silvatica, others serpullum; the Egyptians call it seseli; the Dacians call it kothiata. It grows in mountainous regions and has leaves resembling those of fennel [marathon], but finer and more densely arranged, a hollow and elongated stem, and a thick root which, when cut or stripped of its bark, exudes a sharp [drimys] juice [opos]. The juice is collected as follows: the root is scored [engcharassō], a vessel is placed beneath it, the juice flows out, is dried, and then stored. The finest quality is that which is free of impurities, fragrant, slightly bitter [hypopikros], pungent [daknōdēs], dark in color, and easily liquefied. It has warming [thermantikē] and dispersing [diaphorētikē] properties.

The juice [opos], taken internally at a dose of two obols in water or wine or with a honey-water decoction [melikraton], relieves [lysis] spasms [spasmos], convulsions, and shivering fits; it aids those with difficulty breathing [dysknoia], ruptures [rhēgmata], and cramps [spasmos]; it provokes menstruation [emmēna agei], expels [ekballei] the afterbirth [hysterēn], and assists in difficult childbirths. Applied externally with cedar resin [kedria] or with oil of iris [irinon], it is good for ear pain [ōtalgia]; mixed with vinegar [oxos] it removes [ekleiphanei] vitiligo [leukē] and treats scab [psōra]. The smoke [kapnos] of the root, inhaled through a tube [aulos], relieves toothache [odontalgian]. The plant itself, drunk with wine, acts as an antidote [alexipharmakon] against serpent bites [herpetōn dēgmata]. The juice also clears the sight when applied to the eyes with Attic honey [Attikō meliti]. The root, pounded and applied as a plaster [kataplasma], draws out [helkei] splinters [akanthas] and is useful for headache [kephalalgian] when smeared on the forehead with vinegar.

segments.title_inferred: Dropwort (Filipendula vulgaris) — De Materia Medica IV.37
segments.original_text: Ὀινάνθη ἡ ἑτέρα· οἱ δὲ φιλιπένδουλαν καλοῦσιν. φύεται ἐν τόποις τραχέσι καὶ πετρώδεσι. φύλλα ἔχει ὅμοια σελίνῳ, ἄνθη λευκά, ῥίζαν ἔχουσαν ὡσπερεὶ σταφυλὴν ἐκ πολλῶν μακρῶν ῥιζιδίων, ἐν οἷς κρέμανται στρογγύλα μικρὰ ῥιζίδια ὥσπερ βολβοί. δύναμιν ἔχει ἡ ῥίζα στυπτικήν· βοηθεῖ στρόφοις, κοιλίας ῥεύμασι, νεφροῖς, κύστει· ποιεῖ πρὸς λίθους, εἰληφθεῖσα μετ᾽ οἴνου λευκοῦ. ἕλκη δὲ καθαίρει ξηραινομένη καὶ ἐπιπαττομένη.
segments.translation: The other Oenanthe [wild parsley-type plant], which some call Filipendula. It grows in rough and rocky places. It has leaves similar to celery [selinon], white flowers, and a root resembling a cluster of grapes, composed of many long rootlets from which hang small rounded tubers like bulbs. The root has an astringent [styptikē] property; it is beneficial for intestinal cramps [strophoi], fluxes of the belly, the kidneys, and the bladder; it is effective against bladder stones [lithoi] when taken with white wine. When dried and sprinkled [as a powder], it cleanses ulcers [helkē].

segments.title_inferred: Meadowsweet (Filipendula ulmaria) — De Materia Medica IV.38
segments.original_text: Σπειραία [Speiraia] — καλεῖται δὲ καὶ ἀργυρόλευκος· φύλλα ἔχει πτελέᾳ παραπλήσια, ἄνθη λευκά, εὐώδη, βοτάνη δασεῖα· ῥίζα δὲ αὐτῆς στρυφνή, χρήσιμος πρὸς τὰ ἐντὸς τραύματα καὶ αἱμορραγίας καὶ δυσεντερίαν. οἶνος δὲ ἐξ αὐτῆς σκευαζόμενος πρὸς τοὺς ὑδρωπικοὺς ἁρμόζει. τὰ δὲ φύλλα τριβόμενα καὶ ἐπιτιθέμενα τραύμασι παλαιοῖς ξηραίνει καὶ κολλᾷ.
segments.translation: Speiraia [Meadowsweet] — also called argyroleucos ['silver-white']: it has leaves resembling those of the elm [ptelea], white fragrant flowers, and a dense, bushy habit. Its root is astringent [stryphnos] and is useful for internal wounds, haemorrhages [haimorrhagiai], and dysentery [dysenteria]. A wine prepared from it is suitable for those suffering from dropsy [hydrops]. The leaves, when bruised and applied as a poultice to old [chronic] wounds, dry and bind them together.

segments.title_inferred: Agrimony (Eupatoria / Agrimonia eupatoria)
segments.original_text: Εὐπατόριον· οἱ δὲ ἀρκτοφόνον καλοῦσιν, οἱ δὲ κοινωνία, Ῥωμαῖοι δὲ ἡπατόριον. φύλλα ἔχει ὅμοια φοίνικι τῷ μικρῷ, εἰς ὀξὺ ἀπολήγοντα, ἐπ' ἄκρου δὲ τοῦ βλαστοῦ σπέρμα καθάπερ βοτρύδιον. ῥίζα δὲ λεπτή. βοηθεῖ δὲ ἡπατικοῖς· ἡ δὲ ῥίζα ποθεῖσα μετ' οἴνου ὀφιοδήκτοις.
segments.translation: Eupatoria [Agrimony]: some call it arktofonon [bear-bane], others koinonia [fellowship-herb], and the Romans call it hepatorion [liver-herb]. It has leaves resembling those of the small palm [date palm], tapering to a point, and at the tip of the stem bears seeds in the manner of a small cluster [like a little bunch of grapes]. The root is slender. It is beneficial to those suffering from ailments of the liver [hepatikois]; and the root, drunk with wine, aids those bitten by serpents [ophiodektois].

segments.title_inferred: Wood Avens (Geum urbanum) — De Materia Medica IV.40
segments.original_text: Γευμ· οἱ δὲ κάρυον γῆς καλοῦσιν, οἱ δὲ κεφαλωτόν· φύλλα ἔχει ὅμοια ἀγριμωνίᾳ, μικρότερα δέ, ῥίζαν εὐώδη, ἀρωματικήν, ἐπὶ τὸ ξηρὸν τεινόμενην· ἐφ᾿ ἧς βοτάνης ἀφέψημα πινόμενον δυσεντερίαν ἰᾶται καὶ στομαχικοῖς ἁρμόζει, καὶ ἡπατικοῖς, καὶ πρὸς τὰς ἐντὸς ῥήξεις καὶ σπάσματα, καὶ πρὸς τὸ θερμαίνεσθαι τὸν στόμαχον· τοῖς τε ὑπὸ θηρίου δεδηγμένοις πινομένη ὠφελεῖ.
segments.translation: Wood avens [Geum]: some call it 'earth-nut' [káryon gês], others call it 'head-like' [kephaloton]. It has leaves similar to agrimony [agrimonia], but smaller, and a root that is fragrant and aromatic, tending toward dryness [in quality]. A decoction [aphépsêma] drunk from this herb heals dysentery [dysentería] and is suitable for those suffering from stomach ailments [stomachikois] and liver complaints [hêpatikois], and for internal ruptures [entòs rhêxeis] and spasms [spásmata], and for inflammation of the stomach [to thermaínesthai tòn stómachon]. Drunk, it also benefits those bitten by venomous creatures [thêría].

segments.title_inferred: Soapwort (Strouthion / Saponaria officinalis)
segments.original_text: Στρούθιον· οἱ δὲ λυχνὶς ἀγρία, οἱ δὲ λυχνὶς στεφανωματική, Ῥωμαῖοι σαπωνάρια, οἱ δὲ λανάρια. φύεται ἐν τόποις τραχέσι καὶ λιθώδεσι. φύλλα ἔχει ὅμοια λυχνίδι, πλατύτερα δέ, ἄνθη λευκά, ῥίζαν λεπτήν, μακράν. ἡ ῥίζα αὐτῆς ἑψηθεῖσα ἐν ὕδατι ἀφρὸν ποιεῖ ὡς σάπων, καὶ ῥύπτει τὰ ἱμάτια· διὸ καὶ χρῶνται αὐτῇ οἱ κναφεῖς. πινομένη δὲ ἡ ῥίζα αὐτῆς κατακλᾷ τοὺς λίθους καὶ ἐκβάλλει· ποιεῖ δὲ καὶ πρὸς κηλήν. ἔστι δὲ καὶ οὐρητικὴ σφόδρα καὶ ἱκτερικοῖς ὠφέλιμος.
segments.translation: Strouthion [Soapwort]: called by some 'wild lychnis' [agria lychnis], by others 'garland lychnis' [lychnis stephanomatike]; by the Romans it is called saponaria, and by some lanaria [wool-plant]. It grows in rough and stony places. It has leaves similar to lychnis but broader, white flowers, and a slender, elongated root. The root, when boiled in water, produces a foam like soap [sapon] and cleanses garments; for this reason fullers [knapheis] make use of it. When the root is taken as a drink [pinomene], it breaks up and expels kidney stones [lithous]. It is also effective against rupture [kele / hernia]. Furthermore, it is powerfully diuretic [ourētikē] and beneficial to those suffering from jaundice [ikterikois].

segments.title_inferred: Campion (Silene / Lychnis species) — De Materia Medica IV.42
segments.original_text: Στρουθίον· οἱ δὲ λυχνίδα ἀγρίαν καλοῦσιν, οἱ δὲ πολυκνήμον, Ῥωμαῖοι σαπωνάριαν, οἱ δὲ στρουθίον ἀγριέλαιον· φύεται ἐν τοῖς ἀγροῖς, φύλλα ἔχον ὅμοια σικύῳ, χλωρὰ καὶ λεῖα· ἄνθος λευκόν· ῥίζα παχεῖα, ἔξωθεν μὲν φαιά, ἔνδοθεν δὲ λευκή. ταύτης ἡ ῥίζα ἀφρὸν ποιεῖ βαλλομένη εἰς ὕδωρ· εἰς ὅπερ καὶ χρῶνται οἱ γναφεῖς ἀντὶ σαπώνων. ἡ δὲ ῥίζα πινομένη εὔχρους ποιεῖ, ἐκλευκαίνει τε τὸ δέρμα, ψώραν ἰᾶται, λεπίδας ἀφαιρεῖ, καὶ ταῖς πρὸς εὐμορφίαν κατασκευαῖς ἐπιτηδεία ἐστίν.
segments.translation: Strouthion [σπουθίον, lit. 'sparrow-plant']: some call it wild lychnis [λυχνίς ἀγρία], others polykněmon [πολυκνήμον, 'many-jointed'], while the Romans call it saponaria [lit. 'soap-plant'], and others call it wild-olive strouthion. It grows in cultivated fields [ἀγροί], bearing leaves similar to those of the cucumber [σίκυος] — green and smooth — with a white flower. The root is thick, dark [φαιά] on the outside and white within. This root, when thrown into water, produces a foam [ἀφρόν], which fullers [γναφεῖς] use in place of soap [σάπων]. The root, when taken as a drink [πινομένη], produces a good complexion [εὔχρους], whitens and brightens the skin [ἐκλευκαίνει τὸ δέρμα], heals mange or scabies [ψώρα], removes scales or scurf [λεπίδες] from the skin, and is well-suited to preparations for the enhancement of beauty [πρὸς εὐμορφίαν].

segments.title_inferred: Catchweed (Cleavers / Goosegrass)
segments.original_text: Ἀπαρίνη — φύεται μὲν ἐν φραγμοῖς καὶ χέρσοις τόποις, στέλεχος ἔχουσα τετράγωνον, τραχύ, περιπλεκόμενον τοῖς πλησίον· φύλλα δὲ κατὰ διαστήματα κυκλοτερῶς περικείμενα, ὅμοια τοῖς τῆς ῥυάδος, τραχέα καὶ αὐτά. ὁ δὲ καρπὸς δικέφαλος, τραχύς, προσκολλώμενος τοῖς ἱματίοις. βοηθεῖ δὲ τοῖς ὑπὸ τῶν ἀράχνων δηχθεῖσιν ἐπιτιθεμένη· ὁ δὲ χυλὸς αὐτῆς πινόμενος ὠφελεῖ τοὺς κεχρονισμένους ἴκτερον ἔχοντας· κωλύει δὲ καὶ τοῦ γάλακτος τὴν πῆξιν.
segments.translation: Aparíne [Catchweed / Cleavers] — It grows in hedgerows and waste places, having a square, rough stem that clings to and twines around neighboring plants. The leaves are arranged in whorls at intervals, similar to those of the madder [ῥυάς], and are themselves rough. The fruit is twin-headed [two-lobed], rough, and adheres to garments. Applied as a poultice, it benefits those bitten by spiders [arachnid envenomation]. The juice, taken as a drink, benefits those suffering from chronic jaundice [ἴκτερον κεχρονισμένον]. It also prevents the curdling [congealing] of milk.

segments.title_inferred: Bedstraw (Galium verum) — De Materia Medica IV.44
segments.original_text: Γάλιον· οἱ δὲ γαλλίον καλοῦσιν. φύεται ἐν τόποις τραχέσι καὶ λιθώδεσι. φύλλα ἔχει ὅμοια ἀσπερούλῃ, λεπτότερα δέ, κατὰ σπονδύλους περικείμενα· ἄνθη χρυσόχροα, εὐώδη· βλαστοὶ ἁπαλοί, τετράγωνοι. ῥίζα λεπτή, ἐρυθρά. ἡ δὲ δύναμις αὐτοῦ· σφίγγει, στύφει ἠρέμα. οἱ δὲ βουκολοῦντες χρῶνται αὐτῷ εἰς τὸ πήγνυσθαι τὸ γάλα, ὅθεν καὶ τοὔνομα. τὸ δὲ ἀφέψημα τῶν ῥιζῶν πινόμενον ἀπὸ οἴνου ὠφελεῖ τοὺς ἀπὸ τοξεύματος τετρωμένους. φύλλα δὲ καταπλασσόμενα ἐπὶ τοὺς κατακαύματα ἀρήγει.
segments.translation: Galion [Bedstraw]; some call it Gallion. It grows in rough and stony places. Its leaves resemble those of woodruff [asperula] but are more slender, arranged in whorls around the stem; the flowers are golden-yellow [chrysochroa] and fragrant; the shoots are tender and four-angled [tetragonal]; the root is thin and red. Its [medicinal] faculty [dynamis]: it binds and gently astringes [styphei]. Herdsmen [boukolountes] use it to curdle milk [pēgnusthai to gala], from which circumstance it also derives its name [i.e., galion, from gala, 'milk']. A decoction [aphefsēma] of the roots, drunk in wine, benefits those wounded by an arrow [toxeuma]. The leaves, applied as a poultice [kataplassomena], give relief to burns [katakaumata].

segments.title_inferred: Crosswort (Cruciata laevipes) — De Materia Medica IV.45
segments.original_text: Σταυρωτόν· οἱ δὲ χρυσάνθεμον καλοῦσιν· φύλλα ἔχει τέσσαρα κατὰ διάμετρον ἀνὰ τοὺς γόνατα περικείμενα, ὅθεν καὶ σταυρωτὸν ἐκλήθη· ἄνθος χρυσοειδές· βοτάνη εἰς ὕψος ἀνατεινομένη· ῥίζα λεπτή. Ἕψεται δὲ ἐν οἴνῳ καὶ πίνεται πρὸς τὰς τῶν νεύρων ἐντάσεις καὶ ῥήξεις· τὰ δὲ φύλλα τριβέντα καὶ ἐπιτιθέμενα ἕλκη κολλᾷ.
segments.translation: Crosswort [Σταυρωτόν, 'Stauróton']: some call it chrysanthemon [golden-flower]. It has four leaves arranged diametrically around each node [κατὰ διάμετρον ἀνὰ τοὺς γόνατα], from which arrangement it derives its name 'crosswort' [i.e., 'cross-shaped']. The flower is golden-yellow [χρυσοειδές]; the plant is an upright herb of moderate height; the root is slender. It is boiled in wine and drunk as a remedy for tensions and ruptures of the sinews [νεύρων ἐντάσεις καὶ ῥήξεις]. The leaves, when bruised and applied as a poultice, close and consolidate wounds [ἕλκη κολλᾷ].

segments.title_inferred: Woodruff (Asperula / Galium odoratum) — Dioscorides IV.46
segments.original_text: Ἀσπέρουλα [Asperula] — καλεῖται δὲ καὶ ὠρυγανόφυλλον ὑπό τινων· φύλλα ἔχει ὅμοια ὀριγάνῳ, μικρότερα δέ, ἄνθη λευκά, ῥίζαν λεπτήν. φύεται ἐν σκιεροῖς τόποις καὶ ὑγροῖς. ἡ πόα ξηρανθεῖσα εὐῶδής ἐστι. δύναμιν ἔχει στυπτικήν· ἁρμόζει πρὸς τὰ ἕλκη, πρὸς τὰς φλεγμονὰς τοῦ ἥπατος, καὶ πρὸς τὰς τοῦ στομάχου ἀδυναμίας. καταπλάσσεται δὲ τὰ φύλλα πρὸς τὰ ἕλκη· ἀφέψημα δὲ αὐτῆς πινόμενον ὠφελεῖ τὸ ἧπαρ καὶ τὸν στόμαχον.
segments.translation: Woodruff [Asperula] — called by some 'oregano-leaved' [ōryganóphyllon]: it has leaves resembling those of oregano, but smaller, white flowers, and a slender root. It grows in shaded and moist places. When dried, the herb is fragrant [euōdēs]. It possesses an astringent [styptikē] quality. It is suited to wounds [helkē], to inflammations [phlegmonai] of the liver [hēpar], and to weaknesses [adynamiai] of the stomach [stomachos]. The leaves are applied as a poultice [kataplasma] to wounds; a decoction [aphepsēma] of it, drunk, benefits the liver and the stomach.

segments.title_inferred: Madder (Rubia tinctorum) — De Materia Medica IV.47
segments.original_text: Ἐρυθρόδανον· οἱ δὲ ῥιζάριον καλοῦσιν, οἱ δὲ γαλάτιον, Ῥωμαῖοι ῥουβέαν· ἔστι δὲ βοτάνη γνώριμος τοῖς βαφεῦσι, τοὺς ἐρίους βάπτουσι. φύεται δὲ ἐν χωρίοις τραχέσι καὶ ἀγρίοις· στέλεχος ἔχει τετράγωνον, τραχύ, ἐπὶ πολὺ ἐκτεινόμενον, φύλλα κατὰ διαστήματα ἐν κύκλῳ περικείμενα ὥσπερ τοῦ γαλίου πλατύτερα δέ· ῥίζαν ἔχει λεπτὴν μακράν, ἐρυθράν. σπείρεται ἐν κήποις καὶ ἐκ τῆς ῥίζης φυτεύεται.
segments.translation:
Erythrodanon [ἐρυθρόδανον, 'red-giving']: some call it rhizarion [ῥιζάριον], others galatión [γαλάτιον]; the Romans call it rubia [ῥουβέα]. It is a plant well known to dyers, who use it to dye wool. It grows in rough and wild places. It has a square, rough stem extending at great length, with leaves arranged at intervals in a whorl around it, resembling those of cleavers [galion] but broader. Its root is thin, long, and red. It is sown in gardens and also propagated from the root.

Therapeutic uses: The root [ῥίζα], when boiled in water and drunk, is beneficial for jaundice [ἴκτερος], for obstructions of the spleen [σπλήν], and for affections of the liver [ἧπαρ]. It provokes urine [οὐρητικόν] and moves the menses [ἐμμήνων ἀγωγός]. Taken in drink it is of service to those who have been bruised by falls or who spit blood [αἱμοπτυικοῖς]. It dissolves coagulated blood [αἷμα πεπηγός] within the body. Applied externally, it removes freckles [ἐφήλεις] and skin blemishes. The juice of the plant, boiled with honey, is said to clear the eyesight [ὀφθαλμοὺς διαυγάζει]. In some accounts, a decoction of the root taken in wine [ἐν οἴνῳ] is held to benefit those suffering from sciatic pain [ἰσχιαδικοί] and paralysis [παραλελυμένοι]. The root dried and powdered, mixed with vinegar [ὄξος], is applied to skin eruptions and leprous patches [λέπρα].

segments.title_inferred: Dyers Weld (Luteola / Reseda luteola)
segments.original_text: Λουτεόλη· οἱ δὲ ἐρυθρόδανον ἄγριον καλοῦσιν· φύλλα ἔχει ὅμοια ἀσπαλάθῳ ἢ ἰσάτιδι, μακρότερα δέ· βοτάνη ἐστὶ γνώριμος τοῖς βαφεῦσιν. ἡ ῥίζα αὐτῆς καταπλασσομένη παύει φλεγμονάς.
segments.translation: Luteola [Dyers Weld]: some call it wild madder [ἐρυθρόδανον ἄγριον]. It has leaves similar to those of aspálathos or woad [ἰσάτις], but longer. It is a plant well known to dyers [βαφεῦσιν]. Its root, applied as a poultice [καταπλασσομένη], relieves inflammations [φλεγμονάς].

segments.title_inferred: Woad (Isatis tinctoria) — Dioscorides IV.49
segments.original_text: Ἰσάτις ἡ μὲν ἀγρία, ἡ δὲ ἥμερος. ἡ μὲν ἥμερος βοτάνη ἐστὶ γνώριμος, ᾗ οἱ βαφεῖς χρῶνται· ἔχει δὲ φύλλα ὅμοια λαπάθῳ ἀγρίῳ, μακρότερα δέ, καὶ ἄνθη χρυσοειδῆ. ἡ δὲ ἀγρία μικρότερα μέν, ὅμοια δέ. τὰ δὲ φύλλα ἀμφοτέρων στυπτικά ἐστιν, ἁρμόζοντα πρὸς τὰ ἐπιφλεγμαίνοντα ἕλκη καὶ πρὸς τὰς ἐν αἰδοίοις φλεγμονάς· κόπτεται δὲ καὶ ἐπιπλάσσεται. ἁρμόζει δὲ καὶ πρὸς τὰ χρόνια ἕλκη καὶ πρὸς αἱμορροΐδας σφαγεντῶν τοῦ αἵματος· ὁ δὲ χυλὸς αὐτῆς πινόμενος ἐπέχει αἷμα ἀναγόμενον.
segments.translation: Woad exists in two forms: the cultivated [hēmeros] and the wild [agria]. The cultivated form is a well-known plant used by dyers [bapheis]; it has leaves similar to those of wild dock [lapathon agrion] but longer, and golden-yellow flowers. The wild form is smaller but otherwise similar. The leaves of both varieties are astringent [styptika] and are suited for use against inflamed ulcers [epiphlegmainonta helkē] and inflammations of the genitals [en aidoiois phlegmonai]; they are pounded and applied as a plaster [epiplassetai]. They are also suited for chronic ulcers [chronia helkē] and for hemorrhoids [haimorrhoides] when bleeding is present. The juice [chylos] of the plant, taken as a drink, stanches the spitting of blood [haima agomenon].

segments.title_inferred: Henbane (Hyoscyamos) — Anodyne and Sedative Applications
segments.original_text: Ὑοσκύαμος· οἱ δὲ ὑοσκύαμον, οἱ δὲ ἀπολλινάριον, οἱ δὲ διόσκυαμον καλοῦσιν. Τριῶν δέ εἰσιν εἰδῶν· ὁ μὲν λευκός, ὁ δὲ μέλας, ὁ δὲ μεταξύ. Κρείσσων δὲ ὁ λευκὸς πρὸς τὰς χρείας. Ἡ δὲ ῥίζα αὐτοῦ ἑψηθεῖσα ἐν οἴνῳ καὶ κρατουμένη ἐν τῷ στόματι ὀδονταλγίαν παύει. Τὰ δὲ φύλλα ἑψηθέντα ἐν οἴνῳ καὶ ἐπιτιθέμενα ποδαλγίαν καὶ τὰς περὶ τὰς ἄρθρωσεις ὀδύνας παύει. Ὁ δὲ χυλὸς αὐτοῦ ἐνσταχθεὶς εἰς ὦτα ὀδύνας κοιμίζει. Ὅλως δὲ ναρκωτικόν ἐστι τὸ φυτόν, καὶ ἐπὶ πλεῖον λαμβανόμενον βλάπτει.
segments.translation: Henbane [Hyoscyamos]: some call it hyoscyamon, others apollinarion, others dioscyamon. There are three varieties: the white, the black, and one intermediate between them. The white variety is superior for medicinal purposes. The root, when boiled in wine and held in the mouth, relieves toothache [odontalgía]. The leaves, boiled in wine and applied as a poultice, relieve pain in the feet [podalgía — gout or podagra] and pains about the joints [arthroseis]. The juice of the plant, dripped into the ears, calms ear pain. In general the plant is narcotic [narkōtikon] in nature, and taken in excess it is harmful.

segments.title_inferred: Theory of the Three Treasures: Jeong (Essence), Gi (Energy), and Shin (Spirit) with Plant-Based Tonics — Dongui Bogam Inner Canon, Volume 1
segments.original_text: 동의보감 내경편1 — 정기신론(精氣神論): 정(精 essence), 기(氣 energy), 신(神 spirit) — 한의학의 삼보(三寶)와 식물성 보익약(補益藥)
segments.translation:
Dongui Bogam, Inner Canon (內景篇), Volume 1 — Treatise on Jeong, Gi, and Shin (精氣神論): The Three Treasures of Korean Medicine and Their Plant-Based Tonics.

The Inner Canon opens with the foundational cosmological and physiological framework upon which all of Heo Jun's subsequent clinical theory rests. The Three Treasures — Jeong (精, Essence), Gi (氣, Energy/Vital Force), and Shin (神, Spirit/Mind) — are presented as the primordial constituents of human life, each interdependent and mutually generative.

**Jeong (精 — Essence)**
Jeong is described as the most refined, dense, and material of the three treasures. It is of two kinds: (1) Congenital Jeong (선천지정, 先天之精), inherited from one's parents at the moment of conception and stored in the Kidneys (신장, 腎臟); and (2) Acquired Jeong (후천지정, 後天之精), continuously replenished through the digestion of food and drink via the Spleen and Stomach (비위, 脾胃). Jeong is understood as the biological substrate of reproduction, growth, maturation, and ageing. Its depletion through overwork, excessive sexual activity, or prolonged illness is considered a root cause of constitutional weakness and chronic disease. Plant-based tonics prescribed to supplement Jeong include: Rehmannia root (지황, 地黃; Rehmannia glutinosa), Schisandra fruit (오미자, 五味子; Schisandra chinensis), Cuscuta seed (토사자, 菟絲子; Cuscuta chinensis), and Dioscorea rhizome (산약, 山藥; Dioscorea opposita). These herbs are noted for their sweet, warm, or neutral qualities and their affinity for the Kidney and Liver channels.

**Gi (氣 — Vital Energy/Force)**
Gi is the dynamic, animating force that circulates through the body's channels (경락, 經絡) and organs. It is simultaneously the agent of physiological function and the medium through which Jeong and Shin communicate. The text distinguishes several forms of Gi: Original Gi (원기, 元氣), derived from Congenital Jeong; Pectoral Gi (종기, 宗氣), formed in the chest from inhaled air and ingested nutrients; Nutritive Gi (영기, 營氣), which flows within the vessels nourishing the body; and Defensive Gi (위기, 衛氣), which circulates on the exterior protecting against pathogenic invasion. Depletion or stagnation of Gi manifests as fatigue, breathlessness, poor digestion, and susceptibility to illness. Key plant tonics for supplementing and regulating Gi include: Ginseng (인삼, 人蔘; Panax ginseng) — the pre-eminent Gi tonic in Korean medicine, elevated by Heo Jun above its status in Chinese texts reflecting distinctly Korean clinical emphasis — Astragalus root (황기, 黃芪; Astragalus membranaceus), Atractylodes rhizome (백출, 白朮; Atractylodes macrocephala), and Glycyrrhiza root (감초, 甘草; Glycyrrhiza uralensis). Heo Jun's preferential use of domestically sourced Korean ginseng (토산인삼) over Chinese imports represents a notable and documented divergence from contemporaneous Chinese TCM practice, reflecting both patriotic medical philosophy and the Joseon court's resource policies.

**Shin (神 — Spirit/Mind)**
Shin encompasses consciousness, cognition, emotional life, and the animating intelligence of the organism. It is said to reside primarily in the Heart (심장, 心臟), which in East Asian medicine governs mental and emotional function. Shin depends upon Jeong and Gi as its material basis — when Essence is abundant and Gi flows freely, the Spirit is clear, calm, and luminous; when they are depleted or disordered, the Spirit becomes disturbed, manifesting as anxiety, insomnia, forgetfulness, palpitations, or mental confusion. Plant-based remedies for nourishing and calming the Shin include: Ziziphus seed (산조인, 酸棗仁; Ziziphus jujuba var. spinosa), Polygala root (원지, 遠志; Polygala tenuifolia), Poria fungus (복령, 茯苓; Wolfiporia extensa), Platycladi seed (백자인, 柏子仁; Platycladus orientalis), and Acorus rhizome (석창포, 石菖蒲; Acorus gramineus). The last herb, Acorus gramineus, is notably preferred in Korean practice over the Chinese Acorus calamus, again illustrating Heo Jun's systematic substitution of locally available Korean materia medica.

**Theoretical Integration**
The three treasures are not merely parallel categories but form a hierarchical and cyclical unity: Jeong transforms into Gi, Gi animates and nourishes Shin, and a cultivated Shin — through meditation, regulated emotion, and virtuous conduct — conserves and replenishes Jeong. This transformative chain (정화기, 精化氣; 기화신, 氣化神) is the axial principle of Heo Jun's preventive medicine philosophy, strongly influenced by Neo-Confucian metaphysics (성리학, 性理學) prevalent in Joseon Korea. The therapeutic goal is not merely the treatment of disease but the cultivation of a balanced inner ecology. Compound formulas such as Yukmijihwang-tang (육미지황탕, 六味地黃湯) and Ssanghwa-tang (쌍화탕, 雙和湯) are referenced as representative tonics integrating herbs that address all three levels simultaneously.

segments.title_inferred: Dream Diagnosis and Spirit-Calming Medicines (安神藥): Acanthopanax, Jujube, Polygala, Amber, and Longan
segments.original_text: Dongui Bogam 내경편2 — 夢診 및 睡眠醫學: 安神藥 — 오가피, 대추, 원지, 호박, 용안육
segments.translation:
Dongui Bogam, Inner Landscape Section, Volume 2 — Dream Diagnosis and Sleep Medicine: Herbs that Calm the Spirit [安神藥 (An-sin-yak)]

This section of the Inner Landscape Chapter (內景篇) addresses the diagnosis of illness through dream patterns and the treatment of sleep disturbances through spirit-calming [安神] medicinal plants. In Korean medical theory following both Neijing and indigenous Korean adaptation, dreams [夢, mong] are understood as manifestations of disharmony in the organ systems: disordered dreams reflect imbalance in the Heart [心], Liver [肝], Gallbladder [膽], or Kidney [腎] systems, or invasion by pathogenic influences. The practitioner reads dream content as diagnostic data — for example, fearful dreams of water indicate Kidney deficiency; dreams of fire or excessive agitation indicate Heart-Fire excess; confused or incoherent dreaming may indicate Phlegm obscuring the Heart-orifice.

Heo Jun enumerates the following principal spirit-calming agents [安神藥]:

1. Acanthopanax [五加皮, ogapi / Acanthopanax senticosus or A. sessiliflorus]: Warms and strengthens the sinews and bones, tonifies Kidney and Liver qi, and steadies the spirit when deficiency underlies restlessness and disturbed sleep. Korean practice particularly favored ogapi as a tonic adaptogen distinct from Chinese reliance on ginseng alone, reflecting a notable divergence in Korean herbalism.

2. Jujube [大棗, daecchu / Ziziphus jujuba Mill.]: Nourishes Heart blood and calms the spirit; used in formulas for palpitations, anxiety, and insomnia arising from Blood deficiency. Also harmonizes other herbs in compound formulas.

3. Polygala [遠志, weonji / Polygala tenuifolia Willd.]: Opens the Heart orifices, expels Phlegm, calms the spirit, and strengthens the will [志]. Indicated for forgetfulness, fearful palpitations, insomnia, and excessive or disturbing dreams caused by Phlegm-Fire harassing the Heart.

4. Amber [琥珀, hobak / Succinum — fossilized resin]: Sedates and calms the spirit [鎭心安神], promotes urination, and invigorates blood. Used powdered and taken directly or stirred into decoctions for anxiety, fright palpitations, and convulsive or nightmare-laden sleep. Mineral/resinous in classification [金石部].

5. Longan aril [龍眼肉, yong-annyuk / Dimocarpus longan Lour.]: Nourishes Heart and Spleen, replenishes blood, and calms the spirit. Indicated for insomnia, excessive dreaming, palpitations, and poor memory due to Heart-Spleen dual deficiency — a pattern particularly emphasized in the Gui Pi Tang [歸脾湯] context referenced by Heo Jun.

Preparation methods vary by substance: plant materials are typically decocted [煎], with longan and jujube added late to preserve volatile constituents; polygala root is often processed with licorice water [甘草水炮製] to mitigate its acetic irritant quality; amber is never decocted but ground to fine powder [細末] and swallowed with warm water or rice wine; acanthopanax bark may be decocted or macerated in medicinal wine [藥酒].

segments.title_inferred: Voice and Speech Disorders: Treatments for Aphonia, Hoarseness, and Loss of Voice
segments.original_text: 동의보감 내경편3 — 음성언어병 (音聲言語病): 실음(失音), 음애(音啞), 성음불출(聲音不出) — 길경(桔梗), 감초(甘草), 행인(杏仁), 자소엽(紫蘇葉)
segments.translation:
Dongui Bogam, Inner Canon [Naegyeong-pyeon], Volume 3 — Disorders of the Voice and Speech [音聲言語病, ŭmsŏng ŏnŏ byŏng]: Encompassing aphonia [失音, silŭm, complete loss of voice], hoarseness [音啞, ŭma], and failure of the voice to emerge [聲音不出, sŏngŭm bulch'ul].

The voice is governed by the Lung [肺] and the throat [喉]. When wind-cold [風寒] invades the Lung, or when Lung-qi [肺氣] is depleted and fails to disperse, the throat becomes obstructed and the voice is lost or hoarsened. Treatment must open [宣] and diffuse [散] the Lung, moisten [潤] the throat, and restore the flow of qi.

Key medicinals employed:

1. Bellflower root [桔梗, killgyŏng; Platycodon grandiflorus]: Enters the Lung meridian; opens and diffuses Lung-qi; directs other medicinals upward to the throat; resolves phlegm and relieves sore throat. Principal herb for throat and voice disorders.

2. Licorice root [甘草, kamch'o; Glycyrrhiza uralensis]: Moistens and soothes the throat; harmonises the formula; moderates the actions of the other herbs; clears heat and relieves toxicity in the throat.

3. Apricot kernel [杏仁, haengin; Prunus armeniaca var. ansu, kernel]: Descends and diffuses Lung-qi; stops cough and wheezing; moistens the Lung; used where voice loss is accompanied by cough or Lung dryness.

4. Perilla leaf [紫蘇葉, chasoye'p; Perilla frutescens var. crispa]: Disperses wind-cold from the exterior; promotes the flow of Lung-qi; relieves chest oppression; appropriate when loss of voice is accompanied by exterior cold patterns.

Representative formula: A combination of bellflower root and licorice [桔梗甘草湯, Killgyŏng-kamch'o-tang — Platycodon and Licorice Decoction] serves as the foundational preparation. Apricot kernel and perilla leaf are added according to the presenting pattern. Preparation involves decocting the dried herbs in water and administering the warm liquid, sometimes with the addition of fresh ginger [生薑] to further disperse cold and protect the Stomach.

segments.title_inferred: Nose and Smell Disorders: Nasal Polyps, Sinusitis, and Loss of Smell
segments.original_text: 동의보감 내경편5 — 코와 냄새 장애: 비식, 비연, 후각 상실 — 신이(辛夷), 창이자(蒼耳子), 황련(黃連)
segments.translation: Dongui Bogam, Inner Classics Volume 5 — Disorders of the Nose and Sense of Smell: Nasal Polyps [비식, 鼻息], Sinusitis / Nasal Congestion with Discharge [비연, 鼻淵], and Loss of Smell [후각 상실, 不聞香臭]. The nose is the orifice [규, 竅] of the Lung; when Lung qi is unobstructed, the nose can distinguish fragrant from foul odors. When pathogenic wind-cold or wind-heat invades the Lung channel, or when accumulated heat stagnates in the Gallbladder, fluids congeal and descend, resulting in turbid nasal discharge, polyp formation, or complete loss of olfactory function. Treatment centers on dispersing wind, opening the nasal orifice [通竅], clearing heat, and transforming phlegm-dampness. Principal medicinals include: Magnolia Flower [신이, 辛夷, Magnolia biondii / M. denudata / M. sprengeri pampan.], which enters the Lung and Stomach channels to dispel wind-cold and open the nasal orifice and is indispensable for all nasal obstructions; Xanthium Fruit [창이자, 蒼耳子, Xanthium strumarium L.], which disperses wind-dampness, opens the orifices, and alleviates pain, and is frequently paired with Magnolia Flower in classical nasal formulas; and Coptis Rhizome [황련, 黃連, Coptis chinensis Franch.], which clears Fire and drains Dampness-Heat, addressing cases where Gallbladder or Stomach heat drives turbid fluids upward into the nasal cavity. Additional herbs in related formulas of this section may include Angelica dahurica root [백지, 白芷] to expel wind and dry dampness, Mentha [박하, 薄荷] to vent heat and clear the head, and Asarum [세신, 細辛] to penetrate and warm nasal passages. Preparation methods described include decoction [탕, 湯], powdered medicinal [산, 散] blown or inhaled directly into the nostrils, and medicated sesame oil preparations [고, 膏] applied topically to nasal mucosa. Korean clinical usage in the Dongui Bogam preserves the Chinese classical framework but places notable emphasis on topical intranasal application of powdered Magnolia Flower and Xanthium as a distinctly practical Korean modification, reducing systemic heat-clearing herbs in patients with underlying cold-deficiency constitutions.

segments.title_inferred: Mouth and Tongue Diseases: Oral Ulcers, Bitter Taste, and Tongue Swelling
segments.original_text: 동의보감 내경편6 — 口舌病 : 口瘡, 口苦, 舌腫 — 황련, 황금, 치자, 감초
segments.translation:
Dongui Bogam, Inner Classics Section 6 — Diseases of the Mouth and Tongue

[Oral Ulcers (口瘡, Gucherang / Kǒu Chuāng)]
Oral ulcers arise when pathogenic heat [熱邪, yeolsa] accumulates in the Heart and Spleen meridians. The mouth is governed by the Spleen, and the tongue belongs to the Heart; thus when ministerial fire [相火, sangwa] flares upward, sores and erosions appear on the mucous membranes of the mouth and tongue. Treatment must clear heat and drain fire [淸熱瀉火].

Primary formula: Modified Hwangnyon Haedok-tang (黃連解毒湯加減)
— Hwangnyon (黃連, Coptis rhizome): bitter and cold; drains Heart fire, resolves toxin
— Hwanggeum (黃芩, Scutellaria root): clears heat from the upper burner [上焦] and Lung
— Chija (梔子, Gardenia fruit): drains Triple Burner heat, guides fire downward, cools Blood
— Gamcho (甘草, Licorice root, raw): harmonises the formula, resolves toxin, soothes mucosal surfaces

Preparation: Decoct the four herbs together in two cups [二盞] of water over moderate heat until reduced to one cup [一盞]. Strain and take warm, in two divided doses per day. Alternatively, grind to fine powder and apply topically to ulcerated surfaces for direct local effect.

[Bitter Taste in the Mouth (口苦, Gugo / Kǒu Kǔ)]
Bitter taste indicates Gallbladder heat or Heart fire ascending to the mouth. Hwanggeun (黃芩) and Chija (梔子) are principal herbs; a small amount of raw Gamcho harmonises. If Gallbladder pathology is prominent, add Yondam (龍膽, Gentiana root) to drain Gallbladder fire decisively.

[Tongue Swelling (舌腫, Seoljong / Shé Zhǒng)]
Swelling of the tongue results from Heart fire or Blood heat steaming upward. In severe cases the tongue may fill the mouth and obstruct breathing — a medical emergency. Apply powdered Hwangnyon (黃連) and Pungnyong (硼砂, borax) directly to the swollen tongue. Internally, administer a strong decoction of Hwangnyon, Hwanggeum, and raw Gamcho to clear Heart fire and resolve toxin.

Korean clinical note: Heo Jun emphasises topical powder application directly to oral lesions as a distinctive Korean practice, complementing internal decoctions — a dual-route approach less systematically codified in contemporary Chinese formularies. He also stresses that chronic recurrent oral ulcers (口瘡反復) often indicate underlying Kidney yin deficiency with vacuous fire [虛火] rather than excess fire, requiring modification of the formula with yin-nourishing herbs such as Sookjihwang (熟地黃, prepared Rehmannia).

segments.title_inferred: Teeth and Gum Diseases: Toothache, Loose Teeth, and Bleeding Gums — Oak Gall, Salt, and Prickly Ash Preparations
segments.original_text: 동의보감 내경편7 — 치아 및 잇몸 질환: 치통, 치아동요, 잇몸출혈 — 오배자, 소금 제제, 초피나무
segments.translation:
Dongui Bogam, Inner Landscape Section (內景篇), Volume 7 — Diseases of the Teeth and Gums

[On Toothache (齒痛, chipʻong)]
Toothache arises from Wind-Cold invading the channels of the teeth, or from accumulated Heat in the Stomach channel, or from deficiency of the Kidney. The teeth are the surplus of the bones [齒爲骨之餘], and the bones are governed by the Kidney; therefore, when Kidney essence is depleted, the teeth become loose and painful. Wind-Heat toothache presents with swelling of the gums and pus; Cold toothache presents with sharp, stabbing pain aggravated by cold water; Kidney-deficiency toothache is a dull, persistent aching with loose teeth.

[Korean Oak Gall (五倍子, obaeija) Preparations]
Oak gall [五倍子, Rhus-gall, formed on Rhus javanica by the aphid Melaphis chinensis] is astringent and cold in nature. It consolidates the teeth, stops bleeding of the gums, eliminates Wind, and disperses Heat. Grind oak gall to a fine powder and apply directly to the affected tooth and gum, or mix with a small amount of salt [食鹽] and rub onto the gums daily. This halts bleeding, tightens the gums, and relieves pain. Alternatively, decoct oak gall and use the cooled liquid as a mouth rinse [漱口] to treat swollen and bleeding gums.

[Salt Preparations (鹽製, yomje)]
Salt [食鹽, common salt] enters the Kidney channel and strengthens the teeth. Fine salt rubbed daily on the teeth and gums firms the teeth [固齒], prevents loosening, and removes foul breath. Calcined salt [燒鹽, salt roasted until it turns white-grey] ground to powder and applied to the gums is particularly effective for bleeding gums and for teeth loosened by Kidney deficiency. A paste made from calcined salt combined with powdered Korean oak gall [五倍子] and a small measure of alum [白礬] may be packed around a loose tooth to consolidate the root and stop bleeding. This is a distinctively Korean adaptation [Korean practice note: the combined salt–oak gall–alum paste formula was favoured in Joseon clinical practice and is less prominent in Song or Ming Chinese formularies].

[Prickly Ash (川椒 / 초피, chʻopi) Preparations]
Prickly ash [川椒, Zanthoxylum piperitum or Z. schinifolium in Korean usage] is acrid and warm. It kills worms [蟲], disperses Cold, and stops pain. For toothache due to Wind-Cold or worm-decay [蟲蝕, dental caries conceptualised as worm infestation], chew a small amount of prickly ash berry and hold the juice against the affected tooth. Alternatively, simmer prickly ash in vinegar [苦酒] and use the warm decoction as a prolonged mouth rinse; spit out and do not swallow. This warms the channels, expels Wind and Cold, kills the tooth-worm, and relieves acute pain rapidly.

[On Loose Teeth (齒搖, chiyo)]
Loose teeth result from depletion of Kidney essence and weakness of Stomach Qi failing to nourish the gums. Use the salt-rub method daily; internally supplement the Kidney with Six-Ingredient Rehmannia Pill [六味地黃丸]. If accompanied by gum recession and bleeding, combine with oak gall powder applied topically.

[On Bleeding Gums (齒衄, chinik)]
Bleeding of the gums is caused by upward flaming of Stomach Fire or by Kidney-deficiency Fire. To clear Stomach Fire, rinse with a cold decoction of oak gall. To address Kidney deficiency, use calcined salt rubbed on the gums and take Eight-Ingredient Kidney Qi Pill [八味地黃丸] internally. If bleeding is profuse, apply alum powder [白礬末] directly to the bleeding site to arrest haemorrhage by its astringent action.

[General Dental Hygiene Instructions]
Heo Jun emphasises that teeth should be cleaned each morning with a willow twig [楊枝] or a cloth wrapped around the finger and dipped in fine salt. After eating, rinse the mouth with warm water. Avoid very hot, very cold, and sweet foods, which damage the teeth. Biting on hard objects injures the enamel [傷齒骨].

segments.title_inferred: Throat Diseases: Sore Throat, Tonsillitis, and Throat Abscess — Treatments Using Balloon Flower, Licorice, Mint, and Indigo
segments.original_text: 동의보감 내경편8 — 인후병: 인후통, 편도선염, 인후농양 — 길경, 감초, 박하, 청대
segments.translation:
Dongui Bogam, Inner Chapters (內景篇), Volume 8 — Diseases of the Throat [咽喉病]: Sore Throat [咽喉痛], Tonsillitis [扁桃腺炎], and Throat Abscess [咽喉膿瘍]. Key medicinal herbs employed in treatment: Balloon Flower Root [桔梗, Platycodon grandiflorus], Licorice Root [甘草, Glycyrrhiza uralensis], Mint [薄荷, Mentha haplocalyx / Mentha arvensis], and Indigo Naturalis [靑黛, derived from Indigofera tinctoria and related species].

Throat diseases are understood in Korean medicine as arising primarily from excess Heat [熱] and Fire [火] accumulating in the Lung [肺] and Stomach [胃] meridians, or from Wind-Heat [風熱] invading the exterior. The throat is the gateway of breath and swallowing, and obstruction thereof is considered a medical urgency.

For sore throat [咽喉痛] with redness, swelling, and pain, a decoction [湯劑] combining Balloon Flower Root [桔梗] and Licorice Root [甘草] — forming the classical Platycodi et Glycyrrhizae Decoction [桔梗甘草湯, Gilgyeong Gamcho-tang] — is prescribed to disperse Lung Qi [肺氣], expel phlegm [祛痰], and relieve pain.

For tonsillitis [扁桃腺炎] presenting with acute swelling and inflammation, Mint [薄荷] is added to dispel Wind-Heat [疏風淸熱], cool the throat, and relieve swelling. Mint's acrid and cooling properties [辛涼] direct the formula toward the upper burner [上焦] and the throat region specifically.

For throat abscess [咽喉膿瘍] — a more severe condition involving suppuration — Indigo Naturalis [靑黛] is employed for its strong Heat-clearing [淸熱] and toxin-resolving [解毒] properties, as well as its ability to cool the Blood [涼血] and reduce swelling. Indigo may be applied topically as a powder blown into the throat [吹喉法, chwihu-beop], or incorporated into an internal decoction.

Preparation methods: Standard water decoction [水煎服] is the primary mode of internal administration — herbs are simmered in water and the strained liquid consumed warm in divided doses. For topical application to the throat, finely powdered herbs (particularly Indigo and Licorice) are insufflated directly onto the affected mucosa using a thin bamboo or paper tube [吹藥法]. Gargling preparations [含嗽方] using cooled decoctions are also noted as adjunct treatments.

Korean clinical practice in this period emphasised the combination of internal and external treatments for throat conditions, and the use of insufflation [吹喉法] is a distinguishing feature of Korean and Chinese classical throat medicine that has largely been replaced in modern practice.

segments.title_inferred: Du Huo Ji Sheng Wan Adaptation for Back Pain and Kidney Deficiency
segments.original_text: 獨活寄生丸 治腎虛腰痛 獨活 桑寄生 杜仲 牛膝 細辛 秦艽 茯苓 肉桂心 防風 川芎 人參 甘草 當歸 芍藥 乾地黃 各等分 右爲末 煉蜜丸 梧子大 每服三十丸 溫酒或鹽湯下
segments.translation: Du Huo Ji Sheng Wan [Pubescent Angelica and Loranthus Pill]: Treats kidney deficiency causing lower back pain [신허요통, 腎虛腰痛]. Ingredients in equal parts: Pubescent angelica root [獨活, du huo], loranthus/mistletoe herb [桑寄生, sang ji sheng], eucommia bark [杜仲, du zhong], achyranthes root [牛膝, niu xi], Chinese wild ginger [細辛, xi xin], large-leaf gentian root [秦艽, qin jiao], poria fungus [茯苓, fu ling], cinnamon heartwood [肉桂心, rou gui xin], ledebouriella/siler root [防風, fang feng], Sichuan lovage rhizome [川芎, chuan xiong], ginseng root [人參, ren shen], licorice root [甘草, gan cao], Chinese angelica root [當歸, dang gui], peony root [芍藥, shao yao], dried rehmannia root [乾地黃, gan di huang]. Grind all ingredients into a fine powder. Form into pills with refined honey, the size of firmiana [phoenix tree] seeds [梧子大]. Take thirty pills per dose, swallowed with warm rice wine or salted water [溫酒或鹽湯下].

segments.title_inferred: Chest Pain and Palpitations: Korean Cardiac Medicine from the Dongui Bogam
segments.original_text: Dongui Bogam 내경편10 — 胸痛 및 心悸: 단삼(丹參), 계피(桂皮), 인삼(人蔘), 오미자(五味子) 등을 활용한 심장 관련 치료법
segments.translation:
Dongui Bogam, Inner Classics Section 10 — On Chest Pain [胸痛, hyungpong] and Palpitations [心悸, simgye]: Treatment approaches employing Korean cardiac medicine, including Salvia root [단삼, 丹參, Dansam], Cinnamon bark [계피, 桂皮, Gyepi], Ginseng [인삼, 人蔘, Insam], and Schisandra berry [오미자, 五味子, Omija].

Chest pain arises when the channels of the Heart [心, sim] become obstructed, or when Cold [寒, han] and Phlegm [痰, dam] invade the chest cavity [胸中, hyungjoong], impeding the free flow of Qi [氣, gi] and Blood [血, hyeol]. Palpitations [心悸] may arise from deficiency of Heart Blood [心血虛], fright [驚], or from Fire [火] agitating the Spirit [神, sin].

For chest pain with Blood Stasis [瘀血]: Salvia root [단삼, Salvia miltiorrhiza] moves the Blood, dispels stasis, and calms the Heart Spirit. It is said that 'a single Dansam is worth the same as the Four Substance Decoction [四物湯]' in its ability to nourish and invigorate the Blood.

Cinnamon bark [계피, Cinnamomum cassia] warms the Heart and channels, disperses Cold obstruction, and assists Yang Qi [陽氣] to penetrate the chest. Used where Cold constricts the chest vessels causing gripping pain.

Ginseng [인삼, Panax ginseng] greatly tonifies Original Qi [元氣], strengthens the Heart, and calms the Spirit. Indicated in palpitations with deficiency, shortness of breath, and spontaneous sweating.

Schisandra berry [오미자, Schisandra chinensis] astringes the Heart Qi, nourishes Heart Yin [心陰], and calms palpitations arising from Yin deficiency with floating Yang. Its five flavors act simultaneously upon the five viscera [五臟], with a special affinity for the Heart and Kidney.

Preparation and administration: These herbs are typically combined into decoctions [湯藥, tangnyak] boiled in water, or refined into pills [丸, hwan] with honey. Dansam may also be prepared as a wine-steeping [酒浸, juim] for enhanced Blood-moving action. Dosages are adjusted according to the patient's constitution [體質, chejil], the presence of Excess [實] or Deficiency [虛], and the predominance of Cold or Heat.

Korean divergence from Chinese TCM: Heo Jun emphasizes a characteristically Korean integrative approach, pairing the Chinese classical formulas with locally abundant Korean-grown Schisandra (오미자) and Ginseng (인삼), noting that Korean-cultivated specimens of these herbs possess superior potency [氣味가 더욱 두텁다, 'the Qi and flavor are thicker']. Heo Jun also stresses dietary and emotional regulation [調養] alongside herbal treatment as inseparable components of cardiac care, reflecting a Korean Confucian-medical synthesis less prominent in contemporaneous Chinese texts.

segments.title_inferred: Treatments for Abdominal Masses and Accumulations (Gu Zheng): Zedoary, Sparganium, Turtle Shell, and Earthworm
segments.original_text: Dongui Bogam 내경편11 — 腹中癥瘕積聚 치료: 莪朮, 三稜, 鱉甲, 地龍
segments.translation:
Inner Landscape Section 11 — On Abdominal Masses and Accumulations [gu zheng, 癥瘕積聚]: Treatments

For all fixed abdominal masses [癥, zheng] and movable accumulations [瘕, jia], as well as congealed accumulations [積, ji] and gathered qi masses [聚, ju], the following substances are indicated:

• Zedoary [莪朮, ezhu; Curcuma zedoaria]: Breaks blood stasis, disperses accumulations, and moves congealed qi. Used when masses are firm and accompanied by pain. Prepared by slicing the rhizome and decocting in water, or processing with vinegar [초제, 醋製] to enhance its blood-breaking action and direct the medicinal effect to the liver channel.

• Sparganium [三稜, sanleng; Sparganium stoloniferum]: Strongly breaks blood stasis and dissolves food accumulations. Often paired with zedoary as a complementary herb for dispersing hard, fixed abdominal masses. Frequently vinegar-processed in Korean practice to moderate its harshness and protect the stomach.

• Turtle shell [鱉甲, biejia; Amida sinensis / Pelodiscus sinensis]: Softens hardness and dissipates masses [軟堅散結]. Particularly indicated when masses are accompanied by yin deficiency, night sweats, or low-grade fever — signs that distinguish Korean clinical usage, which emphasized differentiating deficiency patterns before prescribing harsh dispersing herbs.

• Earthworm [地龍, dilong; Pheretima aspergillum or Lumbricus]: Unblocks the channels, dissolves phlegm accumulation, and dissipates masses. In Korean medical practice following Heo Jun, earthworm was also used to address heat lodged within the abdomen contributing to mass formation, a nuance less consistently emphasized in contemporary Chinese formularies.

General preparation notes: These four substances are commonly combined in decoction [탕, 湯] or ground into powder [산, 散] and taken with warm water or rice wine [청주, 淸酒] to aid circulation. Vinegar processing of zedoary and sparganium is a characteristic Korean modification that Heo Jun recommended to reduce toxicity and enhance focal action on static blood in the lower abdomen. Turtle shell should be dry-fried [초, 炒] or sand-roasted [사초, 沙炒] before use to render it friable and to reduce its cold nature.

segments.title_inferred: Edema and Fluid Retention: Water Metabolism Treatments Using Alisma, Polyporus, Poria, and Cinnamon Twig
segments.original_text: 동의보감 내경편12 — 부종 및 수분 정체: 수액 대사 치료 — 택사, 저령, 복령, 계지
segments.translation: Dongui Bogam, Inner Landscape Section [內景篇], Volume 12 — On Edema [浮腫] and Fluid Retention [水滯]: Treatments for the Regulation of Water Metabolism [水代謝]. The following formulas govern the movement and transformation of fluids within the body [水液]. Where water accumulates abnormally in the flesh and limbs, causing visible swelling, or where fluids stagnate in the chest and abdomen, the physician must differentiate between deficiency [虛] and excess [實] patterns, as well as the location and nature of the retained fluid. Key medicinals employed in this section include:택사 [澤瀉, Alisma orientale / water plantain rhizome], which drains dampness downward and promotes urination [利水滲濕], clearing heat from the kidney and bladder channels; 저령 [豬苓, Polyporus umbellatus / zhu ling fungus], which powerfully promotes urination and leaches out dampness without tonifying, making it especially suited to excess-pattern fluid accumulation; 복령 [茯苓, Poria cocos / poria fungus], which strengthens the spleen, calms the mind, and gently percolates dampness, suitable for both deficiency and excess presentations; and 계지 [桂枝, Cinnamomum cassia / cinnamon twig], which warms and opens the yang qi [陽氣], facilitating the movement of fluids through the bladder and Triple Burner [三焦] channel, and assisting the transformation [氣化] of retained water. These four substances frequently appear together in classical formulas for edema, such as the Five-Ingredient Powder with Poria [五苓散, Oryeong-san in Korean pronunciation], which combines all of the above with Atractylodes [白朮] to treat water retention with thirst, dysuria, and floating edema. In Korean clinical practice, particular emphasis is placed on strengthening the Middle Burner [中焦] and spleen function as the root [本] of water metabolism disorders, diverging somewhat from Song and Ming dynasty Chinese approaches that prioritize draining the lower orifices as the primary strategy. Preparations are typically administered as decoction [煎湯] or fine powder [末藥] dissolved in warm water, dosing adjusted according to the patient's constitution and the severity of swelling.

segments.title_inferred: Head and Hair Conditions: Alopecia, Premature Greying, and Scalp Disorders — Dongui Bogam Exterior Forms, Volume 1
segments.original_text: Dongui Bogam 외형편1 — Head and hair: alopecia, premature greying, scalp conditions — fo-ti, eclipta, privet fruit, sesame
segments.translation: Dongui Bogam, Exterior Forms [外形篇], Volume 1 — Diseases of the Head and Hair: This section addresses hair loss [脫髮, talppal], premature whitening of the hair [早白, jobaek], and various scalp disorders. The principal medicinal plants employed include: He Shou Wu [何首烏, 하수오; Fo-Ti, Polygonum multiflorum / Reynoutria multiflora], used to nourish the Liver and Kidney, replenish Essence [精, jeong] and Blood [血, hyeol], and reverse premature greying; Eclipta [旱蓮草, 한련초; Eclipta prostrata], which cools the Blood, nourishes Yin, and promotes hair growth; Privet Fruit [女貞子, 여정자; Ligustrum lucidum fruit], which tonifies the Liver and Kidney Yin and arrests hair whitening; and Sesame [胡麻 / 黑芝麻, 흑임자; Sesamum indicum, black variety], which nourishes the Blood, lubricates dryness, and darkens the hair. In Korean medical understanding, hair [髮, bal] is the 'surplus of the Blood' [血之餘] and simultaneously reflects the sufficiency of Kidney Essence [腎精]. Conditions such as alopecia and greying are therefore interpreted primarily as deficiency patterns of the Liver, Kidney, and Blood, diverging somewhat from purely external or Wind-toxin etiologies emphasized in some Chinese classical sources. Treatments characteristically combine internal tonification with topical applications, including medicated oils and washes prepared from the above herbs.

segments.title_inferred: Eye Diseases: Korean Treatments for Cataracts, Glaucoma, and Night Blindness Using Chrysanthemum, Lycium, and Abalone Shell
segments.original_text: 동의보감 외형편2 — 안병(眼病): 내장(內障), 녹내장(綠內障), 야맹증(夜盲症) — 국화(菊花), 구기자(枸杞子), 석결명(石決明)
segments.translation:
Dongui Bogam, Outer Form Section, Volume 2 — Eye Diseases [眼病, anbyeong]: Korean medical treatments for internal eye obstructions [內障, naejang; cataracts], green-water eye disease [綠內障, nok-naejang; glaucoma], and night blindness [夜盲症, yamyeongjeung].

Principal medicinal substances employed:

1. Chrysanthemum [菊花, gukhwa; Chrysanthemum morifolium Ramat.]: Classified as sweet and slightly bitter in flavour [甘微苦], cool in nature [性涼], and associated with the Liver and Lung channels [肝肺經]. Used to disperse Wind-Heat [散風熱], brighten the eyes [明目], and clear Liver-Fire [淸肝火]. Indicated for redness, swelling, and pain of the eyes; photophobia [羞明]; excessive lacrimation; and early-stage cataract clouding [初期內障]. Prepared as a decoction [煎湯] or formed into pills with honey [蜜丸]. Korean practice commonly incorporates wild mountain chrysanthemum [山菊花, san-gukhwa], considered superior in potency to cultivated varieties — a distinction less emphasised in mainland Chinese texts of the same period.

2. Lycium Berry [枸杞子, gugija; Lycium chinense Mill. / Lycium barbarum L.]: Classified as sweet in flavour, neutral-to-warm in nature [性平微溫], and associated with the Liver and Kidney channels [肝腎經]. Nourishes Liver-Blood [補肝血] and Kidney Essence [補腎精] to brighten the eyes [明目]. Indicated for dimness of vision [視物昏花], night blindness [夜盲], and chronic cataract [久病內障] arising from deficiency of Liver and Kidney. Prepared as a decoction, steeped in wine [藥酒], or combined with rehmannia [熟地黃] and chrysanthemum in pill form. The Dongui Bogam places particular clinical emphasis on lycium as a foundational tonic for age-related visual decline, reflecting Heo Jun's integration of Korean longevity medicine [養生術] into ophthalmological treatment — a broader scope than that typically accorded this herb in contemporaneous Chinese formularies.

3. Abalone Shell [石決明, seokgyeolmyeong; shell of Haliotis diversicolor Reeve or related Haliotis spp.]: Classified as salty in flavour [味鹹], cold in nature [性寒], and associated with the Liver channel [肝經]. Functions to calm the Liver and subdue Yang [平肝潛陽], clear Heat and brighten the eyes [淸熱明目], and remove superficial eye obstructions [去翳]. Indicated for red, painful, and swollen eyes; blurred vision and photophobia due to Liver-Yang Rising [肝陽上亢]; and glaucomatous pressure with headache and dizziness. Must be calcined [煅用, danyong] or decocted for a prolonged period [先煎, first-decocted] before other herbs are added. Raw abalone flesh [鮑魚肉] is additionally noted in Korean usage as a dietary supplement [食補] for eye-brightening — a therapeutically nutritional application [藥食同源] more explicitly developed in the Dongui Bogam than in Chinese source texts, reflecting indigenous Korean food-medicine integration.

Representative formulas referenced in this section include:
— Gukhwa Jihwang-hwan [菊花地黃丸]: Chrysanthemum, rehmannia [熟地黃], lycium berry, and Chinese yam [山藥], formed into honey pills; treats Liver-Kidney deficiency with failing eyesight and early cataract.
— Seokgyeolmyeong-san [石決明散]: Abalone shell (calcined), cassia seed [決明子], plantain seed [車前子], and gardenia fruit [梔子], decocted; treats Liver-Fire Rising with glaucomatous symptoms, red eyes, and headache.
— Yagan-hwan [夜間丸, Night Vision Pill]: Lycium berry, pig or sheep liver [猪肝/羊肝], and coptis [黃連], formed into pills; specifically formulated for night blindness [夜盲症], drawing on the classical organ-correspondence [以臟補臟] principle that consuming liver tissue tonifies the human Liver organ and its associated sense faculty, vision.

segments.title_inferred: Skin Diseases and Rashes: Urticaria, Eczema, and Psoriasis — Korean Approach Using Sophora, Dictamnus, and Kochia Seeds
segments.original_text: 동의보감 외형편3 — 피부병 및 발진: 두드러기, 습진, 건선 (한국적 접근법) — 고삼, 백선피, 지부자
segments.translation:
Dongui Bogam, Exterior Forms [外形篇], Volume 3 — On Skin Diseases and Rashes [皮膚病及發疹]: Urticaria [癮疹], Eczema [濕疹], and Psoriasis [乾癬]

This section addresses disorders of the skin surface [皮部], classified according to the principles of wind [風], dampness [濕], heat [熱], and blood dryness [血燥] as primary pathogenic factors. The Korean medical tradition [東醫] acknowledges significant overlap with Chinese precedents while introducing regionally sourced materia medica and modified preparation methods suited to the Korean constitution [體質] and climate.

[On Urticaria — 癮疹 (Eunjihn)]
Urticaria arises when wind and heat invade the interstices of the skin [腠理], causing the nutritive [營] and defensive [衛] qi to become disharmonized. The eruptions appear suddenly as raised wheals, red or pale, accompanied by intense itching. Treatment prioritizes dispersing wind [祛風], clearing heat [淸熱], and harmonizing the blood [和血].

Primary formula: Bitter Sophora Root Decoction [苦蔘湯, Gosam-tang]
— Sophora root [苦蔘, Gosam]: 8 pun — clears heat, dries dampness, expels wind, alleviates itching
— Dictamnus root bark [白鮮皮, Baekseonpi]: 6 pun — clears heat, resolves dampness, dispels wind-heat from the skin
— Kochia fruit [地膚子, Jibuja]: 5 pun — clears damp-heat, promotes urination, stops itching
— Cicada slough [蟬蛻, Seontae]: 4 pun — disperses wind-heat, alleviates skin eruptions
— Schizonepeta spike [荊芥穗, Hyeonggaesui]: 4 pun — releases the exterior, dispels wind
— Licorice root [甘草, Gamcho]: 3 pun — harmonizes all medicinals
Preparation: Decoct in two cups of water until reduced to one cup. Take warm, twice daily. For external use, a concentrated decoction of sophora root and kochia fruit may be applied as a wash to the affected areas.

Korean divergence: Chinese sources (notably the Bencao Gangmu [本草綱目]) favour the use of gypsum [石膏] and coptis [黃連] for heat-predominant urticaria; Korean practice as recorded here emphasises sophora and dictamnus as the primary anti-pruritic pair, reflecting local abundance of these herbs and the observed tendency in Korean patients toward a damp-heat pattern with relatively less interior fire.

[On Eczema — 濕疹 (Seupjin)]
Eczema is attributed to the accumulation of pathogenic dampness and heat in the skin, often compounded by spleen [脾] deficiency failing to transform fluids. Lesions are weeping, crusted, and intensely itchy. Chronic cases may show blood deficiency and wind-dryness [血虛風燥].

Primary formula: Damp-Clearing Skin Powder [淸濕皮膚散, Cheongseup Pibu-san]
— Sophora root [苦蔘, Gosam]: 1 don — principal herb for damp-heat skin disorders
— Kochia fruit [地膚子, Jibuja]: 8 pun — secondary anti-pruritic, diuretic to drain damp
— Cnidium fruit [蛇床子, Sabedja]: 7 pun — dries dampness, kills parasites [殺蟲], warms the kidney
— Phellodendron bark [黃柏, Hwangbaek]: 6 pun — clears heat, dries dampness
— Atractylodes rhizome [蒼朮, Changchul]: 5 pun — dries dampness, strengthens the spleen
— Poria [茯苓, Bokryeong]: 5 pun — leaches dampness, calms the spirit
— Angelica sinensis [當歸, Danggwi]: 5 pun — nourishes and invigorates the blood; addresses blood deficiency in chronic cases
— Licorice [甘草, Gamcho]: 3 pun
Preparation: Grind to a coarse powder; decoct one measure in water and take twice daily. Alternatively, the powder may be mixed with sesame oil [胡麻油] and applied topically to weeping lesions. For hot, weeping presentations, a cold wash with phellodendron and sophora decoction is recommended.

[On Psoriasis — 乾癬 (Geonseon) / White Scaling Lichen]
Psoriasis [乾癬, dry tinea/lichen] is characterised by dry, scaling, thickened plaques. The pathomechanism is blood dryness and heat generating internal wind [血燥生風], which deprives the skin of nourishment. Treatment must nourish the blood [養血], moisten dryness [潤燥], and expel wind [祛風].

Primary formula: Blood-Nourishing Wind-Expelling Decoction [養血祛風湯, Yanghyeol Geupung-tang]
— Prepared rehmannia [熟地黃, Sukjihwang]: 1 don — chief blood tonic
— Angelica sinensis [當歸, Danggwi]: 8 pun — nourishes and moves the blood
— White peony [白芍藥, Baekjakayak]: 7 pun — nourishes blood, softens the liver
— Dictamnus root bark [白鮮皮, Baekseonpi]: 6 pun — dispels wind-heat from the skin
— Sophora root [苦蔘, Gosam]: 5 pun — clears residual heat, alleviates itching
— Schizonepeta [荊芥, Hyeonggae]: 5 pun — disperses wind
— Sesame seed [胡麻仁, Homarin]: 5 pun — moistens dryness, nourishes the blood
— Licorice [甘草, Gamcho]: 3 pun
Preparation: Decoct in water; take warm twice daily. External application of a paste made from ground sesame seed and dictamnus bark in beeswax base is noted as a Korean folk supplement to internal treatment.

Korean divergence: Chinese classical texts (e.g., Waike Zhengzong [外科正宗]) classify dry lichen primarily as a parasitic or fungal disorder and favour arsenic-containing [砒霜] or mercury-containing external preparations. The Dongui Bogam explicitly cautions against toxic mineral externals in these conditions, preferring purely botanical approaches and emphasising constitutional blood nourishment — a philosophically distinct position reflecting the Korean emphasis on harmonising inner deficiency rather than aggressively attacking the external lesion.

segments.title_inferred: Traumatic Injury Medicine: San Qi, Dragon's Blood, and Catechu in Wound Treatment
segments.original_text: 동의보감 외형편4 — 창상 및 외상: 삼칠(三七), 혈갈(血竭), 아선약(兒茶)
segments.translation:
Dongui Bogam, Exterior Forms Section 4 — On Wounds and Traumatic Injuries [외상(外傷)]: Treatment employing San Qi [三七, Panax pseudoginseng], Dragon's Blood [血竭, Dracaena/Daemonorops resin], and Catechu [兒茶, Acacia catechu extract].

San Qi [三七]: Known also as Tian Qi [田七], this root is foremost among remedies for traumatic injury [타박상, 打撲傷]. It arrests hemorrhage [지혈(止血)], disperses blood stasis [화어(化瘀)], and alleviates pain [진통(鎭痛)]. For external wounds with bleeding, the powdered root is applied directly to the wound surface. For internal injuries with blood stasis and swelling, it is decocted or taken as powder in warm water or rice wine [약주(藥酒)]. Heo Jun notes that San Qi surpasses most hemostatic agents in its dual capacity to both stop bleeding and resolve already-stagnated blood — a quality rarely shared by a single medicinal. This reflects a characteristically Korean emphasis on combining hemostasis with stasis-resolution rather than privileging one action over the other, diverging from some Chinese formularies that separate these indications into distinct prescriptions.

Dragon's Blood [血竭, 혈갈]: The deep red resin derived from Daemonorops or Dracaena species. It is applied topically in powdered form to staunch bleeding from lacerations and abrasions, and promotes the healing of flesh [생기(生肌)]. Internally, small doses are incorporated into pill or powder formulas to break up blood stasis and relieve pain following falls, fractures, or contusions. It is considered especially effective when the skin is broken and blood has pooled beneath the surface.

Catechu [兒茶, 아선약]: The dried aqueous extract of Acacia catechu heartwood. Rich in tannins, it is employed primarily as an astringent and hemostatic agent for external wounds, ulcers, and suppurating sores. Applied as a powder or paste, it promotes tissue contraction, dries excessive moisture at wound sites, and prevents infection of open injuries. In combination formulas it is paired with San Qi or Dragon's Blood to address wounds that present with both active bleeding and early-stage putrefaction.

Combined Use: The three substances are frequently prescribed together in traumatic injury formulas. A standard combined powder [산제(散劑)] may direct: take equal parts San Qi root powder, Dragon's Blood resin powder, and Catechu extract powder; blend thoroughly; apply externally to wounds or take 1–2 pun [分] internally with warm rice wine following injury. This formulation addresses hemorrhage, blood stasis, pain, and wound infection simultaneously.

segments.title_inferred: Bone-Knitting Formulas for Fractures and Skeletal Injuries (외형편 5)
segments.original_text: 骨折及骨傷 治骨折骨傷方 杜仲 續斷 骨碎補 牛膝
segments.translation:
Fractures and Bone Injuries — Formulas for Treating Fractures and Skeletal Trauma

The following herbs are employed as principal agents in knitting broken bones and repairing skeletal injuries:

1. Eucommia [杜仲, Duzhong / 두충]: Tonifies the Liver and Kidney; strengthens sinews and bone. Used in fracture recovery to consolidate the skeletal matrix and support ligamentous repair. Typically administered as a decoction of the dried bark, often dry-fried (炒) to enhance astringent and tonifying properties.

2. Dipsacus [續斷, Xuduan / 속단, lit. 'joins the severed']: Named explicitly for its capacity to reunite broken tissue. Invigorates blood, reduces swelling, and promotes union of fractured bone. Prepared as a decoction or ground into powder for oral ingestion; may be combined with wine (酒) to enhance circulation to the injury site.

3. Drynaria [骨碎補, Gusuibu / 골쇄보, lit. 'mender of shattered bone']: The rhizome of Drynaria fortunei. Strongly associated in Korean and Chinese medicine with direct osseous repair. Promotes blood circulation in the periosteal region, alleviates pain, and consolidates fractured bone ends. Prepared by removing the golden hairs from the rhizome, then decocting or pulverizing; may be applied topically as a poultice in Korean practice.

4. Achyranthes [牛膝, Niuxi / 우슬, lit. 'ox-knee']: Guides medicinal action downward to the lower extremities; invigorates blood stasis, strengthens bone and sinew. Particularly indicated for fractures of the lower limbs, pelvis, and lumbar region. Decocted with wine or combined in pill form.

Korean Divergence Note: Heo Jun's formulation emphasis places Drynaria (골쇄보) and Dipsacus (속단) as co-primary agents of equal weight — a pairing more prominently featured in Korean practice than in contemporaneous Chinese formularies, which tended to subordinate Dipsacus to Eucommia. Additionally, topical application of Drynaria poultice directly to fracture sites reflects a distinctly Korean integrative approach combining internal and external [內外兼治] therapy.

segments.title_inferred: Anal and Rectal Conditions: Hemorrhoids and Rectal Prolapse — Sophora Flower, Sanguisorba, Cimicifuga
segments.original_text: 동의보감 외형편6 — 항문 및 직장 질환: 치질, 탈항 — 괴화, 지유, 승마
segments.translation:
Dongui Bogam, Exterior Forms [외형편], Volume 6 — Conditions of the Anus and Rectum: Hemorrhoids [치질, 痔疾] and Rectal Prolapse [탈항, 脫肛]

Key medicinal herbs discussed in this section include:

1. Sophora flower [괴화, 槐花] — the dried flower buds and flowers of Sophora japonica L. Used to cool the blood and stop bleeding, particularly for hemorrhoidal bleeding and blood in the stool. Typically prepared as a decoction or dry-roasted [초, 炒] to enhance astringent and hemostatic properties.

2. Sanguisorba [지유, 地楡] — the root of Sanguisorba officinalis L. Employed to cool blood-heat and staunch bleeding from the lower body, especially hemorrhoidal and rectal bleeding. Often combined with Sophora flower. May be prepared as a decoction or made into a powder [산, 散].

3. Cimicifuga [승마, 升麻] — the rhizome of Cimicifuga heracleifolia Kom. or related species. Used in the context of rectal prolapse to raise sunken [하함, 下陷] qi of the middle burner [중기, 中氣], thereby lifting the prolapsed rectum. Also possesses heat-clearing and detoxifying [청열해독, 清熱解毒] properties relevant to inflamed hemorrhoids. Typically prepared as a decoction, often paired with astragalus [황기, 黃芪] and ginseng [인삼, 人蔘] in formulas addressing qi deficiency prolapse.

Therapeutic Approach:
Hemorrhoids are categorized by their underlying pathomechanism — commonly damp-heat [습열, 濕熱] accumulating in the large intestine, or blood stasis [어혈, 瘀血] and qi deficiency failing to hold tissue in place. Treatment addresses both the root [본, 本] and branch [표, 標]: cooling blood and stopping bleeding for active hemorrhoidal bleeding, and tonifying and raising qi for rectal prolapse. External washes [훈세법, 薰洗法] using decoctions of these and related herbs are noted as a distinctively emphasized Korean clinical application, complementing internal formula use.

Note on Korean divergence from Chinese TCM:
The Dongui Bogam places particular emphasis on accessible local Korean plant substitutes and on combined internal-external treatment protocols (fumigation and washing of the anal region) for hemorrhoids and prolapse, reflecting the pragmatic adaptation of classical Chinese methods to Korean medical and botanical resources available at the time of compilation (1613 CE).

segments.title_inferred: Epidemic Diseases and Pestilence: Korean Protocols for Communicable Disease
segments.original_text: 동의보감 잡병편1 — 역병(疫病) 및 온역(瘟疫): 전염병에 관한 조선 의학 치료 원칙 — 황금(黃芩), 황련(黃連), 연교(連翹), 금은화(金銀花)
segments.translation:
Dongui Bogam, Japbyeong-pyeon (Miscellaneous Diseases Section), Volume 1 — Epidemic Diseases [yeokbyeong, 疫病] and Pestilential Fevers [on-yeok, 瘟疫]: Korean Medical Protocols for Communicable Disease — Principal herbs: Scutellaria root [Hwanggeum, 黃芩], Coptis rhizome [Hwangnyeon, 黃連], Forsythia fruit [Yeongyo, 連翹], Honeysuckle flower [Geumeunhwa, 金銀花].

This section of the Japbyeong-pyeon addresses epidemic and pestilential diseases [yeok, 疫] — illnesses understood to spread through a community as a result of aberrant seasonal qi [sigi, 時氣], toxic miasmic influences [yeokgi, 疫氣], or climatic irregularities. Such diseases were distinguished from ordinary cold-damage disorders [sanghan, 傷寒] by their tendency to affect many persons simultaneously regardless of individual constitution, and by the characteristic presentation of intense heat toxin [yeoldok, 熱毒] with rapid progression.

Therapeutic Approach:
