— Lymph node enlargement: Reported in some trials at high doses, mechanism unclear.
— Potential hepatotoxicity at very high doses in animal studies; not conclusively demonstrated in human clinical trials at therapeutic doses.
— Drug interactions: Potential additive effect with anticoagulants [warfarin, heparin] — andrographolide has demonstrated antiplatelet activity in vitro; caution advised. Possible potentiation of antihypertensive medications. May interfere with immunosuppressive therapy due to immunostimulatory properties.

CONTRAINDICATIONS:
— Pregnancy: Contraindicated. Andrographolide has demonstrated antifertility effects and abortifacient activity in animal studies (anti-implantation effects at high doses in rats); safety in human pregnancy not established.
— Lactation: Insufficient data; avoid use.
— Pediatric use: No well-controlled safety data in children under 12 years.
— Known hypersensitivity to Andrographis paniculata or plants of the Acanthaceae family.
— Use with caution in patients on anticoagulant therapy or with bleeding disorders.
— Autoimmune conditions: Theoretical concern with immunostimulatory activity in conditions such as systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE), rheumatoid arthritis, or multiple sclerosis.

DOSAGE — WHO RECOMMENDED (ADULTS):
— Crude dried herb: 1.5–3.0 g three times daily (total 4.5–9.0 g/day), as infusion or decoction.
— Powdered herb (encapsulated): 500 mg–2 g per dose, three times daily.
— Standardized dry extract (standardized to andrographolide content, typically 10–30%): 100–200 mg three times daily (providing approximately 30–60 mg andrographolide per dose), equivalent to approximately 1200 mg andrographolide per day in clinical trials with demonstrated efficacy for URTI.
— Liquid extract (1:1, 45% ethanol): 1–2 mL three times daily.
— Duration of use: Acute conditions (URTI, diarrhea) — up to 7–10 days. Prolonged use beyond 3 months not recommended without medical supervision due to insufficient long-term safety data.

segments.title_inferred: Jimsonweed Leaf (Toxic Plant Warning) — Folium Stramonii
segments.original_text:
WHO Monograph Plant: Folium Stramonii. Common name: Jimsonweed (toxic plant warning). Latin name: Datura stramonium. Source: WHO Monographs on Selected Medicinal Plants, Volume 2.

1. DEFINITION: Folium Stramonii consists of the dried leaf, or dried leaf and flowering top, of Datura stramonium L. (Solanaceae). The drug contains not less than 0.25% total alkaloids, calculated as hyoscyamine, per the European Pharmacopoeia standard.

2. BOTANICAL DESCRIPTION: Annual herb, 0.5–2.0 m tall. Stem erect, branched, glabrous to sparsely pubescent. Leaves alternate, petiolate, ovate, irregularly sinuate-dentate to shallowly lobed, 8–20 cm long; upper surface dark green, lower surface paler. Flowers solitary, axillary; corolla white to pale violet, funnel-shaped, 6–10 cm long. Fruit a spiny, ovoid capsule (the 'thorn apple'), 3–5 cm, dehiscing into 4 valves. Seeds numerous, reniform, dark brown to black.

3. GEOGRAPHICAL DISTRIBUTION: Native to Central America and the Caribbean; now a cosmopolitan weed distributed throughout temperate and subtropical regions worldwide, including Europe, Asia, Africa, North America, and Australia. Commonly found on disturbed ground, roadsides, waste places, and agricultural margins. Cultivated commercially in Eastern Europe and India for pharmaceutical alkaloid extraction.

4. CHEMICAL CONSTITUENTS: Total tropane alkaloids (minimum 0.25%, typically 0.2–0.45%): predominantly l-hyoscyamine (atropine precursor, the primary active alkaloid), with scopolamine [hyoscine] (0.06% in leaves), and trace amounts of apoatropine, meteloidine, and other minor alkaloids. Upon drying and processing, l-hyoscyamine racemises partially to dl-atropine. Also contains flavonoids (quercetin, kaempferol glycosides), withanolides (daturalactones), fatty acids, and essential oil components. Alkaloid content is highest in leaves collected at early flowering stage.

5. MEDICINAL USES — Clinical Evidence: (a) Bronchospasm and asthma: Anticholinergic bronchodilation via muscarinic receptor antagonism has been demonstrated in controlled trials for obstructive airway disease, though pharmaceutical-grade isolated atropine and ipratropium bromide have superseded crude stramonium preparations in clinical practice. Stramonium cigarettes were formerly a standard asthma treatment in European medicine. (b) Parkinson's disease (historical): Hyoscyamine and scopolamine were used pre-levodopa era for tremor reduction; clinical utility confirmed but now obsolete for this indication due to safer alternatives. (c) Motion sickness: Scopolamine component has documented efficacy in controlled trials (transdermal scopolamine patch trials, not crude Folium Stramonii per se).

6. MEDICINAL USES — Traditional: Listed in numerous historical pharmacopoeias including the British Pharmacopoeia, United States Pharmacopeia (USP), and European Pharmacopoeia. Traditional uses documented across cultures include: relief of asthma and bronchitis (smoked or as powder inhalation); treatment of Parkinson's disease tremor and rigidity; antispasmodic for gastrointestinal and urinary colic; topical application for pain relief (neuralgia, rheumatism); sedation and pre-anaesthetic medication. Used in Ayurvedic medicine (as Dhatura) for respiratory conditions, pain, and as a narcotic. Used in traditional European herbalism as an antispasmodic.

7. PHARMACOLOGY: Mechanism of action: Hyoscyamine and scopolamine are competitive antagonists at muscarinic acetylcholine receptors (mAChRs), blocking the parasympathetic nervous system. Key effects include: bronchodilation (M3 receptor blockade in airway smooth muscle); reduced secretions (salivary, bronchial, gastric); mydriasis and cycloplegia; tachycardia (M2 blockade); CNS effects ranging from sedation (scopolamine, low dose) to hallucination and delirium (high dose, atropine). In vitro studies confirm potent antimuscarinic activity. In vivo animal studies demonstrate bronchodilatory, antispasmodic, and CNS depressant/excitant effects dose-dependently. Analgesic effects partly attributed to CNS penetration of scopolamine.

8. CLINICAL EVIDENCE — Key Trials: (a) Stramonium Inhalation in Asthma (historical controlled studies, UK and Europe, mid-20th century): Patients with bronchial asthma showed significant improvement in peak expiratory flow rate following inhalation of standardised stramonium powder preparations compared to placebo; effect attributed to anticholinergic bronchodilation. Outcomes limited by non-standardised dosing and crude endpoints by modern standards. (b) Hyoscyamine/Atropine Comparative Studies: Multiple trials (post-1950) comparing purified hyoscyamine to placebo in gastrointestinal hypermotility demonstrated significant reduction in colonic spasm and pain (p<0.05); crude Folium Stramonii extracts showed comparable but less reproducible results. (c) Scopolamine in Motion Sickness (landmark trials, Brand et al.): Transdermal scopolamine (derived from Datura/Hyoscyamus alkaloid extraction) demonstrated 75–80% reduction in motion sickness incidence vs. placebo in double-blind crossover trials; directly applicable to the scopolamine constituent of Folium Stramonii.

9. ADVERSE REACTIONS: TOXIC PLANT — ALL PREPARATIONS CARRY SIGNIFICANT RISK. Common anticholinergic effects: dry mouth, urinary retention, constipation, blurred vision, photophobia, tachycardia, flushing, anhidrosis. CNS effects: confusion, disorientation, hallucinations (visual and auditory), agitation, delirium ('anticholinergic toxidrome'). Severe toxicity: hyperthermia, convulsions, coma, respiratory depression, cardiovascular collapse, death. Therapeutic index is extremely narrow. Toxicity frequently reported from accidental ingestion (especially children), recreational misuse, and adulteration of herbal products. Drug interactions: additive anticholinergic effects with antihistamines, tricyclic antidepressants, antipsychotics, other anticholinergics; may reduce absorption of orally administered drugs by slowing gastric motility.

10. CONTRAINDICATIONS: Absolute contraindications include: glaucoma (narrow-angle); prostatic hypertrophy with urinary retention; tachyarrhythmias; myasthenia gravis; megacolon; paralytic ileus; pyloric stenosis; pregnancy (teratogenic risk; hyoscyamine crosses placental barrier); lactation (alkaloids excreted in breast milk; risk to infant). Use in children is contraindicated except under strict specialist supervision. Not to be used by patients with hepatic or renal impairment without dose adjustment.

11. DOSAGE — WHO-Recommended Adult Dosage: WHO Monographs Vol. 2 notes that due to the very narrow therapeutic index and high toxicity risk, Folium Stramonii is not recommended for self-medication or over-the-counter use. Where pharmaceutical use is indicated (specialist supervision only): Standardised leaf powder: 50–100 mg per dose (corresponding to approximately 0.125–0.25 mg total alkaloids), maximum 3 times daily. Tincture (1:10 in 70% ethanol): 0.5–1.0 ml up to 3 times daily. Stramonium cigarettes (historical, now largely abandoned): 0.5 g dried leaf per cigarette, not more than 6 cigarettes daily. All dosing must be calculated against verified alkaloid content. Paediatric use: Not recommended.
segments.translation:
Folium Stramonii — the dried leaf (and sometimes flowering top) of Datura stramonium L. (Jimsonweed, thorn apple) — is a historically significant but profoundly toxic medicinal plant. Its primary active compounds are the tropane alkaloids l-hyoscyamine and scopolamine [hyoscine], which act by blocking muscarinic acetylcholine receptors throughout the body, thereby inhibiting the parasympathetic nervous system. This mechanism produces bronchodilation (relaxation of airway smooth muscle), reduced glandular secretions, relief of intestinal and urinary spasm, pupil dilation, and — at higher doses — powerful central nervous system effects including sedation, hallucination, and delirium.

Historically, preparations of Jimsonweed leaf were used across European, Ayurvedic, and indigenous American traditions to treat asthma (by smoke inhalation), gastrointestinal colic, Parkinson's disease tremor, and pain. These uses are pharmacologically coherent given the antimuscarinic activity of hyoscyamine and scopolamine. Clinical evidence supports the efficacy of these alkaloids for bronchospasm, gastrointestinal hypermotility, and motion sickness — but modern practice uses pharmaceutical-grade purified derivatives (atropine, ipratropium bromide, transdermal scopolamine) rather than crude plant preparations, which carry unpredictable alkaloid concentrations.

The therapeutic index of Folium Stramonii is extremely narrow: the gap between a clinically active dose and a toxic or lethal dose is small. Poisoning produces the classical 'anticholinergic toxidrome' — dry mouth, flushing, tachycardia, urinary retention, confusion, hallucinations, and hyperthermia ('hot as a hare, blind as a bat, dry as a bone, red as a beet, mad as a hatter'). Severe poisoning can cause convulsions, coma, and death. Accidental poisonings, particularly in children who ingest seeds or fruit, are well-documented and potentially fatal. Recreational misuse for hallucinogenic effects carries high mortality risk.

The WHO Monograph for Folium Stramonii reflects its status as a plant with genuine pharmacological utility — the alkaloids it contains are the basis of important modern drugs — but emphasises that crude preparations are NOT recommended for self-medication. Any clinical use requires specialist supervision, standardised alkaloid-content preparations, and strict adherence to dosage limits. It is absolutely contraindicated in glaucoma, urinary obstruction, tachyarrhythmia, myasthenia gravis, pregnancy, and lactation.
segments._label: WHO Monographs — Jimsonweed (toxic plant warning) (Folium Stramonii)

segments.title_inferred: Black Cohosh (Rhizoma Cimicifugae Racemosae)
segments.original_text: Rhizoma Cimicifugae Racemosae — the dried rhizome and roots of Cimicifuga racemosa (L.) Nutt. (Ranunculaceae). Principal chemical constituents include: triterpene glycosides (actein, 27-deoxyactein [cimiracemoside A], cimicifugoside; collectively 0.4–0.6% dry weight); phenolic acids (isoferulic acid, fukinolic acid); flavonoids (formononetin — presence disputed in modern analyses); resinous constituents (cimicifugin/macrotys resin); alkaloids (cytisine, N-methylcytisine — trace); tannins; and volatile oil. Source: WHO Monographs on Selected Medicinal Plants, Volume 2.
segments.translation:
**DEFINITION:** Rhizoma Cimicifugae Racemosae consists of the dried rhizome and attached roots of Cimicifuga racemosa (L.) Nutt. (family Ranunculaceae), harvested in autumn after seed set. The drug as used pharmaceutically includes both the horizontal rhizome and the numerous fibrous roots. The material corresponds to standards described in the United States Pharmacopeia and the German Commission E monograph.

**BOTANICAL DESCRIPTION:** Cimicifuga racemosa is a tall, perennial herbaceous plant reaching 1–2.5 metres in height. The leaves are large, alternate, two- to three-times pinnately compound, with toothed leaflets. The inflorescence is a long, wand-like raceme bearing numerous small white flowers with many stamens and a characteristic unpleasant odour (hence the common name 'bugbane,' from Latin cimex = bug, fugo = to drive away). The rhizome is dark brown to black externally (giving the name 'black cohosh'), knotted, and hard, with a bitter, acrid taste.

**GEOGRAPHICAL DISTRIBUTION:** Native to eastern North America, ranging from southern Ontario and the eastern United States west to Missouri and south to Georgia. It grows in open woodland, forest margins, and shaded slopes. Commercial supply derives primarily from wild-harvested plants in the Appalachian region (United States); limited cultivation is practised in Germany and North America. Overharvesting of wild populations has raised sustainability concerns.

**CHEMICAL CONSTITUENTS:** The pharmacologically significant constituents include: (1) Triterpene glycosides — the primary marker compounds, including actein, 27-deoxyactein (cimiracemoside A), and cimicifugoside, present at approximately 0.4–0.6% of dry rhizome weight; (2) Phenolic acids — isoferulic acid and fukinolic acid, which may contribute to anti-inflammatory activity; (3) Formononetin — a phyto-oestrogen (isoflavone) historically cited as a key oestrogenic constituent, but its presence has been disputed or found absent in multiple recent high-resolution analyses of authenticated plant material; (4) Resinous compounds — cimicifugin (macrotys resin); (5) Alkaloids — cytisine and N-methylcytisine in trace quantities; (6) Tannins and volatile oil in minor amounts.

**MEDICINAL USES (Clinical evidence):** The primary evidence-based indication is the management of climacteric [menopausal] symptoms, particularly hot flushes [vasomotor symptoms], night sweats, sleep disturbance, and psychological symptoms (irritability, anxiety, depressed mood) associated with the perimenopause and menopause. Multiple randomised controlled trials and systematic reviews support its efficacy for vasomotor symptom reduction relative to placebo, and several trials suggest equivalence or comparability to low-dose conjugated oestrogens or tibolone for mild-to-moderate menopausal symptoms. It is also used for premenstrual syndrome and dysmenorrhoea [painful menstruation], with supporting evidence from clinical use and historical pharmacopoeial recognition.

**MEDICINAL USES (Traditional):** Traditional use by Native American peoples (particularly the Algonquin, Iroquois, and Cherokee) includes treatment of rheumatic complaints, musculoskeletal pain, menstrual irregularities, labour facilitation, and as an emmenagogue [agent promoting menstrual flow]. European (primarily German) phytomedicine has employed standardised extracts since the 1950s for climacteric disorders. The German Commission E approved black cohosh for premenstrual discomfort, dysmenorrhoea, and neurovegetative symptoms of menopause.

**PHARMACOLOGY:** The mechanism of action remains partially elucidated and has been revised significantly. Early hypotheses centred on direct oestrogenic activity via oestrogen receptor binding, attributed to formononetin; however, controlled receptor-binding studies using authenticated Cimicifuga racemosa extracts have largely failed to demonstrate significant oestrogen receptor (ER-α or ER-β) agonism or to detect formononetin. Current mechanistic proposals include: (1) Selective oestrogen receptor modulation (SERM-like activity) at ER-β in specific tissues; (2) Serotonergic activity — in vitro and in vivo evidence suggests triterpene glycosides and phenolic constituents may interact with serotonin receptors (5-HT1A, 5-HT7), providing a plausible mechanism for vasomotor and mood effects independent of oestrogen pathways; (3) Dopaminergic modulation; (4) Inhibition of luteinising hormone (LH) surge — demonstrated in ovariectomised rat models, which may underlie vasomotor symptom relief; (5) Anti-inflammatory activity — isoferulic acid inhibits pro-inflammatory cytokine release in vitro. In vivo studies in rodent models confirm uterotrophic neutrality (no stimulation of uterine weight), supporting non-classical, non-uterotrophic activity distinct from conventional oestrogens.

**CLINICAL EVIDENCE (Selected Trials):**
— *Stoll (1987):* A randomised, double-blind trial (n=80) comparing a standardised isopropanolic extract of black cohosh (Remifemin®, 8 mg triterpene glycosides/day) versus conjugated oestrogens (0.625 mg/day) versus diazepam over 12 weeks in menopausal women. The Kupperman Menopausal Index and Hamilton Anxiety Scale both showed significant improvement in the black cohosh group, comparable to oestrogen and superior to diazepam for psychological symptoms. Vaginal cytology (superficial cell counts) improved in all active treatment groups.
— *Liske et al. (2002):* A randomised double-blind trial comparing two doses of isopropanolic black cohosh extract (39 mg vs. 127.3 mg/day) over 24 weeks (n=152 perimenopausal women). Both doses significantly reduced Kupperman Index and Self-Assessment Depression Scale scores, with no dose-response difference, suggesting a ceiling effect. Hormone levels (FSH, LH, prolactin, oestradiol) were not significantly altered, supporting a non-oestrogenic mechanism.
— *Osmers et al. (2005):* A multicentre, randomised, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial (n=304) using isopropanolic black cohosh extract (40 mg/day) over 12 weeks. Statistically significant reductions in the Menopause Rating Scale (MRS) total score versus placebo were demonstrated, with particular benefit for hot flush frequency and severity. Safety parameters including liver enzymes, hormone levels, and endometrial thickness were not significantly different from placebo.

**ADVERSE REACTIONS:** Generally well-tolerated at recommended doses. Reported adverse effects include: mild gastrointestinal complaints (nausea, gastric discomfort); headache; dizziness; and, rarely, skin reactions. Rare cases of hepatotoxicity [liver damage] have been reported post-market, prompting regulatory warnings in several countries; a causal relationship with black cohosh has not been conclusively established in most cases (many involve polyherbal products or confounding factors), but the association warrants clinical vigilance. No known significant pharmacokinetic drug interactions have been definitively established, though theoretical interactions with hormone therapies, tamoxifen, and hepatotoxic drugs are noted.

**CONTRAINDICATIONS:** (1) Known hypersensitivity to plants of the Ranunculaceae family; (2) Pregnancy — traditional use as an emmenagogue and uterine stimulant, and preliminary in vitro data, contraindicate use during pregnancy; (3) Lactation — safety not established; (4) Oestrogen-sensitive conditions (breast cancer, endometrial cancer, uterine fibroids) — formerly considered a relative contraindication due to presumed oestrogenic activity; current evidence does not support uterotrophic activity, but caution is advised pending further oncological safety data, particularly in breast cancer patients or survivors; (5) Hepatic disease — given post-marketing hepatotoxicity signals, use with caution and monitoring in patients with pre-existing liver conditions.

**DOSAGE (WHO-recommended adult dosage):** Oral administration. The daily dose is equivalent to 40 mg of herbal drug (dried rhizome), standardised preparations typically providing 1–2 mg triterpene glycosides calculated as 27-deoxyactein per dose. Proprietary standardised dry extracts (isopropanolic or ethanolic): 20–40 mg extract per day in divided doses. Tincture (1:10, 60% ethanol): 0.4–2 mL three times daily. Duration of use: clinical trials have used treatment periods of 6–24 weeks; the German Commission E recommends limiting continuous use to 6 months without medical review, though longer-term use (up to 12 months) has been reported in clinical studies without evidence of cumulative harm. Not recommended for children or adolescents.
segments._label: WHO Monographs — Black cohosh (Rhizoma Cimicifugae Racemosae)

segments.title_inferred: Myrrh (Gummi-resina Myrrha)
segments.original_text: Gummi-resina Myrrha — Commiphora molmol (Engl.) Engl. ex Tschirch (Burseraceae). Oleo-gum-resin obtained by incision or natural exudation from the stem and branches. Chemical constituents: volatile oil (2–8%, including furanosesquiterpenes: curzerene, furanoeudesma-1,3-diene, lindestrene, curzerenone); resin fraction (25–40%, including commiphoric acids, heerabomyrrhols, commiferin); gum fraction (30–60%, polysaccharides including arabinose, galactose, glucuronic acid, 4-O-methylglucuronic acid); sterols (beta-sitosterol, cholesterol); terpenoids (alpha- and beta-amyrin, lupeol). Total ash NMT 15.0%; alcohol-soluble extractive NLT 30.0%.
segments.translation:
Myrrh is the dried oleo-gum-resin harvested by incision or spontaneous exudation from the stem and branches of Commiphora molmol, a thorny shrub or small tree of the family Burseraceae. The raw material consists of irregular, reddish-brown to yellow-brown masses or tears with a characteristic balsamic, slightly bitter odour and taste.

**BOTANICAL DESCRIPTION:** Commiphora molmol is a small, heavily branched shrub or tree reaching 3–4 metres, with knotted branches terminating in sharp spines. The bark is papery and peeling, pale grey to yellowish. Leaves are trifoliate, small, and sparsely distributed; leaflets are obovate and glabrous. Flowers are small, reddish, borne in axillary panicles. Fruit is an ovoid drupe. Resin canals in the bark exude the characteristic myrrh oleo-gum-resin when wounded.

**GEOGRAPHICAL DISTRIBUTION:** Native to the horn of Africa (Ethiopia, Somalia, Djibouti, Eritrea) and the Arabian Peninsula (Yemen, Oman). Commercially harvested primarily in Somalia and Ethiopia. Not widely cultivated; supply depends predominantly on wild-harvested material.

**CHEMICAL CONSTITUENTS:**
- Volatile oil (2–8%): furanosesquiterpenes including curzerene, furanoeudesma-1,3-diene, lindestrene, and curzerenone are principal active components.
- Resin fraction (25–40%): commiphoric acids (alpha-, beta-, gamma-), heerabomyrrhols, commiferin; responsible for much of the antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory activity.
- Gum fraction (30–60%): complex acidic polysaccharides containing arabinose, galactose, glucuronic acid, and 4-O-methylglucuronic acid.
- Sterols: beta-sitosterol, cholesterol.
- Terpenoids: alpha- and beta-amyrin, lupeol.

**MEDICINAL USES — CLINICAL EVIDENCE:**
Myrrh preparations are most strongly supported by clinical evidence for topical use in oral and pharyngeal inflammation (stomatitis, gingivitis, pharyngitis) and for activity against the trematode parasite Schistosoma mansoni and Fasciola hepatica (fascioliasis). The oleo-gum-resin has documented wound-healing and mild analgesic properties relevant to oral mucosal disease.

**MEDICINAL USES — TRADITIONAL:**
Traditionally used across Arabian, African, Ayurvedic, and European herbal medicine for: (1) topical treatment of mouth ulcers, gingivitis, stomatitis, sore throat, and tonsillitis; (2) as an astringent and wound-healing agent for minor skin wounds; (3) internal use as a carminative and mild gastrointestinal stimulant; (4) treatment of amenorrhoea and dysmenorrhoea in Arabic traditional medicine; (5) as an inhalant for bronchitis and upper respiratory catarrh. Listed in the British Pharmacopoeia, European Pharmacopoeia, and United States Pharmacopeia–National Formulary (USP–NF).

**PHARMACOLOGY:**
- *Anti-inflammatory:* Furanosesquiterpenes, especially furanoeudesma-1,3-diene and curzerene, inhibit prostaglandin synthesis and display activity at opioid receptors in animal models, providing a mechanistic basis for analgesic and anti-inflammatory effects. In vitro studies demonstrate inhibition of leukotriene B4 and prostaglandin E2 production.
- *Antimicrobial:* Resin and volatile oil fractions exhibit broad-spectrum antimicrobial activity in vitro against Staphylococcus aureus, Streptococcus species, Escherichia coli, Candida albicans, and other oral pathogens. Minimum inhibitory concentrations (MICs) of 0.5–4.0 mg/mL have been reported for relevant oral bacteria.
- *Antiparasitic:* Mirazid (a myrrh-derived preparation, 300 mg capsules of Commiphora molmol oleoresin) has demonstrated antischistosomal and antifascioliasis activity in several Egyptian clinical trials. Proposed mechanism includes disruption of the tegument of the parasite and impairment of its glucose uptake.
- *Wound healing:* Aqueous and ethanolic extracts accelerate wound closure in rodent incision and excision wound models, attributed to stimulation of fibroblast proliferation and angiogenesis.
- *Antioxidant:* Demonstrated free-radical scavenging activity (DPPH assay) attributed to phenolic and terpenoid constituents.

**CLINICAL EVIDENCE:**
1. *Fascioliasis (Fasciola hepatica):* An open-label clinical trial (Massoud et al., 2001; n=48 patients with confirmed fascioliasis) treated participants with Mirazid (Commiphora molmol oleoresin, 300 mg orally twice daily for six days, fasting). Stool examination showed a cure rate of approximately 91.7% at three-month follow-up with significant reduction in egg counts and relief of symptoms (abdominal pain, fever). No serious adverse events were recorded. This represented a significant finding given the limited treatment options for fascioliasis at the time.
2. *Schistosomiasis (Schistosoma mansoni):* A clinical trial (Sheir et al., 2001; n=204 patients) using Mirazid (600 mg/day orally for three consecutive days) reported a cure rate of 91.7% for S. mansoni infection at 12 weeks post-treatment, with an egg reduction rate exceeding 95%. Compared favourably to praziquantel in this non-randomised cohort. Subsequent larger randomised studies produced more modest results (~40–60% cure rates), indicating that initial results may have been optimistic; efficacy remains under review.
3. *Oral mucositis / Stomatitis:* A controlled clinical study of a myrrh-containing mouthwash (standardised tincture, 5% in 70% ethanol, used as a rinse 3–4 times daily) in patients with chemotherapy-induced oral mucositis demonstrated statistically significant improvement in pain scores and mucosal healing rates compared to standard chlorhexidine rinse at two weeks (p<0.05). Confirms traditional use for oral inflammatory conditions.

**ADVERSE REACTIONS:**
- Topical: Occasional contact dermatitis; allergic reactions in sensitised individuals.
- Oral/systemic: At recommended doses, adverse effects are uncommon. Higher doses may cause diarrhoea, nausea, and abdominal cramps. Large doses may cause tachycardia and hypotension (observed in animal studies).
- Drug interactions: May potentiate the effects of anticoagulant drugs (warfarin) based on in vitro platelet aggregation inhibition data; caution advised. Potential additive hypoglycaemic effect with antidiabetic agents (in vitro data).

**CONTRAINDICATIONS:**
- Pregnancy: Contraindicated; myrrh has historically been used as an emmenagogue and abortifacient; animal data support uterine stimulant activity.
- Known hypersensitivity to myrrh or other members of the Burseraceae family.
- Inflammatory kidney disease (large internal doses may irritate renal tissue).

**DOSAGE (WHO-recommended, adult):**
- *Tincture* (1:5 in 90% ethanol): 2.5–5.0 mL, taken up to three times daily.
- *Topical mouthwash/gargle:* Tincture diluted 1:5–1:10 in water, used undiluted as a paint or diluted as a mouthwash for oral mucosal conditions, 3–4 times daily.
- *Powdered gum-resin:* 1–2 g per dose, taken two to three times daily (traditional oral use).
- *Mirazid capsules* (standardised oleoresin, antiparasitic use): 600 mg/day for three days, as used in clinical trials; to be used only under medical supervision.

segments.title_inferred: Lavender (Flos Lavandulae) — WHO Monograph Entry
segments.original_text: Flos Lavandulae. Lavandula angustifolia Mill. (syn. Lavandula officinalis Chaix; Lavandula vera DC.). Family: Lamiaceae. Dried flowers and flowering tops. Primary chemical constituents: essential oil (1–3% v/w), comprising linalool (20–45%), linalyl acetate (25–46%), 1,8-cineole, camphor, beta-ocimene, terpinen-4-ol, lavandulyl acetate, lavandulol. Flavonoids including luteolin, luteolin-7-glucoside, and ursolic acid. Tannins (5–10%), hydroxycinnamic acid derivatives (rosmarinic acid, chlorogenic acid), and coumarins (herniarin, umbelliferone). WHO Monographs on Selected Medicinal Plants, Volume 2 (2004).
segments.translation:
DEFINITION: Flos Lavandulae consists of the dried flowers and flowering tops of Lavandula angustifolia Mill. (Lamiaceae), harvested just before full bloom. The drug should contain not less than 13 ml/kg of essential oil on a dry basis, per European Pharmacopoeia standards. The essential oil (Aetheroleum Lavandulae) is obtained by steam distillation of the fresh flowering tops.

BOTANICAL DESCRIPTION: Lavandula angustifolia is an aromatic, evergreen, woody perennial shrub, typically 30–100 cm in height. Leaves are linear to lanceolate (2–6 cm), grey-green, entire, opposite, and densely hairy when young, giving a silvery appearance. Stems are quadrangular [four-angled, characteristic of Lamiaceae]. Flowers are small, tubular, bilabiate [two-lipped], violet-blue to purple, borne in interrupted whorls on slender, erect spikes (4–8 cm). Calyx is tubular, 13-nerved, greyish-purple. Bracts are rhomboidal [diamond-shaped], veined. The entire plant is strongly aromatic.

GEOGRAPHICAL DISTRIBUTION: Native to the western Mediterranean region, particularly the dry, rocky hillsides of France, Spain, and the northwestern Italian coast, at elevations of 600–1,500 m. Now extensively cultivated across Europe (France, Bulgaria, United Kingdom), North America, Australia, and Japan. France and Bulgaria represent the principal commercial sources of lavender oil. Naturalized widely beyond its native range.

CHEMICAL CONSTITUENTS: The pharmacologically active fraction is dominated by the essential oil (1–3% v/w in dried flowers), whose composition is defined by: linalyl acetate [the primary ester, 25–46%], linalool [the primary alcohol, 20–45%], and minor constituents including 1,8-cineole (<2.5%), camphor (<1.2%), beta-ocimene, terpinen-4-ol, lavandulyl acetate, and lavandulol. European Pharmacopoeia specifications require linalool ≥20% and linalyl acetate ≥25% for genuine drug identity. Additional constituents include flavonoids (luteolin, luteolin-7-O-glucoside), polyphenolic acids (rosmarinic acid, chlorogenic acid), tannins (5–10%), and coumarins (herniarin, umbelliferone, at trace levels).

MEDICINAL USES — CLINICAL EVIDENCE: The principal evidence-based indications are: (1) Anxiety and restlessness — supported by multiple randomised controlled trials [RCTs] and a systematic review demonstrating anxiolytic [anxiety-reducing] efficacy of oral lavender oil preparations and inhalation aromatherapy; (2) Sleep disturbance / insomnia — inhalation of lavender essential oil has demonstrated statistically significant improvements in sleep quality in controlled trials; (3) Agitation in dementia — aromatherapy with lavender has shown benefit in reducing agitated behaviour in patients with dementia in small RCTs.

MEDICINAL USES — TRADITIONAL: Lavender flowers have been used in European herbal medicine since at least the 1st century CE (Dioscorides). Traditional and pharmacopoeial uses include: nervous exhaustion and irritability; functional abdominal complaints (flatulence, bloating, mild spasmodic gastrointestinal discomfort); headache; minor wounds, burns, and insect bites (topical); and as a mild circulatory stimulant. Listed in the pharmacopoeias of France, Germany (Commission E), Austria, Spain, Portugal, and Switzerland, and in the British Herbal Pharmacopoeia.

PHARMACOLOGY: (a) CNS / anxiolytic effects: Linalool and linalyl acetate demonstrate inhibitory [calming] effects on the central nervous system [CNS] in animal models, including reduced locomotor activity and potentiation of pentobarbital-induced sleep. Proposed mechanisms include modulation of GABA-A receptor activity (similar in profile to benzodiazepines, but at distinct binding sites), inhibition of glutamate NMDA receptor activity, and suppression of voltage-gated calcium channels. In vitro data suggest linalool inhibits acetylcholinesterase [the enzyme that breaks down acetylcholine], which may contribute to sedative effects. (b) Antispasmodic [muscle-relaxing] activity: Essential oil and linalool reduce smooth muscle contraction in isolated guinea pig ileum and trachea preparations; the mechanism is attributed to calcium channel antagonism. (c) Antimicrobial activity: The essential oil shows broad-spectrum bacteriostatic [growth-inhibiting] and bactericidal [killing] activity in vitro against Staphylococcus aureus, Escherichia coli, and Candida albicans. Clinical significance of this effect has not been established in controlled trials. (d) Anti-inflammatory activity: Rosmarinic acid inhibits prostaglandin biosynthesis [the production of inflammatory signalling molecules] in vitro. Linalool reduces carrageenan-induced paw oedema [swelling] in rodent models.

CLINICAL EVIDENCE — KEY TRIALS:
1. Anxiety (oral lavender oil, 'Silexan' preparation, 80 mg/day): A double-blind RCT (Woelk & Schläfke, 2010; n=77) compared an oral lavender oil capsule formulation to lorazepam 0.5 mg in patients with generalised anxiety disorder [GAD]. After 6 weeks, both groups demonstrated equivalent, statistically significant reductions on the Hamilton Anxiety Rating Scale [HAM-A], with no sedation or dependence observed in the lavender group. Concluded non-inferiority to lorazepam for mild-to-moderate anxiety.
2. Sleep quality (aromatherapy inhalation): A controlled crossover study (Lewith et al., 2005; n=10 with insomnia) demonstrated that nocturnal inhalation of lavender essential oil significantly increased slow-wave [deep, restorative] sleep and reduced nighttime waking compared to placebo, particularly in women and younger participants.
3. Anxiety in pre-operative patients: A systematic review (Cho et al., 2013) of 5 RCTs using lavender aromatherapy in pre-operative settings found a statistically significant reduction in state anxiety scores compared to controls, though heterogeneity [variation] between trials was noted.

ADVERSE REACTIONS: Lavender is generally well-tolerated at recommended doses. Documented adverse effects include: contact dermatitis [skin allergy] with topical application (patch-test sensitisation reported, particularly with oxidised [degraded] essential oil containing linalool hydroperoxides); rare nausea or gastrointestinal discomfort with oral preparations; gynaecomastia [breast tissue enlargement in males] reported anecdotally in prepubertal boys following topical use of lavender-containing products, suggesting potential weak oestrogenic / anti-androgenic [hormone-modulating] activity in vitro — clinical significance unestablished. Phototoxicity has not been demonstrated for lavender (in contrast to citrus oils). No clinically significant drug interactions are documented; theoretical caution with CNS depressants [sedative medications] due to additive sedation.

CONTRAINDICATIONS: Hypersensitivity [allergy] to lavender or other Lamiaceae plants. Oral use is not recommended in pregnancy (due to uterotonic [uterus-stimulating] potential demonstrated in vitro at high doses) or lactation without medical supervision. Topical application to young children should be used with caution given the report of oestrogenic effects at unquantified doses.

DOSAGE — WHO RECOMMENDED (Adults): (1) Dried flowers / herb tea [infusion]: 1–2 teaspoons (approximately 1.5–2 g) per 150 ml boiling water, steeped 10–15 minutes, 1–3 times daily. (2) Lavender essential oil (aromatherapy / inhalation): 2–4 drops added to hot water or a diffuser; for topical use, diluted to 1–3% in a carrier oil. (3) Oral lavender oil capsules (standardised preparation): 80–160 mg daily of a standardised oral preparation (e.g., Silexan 80 mg), as studied in clinical trials. (4) Tincture (1:5 in 60% ethanol): 2–4 ml three times daily. (5) Topical: Lavender oil diluted in carrier oil (1–3%) applied to affected areas. Note: Undiluted [neat] essential oil should not be applied directly to mucous membranes or broken skin.

segments.title_inferred: Evening Primrose Oil
segments.original_text: Oleum Oenotherae Biennis (Evening Primrose Oil) — WHO Monographs on Selected Medicinal Plants, Volume 2. Pharmaceutical definition: The fixed oil obtained by cold expression or solvent extraction from the ripe seeds of Oenothera biennis L. (Onagraceae). Key chemical constituents: cis-linoleic acid [LA; 18:2n-6] (65–80% of total fatty acids), gamma-linolenic acid [GLA; 18:3n-6] (8–14% of total fatty acids), oleic acid (6–11%), palmitic acid (5–8%), stearic acid (1.5–3.5%), minor sterols including beta-sitosterol, campesterol, and stigmasterol. GLA content is the principal marker of pharmaceutical quality; minimum GLA specification typically set at 8% by gas chromatography.
segments.translation:
Evening primrose oil (EPO) is a fixed vegetable oil cold-pressed or solvent-extracted from the ripe seeds of the biennial herb Oenothera biennis. Its clinical importance rests almost entirely on its unusually high content of gamma-linolenic acid (GLA), an omega-6 polyunsaturated fatty acid that is rare in the human diet. GLA bypasses the rate-limiting enzyme delta-6-desaturase and is rapidly elongated in vivo to dihomo-gamma-linolenic acid (DGLA), a direct precursor of the series-1 prostaglandins (particularly PGE1) and a competitive substrate that partially suppresses the synthesis of pro-inflammatory series-2 prostaglandins and leukotrienes derived from arachidonic acid. This biochemical shift underpins the oil's anti-inflammatory and membrane-stabilising effects proposed across multiple conditions.

BOTANICAL DESCRIPTION: Oenothera biennis is a biennial herb in the family Onagraceae, producing a basal rosette of lanceolate leaves (10–30 cm) in its first year and an erect flowering stem (0.5–1.5 m) in its second. Flowers are bright yellow, four-petalled, 2.5–5 cm in diameter, opening in the evening — hence the common name. Fruit is an elongated capsule (2–4 cm) containing numerous small brown seeds; these seeds contain 14–25% fixed oil by weight, of which GLA constitutes 8–14%.

GEOGRAPHICAL DISTRIBUTION: Native to eastern and central North America; naturalised and widely cultivated in Europe (especially United Kingdom, Germany, Poland), China, and Australia as a commercial oil crop. Cultivated varieties have been selectively bred to maximise GLA yield.

CHEMICAL CONSTITUENTS (% of total fatty acids, typical commercial seed oil): cis-linoleic acid [LA] 65–80%; gamma-linolenic acid [GLA] 8–14%; oleic acid 6–11%; palmitic acid 5–8%; stearic acid 1.5–3.5%; trace sterols (beta-sitosterol, campesterol, stigmasterol). Vitamin E (tocopherols) present as natural antioxidants at approximately 50–100 mg/100 g oil.

MEDICINAL USES — CLINICAL EVIDENCE: (1) Atopic eczema / atopic dermatitis: The most extensively studied indication. Multiple randomised, double-blind, placebo-controlled trials and meta-analyses have evaluated EPO (providing 320–480 mg GLA/day in adults) for symptom relief in atopic dermatitis. A 1989 meta-analysis by Morse et al. (Br J Dermatol) pooling nine double-blind trials (n = 311) reported statistically significant improvements in overall symptom severity, pruritus, scaling, and erythema versus placebo. A later 2003 Cochrane-type systematic review (Bamford et al.) found the evidence mixed and noted heterogeneity across trials, urging cautious interpretation. The WHO monograph nonetheless recognises atopic eczema as a use supported by clinical data. (2) Mastalgia (cyclical breast pain): Randomised placebo-controlled trials demonstrated that EPO (3–4 g/day, providing ~270–360 mg GLA) produced statistically significant reductions in cyclical mastalgia score compared with placebo over 3–6 months, with a response rate of approximately 44–58% versus 19–25% placebo response. Studies by Gateley et al. (1992, Lancet) established this as a first-line non-hormonal option. (3) Rheumatoid arthritis: Several small RCTs (e.g., Brzeski et al., 1991; Belch et al., 1988) used EPO alone or combined with fish oil (providing 6–11 g EPO/day) and reported modest reductions in morning stiffness and NSAID requirements, though effects were not consistently maintained after discontinuation. (4) Premenstrual syndrome (PMS) and diabetic neuropathy: Preliminary positive RCT data exist but evidence is considered insufficient for definitive clinical recommendation per WHO monograph standards.

MEDICINAL USES — TRADITIONAL: In traditional North American indigenous medicine, various parts of O. biennis (roots, leaves, seeds) were used as food and as topical applications for bruising and skin irritations. European folk medicine adopted seed oil preparations in the 20th century following identification of GLA. Listed in the British Pharmacopoeia and British Herbal Compendium as a recognised therapeutic agent for atopic eczema.

PHARMACOLOGY — KEY MECHANISMS: (a) GLA → DGLA conversion: Following oral absorption, GLA is elongated by elongase enzymes to DGLA, which competes with arachidonic acid [AA] for cyclooxygenase and lipoxygenase enzymes, reducing production of pro-inflammatory PGE2 and LTB4. DGLA is preferentially converted to anti-inflammatory PGE1. (b) Membrane phospholipid incorporation: DGLA and GLA incorporate into cell membrane phospholipids, altering membrane fluidity and receptor function — relevant to lymphocyte and keratinocyte physiology in atopic disease. (c) In vitro studies: EPO and GLA suppress proliferation of stimulated T-lymphocytes and reduce IL-2 and TNF-alpha production in human peripheral blood mononuclear cells. (d) In vivo animal studies: Topical and systemic GLA reduced ear oedema in arachidonic acid-induced mouse ear inflammation models and reduced adjuvant-induced arthritis severity in rats.

ADVERSE REACTIONS: EPO is generally well tolerated at recommended doses. Reported adverse effects are mild and infrequent: nausea, loose stools, headache (incidence <3% in clinical trials). Rare reports of inflammation, thrombosis risk at very high doses (theoretical, based on LA pro-inflammatory potential at excessive intake). Clinically significant adverse events are not established in trials. DRUG INTERACTIONS: Theoretical interaction with anticoagulants (warfarin) and antiplatelet drugs due to PGE1-mediated platelet aggregation inhibition; clinical significance unestablished. Isolated case reports have suggested possible lowering of seizure threshold when EPO is co-administered with phenothiazine antipsychotics — clinical significance disputed but caution is advised.

CONTRAINDICATIONS: Known hypersensitivity to EPO or Onagraceae plants. Use with caution in patients with epilepsy or taking epileptogenic drugs (phenothiazines). Avoid in patients with bleeding disorders or those on anticoagulant therapy without medical supervision. Safety in pregnancy not firmly established; avoid in first trimester without medical advice.

DOSAGE — WHO-RECOMMENDED ADULT DOSES: Atopic eczema: 6–8 g EPO/day (providing approximately 480–640 mg GLA/day) orally in divided doses with food. Mastalgia: 3–4 g EPO/day (approximately 240–320 mg GLA/day) orally, taken throughout the menstrual cycle for a minimum of 3 months to assess response. Rheumatoid arthritis (supportive use): 6–11 g EPO/day in divided doses. Preparations are typically gelatin soft-gel capsules standardised to minimum 8% GLA content. Topical formulations (creams containing EPO) are also used for atopic dermatitis but systemic absorption and efficacy data are less robust.
segments.preparation: Primary form: Soft gelatin capsules containing cold-pressed or solvent-extracted seed oil, standardised to a minimum of 8% gamma-linolenic acid (GLA) by gas chromatography. Also available as bulk liquid oil. Topical creams and emollients containing EPO are used adjunctively in dermatological practice. Adult oral doses: (1) Atopic eczema — 6–8 g oil/day (divided doses, with meals), equating to 480–640 mg GLA/day. (2) Cyclical mastalgia — 3–4 g oil/day for a minimum 3-month treatment cycle. (3) Rheumatoid arthritis (adjunctive) — 6–11 g oil/day. Storage: protect from light and heat; refrigerate after opening to prevent oxidative rancidity.
segments.condition: Primary evidence-based indications: (1) Atopic eczema / atopic dermatitis — most extensively documented, supported by multiple RCTs and a pooled meta-analysis showing significant reduction in pruritus, scaling, and erythema. (2) Cyclical mastalgia (cyclical breast pain) — RCT-supported, recognised as first-line non-hormonal therapy in UK clinical guidelines. Secondary/exploratory indications with preliminary evidence: rheumatoid arthritis (adjunctive reduction of stiffness and NSAID use), premenstrual syndrome, diabetic neuropathy.
segments._label: WHO Monographs — Evening primrose (Oleum Oenotherae Biennis)

segments.title_inferred: Guaraná (Semen Guarana) — WHO Monograph Entry
segments.original_text: Semen Guarana — Paullinia cupana Kunth ex Humboldt, Bonpland & Kunth (Sapindaceae). Dried seeds of the cultivated plant. Major chemical constituents: methylxanthines (caffeine 3.0–7.5% dry weight, theobromine 0.0–0.3%, theophylline trace amounts); tannins [proanthocyanidins, catechins] 5–10%; saponins; starch. Secondary metabolites: terpenes, resin, small quantities of theobromine and theophylline. Guaranine historically synonymous with caffeine isolated from this seed. WHO Monographs on Selected Medicinal Plants, Volume 2.
segments.translation:
## DEFINITION
Semen Guarana consists of the dried, roasted or unroasted seeds of *Paullinia cupana* Kunth (family Sapindaceae), freed from the seed coat. The seed is the principal pharmacopoeial plant part. The pharmaceutical preparation typically involves drying and grinding the seeds into powder, paste ('guaraná bread'), or extracting into liquid forms.

## BOTANICAL DESCRIPTION
*Paullinia cupana* is a woody, climbing liana reaching 10–12 metres in length. Leaves are alternate, pinnately compound with 5 leaflets, ovate to elliptic, with serrated margins. Flowers are small, white to pale yellow, arranged in axillary panicles. Fruit is a pear-shaped, three-valved red capsule; upon dehiscence, it reveals a black seed partially covered by a white aril, giving the appearance of an eyeball — a feature central to Amazonian indigenous mythology. Seeds are approximately 1–1.5 cm in diameter, hard, and contain significant starch reserves alongside the xanthine alkaloids.

## GEOGRAPHICAL DISTRIBUTION
Native to the Amazon Basin, particularly the Maués-Açú river region of Amazonas State, Brazil, and adjacent regions of Venezuela. Cultivated extensively in Bahia and Amazonas states, Brazil, which supply the vast majority of global commercial guaraná. Brazil remains the world's primary producer and exporter. Naturalized or cultivated in parts of Colombia and Venezuela. International cultivation is limited; plantations outside South America are experimental.

## CHEMICAL CONSTITUENTS
- **Caffeine [guaranine]**: 3.0–7.5% of dry seed weight — the principal bioactive compound; substantially higher than coffee beans (~1–2%) or tea leaves (~2–3%).
- **Theobromine**: 0.0–0.3% — methylxanthine with mild stimulant and bronchodilatory activity.
- **Theophylline**: trace concentrations — methylxanthine with bronchodilatory properties.
- **Tannins [proanthocyanidins, catechins, epicatechins]**: 5–10% — contribute astringent properties and likely modulate caffeine absorption kinetics.
- **Saponins**: present in minor quantities.
- **Starch**: major structural carbohydrate component.
- **Resins and terpenes**: minor constituents.

Note: The high tannin content is pharmacologically significant — proanthocyanidins form complexes with caffeine, creating a slow-release matrix hypothesised to produce a more gradual and sustained stimulant effect compared to equivalent doses of pure caffeine, though robust clinical confirmation of this kinetic difference remains limited.

## MEDICINAL USES — CLINICAL EVIDENCE
Clinically supported indications (moderate to limited evidence):
1. **Cognitive performance and alertness**: Enhancement of attention, reaction time, and memory — supported by multiple randomised controlled trials (RCTs) in healthy adults.
2. **Physical fatigue and endurance**: Reduction of fatigue perception during exercise; used as an ergogenic aid.
3. **Weight management (adjunct)**: Modest thermogenic and appetite-modulating effects; studied primarily in combination products.

## MEDICINAL USES — TRADITIONAL
In Amazonian indigenous medicine (Maués-Sateré people and related groups), guaraná has been used for centuries as:
- A general tonic and stimulant to combat fatigue and increase endurance during hunting.
- A treatment for diarrhoea, fever, and headache.
- An aphrodisiac.
- A remedy for dysentery and intestinal complaints (attributed to tannin content).
Listed or recognised in the Brazilian pharmacopoeia and referenced in several South American national formularies. Used widely in Brazil as a popular beverage and food additive.

## PHARMACOLOGY
**Primary mechanism**: Caffeine acts as a competitive, non-selective antagonist at adenosine receptors (A1 and A2A subtypes), blocking the inhibitory neuromodulatory effects of endogenous adenosine. This produces CNS stimulation, increased dopamine signalling, elevated catecholamine release, enhanced alertness, and reduced perception of fatigue.

**Secondary mechanisms**:
- Inhibition of phosphodiesterase enzymes, increasing intracellular cyclic AMP concentrations (at higher doses).
- Positive inotropic and chronotropic cardiac effects.
- Mild diuretic effect via renal tubular mechanisms.
- Thermogenesis via sympathomimetic stimulation of brown adipose tissue and increased lipolysis (relevant to weight management claims).
- Tannin fraction: demonstrated in vitro antioxidant activity; inhibition of lipid peroxidation; possible modulation of gastrointestinal transit.

**In vitro studies**: Guaraná seed extracts have demonstrated antioxidant activity in DPPH radical scavenging assays. Antiplatelet aggregation activity has been demonstrated in vitro, suggesting potential cardiovascular effects.

**In vivo (animal) studies**: Rodent models confirm CNS stimulant activity; dose-dependent reduction of fatigue markers; anti-obesity effects observed in high-fat diet models with guaraná supplementation.

## CLINICAL EVIDENCE — KEY TRIALS

**Trial 1 — Cognitive Enhancement (Kennedy et al., 2004, Psychopharmacology):**
A randomised, double-blind, placebo-controlled crossover trial in healthy young adults (n=26) evaluated single doses of guaraná extract (37.5 mg, 75 mg, 150 mg, 300 mg) versus placebo. The 75 mg dose produced statistically significant improvements in secondary memory performance and speed of attention (p<0.05) on the Cognitive Drug Research battery. Interestingly, lower doses outperformed higher doses on several measures, an effect the authors attributed to possible tannin-mediated modulation rather than caffeine dose alone.

**Trial 2 — Fatigue Reduction in Cancer Patients (de Oliveira Campos et al., 2011, Journal of Alternative and Complementary Medicine):**
RCT (n=75) in patients with solid tumours undergoing chemotherapy. Guaraná extract (50 mg twice daily) versus placebo over 21 days. Guaraná group showed statistically significant reductions in fatigue as measured by the Chalder Fatigue Scale (p=0.004) and improved sleep quality and depression scores compared to placebo. No significant adverse events attributable to guaraná.

**Trial 3 — Weight Management (Boozer et al., 2001, International Journal of Obesity — combination product):**
RCT investigating a herbal combination of Ma huang (ephedra) and guaraná in overweight adults over 8 weeks. The guaraná/ephedra group lost significantly more weight (−4.0 kg vs −0.8 kg placebo, p<0.001) with improved lipid profiles. Note: results cannot be cleanly attributed to guaraná alone due to combination design; ephedra synergy is a significant confounder. This combination is no longer recommended due to cardiovascular risks of ephedra alkaloids.

## ADVERSE REACTIONS
- **Common**: Insomnia, nervousness, restlessness, tachycardia, palpitations, headache, gastrointestinal upset (nausea, gastric irritation) — all consistent with caffeine toxidrome.
- **At high doses**: Hypertension, tremor, diuresis, anxiety, cardiac arrhythmia.
- **Drug interactions**:
  - **Additive CNS/cardiovascular stimulation**: with other caffeine-containing products, ephedrine, pseudoephedrine, sympathomimetic agents.
  - **Anticoagulants [warfarin]**: Antiplatelet activity of tannin fraction may potentiate bleeding risk — use with caution.
  - **MAO inhibitors**: Risk of hypertensive crisis.
  - **Adenosine**: Pharmacodynamic antagonism; may reduce therapeutic efficacy of adenosine in cardiac arrhythmia treatment.
  - **Iron absorption**: High tannin content may reduce non-haem iron absorption — space administration from iron supplements.
  - **Fluoroquinolone antibiotics and cimetidine**: May inhibit caffeine metabolism (CYP1A2), increasing caffeine plasma levels.

## CONTRAINDICATIONS
- Known hypersensitivity to guaraná or any Sapindaceae species.
- Cardiovascular disease, including coronary artery disease, uncontrolled hypertension, arrhythmias.
- Anxiety disorders, panic disorder.
- Peptic ulcer disease (gastric irritant).
- Pregnancy: caffeine crosses the placenta; doses exceeding 200 mg/day caffeine total intake associated with fetal growth restriction — guaraná should be avoided or strictly limited.
- Lactation: caffeine excreted in breast milk; neonatal irritability and sleep disturbance reported.
- Children and adolescents: not recommended.
- Insomnia or sleep disorders.

## DOSAGE — WHO MONOGRAPH (Adult)
- **Powdered seed**: 1–2 g per dose, up to 3× daily (providing approximately 40–120 mg caffeine per dose depending on seed caffeine content).
- **Dry extract (standardised)**: Doses vary by standardisation; typically 200–800 mg/day of extract standardised to caffeine content.
- **Fluid extract (1:1)**: 1–2 mL, 1–3× daily.
- **Total daily caffeine from all sources** should generally not exceed 400 mg in healthy adults per WHO/international consensus guidance.
- Individual doses should be taken in the morning and early afternoon to avoid sleep interference.

segments.title_inferred: Valerian Root
segments.original_text: Radix Valerianae — Valeriana officinalis L. s.l. (Valerianaceae). Dried rhizome, roots, and stolons. Key chemical constituents: valepotriates (isovalerate esters, including isovaltrate and valtrate, 0.5–2.0% in fresh root, largely degraded on drying); sesquiterpene carboxylic acids of the volatile oil (valerenic acid, acetoxyvalerenic acid, hydroxyvalerenic acid, typically 0.3–0.8% in dried root); volatile oil (0.3–0.7%, containing bornyl isovalerate, isovaleric acid, camphene, β-pinene); flavonoids (linarin, hesperidin); free amino acids (γ-aminobutyric acid [GABA], glutamine, arginine); alkaloids (actinidine, chatinine, valerianine, valerine) in trace quantities; lignans; and caffeic acid derivatives.
segments.translation: Valerian root is derived from the dried underground parts (rhizome, roots, and stolons) of Valeriana officinalis L. s.l. and is principally used as a mild sedative and sleep-quality promoter in adults with restlessness, nervous tension, and sleep disturbances of non-organic origin. Its pharmacological activity is attributed to multiple converging mechanisms: valerenic acid and its derivatives inhibit the enzymatic breakdown of GABA in the central nervous system and may act as partial agonists or positive allosteric modulators at GABA-A receptors, thereby potentiating inhibitory neurotransmission; free GABA and glutamine in the extract may contribute directly to GABAergic tone; and the valepotriates (most relevant in fresh or carefully dried material) exhibit additional central depressant and spasmolytic activity. Clinical trials, including randomised double-blind placebo-controlled studies, have demonstrated statistically significant improvements in subjective sleep quality, sleep latency, and nocturnal waking frequency after repeated dosing, with effect sizes comparable to low-dose synthetic sedatives but with a more favourable short-term tolerability profile. Traditional uses recorded across European, North American, and Asian pharmacopoeias extend to anxiety, nervous headache, mild gastrointestinal spasms associated with nervous states, and as an adjunct in smoking cessation. Pharmacopeial monographs (European Pharmacopoeia, German Commission E, British Herbal Pharmacopoeia) recognise the dried root and root preparations as official medicines for these indications.
segments.preparation: ADULT ORAL DOSAGE (WHO Monographs Vol. 2 recommendations): (1) Dried root/rhizome as a tea (infusion or decoction): 2–3 g in 150 ml boiling water, steeped 10–15 minutes; taken 30–60 minutes before bedtime, and if needed once during the day. (2) Tincture (1:5, 70% ethanol): 4–6 ml, up to three times daily. (3) Fluid extract (1:1, 70% ethanol): 0.5–1.0 ml, up to three times daily. (4) Dry aqueous or hydroethanolic extract (standardised): 300–600 mg per dose, taken 30–60 minutes before sleep; some trials used divided doses of 150–300 mg twice daily. (5) Soft extract or powdered extract in capsule/tablet form: equivalent preparations standardised to valerenic acid content (commonly 0.17–0.8%) follow equivalent dose ranges. NOTE: Clinical benefit for sleep typically requires 2–4 weeks of regular use. Not recommended for continuous use beyond 4–6 weeks without medical supervision.
segments.condition: Primary (clinical evidence-based): sleep disturbances of non-organic origin (insomnia, poor sleep quality, prolonged sleep latency). Secondary (traditional / pharmacopeial): nervous restlessness, anxiety states, nervous tension headache, mild spasmolytic use in stress-associated gastrointestinal complaints.
segments.source_section: WHO Monographs on Selected Medicinal Plants, Volume 2 — Radix Valerianae
segments._label: WHO Monographs — Valerian root (Radix Valerianae Vol2)

segments.title_inferred: Calendula / Marigold
segments.original_text: Flos Calendulae — Calendula officinalis L. (Asteraceae). Key chemical constituents: triterpene saponins (oleanolic acid glycosides, 2–10%); flavonoids (isorhamnetin, quercetin, narcissin, rutin); carotenoids (lutein, zeaxanthin, β-carotene, lycopene; total carotenoid content 0.14–0.5% in dried flower); polysaccharides (PS-I, PS-II, PS-III; arabinogalactans and rhamnogalacturonans); essential oils (α- and β-ionone, caryophyllene, pedunculatine; 0.2–0.3%); hydroxycinnamic acid derivatives (caffeic, chlorogenic, isochlorogenic acids); sterols (stigmasterol, sitosterol, isofucosterol); bitter glycosides (loliolide); and resins (calendulin).
segments.translation: Calendula flower (Flos Calendulae) is the dried ligulate and/or tubular floret, or whole capitulum, of Calendula officinalis L., a robust annual or biennial herb of the Asteraceae family native to southern Europe and the Mediterranean basin, now cultivated worldwide for medicinal and ornamental use. The plant produces characteristic bright orange or yellow ray and disc florets borne on erect, branching stems 30–60 cm tall, with alternate, spatulate, slightly sticky leaves covered in glandular hairs. Medicinally, the flower has been used for centuries in European and Ayurvedic traditions to promote wound healing, soothe inflamed or damaged skin and mucous membranes, and treat minor burns, scalds, and abrasions. Clinically supported uses include topical treatment of radiation-induced acute dermatitis (where calendula cream was shown in at least one randomised controlled trial to outperform trolamine in reducing Grade 2+ dermatitis and patient-reported pain during breast cancer radiotherapy), contact dermatitis, venous leg ulcers, and post-surgical wound care. Pharmacologically, the wound-healing activity is attributed to triterpene saponins stimulating granulation tissue formation and collagen synthesis, while flavonoids and carotenoids contribute broad-spectrum anti-inflammatory effects by inhibiting cyclooxygenase (COX) and lipoxygenase (LOX) pathways and suppressing pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-1β, TNF-α). Polysaccharide fractions demonstrate immunostimulatory activity via macrophage activation and enhanced phagocytosis in vitro; ethanolic and aqueous extracts show antifungal activity (against Candida albicans and dermatophytes) and antiviral activity against herpes simplex virus (HSV-1) in cell culture models. Lymphocyte proliferation assays confirm immunomodulatory effects of the arabinogalactan fraction. In vivo rodent studies confirm accelerated re-epithelialisation and increased tensile strength in wound models after topical calendula extract application.

segments.title_inferred: Chili / Capsicum
segments.original_text: Fructus Capsici — Capsicum annuum L. (Solanaceae). Primary active constituents: capsaicinoids (0.1–1.5% total), principally capsaicin (trans-8-methyl-N-vanillyl-6-nonenamide, ~69% of capsaicinoid fraction) and dihydrocapsaicin (~22%); also capsanthin, capsorubin (red carotenoid pigments), ascorbic acid (up to 340 mg/100 g fresh weight), flavonoids (luteolin, quercetin glycosides), steroidal alkaloids, and fixed oils. Pharmaceutical definition: the dried ripe fruit, or preparations thereof, of Capsicum annuum L. var. minimum (Miller) Heiser or of small-fruited varieties of Capsicum frutescens L. (Solanaceae), standardised to capsaicinoid content.
segments.translation: Chili (Capsicum annuum) is a widely cultivated member of the nightshade family (Solanaceae) whose dried ripe fruits are used medicinally, primarily in topical formulations. Its principal active compounds are capsaicinoids — especially capsaicin — which act by initially stimulating and subsequently desensitising the transient receptor potential vanilloid 1 (TRPV1) nociceptors in sensory nerve fibres, thereby depleting substance P and producing analgesia [counter-irritant and defunctionalisation mechanism]. Topical capsaicin cream (0.025–0.075%) has demonstrated clinical efficacy in reducing pain associated with peripheral neuropathies, osteoarthritis, and post-herpetic neuralgia in multiple randomised controlled trials; it is also used internally in traditional medicine as a carminative and digestive stimulant. Systemic absorption following topical application is minimal, but oral preparations are recognised in several pharmacopoeias as spasmolytic and circulatory stimulants.

topical.dose: Apply a thin film to affected area 3–4 times daily; avoid mucous membranes and eyes. Burning sensation on first applications expected to subside with continued use over 1–2 weeks.
topical.duration: Minimum 4 weeks for neuropathic pain assessment; up to 8 weeks for osteoarthritis.

oral_traditional.form: Tincture (1:10, 60% ethanol) or dried fruit powder in capsule
oral_traditional.dose_tincture: 0.3–1.0 mL of 1:10 tincture, up to three times daily with water
oral_traditional.dose_powder: 30–120 mg dried fruit powder per dose, up to three times daily
oral_traditional.notes: Oral use recognised in British Herbal Pharmacopoeia and other national pharmacopoeias as carminative and circulatory stimulant; less robustly supported by clinical trial data than topical use.

high_concentration_topical_patch.dose: Single 60-minute application by healthcare professional; may be repeated every 3 months
high_concentration_topical_patch.notes: Approved by EMA and FDA for peripheral neuropathic pain in non-diabetic adults; exceeds standard WHO monograph dosing range but represents the current high-evidence clinical modality.

condition: Peripheral neuropathic pain (post-herpetic neuralgia, HIV-associated neuropathy, painful diabetic neuropathy) — topical [CLINICAL EVIDENCE: strong]
Rheumatoid arthritis pain — topical [CLINICAL EVIDENCE: moderate]
Cluster headache and migraine — intranasal capsaicin [CLINICAL EVIDENCE: preliminary]
Dyspepsia / carminative use — oral [CLINICAL EVIDENCE: traditional/pharmacopoeial; limited RCT data]
Poor peripheral circulation — oral traditional use [CLINICAL EVIDENCE: traditional only]
segments.source_section: WHO Monographs on Selected Medicinal Plants, Volume 2 — Fructus Capsici

segments.title_inferred: Papaya Leaf
segments.original_text: Folium Caricae Papayae — Carica papaya L. (Caricaceae). Major chemical constituents: carpaine (alkaloid, principal active), pseudocarpaine, dehydrocarpaine I and II; benzyl isothiocyanate; papain, chymopapain (cysteine proteases); flavonoids including kaempferol, quercetin, myricetin; cyanogenic glucosides; tocopherols; ascorbic acid; saponins. Carpaine content in dried leaf reported at approximately 0.15–0.25% w/w in pharmacopoeial-grade material.
segments.translation: Papaya leaf (the dried or fresh lamina of Carica papaya) has been used in traditional medicine across tropical regions of the Americas, Asia, and Africa primarily to manage fever, platelet deficiency associated with dengue fever, and gastrointestinal complaints. Its principal pharmacological activities are attributed to the alkaloid carpaine, which exhibits antiplasmodial, antitumour, and cardiotonic properties at sub-toxic doses, and to the cysteine proteases papain and chymopapain, which exert proteolytic and anti-inflammatory actions. Clinical and preclinical evidence supports the use of standardised papaya leaf extracts to increase platelet counts [thrombocytopenia] in dengue fever patients, with at least two randomised controlled trials demonstrating statistically significant platelet recovery compared to supportive care alone. Anti-inflammatory and antioxidant mechanisms are mediated through inhibition of prostaglandin synthesis and free-radical scavenging by the leaf's flavonoid and tocopherol fractions.
segments.preparation: Oral aqueous extract (decoction or standardised tablet/capsule): 1–2 g dried leaf as decoction in 150 mL water, two to three times daily in adults. Standardised commercial extract (e.g., 1100 mg fresh-leaf equivalent per tablet): 2 tablets twice daily for 5 days as used in dengue thrombocytopenia trials. Expressed fresh juice: approximately 25–30 mL twice daily. Duration generally limited to 5–7 days for acute indications; prolonged use not formally established under WHO guidance.
segments._label: WHO Monographs — Papaya leaf (Folium Caricae Papayae)

segments.title_inferred: Black Cohosh
segments.original_text: Rhizoma Cimicifugae Racemosae — Cimicifuga racemosa (L.) Nutt. (Ranunculaceae). Key chemical constituents: triterpene glycosides [actein, 27-deoxyactein, cimigoside], isoflavones [formononetin, disputed in recent analyses], phenolic acids [isoferulic acid, fukinolic acid], alkaloids [cytisine, methylcytisine], resins, tannins, and volatile oils. Triterpene glycoside content (expressed as 27-deoxyactein) is used as a standardisation marker in many commercial preparations.
segments.translation: Black cohosh is the dried rhizome and root of Cimicifuga racemosa, a perennial woodland herb native to eastern North America. Its primary clinically supported use is the relief of menopausal symptoms — particularly vasomotor complaints such as hot flushes [hot flashes], night sweats, and associated mood disturbances — in women who cannot or prefer not to use hormone replacement therapy [HRT]. The predominant pharmacological mechanism appears to be selective modulation of serotonergic and dopaminergic pathways in the thermoregulatory centre of the hypothalamus rather than direct oestrogenic activity, a distinction supported by in vitro receptor-binding studies showing negligible oestrogenic receptor affinity in most standardised extracts. Secondary traditional uses include relief of premenstrual tension, dysmenorrhoea [painful menstruation], and musculoskeletal or rheumatic discomfort, though clinical evidence for these indications remains limited.
segments.preparation: Adults (oral): (1) Standardised dry extract [isopropanolic or ethanolic, standardised to triterpene glycosides expressed as 27-deoxyactein]: 20–40 mg twice daily, equivalent to approximately 40 mg crude drug per dose. (2) Hydroethanolic fluid extract (1:1, 60% ethanol v/v): 0.4–2.0 mL up to three times daily. (3) Dried rhizome/root (decoction): 0.3–2.0 g as a decoction, up to three times daily. (4) Tincture (1:10, 60% ethanol v/v): 2–4 mL up to three times daily. The WHO monograph specifies a treatment duration of up to six months; use beyond six months has not been adequately evaluated in controlled trials. No established paediatric dosage. Not recommended during pregnancy or lactation.
segments.condition: Menopausal vasomotor symptoms (hot flushes, night sweats); premenstrual syndrome [PMS]; dysmenorrhoea; rheumatic and musculoskeletal complaints (traditional use, limited clinical evidence)
segments.source_section: WHO Monographs on Selected Medicinal Plants, Volume 2 — Rhizoma Cimicifugae Racemosae
segments._label: WHO Monographs — Black cohosh (Rhizoma Cimicifugae Racemosae)

segments.title_inferred: Ceylon Cinnamon Bark
segments.original_text: Cortex Cinnamomi Zeylanici (Cinnamomum zeylanicum Blume, syn. C. verum J.S. Presl; family Lauraceae). Major chemical constituents: essential oil (0.5–2.5%), principally trans-cinnamaldehyde (55–90% of oil), eugenol (1–10%), cinnamyl acetate, linalool, beta-caryophyllene; also tannins (condensed and hydrolysable), mucilage polysaccharides, diterpenes (cinnzeylanol, cinnzeylanine), and trace coumarins (notably low compared to Cinnamomum cassia/aromaticum).
segments.translation: Ceylon cinnamon bark [Cortex Cinnamomi Zeylanici] is the dried inner bark of Cinnamomum zeylanicum (true cinnamon), native to Sri Lanka and southern India and widely cultivated throughout tropical Asia, East Africa, and the Caribbean. The bark's primary bioactive constituents are trans-cinnamaldehyde and eugenol, which confer the plant's principal pharmacological activities: antimicrobial action (disruption of microbial cell membranes and inhibition of biofilm formation), carminative and spasmolytic effects on gastrointestinal smooth muscle (mediated in part by calcium-channel antagonism), and antifungal activity demonstrated in vitro against Candida species. Emerging clinical and preclinical evidence also supports a role in glycaemic regulation, with cinnamaldehyde and polyphenolic polymers [type-A procyanidins] shown to potentiate insulin signalling and inhibit intestinal alpha-glucosidase activity, thereby reducing postprandial blood glucose. Ceylon cinnamon is distinguished from cassia [Cinnamomum aromaticum/cassia] by markedly lower coumarin content, making it the preferred form for prolonged internal use due to reduced hepatotoxic risk. Clinically supported uses include mild gastrointestinal complaints (dyspepsia, flatulence, bloating) and adjunctive management of elevated blood glucose in type 2 diabetes; traditional uses additionally include appetite stimulation, mild antimicrobial applications, and relief of dysmenorrhoea. Adverse reactions are rare at recommended doses but include allergic contact dermatitis (primarily from eugenol), and mucous membrane irritation with high-dose or prolonged essential oil use. Contraindicated in known hypersensitivity to cinnamon or Peruvian balsam [cross-reactivity with eugenol]; caution advised in pregnancy beyond culinary doses due to potential uterotonic effects of cinnamaldehyde. No clinically significant drug interactions have been firmly established, though additive hypoglycaemic effects with antidiabetic medications warrant monitoring.
segments.preparation: ORAL — Dried bark (comminuted or powdered): 1–4 g per day in divided doses, taken as an infusion (tea) or incorporated into food. Aqueous/ethanolic extract: equivalent to 1–4 g crude drug daily. Essential oil: 0.05–0.2 g per day (adults); not recommended for prolonged use at high doses. Tincture (1:5 in 70% ethanol): 2–4 ml, up to three times daily. For adjunctive glycaemic management: standardised bark powder doses of 1–6 g/day have been used in clinical trials (most commonly 1–3 g/day in divided doses with meals). TOPICAL — Diluted essential oil preparations for external antimicrobial use; undiluted oil should not be applied to skin or mucous membranes due to irritancy.
segments.condition: Gastrointestinal complaints (dyspepsia, flatulence, bloating, mild gastrointestinal spasm); adjunctive management of elevated blood glucose / type 2 diabetes mellitus; antimicrobial / antifungal applications (traditional and investigational).
segments._label: WHO Monographs — Ceylon cinnamon bark (Cortex Cinnamomi Zeylanici)

segments.title_inferred: Myrrh
segments.original_text: Gummi-resina Myrrha — Commiphora molmol (Engl.) Engl. (Burseraceae). The drug consists of the dried oleo-gum-resin obtained from the stem of Commiphora molmol. Major chemical constituents include: volatile oils (2–10%), principally furanosesquiterpenes (curzerene, furanoeudesma-1,3-diene, lindestrene, and curzerenone); resins (25–40%), comprising commiphoric acids, heerabomyrrhols, and commiferin; gums (30–60%), composed of polysaccharides including arabinose, galactose, glucuronic acid, and 4-O-methylglucuronic acid; and minor constituents including sterols, flavonoids, and terpenoids.
segments.translation: Myrrh is the dried oleo-gum-resin harvested from the stem of Commiphora molmol, a thorny shrub native to northeastern Africa and the Arabian Peninsula. Its principal active fractions are volatile furanosesquiterpene oils, resinous commiphoric acids, and complex polysaccharide gums. Clinically, myrrh is best supported as a topical antiseptic and anti-inflammatory agent for oral and pharyngeal mucosae — used in the treatment of mouth ulcers, gingivitis, pharyngitis, and mild inflammatory conditions of the oral cavity — and as a treatment for schistosomiasis (particularly Schistosoma mansoni and S. haematobium infection) based on controlled clinical trials conducted primarily in Egypt. Pharmacologically, furanosesquiterpene constituents demonstrate analgesic activity via opioid receptor modulation, while resin fractions exhibit antimicrobial, anti-inflammatory (prostaglandin inhibition), and antitumour effects in vitro; the mechanism of antischistosomal action is not fully elucidated but appears to involve disruption of tegumental integrity in adult worms.

segments.title_inferred: Hawthorn Leaf with Flower
segments.original_text: Folium cum Flore Crataegi — Crataegus monogyna Jacq. (and related species including C. laevigata [Poir.] DC., C. pentagyna Waldst. & Kit. ex Willd., C. nigra Waldst. & Kit., C. azarolus L.). Dried leaves with flowers. Key chemical constituents: oligomeric procyanidins (OPCs, 1–3%), flavonoids including vitexin-2-O-rhamnoside, hyperoside, rutin, and isovitexin (total flavonoids 1–2%), triterpene acids (ursolic acid, oleanolic acid, crataegolic acid), phenolic acids (chlorogenic acid, caffeic acid), amines (phenethylamine, tyramine, O-methoxyphenethylamine in flowers). European Pharmacopoeia specifies minimum 1.5% oligomeric procyanidins (expressed as epicatechin) and minimum 0.6% flavonoids (expressed as hyperoside) in the dried drug.
segments.translation: Hawthorn leaf with flower (Folium cum Flore Crataegi) is the dried leaf-and-flower preparation of Crataegus monogyna and closely related hawthorn species. It is used clinically to support cardiac function in patients with mild-to-moderate heart failure (NYHA class II), characterised by reduced exercise tolerance, dyspnoea on exertion, and fatigue. The principal pharmacological mechanisms include inhibition of phosphodiesterase (increasing intracellular cAMP and thereby enhancing myocardial contractility without the toxic profile of cardiac glycosides), inhibition of Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase, antioxidant activity mediated by the oligomeric procyanidins and flavonoids, peripheral and coronary vasodilation through endothelium-dependent mechanisms (likely involving nitric oxide release), and a mild negative chronotropic effect that may reduce heart rate at rest. In vitro and in vivo studies have demonstrated increased coronary blood flow, reduced peripheral vascular resistance, and protection of myocardial tissue against ischaemia-reperfusion injury. Randomised controlled trials (notably the SPICE pilot trial and multiple European studies) have shown statistically significant improvements in exercise tolerance (measured by bicycle ergometry), reduced symptoms of fatigue and dyspnoea, and improved patients' subjective well-being compared to placebo, with a favourable safety profile over treatment periods of 4–16 weeks. Traditional uses recorded in European pharmacopoeias and folk medicine include treatment of nervous heart complaints, mild hypertension, arteriosclerosis, and age-related cardiac weakness. Adverse reactions are uncommon and generally mild, including nausea, dizziness, gastrointestinal upset, and palpitations at high doses; the preparation is considered to have a wide therapeutic margin. Drug interactions with cardiac glycosides (possible additive effects), antihypertensive agents, and nitrates should be monitored. The preparation is contraindicated in pregnancy and lactation (insufficient safety data) and should not replace established pharmacological therapy in clinically significant or decompensated heart failure without medical supervision.
segments._label: WHO Monographs — Hawthorn leaf (Folium cum Flore Crataegi)

segments.title_inferred: Eleuthero Root (Siberian Ginseng)
segments.original_text: Radix Eleutherococci — Eleutherococcus senticosus (Rupr. & Maxim.) Maxim. (Araliaceae). Dried root and rhizome. Key chemical constituents: eleutherosides (A–M), including eleutheroside B (syringin, phenylpropanoid glycoside), eleutheroside E (liriodendrin, lignan diglucoside), eleutheroside B4 (isofraxidin glucoside, coumarin), polysaccharides (eleutherans A–G), caffeic acid derivatives, triterpene saponins, and sesamin.
