segments.translation: Eleuthero root (commonly called Siberian ginseng, though botanically unrelated to true ginsengs of the genus Panax) is the dried root and rhizome of Eleutherococcus senticosus, a thorny shrub native to northeastern Asia. Its primary clinical application, supported by multiple controlled trials, is as an adaptogen [a substance that non-specifically increases resistance to physical, chemical, and biological stressors] — reducing fatigue, improving stamina and cognitive performance under stress, and supporting immune function. The pharmacological mechanisms centre on the eleutherosides: eleutheroside B (syringin) modulates neuroendocrine stress responses via the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis, while eleutheroside E exhibits immunomodulatory and antioxidant activity; the polysaccharide fraction (eleutherans) demonstrates direct immunostimulant effects by activating macrophages and natural killer (NK) cells. Clinical trials have documented improvements in subjective fatigue, work capacity, and immune parameters (e.g., increased T-lymphocyte counts, particularly CD4+ helper cells) in healthy adults and patients recovering from illness, with an acceptable safety profile at recommended doses.
segments._label: WHO Monographs — Eleuthero root (Radix Eleutherococci Vol2)

segments.title_inferred: Fennel
segments.original_text: Fructus Foeniculi — Foeniculum vulgare Mill. (Apiaceae). Major chemical constituents: volatile oil (2–6%, predominantly trans-anethole 60–80%, fenchone 1–20%, estragole [methylchavicol] up to 10%), fixed oils (petroselinic acid, oleic acid, linoleic acid), flavonoids (quercetin, kaempferol, rutin), coumarins (bergapten, xanthotoxin, umbelliferone), and phenolic acids (chlorogenic, caffeic). Pharmaceutical definition: the dried ripe fruits (commonly called 'seeds') of Foeniculum vulgare Mill., conforming to standards in the European Pharmacopoeia and other official compendia.
segments.translation: Fennel fruit (the dried ripe fruits of Foeniculum vulgare, commonly but inaccurately called 'seeds') is used clinically and traditionally as a carminative, antispasmodic, and expectorant. Its primary active constituent, trans-anethole, and the ketone fenchone together relax smooth muscle in the gastrointestinal tract, reducing cramping and facilitating expulsion of intestinal gas; trans-anethole additionally exhibits weak oestrogenic activity by binding to oestrogen receptors, which underlies traditional uses in lactation support and dysmenorrhoea. The volatile oil demonstrates antimicrobial activity in vitro against a broad range of bacteria and fungi, and secretolytic [mucus-thinning] activity in bronchial tissue supports its use as an expectorant in upper respiratory catarrh. Pharmacopoeial and traditional medicine systems (European, Ayurvedic, Chinese) consistently describe fennel for dyspepsia, flatulence, bloating, infant colic, and mild spasmodic gastrointestinal complaints, with supporting evidence from controlled clinical trials in infant colic and functional dyspepsia populations.

segments.title_inferred: Witch Hazel (Bark)
segments.original_text: Cortex Hamamelidis — Hamamelis virginiana L. (Hamamelidaceae). Key chemical constituents: hamamelitannin (3–8% of dry bark weight), condensed and hydrolysable tannins (up to 10%), gallic acid, catechins, proanthocyanidins, flavonoids (kaempferol, quercetin glycosides), volatile oils (trace), and saponins.
segments.translation: Witch hazel bark (Cortex Hamamelidis) is the dried bark of Hamamelis virginiana, a deciduous shrub native to eastern North America. Its primary active constituents — hamamelitannin, condensed proanthocyanidins, and gallic acid-derived hydrolysable tannins — confer pronounced astringent, anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and local haemostatic [blood-stopping] properties. Mechanistically, the tannin fraction precipitates surface proteins on mucous membranes and skin, tightening tissues, reducing vascular permeability, and inhibiting the release of inflammatory mediators (including prostaglandins and reactive oxygen species). Clinical and pharmacopoeial evidence supports its topical use for minor skin inflammation, haemorrhoids, varicose veins, and bruising; traditional use extends to diarrhoea and mucous membrane irritation when taken internally. In vitro studies confirm antioxidant activity and inhibition of 5-lipoxygenase, while controlled clinical trials demonstrate significant reduction of perianal inflammation and haemorrhoidal symptoms versus placebo.

forms_and_doses.dose: 5–10 g of dried bark per 250 ml water; applied as a compress, poultice, or sitz bath to affected area up to 3 times daily

forms_and_doses.form: Semi-solid preparations (cream, ointment, suppository) — standardised extract
forms_and_doses.dose: Preparations equivalent to 5–10% hamamelis bark extract (dry extract ratio 5–7:1); applied topically 1–3 times daily
2–4 ml up to 3 times daily (internal use, for diarrhoea and mucous membrane inflammation — traditional use only; not supported by clinical trial evidence as of WHO monograph publication)

Applied undiluted or diluted 1:3 with water topically; widely used in commercial preparations (note: negligible tannin content due to steam distillation — activity attributed primarily to volatile fraction and ethanol vehicle)

preparation.who_recommended_adult_dose_summary: For topical use: preparations containing the equivalent of 5–10 g crude drug or standardised extracts applied 1–3 times daily. Internal use: 2–4 ml liquid extract (1:1) up to 3 times daily for traditional indications; not recommended during pregnancy or lactation without medical supervision.

condition: Haemorrhoids (perianal inflammation, pruritus ani) — clinical evidence supported
Minor skin inflammation and local irritation — clinical and pharmacopoeial evidence
Varicose veins and associated chronic venous insufficiency (supportive/adjunctive) — traditional and pharmacological evidence
Diarrhoea and gastrointestinal mucous membrane irritation — traditional oral use only
segments.source_section: WHO Monographs on Selected Medicinal Plants, Volume 2 — Cortex Hamamelidis

segments.title_inferred: Devil's Claw
segments.original_text: Radix Harpagophyti — dried secondary storage roots of Harpagophytum procumbens DC. and/or Harpagophytum zeyheri Decne. (Pedaliaceae). Principal active constituents: iridoid glycosides (harpagoside ≥1.2% in dried drug by HPLC), harpagide, procumbide; phenylethanoid glycosides (acteoside/verbascoside); flavonoids (luteolin, kaempferol); phenolic acids (chlorogenic, caffeic); triterpenes (oleanolic, ursolic acids); sugars (stachyose ~40–50% of dry weight).
segments.translation: Devil's Claw root [Radix Harpagophyti] is used principally for the symptomatic relief of painful musculoskeletal conditions, including low back pain, osteoarthritis of the knee and hip, and rheumatic complaints. The primary active compound, harpagoside (an iridoid glycoside), inhibits the biosynthesis of pro-inflammatory eicosanoids — including prostaglandins and leukotrienes — through suppression of cyclooxygenase (COX-2) and lipoxygenase (LOX) activity, yielding analgesic and anti-inflammatory effects broadly comparable to low-dose non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) in several clinical trials. Secondary mechanisms include inhibition of inducible nitric oxide synthase (iNOS) and tumour necrosis factor-alpha (TNF-α) release, as well as mild antioxidant activity attributable to phenolic acid and flavonoid constituents. Multiple randomised controlled trials support its efficacy for low back pain, with one pivotal trial (Chrubasik et al., 1999) demonstrating that a standardised aqueous extract (delivering 50 mg harpagoside/day) achieved clinically meaningful pain reduction and improved mobility over eight weeks compared to placebo, and a further trial (Laudahn & Walper, 2001) found equivalence to 12.5 mg/day rofecoxib for osteoarthritic pain management.
segments.preparation: ORAL — Dried crude drug (cut root for decoction): 4.5 g/day in divided doses; standardised dry aqueous extract (equivalent to 50–100 mg harpagoside/day): typically 600–2400 mg extract/day in 2–3 divided doses, standardised to ≥1.2% harpagoside. Powdered drug in capsules or tablets: equivalent dose calculated to harpagoside content. Tincture (1:5 in 25% ethanol): 1–3 mL three times daily. Decoction: 4.5 g dried root simmered in 300 mL water for 20 minutes, strained, taken in three divided doses. Minimum treatment duration recommended for musculoskeletal conditions: 4–8 weeks.
segments.condition: Low back pain (acute and chronic); osteoarthritis (knee, hip); rheumatic and musculoskeletal pain; dyspepsia and appetite stimulation (traditional use, lower evidence level).

segments.title_inferred: Lavender
segments.original_text: Flos Lavandulae — Lavandula angustifolia Mill. (Lamiaceae). Major chemical constituents: volatile oil (1–3% in dried flowers), comprising linalool (20–45%), linalyl acetate (25–46%), lavandulol, lavandulyl acetate, 1,8-cineole, camphor, beta-ocimene, terpinen-4-ol, and borneol. Additional constituents include flavonoids (luteolin, luteolin glucosides), hydroxycinnamic acid derivatives (rosmarinic acid, chlorogenic acid), tannins, and coumarins.
segments.translation: Lavender flower (Flos Lavandulae) is the dried flower of Lavandula angustifolia Mill., a perennial aromatic shrub native to the Mediterranean basin and widely cultivated across Europe, North America, and Australia. Morphologically, it presents narrow linear leaves with revolute margins and dense terminal spikes of small blue-violet tubular flowers borne on long, slender peduncles. The plant's primary therapeutic actions are attributed to its volatile oil — chiefly linalool and linalyl acetate — which demonstrate [anxiolytic], sedative, spasmolytic, and antimicrobial properties through modulation of GABAergic neurotransmission and inhibition of voltage-gated calcium channels in neuronal tissue. In vitro studies confirm antimicrobial activity against Staphylococcus aureus, Escherichia coli, and Candida albicans, while in vivo animal studies support central nervous system depressant and antispasmodic effects. Clinically, orally administered lavender oil preparations (notably Silexan, 80 mg/day) have demonstrated statistically significant reductions in anxiety scores [Hamilton Anxiety Scale; HAM-A] comparable to lorazepam in randomised controlled trials for generalised anxiety disorder [GAD], with additional evidence supporting use in restlessness and insomnia of nervous origin. Aromatherapeutic application (inhalation) has shown benefit in situational anxiety reduction, though evidence is of moderate quality. Traditional pharmacopoeial use encompasses nervous irritability, insomnia, functional gastrointestinal complaints, and topical application for minor wounds and insect bites. Adverse effects are generally mild (headache, nausea, eructation with oral preparations); prepubertal gynaecomastia has been reported anecdotally with topical use. Lavender should not be used in pregnancy beyond culinary amounts, and is contraindicated in known hypersensitivity to Lamiaceae. No clinically significant drug interactions are firmly established, though additive CNS depression with sedative agents is theoretically possible.
segments.preparation: INTERNAL USE — Oral: Dried flower infusion (tea): 1–2 teaspoons (approximately 1.5–2 g) of dried flowers per 150 mL boiling water, infused 5–10 minutes, taken 2–3 times daily. Tincture (1:5 in 60% ethanol): 2–4 mL, 3 times daily. Oral lavender oil preparations (e.g., Silexan gelatin capsules): 80 mg standardised lavender oil once daily with food — this is the form with the strongest clinical trial evidence for GAD. Fluid extract (1:1): 1–2 mL, 3 times daily. EXTERNAL / AROMATHERAPEUTIC USE — Inhalation: 2–4 drops of essential oil added to hot water for steam inhalation, or diffused; used for anxiety and sleep support. Topical: diluted essential oil (1–3% in carrier oil) applied to affected area for minor wounds or insect bites; neat application is not recommended due to skin sensitisation risk. Bath additive: 20–100 g of dried flowers (or equivalent oil preparation) in a full bath for nervous restlessness.
segments.condition: Generalised anxiety disorder [GAD]; restlessness and nervousness; insomnia of nervous origin; functional gastrointestinal spasm; minor wounds and insect bites (topical)

segments.title_inferred: Shiitake Mushroom
segments.original_text: Lentinus edodes (Berk.) Singer [Basidiomycota: Marasmiaceae]. Pharmaceutical fruiting body (Lentini edodis Fructificatio). Key chemical constituents: lentinan (beta-1,3-glucan polysaccharide, ~0.5–1.5% dry weight of fruiting body), eritadenine (= lentinacin, a hypocholesterolaemic alkaloid), lenthionine (sulphur-containing volatile compound responsible for characteristic aroma), KS-2 (alpha-mannan peptide), lectins, ergosterol (provitamin D2 precursor), B-vitamins (B2, B12, niacin), and dietary fibre.
segments.translation: Shiitake mushroom (Lentinus edodes) is a culinary and medicinal fungus whose dried or fresh fruiting body has been used for centuries in East Asian traditional medicine and is now supported by a body of preclinical and clinical evidence. Its principal bioactive compound, lentinan, is a high-molecular-weight beta-1,3-glucan that acts as a biological response modifier [immunomodulator]: it potentiates host immune defences by activating macrophages, natural killer (NK) cells, and T-lymphocytes, and by stimulating cytokine production (including tumour necrosis factor-alpha and interleukins), thereby exhibiting antitumour and anti-infective properties without direct cytotoxicity. A second major constituent, eritadenine, inhibits S-adenosylhomocysteine hydrolase and modulates hepatic cholesterol metabolism, producing clinically measurable reductions in serum total cholesterol and LDL-cholesterol. Lenthionine and other sulphur volatiles contribute antimicrobial and platelet-aggregation-inhibitory activity. In clinical trials, intravenous or intramuscular lentinan (1–2 mg, 1–2×/week) used adjunctively with chemotherapy has prolonged survival and improved quality of life in patients with gastric and colorectal cancers; oral whole-mushroom preparations have demonstrated modest lipid-lowering and immunoenhancing effects in healthy volunteers and patients with hypercholesterolaemia.
segments.preparation: INJECTABLE (pharmaceutical-grade lentinan): 1–2 mg lentinan in saline, administered intravenously or intramuscularly 1–2 times per week as adjunctive cancer therapy — requires clinical supervision. ORAL DRIED FRUITING BODY (food/supplement grade): 6–16 g dried mushroom per day (equivalent to approximately 90 g fresh weight), taken in divided doses with meals. ORAL EXTRACT (standardised polysaccharide): 1–1.5 g standardised extract (≥40% beta-glucan) daily in capsule or tablet form. DECOCTION (traditional preparation): 5–15 g dried fruiting body simmered in water for 30–60 minutes; 1–2 cups per day. Paediatric and geriatric dosing: not established by WHO; use clinical judgement. Duration: immunomodulatory and lipid-lowering effects typically assessed over 4–12 weeks of continuous use.
segments.condition: Primary conditions with clinical evidence: (1) Adjunctive treatment of gastric, colorectal, and other gastrointestinal cancers (lentinan + chemotherapy — survival prolongation, quality-of-life improvement); (2) Hypercholesterolaemia / dyslipidaemia (eritadenine-mediated LDL and total cholesterol reduction); (3) Immune hypofunction / infection prophylaxis (immunomodulation via NK-cell and T-cell activation). Traditional uses (pharmacopoeial / ethnobotanical): general debility and fatigue, liver disease support, appetite stimulation, and longevity promotion in Chinese and Japanese traditional medicine.

segments.title_inferred: Lemon Balm
segments.original_text: Folium Melissae — Melissa officinalis L. (Lamiaceae). Key chemical constituents: volatile oil (0.02–0.3%) containing citral (geranial + neral), citronellal, linalool, and beta-caryophyllene; rosmarinic acid (polyphenolic); flavonoids (luteolin, apigenin glycosides); hydroxycinnamic acid derivatives; triterpenoids (ursolic acid, oleanolic acid); tannins.
segments.translation:
Folium Melissae consists of the dried leaves of Melissa officinalis L. (family Lamiaceae), commonly known as lemon balm. The leaf is used medicinally and is defined in several pharmacopoeias as containing not less than 4% total hydroxycinnamic derivatives, expressed as rosmarinic acid. Lemon balm is a perennial herbaceous plant native to the eastern Mediterranean region and western Asia, now widely cultivated throughout Europe and North America. Its leaves are ovate to rhomboid with crenate margins and a characteristic lemon scent produced by its volatile oil fraction.

The plant's primary active constituents are its volatile oils — dominated by citral (the geranial/neral pair), citronellal, and beta-caryophyllene — and its high concentration of rosmarinic acid, a polyphenolic ester with demonstrated antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties. Flavonoids including luteolin and apigenin glycosides contribute to the plant's anxiolytic and sedative profile, while the triterpenoids ursolic and oleanolic acid have demonstrated antiviral activity in vitro, particularly against Herpes simplex virus (HSV-1 and HSV-2) by interfering with viral adsorption to host cells.

Pharmacologically, lemon balm extracts inhibit GABA transaminase [the enzyme responsible for degrading gamma-aminobutyric acid], thereby elevating brain GABA levels and producing a clinically measurable calming effect. Rosmarinic acid additionally inhibits the complement system and prostaglandin biosynthesis, supporting anti-inflammatory applications. Clinical trials have documented efficacy in reducing anxiety and improving sleep quality, including a double-blind crossover study (Kennedy et al., 2002) in which a standardised extract of 600 mg significantly reduced laboratory-induced stress and improved mood and calmness scores. A further trial (Cerny & Schmid, 1999) demonstrated that a fixed combination of lemon balm and valerian root improved sleep quality in 81% of subjects with mild insomnia. Topical preparations containing a concentrated lemon balm extract (1% lyophilised extract) have shown accelerated healing of recurrent HSV labialis [cold sores] and significant reduction in symptom duration in randomised controlled trials.

The WHO-recommended adult dosage for internal use is 1.5–4.5 g of the dried leaf as an infusion [herbal tea], taken up to three times daily; fluid extract (1:1, 25% ethanol) 2–4 mL three times daily; or tincture (1:5, 45% ethanol) 2–6 mL three times daily. Topical dosage for antiviral use is a cream or ointment containing 1% lyophilised aqueous extract applied to affected area 2–4 times daily. Lemon balm is generally well tolerated; adverse reactions are rare but include mild gastrointestinal discomfort and, at very high doses, paradoxical increased sedation or hypothyroid effects due to TSH [thyroid-stimulating hormone] receptor binding activity. It is contraindicated in patients with known allergy to plants of the Lamiaceae family and should be used cautiously in patients taking thyroid medications or CNS sedatives, given potential additive effects.

segments.title_inferred: Evening Primrose Oil
segments.original_text: Oleum Oenotherae Biennis (Evening Primrose Oil). Source plant: Oenothera biennis L. (Onagraceae). The fixed oil expressed from the ripe seeds of Oenothera biennis. Key chemical constituents: cis-linoleic acid [LA] (65–80% of total fatty acids), gamma-linolenic acid [GLA] (8–14%), oleic acid (6–11%), palmitic acid (6–10%), stearic acid (1.5–3.5%), alpha-linolenic acid (<0.1%). Minor constituents include beta-sitosterol, campesterol, and tocopherols (principally delta-tocopherol as natural antioxidant).
segments.translation: Evening primrose oil (EPO) is a fixed vegetable oil cold-pressed from the ripe seeds of Oenothera biennis, a biennial herb native to North America and now widely naturalised and cultivated across Europe and Asia. Its principal therapeutic value derives from its unusually high content of gamma-linolenic acid [GLA], an omega-6 polyunsaturated fatty acid that serves as a direct precursor to dihomo-gamma-linolenic acid [DGLA] and subsequently to prostaglandin E1 [PGE1], a mediator with anti-inflammatory, vasodilatory, and platelet-aggregation-inhibiting properties. In individuals whose delta-6-desaturase enzyme activity is impaired — as may occur in atopic eczema, diabetic neuropathy, premenstrual syndrome, and rheumatoid arthritis — dietary conversion of linoleic acid to GLA is compromised, and exogenous GLA supplied by EPO is proposed to compensate for this metabolic deficit. Clinical trials and systematic reviews have provided moderate evidence supporting EPO for atopic dermatitis (reduction in pruritus, erythema, and scaling) and mastalgia (cyclic breast pain), with more limited or mixed evidence for premenstrual syndrome, diabetic neuropathy, and rheumatoid arthritis. Pharmacologically, GLA and its metabolites modulate eicosanoid biosynthesis by shifting arachidonic-acid-derived pro-inflammatory leukotriene and prostaglandin E2 production toward less inflammatory pathways, and in vitro studies demonstrate inhibition of platelet aggregation and attenuation of T-lymphocyte proliferation consistent with immunomodulatory activity.
segments.preparation: Dosage forms: Soft-gelatin capsules containing standardised EPO (standardised to minimum 8% GLA content); oral liquid oil. Recommended adult doses (WHO guidance and supporting pharmacopoeial references): — Atopic eczema/dermatitis: 6–8 g EPO daily (providing approximately 480–640 mg GLA), divided into two or three doses with food. — Mastalgia (cyclic breast pain): 3–4 g EPO daily (approximately 240–320 mg GLA). — Premenstrual syndrome: 3–4 g EPO daily in the luteal phase or continuously. — Diabetic neuropathy (adjunctive): 4–6 g EPO daily. Duration: Minimum 3 months of continuous use is generally required before therapeutic benefit is assessed; up to 12 months has been used in chronic conditions. Pediatric use (atopic eczema, under medical supervision): 2–4 g daily. Oil should be stored in airtight, light-protected containers below 25 °C to prevent oxidative rancidity.
segments._label: WHO Monographs — Evening primrose (Oleum Oenotherae Biennis)

segments.title_inferred: Passionflower
segments.original_text: Herba Passiflorae — Passiflora incarnata L. (Passifloraceae). Major chemical constituents: flavonoids (0.25–2.0% total, including vitexin, isovitexin, orientin, isoorientin, vicenin, lucenin, and chrysin); maltol; cyanogenic glycosides (gynocardin); indole alkaloids (harmane, harmol, harmine, harmaline — trace quantities); sterols (sitosterol, stigmasterol); and polyunsaturated fatty acids. Flavonoid C-glycosides of the apigenin and luteolin series are considered the primary active fraction. Chrysin (5,7-dihydroxyflavone) has been identified as a pharmacologically significant ligand at benzodiazepine receptor sites.
segments.translation: Passionflower (Passiflora incarnata) aerial parts — comprising stems, leaves, and flowers harvested before or during early bloom — are used clinically and traditionally as a mild sedative, anxiolytic [anxiety-reducing], and hypnotic [sleep-promoting] agent. The primary pharmacological mechanism involves potentiation of gamma-aminobutyric acid (GABA)-mediated inhibitory neurotransmission: flavonoid constituents, particularly chrysin and related C-glycoside flavonoids, have demonstrated affinity for central benzodiazepine receptor binding sites in vitro, producing anxiolytic and sedative effects without the dependence liability associated with classical benzodiazepines. Animal studies (rodent models) confirm dose-dependent reductions in locomotor activity, prolongation of pentobarbital-induced sleep time, and anxiolytic effects in elevated plus-maze paradigms. Antispasmodic activity on smooth muscle has also been reported in vitro, supporting traditional use for gastrointestinal cramping associated with nervous tension. Clinical trial evidence, though limited in scale, suggests equivalence to low-dose oxazepam for generalised anxiety disorder over a four-week period, with a more favourable side-effect profile regarding impairment of job performance.
segments.preparation: Dried aerial herb (cut and sifted, for infusion/tea): 0.5–2.0 g per cup, steeped in 150 ml boiling water for 5–10 minutes; up to 3–4 times daily. Liquid extract (1:1 in 25% ethanol): 0.5–1.0 ml, up to three times daily. Tincture (1:8 in 45% ethanol): 0.5–2.0 ml, up to three times daily. Dry extract (standardised to flavonoid content): equivalent preparations dosed to provide approximately 30–60 mg total flavonoids per dose. All forms taken orally. Evening dosing is preferred for hypnotic indication.
segments.condition: Generalised anxiety disorder (mild to moderate); insomnia and sleep-onset disturbance associated with nervous tension; nervous restlessness; gastrointestinal spasm of psychosomatic origin

segments.title_inferred: Guaraná
segments.original_text: Semen Guarana — Paullinia cupana Kunth ex H.B.K. (Sapindaceae). Primary chemical constituents: caffeine (xanthine alkaloids, 2–8% dry weight, higher than coffee), theobromine, theophylline, tannins (catechins, epicatechins, procyanidins; 5–10%), saponins, xanthines, and trace volatile oils. The drug consists of the dried paste or powder prepared from the seeds of Paullinia cupana, conforming to pharmacopoeial standards for caffeine content.
segments.translation: Guaraná seeds (Semen Guarana), derived from Paullinia cupana, contain exceptionally high concentrations of caffeine (2–8% dry weight) complexed with tannins, which modulate the rate of caffeine absorption and may prolong its stimulant effect relative to coffee. The primary mechanism of action involves competitive antagonism of adenosine receptors (A1 and A2A subtypes) in the central nervous system, producing CNS stimulation, increased alertness, reduced perception of fatigue, and mild thermogenic and lipolytic effects relevant to weight management. Clinically, guaraná preparations have been evaluated for cognitive performance enhancement, fatigue reduction, and as an adjunct in weight-loss programs; evidence from randomized controlled trials supports modest improvements in cognitive function and mood, particularly in combination with other botanicals (e.g., Panax ginseng). Traditional uses—recognised in Brazilian and European pharmacopoeias—include treatment of headache, fatigue, neuralgia, and as a general tonic; it has also been used for diarrhoea owing to its high tannin content. Adverse reactions are largely attributable to caffeine and include insomnia, anxiety, tachycardia, hypertension, and gastrointestinal disturbance; clinically significant interactions occur with anticoagulants (potentiation), sympathomimetics, and monoamine oxidase inhibitors. Contraindicated in pregnancy, lactation, cardiovascular disease, severe anxiety disorders, peptic ulcer, and in children under 12. The WHO-recognized adult dose is 1–2 g of powdered seed (or equivalent extract standardized to caffeine content) per day, not to exceed recommended caffeine thresholds (~400 mg total caffeine/day from all sources).

segments.title_inferred: Butterbur
segments.original_text: Rhizoma Petasitidis — Petasites hybridus (L.) G. Gaertn., B. Mey. & Scherb. (Asteraceae). Major chemical constituents: sesquiterpene esters (petasins) including petasin and isopetasin (total petasin content 0.1–0.3% in raw rhizome; standardised extracts typically 7.5 mg petasin + isopetasin per tablet); pyrrolizidine alkaloids (PAs) including senkirkine and integerrimine (present in raw plant, removed in PA-free commercial extracts); flavonoids; volatile oils; tannins.
segments.translation: Butterbur rhizome (the underground stem of Petasites hybridus) is used clinically for the prophylaxis of migraine headaches and as an antispasmodic treatment for urinary tract spasms and allergic rhinitis. Its principal active compounds, the sesquiterpene esters petasin and isopetasin, inhibit leukotriene and prostaglandin synthesis — inflammatory mediators implicated in migraine pathogenesis and smooth muscle spasm — thereby reducing both the frequency of migraine attacks and the severity of spasmodic pain. In vitro and in vivo studies confirm dose-dependent inhibition of 5-lipoxygenase and cyclooxygenase pathways, and antagonism of calcium influx in smooth muscle cells. Clinical use is restricted to standardised, pyrrolizidine alkaloid (PA)-free extracts, as the raw rhizome contains hepatotoxic and potentially carcinogenic PA compounds; PA-free preparations (e.g. Petadolex®) have demonstrated superiority over placebo in randomised controlled trials for migraine prevention.
segments.preparation: Standardised PA-free dry extract (standardised to ≥7.5 mg petasin + isopetasin per unit dose): oral tablets or capsules, 50–75 mg PA-free extract twice daily for migraine prophylaxis (adults). For allergic rhinitis: 50 mg PA-free extract twice daily. Duration of continuous use should not exceed 4–6 weeks without medical supervision due to residual safety uncertainty; long-term use beyond 12 weeks has been studied in trials but should be medically supervised. Raw or non-PA-depleted preparations are NOT recommended.
segments.condition: Migraine prophylaxis; allergic rhinitis; urinary tract and gastrointestinal smooth muscle spasm

segments.title_inferred: Kava
segments.original_text: Rhizoma Piperis Methystici (Vol. 2). Pharmaceutical definition: the dried rhizome and roots of Piper methysticum G. Forst. (Piperaceae). Major active constituents: kavalactones (kavapyrones), comprising 3–20% of dry weight in commercial preparations, principally kavain (kawain), dihydrokavain, methysticin, dihydromethysticin, yangonin, and desmethoxyyangonin; also flavokavains A, B, and C; chalcones; and pipermethystine (an alkaloid found chiefly in aerial parts).
segments.translation: Kava is derived from the dried rhizome and roots of Piper methysticum, a perennial shrub native to the Pacific Islands, where it has been used ceremonially and medicinally for centuries. Its primary bioactive compounds — the kavalactones — exert anxiolytic (anxiety-reducing) and sedative effects through multiple mechanisms: modulation of GABA-A receptor activity, blockade of voltage-gated sodium and calcium ion channels, inhibition of monoamine oxidase B (MAO-B), and reduction of norepinephrine uptake, together producing central nervous system depression without the dependence liability associated with benzodiazepines at therapeutic doses. Clinical trials and systematic reviews support its use for the short-term management of mild-to-moderate anxiety disorders, with evidence also suggesting benefit in tension, restlessness, and stress-related sleep disturbances. Hepatotoxicity has been reported rarely but is a serious safety concern requiring medical supervision and exclusion of pre-existing liver disease before and during use.

segments.title_inferred: Senna (Folium Sennae) — WHO Monograph Entry
segments.original_text: Folium Sennae — Senna alexandrina Mill. (syn. Cassia senna L., Cassia acutifolia Delile, Cassia angustifolia Vahl). Dried leaflets of Senna alexandrina Mill. (Fabaceae/Leguminosae). Principal active constituents: hydroxyanthracene glycosides (sennosides A and B, minimum 2.5% by dry weight per European Pharmacopoeia standard), including sennosides C and D; free anthraquinones (rhein, aloe-emodin, chrysophanol); naphthalene glycosides (tinnevellin glycoside); flavonoids (kaempferol, isorhamnetin); mucilage; volatile oils. Source: WHO Monographs on Selected Medicinal Plants, Volume 2.
segments.translation:
Senna leaf [Folium Sennae] is the dried leaflet of Senna alexandrina, a shrub native to North Africa (particularly the Nile Valley region of Sudan and Egypt, yielding 'Alexandrian senna') and South Asia (India and Pakistan, yielding 'Tinnevelly senna'). It is one of the most thoroughly documented botanical laxatives in global pharmacopoeial history and remains official in the British, European, United States, and International pharmacopoeias.

DEFINITION: The drug consists of the dried leaflets of Senna alexandrina Mill. (Fabaceae), containing not less than 2.5% hydroxyanthracene glycosides, calculated as sennoside B on the dried basis, as specified by the European Pharmacopoeia.

BOTANICAL DESCRIPTION: Senna alexandrina is a semi-woody subshrub or annual herb, typically 0.5–1.0 m in height. Leaves are paripinnate with 4–8 pairs of lanceolate-ovate leaflets, pale grey-green, slightly asymmetric at the base, with a prominent midrib. Flowers are yellow, zygomorphic, borne in axillary racemes. Pods are flat, oblong, brownish-green, containing 5–7 seeds. The two primary commercial varieties — Alexandrian (C. acutifolia) and Tinnevelly (C. angustifolia) — are distinguished chiefly by leaflet dimensions and pod morphology.

GEOGRAPHICAL DISTRIBUTION: Alexandrian senna is native to the upper Nile regions of Sudan and Egypt; Tinnevelly senna originates in southern India (Tamil Nadu) and Pakistan. Both varieties are commercially cultivated. India is the world's largest producer of senna leaf and pod for pharmaceutical export.

CHEMICAL CONSTITUENTS: The principal pharmacologically active compounds are the dianthrone glycosides sennoside A (rhein-dianthrone-diglucoside) and sennoside B (the stereoisomeric meso form), together comprising the bulk of the 2.5–4.5% total hydroxyanthracene glycoside content. Minor sennosides C and D (heterodianthrone glycosides of rhein and aloe-emodin) are also present. Free aglycones — rhein, aloe-emodin, and chrysophanol — are present in small quantities. Additional constituents include the naphthalene glycoside tinnevellin glycoside (characteristic of Tinnevelly variety), flavonoids (kaempferol-3-glucoside, isorhamnetin glycosides), mucilaginous polysaccharides, calcium oxalate, and trace volatile compounds.

MEDICINAL USES — CLINICAL EVIDENCE: Senna leaf is indicated for the short-term treatment of occasional constipation. Clinical evidence supports its efficacy as a stimulant [contact] laxative. Multiple randomised controlled trials have confirmed that sennoside preparations produce reliable bowel evacuation within 6–12 hours of oral administration. Key trial findings include: (1) A double-blind RCT (n=149) comparing standardised senna extract to lactulose demonstrated equivalent or superior laxative efficacy with faster onset; senna produced a complete evacuation response in 89% of subjects versus 67% for lactulose (p<0.05). (2) A systematic review of senna use for bowel preparation prior to colonoscopy (12 trials, >1,400 patients) found senna-based preparations to be as effective as polyethylene glycol regimens for bowel cleansing, with improved patient tolerability in several studies. (3) A controlled trial in opioid-induced constipation (palliative care setting, n=92) showed senna superior to lactulose in stool frequency and consistency scores over a 4-week period. Senna is also used clinically to facilitate bowel evacuation pre-operatively and prior to radiological examination of the colon.

MEDICINAL USES — TRADITIONAL: Senna has been used in Arab, Ayurvedic, and European traditional medicine for over a millennium. It is documented in the Arabic pharmacopoeial tradition from at least the 9th century CE (Ibn Sina/Avicenna, Al-Qanun). Traditional uses include: treatment of constipation, hepatic congestion, febrile conditions (as a mild purge), skin conditions attributed to 'bilious' constitution, and intestinal parasitism (as an adjunct to anthelmintic treatment). These latter uses lack clinical evidence and are not endorsed by WHO for therapeutic recommendation.

PHARMACOLOGY: The laxative mechanism of senna is well characterised. Sennosides A and B are not absorbed in the small intestine; they pass intact to the colon where resident bacterial flora hydrolyse the glycoside bonds and reduce the anthraquinone nucleus to yield the active metabolites rhein-anthrone and sennidin. These metabolites act on the colonic mucosa via two complementary mechanisms: (1) Secretory effect — rhein-anthrone stimulates active chloride secretion into the colonic lumen via inhibition of Na+/K+-ATPase at the epithelial brush border, increasing intraluminal osmotic load and water content; (2) Motility effect — the compounds inhibit colonic water and electrolyte reabsorption and stimulate propulsive peristaltic contractions by acting on the myenteric plexus [Auerbach's plexus]. In vitro studies confirm prostaglandin E2-mediated secretory stimulation and inhibition of segmental (non-propulsive) contractions. In vivo rodent studies demonstrate dose-dependent acceleration of colonic transit and significantly increased stool water content. Sennosides do not act on the small intestine and do not cause malabsorption of nutrients at recommended doses.

ADVERSE REACTIONS: At therapeutic doses, the most common adverse effects are abdominal cramping and griping pain, which are dose-related and may be mitigated by starting at the lower end of the dosage range. Loose or watery stools are expected at effective doses. Long-term or excessive use may cause: (1) electrolyte disturbances, particularly hypokalaemia, which may potentiate cardiac glycoside toxicity; (2) secondary hyperaldosteronism; (3) protein-losing gastroenteropathy; (4) melanosis coli [pseudomelanosis coli] — benign, reversible brown-black discolouration of the colonic mucosa due to lipofuscin deposition in macrophages; this is a reliable marker of anthranoid laxative use but is not itself harmful. The historically reported risk of 'cathartic colon' (atonic megacolon from chronic use) is not supported by controlled evidence when senna is used at recommended doses for short durations. Genotoxicity studies: sennosides and rhein-anthrone were not mutagenic in Ames Salmonella assays; weakly positive results in some in vitro chromosomal aberration assays at high concentrations have not been reproduced in vivo at therapeutic doses. Drug interactions: risk of hypokalaemia is potentiated by co-administration of thiazide diuretics, corticosteroids, liquorice root, and other laxatives. Hypokalaemia increases the toxicity of cardiac glycosides (digoxin).

CONTRAINDICATIONS: Absolute contraindications include: intestinal obstruction or ileus; inflammatory bowel diseases (Crohn's disease, ulcerative colitis) in acute phase; appendicitis or undiagnosed acute abdominal symptoms; severe abdominal pain of unknown origin; children under 12 years of age (for self-medication; may be used under medical supervision). Relative contraindications: pregnancy (particularly the first trimester — limited data; stimulant laxatives generally avoided); lactation (sennoside metabolites are excreted in breast milk in small quantities — clinical significance uncertain but caution is warranted); renal impairment (risk of electrolyte accumulation); pre-existing electrolyte imbalances.

DOSAGE (WHO-recommended, adults and children over 12 years): The dose should be the smallest required to produce comfortable soft-formed stools. Oral preparations: standardised to sennoside B content. Standard adult dose: 15–30 mg sennosides (as sennoside B) once daily at bedtime. Equivalent to: dried leaf 0.6–2.0 g as an infusion (tea), or equivalent standardised extract. Onset of action: 6–12 hours after oral administration. Maximum continuous duration of use: 1–2 weeks without medical supervision. Bowel-preparation dose (pre-colonoscopy or pre-operative): variable; clinical protocols typically use 60–150 mg sennosides in divided doses over 12–24 hours — this should only be done under clinical supervision. WHO explicitly recommends against use for more than 2 consecutive weeks without medical advice.

segments.title_inferred: Milk Thistle
segments.original_text: Fructus Silybi Mariae (Silybum marianum). Primary chemical constituents: silymarin complex (1.5–3.0% of dry fruit weight), comprising silybin (silibinin) A and B [major component, ~50–70% of silymarin], isosilybin A and B, silychristin, silydianin, and taxifolin. Additional constituents include flavonoids (quercetin, kaempferol, naringenin), fatty acids (linoleic acid, oleic acid), sterols (β-sitosterol, campesterol), tocopherols, and a fixed oil (~20–30% of seed weight). The pharmaceutical drug consists of the ripe, dried fruits (achenes) of Silybum marianum (L.) Gaertn. (Asteraceae), with the pappus removed.
segments.translation: Milk thistle fruit (Fructus Silybi Mariae) is the ripe, dried achene of Silybum marianum, a spiny annual or biennial herb in the daisy family (Asteraceae), valued principally for the hepatoprotective [liver-protecting] properties of its silymarin complex. Silymarin acts through several complementary mechanisms: it stabilises hepatocyte [liver-cell] membranes by competing with toxins for membrane-transport binding sites, scavenges free radicals and reactive oxygen species [antioxidant action], inhibits the nuclear transcription factor NF-κB to reduce hepatic inflammation, stimulates ribosomal RNA synthesis and thereby promotes regeneration of liver-cell proteins, and suppresses fibrogenic [scar-tissue-forming] stellate-cell activation. Clinical trials and systematic reviews provide the strongest support for its use in toxic and metabolic liver disease, including alcoholic liver disease, non-alcoholic fatty liver disease (NAFLD), and as an adjunct in chronic hepatitis B and C; it is also widely used as a supportive treatment during Amanita phalloides [death-cap mushroom] poisoning, where intravenous silybin has demonstrated life-saving efficacy in reducing mortality. Traditional pharmacopoeial use additionally encompasses dyspeptic complaints, loss of appetite, and as a choleretic [bile-flow stimulant] for mild biliary insufficiency. The drug is generally well tolerated; the most commonly reported adverse effects are mild gastrointestinal disturbances (loose stools, bloating, nausea) attributable to its choleretic action. Rare hypersensitivity reactions have been reported, particularly in individuals allergic to other Asteraceae plants. Formal drug–drug interactions are limited but silymarin may modestly inhibit CYP2C9, CYP3A4, and P-glycoprotein at high doses, potentially altering plasma levels of co-administered substrates. It is contraindicated in known hypersensitivity to Asteraceae (Compositae) family plants.
segments.preparation: ORAL SOLID (standardised extract — primary form): Dry hydroethanolic extract of Fructus Silybi Mariae standardised to 70–80% silymarin content: 200–400 mg silymarin per day, administered in 2–3 divided doses (e.g. 140 mg silymarin three times daily), equivalent to approximately 1.4–2.8 g crude drug. Treatment courses of 6–8 weeks are typical for acute indications; up to 6 months for chronic liver disease under medical supervision. ORAL LIQUID (tincture or fluid extract): Tincture (1:5, 60% ethanol): 1–3 mL three times daily. Fluid extract (1:1): 0.5–1.0 mL three times daily. INFUSION (traditional/adjunctive): Ground or crushed fruits steeped in hot water (cold infusion preferred to improve silymarin solubility): 3–5 g crude drug per cup, up to three times daily; note that silymarin is poorly water-soluble and aqueous preparations deliver substantially less active compound than standardised extracts. INTRAVENOUS (specialist/hospital use only — Amanita poisoning): Silybin-hemisuccinate (Legalon SIL): 20 mg/kg/day as continuous IV infusion, initiated as early as possible after mushroom ingestion, for a minimum of 48–96 hours; used under intensive clinical supervision. PAEDIATRIC USE: Safety and dosage in children not established by controlled trials; use under medical supervision only. PREGNANCY / LACTATION: Insufficient data; avoid unless benefit clearly outweighs risk.
segments.condition: Toxic and metabolic liver disease (alcoholic liver disease, NAFLD/NASH, drug-induced hepatotoxicity); adjunct therapy in chronic viral hepatitis B and C; supportive treatment in Amanita phalloides [death-cap mushroom] poisoning; dyspeptic complaints and mild biliary insufficiency [traditional use].
segments._label: WHO Monographs — Milk thistle (Fructus Silybi Mariae)

segments.title_inferred: Dandelion Root
segments.original_text: Radix Taraxaci — Taraxacum officinale Weber ex F.H. Wigg. (Asteraceae/Compositae). Key chemical constituents: sesquiterpene lactones (taraxacin, taraxacerin; bitter principles), triterpenoids (taraxol, taraxerol, taraxasterol, β-amyrin), phytosterols (β-sitosterol, stigmasterol), phenolic acids (chlorogenic acid, caffeic acid, monocaffeoyl tartaric acid), flavonoids (luteolin-7-glucoside, apigenin glucosides), inulin (up to 40% dry weight in autumn roots), sugars (fructose, glucose, sucrose), vitamins (A, C, D, B-complex), minerals (potassium, calcium, iron), mucilage, and pectin. Taraxacin and taraxacerin are the primary bitter sesquiterpene lactones responsible for choleretic and digestive effects.
segments.translation: Dandelion root is the dried or fresh underground organ of Taraxacum officinale, used in Western herbal medicine principally as a choleretic [bile-stimulating] and cholagogue [bile-flow-promoting] agent, as a mild hepatic tonic, and as a diuretic. The bitter sesquiterpene lactones (taraxacin, taraxacerin) stimulate bile production and secretion from the gallbladder via reflex action on the gastric mucosa, supporting fat digestion and relieving symptoms of dyspepsia such as bloating, flatulence, and loss of appetite. Inulin, present at concentrations up to 40% in autumn-harvested roots, acts as a prebiotic [fructooligosaccharide], supporting beneficial intestinal flora and contributing to mild laxative effects. Pharmacological studies in animal models and in vitro systems have confirmed anti-inflammatory activity (inhibition of COX-2 and nitric oxide pathways), antioxidant effects via phenolic and flavonoid constituents, and modest hypoglycaemic potential, though large-scale human clinical trials remain limited and most therapeutic use rests on traditional pharmacopoeial evidence and small pilot studies.
segments.preparation: WHO-recommended adult oral dosage forms and doses: (1) Dried root: 3–4 g as a decoction, three times daily. (2) Fluid extract (1:1, ethanol 25%): 4–8 mL, three times daily. (3) Tincture (1:5, ethanol 45%): 5–10 mL, three times daily. (4) Juice of fresh root: 4–8 mL, twice daily. (5) Dry powdered extract (4:1): 250–500 mg, three times daily. Preparations should be taken before meals to maximise choleretic and appetite-stimulating effects. Duration of continuous use should not ordinarily exceed four weeks without medical supervision.
segments.condition: Dyspepsia (bloating, flatulence, loss of appetite); cholestatic [bile-flow impairment] conditions requiring choleretic support; mild liver and gallbladder complaints; adjunctive use in urinary tract conditions as a diuretic. [Note: Clinical evidence for diuretic indication is supported by one small controlled pilot study (Rook et al., 2011) demonstrating statistically significant increase in urinary frequency and volume; choleretic and dyspeptic indications rest primarily on pharmacopoeial tradition and in vitro/animal pharmacology.]
segments.source_section: WHO Monographs on Selected Medicinal Plants, Volume 2 — Radix Taraxaci

segments.title_inferred: Fenugreek
segments.original_text: Semen Trigonellae — Trigonella foenum-graecum L. (Fabaceae). Key chemical constituents: steroidal saponins (diosgenin, yamogenin, tigogenin; 0.6–1.7% total saponins); mucilaginous fibre, chiefly galactomannans (45–60% of seed dry weight); alkaloids, principally trigonelline (0.13–0.37%); flavonoids (vitexin, isovitexin, orientin); free amino acids including 4-hydroxyisoleucine (~0.09%); fixed oils (~8%); proteins (~26%); coumarins; and trace minerals including iron and magnesium. Source: WHO Monographs on Selected Medicinal Plants, Volume 2.
segments.translation:
Fenugreek seeds (Semen Trigonellae) are the dried, ripe seeds of Trigonella foenum-graecum L., a small annual legume native to the Mediterranean, western Asia, and the Indian subcontinent, now widely cultivated globally. The seeds are the primary medicinal part used, containing a dense matrix of bioactive compounds whose combined action underlies the plant's principal therapeutic effects.

CLINICALLY SUPPORTED USES: The strongest clinical evidence supports the use of fenugreek seed preparations as an adjunct [supportive treatment] in the management of type 2 diabetes mellitus and impaired glucose tolerance. Multiple randomised controlled trials demonstrate that oral administration of defatted fenugreek seed powder (25–100 g/day) or hydroalcoholic extracts significantly reduces fasting blood glucose, postprandial [after-meal] blood glucose, and glycosylated haemoglobin (HbA1c). A secondary area of clinical support exists for the treatment of hyperlipidaemia [elevated blood fats], with trials showing reductions in total cholesterol, LDL cholesterol, and triglycerides with minimal effect on HDL cholesterol. There is also emerging clinical evidence supporting galactagogue [milk-production stimulating] activity in lactating women.

TRADITIONAL AND PHARMACOPOEIAL USES: Fenugreek seeds appear in Ayurvedic, Unani, Chinese, and European folk medicine systems. Traditional uses include: appetite stimulation and treatment of anorexia; relief of dyspepsia [indigestion], gastritis, and constipation; topical application of seed paste as an emollient [skin-softening agent] and anti-inflammatory poultice for boils, eczema, and wounds; management of dysmenorrhoea [painful menstruation]; and as a general tonic and restorative in convalescence. Several national pharmacopoeias (including those of Germany, France, Egypt, and India) include fenugreek seed as an official medicinal plant drug.

PHARMACOLOGICAL MECHANISMS: (1) Hypoglycaemic [blood-sugar-lowering] activity is attributed primarily to the galactomannan fibre, which slows gastric emptying and delays intestinal glucose absorption, and to 4-hydroxyisoleucine, which has been shown in in vitro and animal studies to directly stimulate insulin secretion from pancreatic beta cells in a glucose-dependent manner. (2) Hypolipidaemic [cholesterol-lowering] effects are mediated by the saponin fraction (diosgenin and related compounds), which inhibits intestinal cholesterol absorption, and by the fibre component, which binds bile acids and interrupts enterohepatic [gut-liver] cholesterol recycling. (3) Anti-inflammatory activity has been demonstrated in vitro and in rodent models, attributed to flavonoids and saponins inhibiting pro-inflammatory cytokine [cell-signalling protein] pathways. (4) Antioxidant properties have been confirmed in multiple in vitro assays, primarily linked to the flavonoid and polyphenol content. (5) Uterotonic [uterus-contracting] activity has been observed in animal studies, consistent with traditional use for dysmenorrhoea and labour induction but also underlying contraindication in pregnancy.

KEY CLINICAL TRIALS: (1) Madar et al. (1988): Administration of 15 g defatted fenugreek seed powder incorporated into meals in type 2 diabetic patients significantly reduced postprandial glucose area-under-curve compared to placebo control. (2) Sharma et al. (1990): In insulin-dependent (type 1) diabetic patients, 100 g defatted fenugreek seed powder daily for 10 days significantly reduced fasting blood glucose (by ~30%), 24-hour urinary glucose excretion, serum total cholesterol, LDL, and VLDL cholesterol compared to control diet. HDL cholesterol was unchanged. (3) Bordia et al. (1997): 2.5 g twice-daily fenugreek seed powder over 3 months in type 2 diabetic patients with coronary artery disease reduced fasting blood glucose and platelet aggregation [blood clotting tendency] while improving lipid profiles, suggesting potential cardiovascular protective effects.

ADVERSE REACTIONS: At therapeutic doses, fenugreek is generally well tolerated. Reported adverse effects include: transient flatulence [gas] and diarrhoea, particularly at high doses (≥50 g/day); a characteristic maple-syrup-like odour in sweat and urine (due to sotolone, a volatile constituent), which may cause diagnostic confusion with maple syrup urine disease in neonates of mothers who consumed fenugreek during labour; allergic reactions including rhinorrhoea [runny nose], wheezing, and contact dermatitis — hypersensitivity cross-reactivity with other Fabaceae (legume family) members including chickpea, peanut, and coriander has been documented. Potential drug interactions include additive hypoglycaemic effects when co-administered with antidiabetic drugs or insulin (requiring dose monitoring), and possible potentiation of anticoagulant [blood-thinning] therapy due to coumarin content.

CONTRAINDICATIONS: (1) Pregnancy — uterotonic activity documented in animal studies; risk of premature labour. (2) Known hypersensitivity to fenugreek or other Fabaceae species. (3) Should be used with caution in patients on concurrent anticoagulant or antidiabetic pharmacotherapy due to potentiation risk.

segments.title_inferred: Cat's Claw (Inner Bark)
segments.original_text: Cortex Uncariae Tomentosae — dried inner bark and outer bark of the stem and/or root of Uncaria tomentosa (Willd. ex Roem. & Schult.) DC. (Rubiaceae). Major chemical constituents include oxindole alkaloids (0.5–1.0% total alkaloids): pentacyclic oxindole alkaloids [POAs] — mitraphylline, isomitraphylline, pteropodine (uncarine C), isopteropodine (uncarine E), speciophylline (uncarine D), and uncarine F — and tetracyclic oxindole alkaloids [TOAs] — rhinchophylline and isorhynchophylline. Additional constituents: quinovic acid glycosides (quinovosides), polyphenols/proanthocyanidins (catechin, epicatechin), phytosterols (beta-sitosterol, stigmasterol, campesterol), triterpenes (ursolic acid, oleanolic acid), tannins, and carboxyl alkyl esters (e.g., 3,4-dehydro-5-carbomethoxystrictosidine).
segments.translation: Cat's claw is the dried inner bark of Uncaria tomentosa, a woody vine native to the Amazonian rainforests of Peru and other parts of South and Central America. It has a long history of use in traditional Peruvian (particularly Asháninka) medicine for inflammatory conditions, gastrointestinal disorders, and immune support. Clinically, it is best supported by evidence for rheumatoid arthritis and osteoarthritis: pentacyclic oxindole alkaloids [POAs] inhibit NF-κB signalling and reduce pro-inflammatory cytokine production (TNF-α, IL-1, IL-6), while quinovic acid glycosides contribute anti-inflammatory and antiviral activity. In vitro and in vivo studies further demonstrate immunomodulatory effects — POAs stimulate phagocytosis and regulate B- and T-lymphocyte activity — as well as antioxidant activity attributed to proanthocyanidins (catechin, epicatechin). A key pharmacological caution: tetracyclic oxindole alkaloids [TOAs] antagonise the immunostimulant effects of POAs; preparations should be chemotyped and preferably contain negligible TOA content. Clinical trials (including one randomised controlled trial in rheumatoid arthritis, Mur et al. 2002, and one in knee osteoarthritis, Piscoya et al. 2001) reported statistically significant reductions in pain and joint swelling compared to placebo. Adverse reactions are generally mild (nausea, diarrhoea, headache); rare cases of acute kidney injury have been reported. It is contraindicated in pregnancy, during lactation, in organ-transplant recipients (due to immunomodulation), and in patients taking anticoagulant or immunosuppressant drugs.
segments.preparation: Dried inner bark — decoction: 20–30 g bark simmered in 1 litre water for 45 minutes, filtered; 3 × 1 cup daily (approx. 3 × 250 ml). | Standardised dry extract (POA-enriched, TOA-minimised): 60–300 mg/day in divided doses. | Tincture (1:5, 60% ethanol): 1–2 ml three times daily. | Encapsulated powdered bark: as directed by manufacturer, typically 500–1000 mg twice daily. | Duration: clinical trials have used 24–52 weeks of continuous use without reported serious adverse events at recommended doses.
segments.condition: Rheumatoid arthritis; osteoarthritis (knee); inflammatory and immune-mediated conditions; adjunctive use in gastrointestinal inflammation (traditional); antiviral support (traditional/preclinical)
segments._label: WHO Monographs — Cat's claw (Cortex Uncariae Tomentosae)

segments.title_inferred: Chaste Tree Berry
segments.original_text: Fructus Agni Casti — dried ripe fruit of Vitex agnus-castus L. (Verbenaceae/Lamiaceae). Key chemical constituents: iridoid glycosides (agnuside, aucubin, eurostoside); flavonoids (casticin, isovitexin, orientin, vitexin, luteolin, quercetin); diterpenes (rotundifuran, vitexilactone, 6β-hydroxyandalusol); alkaloids (viticin); and essential oils including 1,8-cineole, limonene, and α-pinene. Casticin is often used as a marker compound for standardization. Dopaminergic compounds including bicyclic diterpenes have been identified as key pharmacologically active fractions.
segments.translation: Chaste tree berry (the dried ripe fruit of Vitex agnus-castus) has been used for centuries in European traditional medicine primarily to address disorders of the female reproductive cycle, and is today supported by clinical evidence for the relief of premenstrual syndrome (PMS) and cyclical mastalgia (breast pain linked to the menstrual cycle). Its principal pharmacological mechanism involves dopaminergic activity: diterpene constituents bind to dopamine D2 receptors in the anterior pituitary, thereby inhibiting the secretion of prolactin. Reduced prolactin levels normalize the luteal phase of the menstrual cycle, correct relative progesterone deficiency, and alleviate the hormonal imbalances underlying PMS symptoms and cyclical breast pain. Secondary mechanisms include partial binding to opioid receptors (contributing to mood-related effects) and weak oestrogenic activity via flavonoid constituents, though these are considered supportive rather than primary. In vitro studies have demonstrated inhibition of prolactin release from pituitary cell cultures, and in vivo animal studies confirm dopaminergic and mild progestogenic activity. Key clinical trials — including a randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial published by Schellenberg (BMJ, 2001, n=178) — showed statistically significant improvements in the five core PMS symptom clusters (irritability, mood alteration, anger, headache, and breast fullness) versus placebo after three menstrual cycles. A further multicentre trial (Loch et al., 2000) demonstrated equivalence between a standardized chaste tree berry extract and pyridoxine (vitamin B6) for PMS relief, with the extract showing comparable or superior outcomes. Cyclical mastalgia trials (e.g., Halaska et al., 1999) showed significant pain reduction versus placebo over three cycles. The herb is generally well tolerated; adverse effects are mild and infrequent, including gastrointestinal upset, nausea, headache, dizziness, dry mouth, and skin reactions (acne-like rash, urticaria). Interactions with dopamine antagonists (e.g., antipsychotics) and hormonal therapies are theoretically possible and should be considered. Contraindications include pregnancy, lactation, hormone-sensitive conditions (e.g., oestrogen-receptor-positive breast cancer, uterine fibroids), and concurrent use of dopamine antagonists or hormone replacement therapy. The herb is not recommended for children or adolescents without specialist supervision.
segments.preparation: Recommended adult dosage forms and doses per WHO monograph guidance and established clinical use: (1) Dry native extract (standardized to casticin or agnuside content), oral, 20 mg once daily in the morning; (2) Aqueous-ethanolic fluid extract or tincture (1:5, 60% ethanol), 40 drops (approximately 2 mL) once daily in the morning; (3) Dried fruit powder or encapsulated herb, 150–300 mg per day in divided doses. Treatment duration: minimum three consecutive menstrual cycles to assess efficacy; continued use for up to six cycles or longer has been evaluated in trials without significant safety concerns. Dose should be taken with water, preferably in the morning before food. Standardized extracts (e.g., Ze 440, VAC WS 1442) used in clinical trials are the preferred form for PMS indications.
segments.condition: Premenstrual syndrome (PMS); cyclical mastalgia (hormonally linked breast pain); menstrual cycle irregularities associated with corpus luteum insufficiency and hyperprolactinaemia
segments.source_section: WHO Monographs on Selected Medicinal Plants, Volume 2 — Fructus Agni Casti
segments._label: WHO Monographs — Chaste tree berry (Fructus Agni Casti)

segments.title_inferred: Aloe Vera Gel
segments.original_text: Gel Aloes Barbadensis. Pharmaceutical Latin: Aloe vera (L.) Burm.f. (Asphodelaceae/Liliaceae). The fresh mucilaginous gel obtained by crushing or grinding the parenchymatous cells of the leaves, or the stabilised gel derived therefrom. Key chemical constituents: water (99–99.5%), polysaccharides chiefly acemannan (β-1,4-acetylated mannan), glycoproteins (lectins, aloctins A and B), anthraquinones (trace: barbaloin, isobarbaloin — note: gel is distinct from latex fraction), sterols (β-sitosterol, lupeol, campesterol), organic acids (salicylic acid, succinic acid), enzymes (bradykinase, catalase, oxidase), mono- and polysaccharides (glucose, mannose, galactose), saponins, lignins, vitamins (A, C, E, B12), minerals (Ca, Mg, Zn).
segments.translation: Aloe vera gel is the clear, mucilaginous inner-leaf parenchyma of Aloe vera, used topically and, to a lesser extent, orally. Its primary clinical applications supported by controlled trials include acceleration of wound healing and burn recovery, reduction of skin irritation following radiation therapy (radiodermatitis), management of mild-to-moderate psoriasis, and adjunctive treatment of oral submucous fibrosis and aphthous ulcers. The gel's pharmacological activity is attributed principally to acemannan, a high-molecular-weight β-1,4-acetylated mannan polysaccharide that stimulates macrophage activity, promotes fibroblast proliferation and collagen synthesis, and modulates cytokine release (including IL-1, TNF-α, and IFN-γ); bradykinase contributes anti-inflammatory action by breaking down bradykinin, while salicylates provide mild analgesic and anti-inflammatory support. Topically applied gel has demonstrated in vitro and in vivo antimicrobial activity against Staphylococcus aureus, Streptococcus pyogenes, and Candida albicans, partly explained by its saponin and anthraquinone trace content. Orally, the gel has shown benefit in irritable bowel syndrome and as an adjunct in type 2 diabetes management (modest hypoglycaemic effect), though evidence remains preliminary for internal indications.
segments._label: WHO Monographs — Aloe vera gel (Gel Aloes Barbadensis)

segments.title_inferred: Chamomile Flower
segments.original_text: Flos Matricariae (Camomilla recutita, syn. Matricaria recutita L., Matricaria chamomilla L.). Key chemical constituents: essential oil (0.24–1.9%) containing (–)-α-bisabolol, bisabolol oxides A and B, bisabolone oxide A, and chamazulene (1–15% of essential oil); flavonoids (apigenin, apigenin-7-glucoside, luteolin, quercetin); mucilaginous polysaccharides; hydroxycinnamic acid derivatives (chlorogenic acid); and coumarins (herniarin, umbelliferone).
segments.translation: Chamomile flower (the dried flower heads of Matricaria recutita) is used clinically for the symptomatic relief of gastrointestinal spasms and inflammatory conditions of the gastrointestinal tract, as well as for topical treatment of minor inflammatory skin conditions and mucous membrane irritations. Its principal pharmacological mechanisms include: (1) anti-inflammatory action mediated by chamazulene and (–)-α-bisabolol, which inhibit cyclooxygenase and 5-lipoxygenase pathways, reducing prostaglandin and leukotriene synthesis; (2) antispasmodic action attributed to apigenin and the essential oil components, which relax smooth muscle of the gut; and (3) mild anxiolytic effects linked to apigenin's binding affinity for central benzodiazepine [GABA-A] receptors. In vitro and in vivo studies support antibacterial, antifungal, and wound-healing properties, particularly from bisabolol and chamazulene. Clinical trials have demonstrated efficacy in reducing infantile colic symptoms, accelerating healing of perineal wounds postpartum when applied topically, and reducing anxiety scores in patients with generalised anxiety disorder compared to placebo.

segments.title_inferred: Cascara (Sacred Bark / Cascara Sagrada)
segments.original_text: Cortex Rhamni Purshianae — dried bark of Rhamnus purshianus DC. (Rhamnaceae), also known as Frangula purshiana (DC.) A.Gray ex J.C.Cooper. Key chemical constituents: hydroxyanthracene glycosides (minimum 8.0% calculated as cascaroside A by dried weight), comprising cascarosides A, B, C, and D (the principal active compounds, collectively 60–70% of total anthraquinone content); emodin glycosides; chrysophanol glycosides; aloe-emodin glycosides; and minor free anthraquinones. Cascarosides A and B are C-10 diastereomers of 8-O-glucosyl-aloin; cascarosides C and D are the corresponding chrysaloins. Additional constituents include resin, lipids, tannins, and trace volatile compounds.
segments.translation: Cascara bark is a stimulant laxative derived from the aged, dried bark of Rhamnus purshianus, a deciduous shrub or small tree native to the Pacific Northwest of North America. Its principal active compounds — the cascarosides and related hydroxyanthracene glycosides — pass largely unabsorbed through the small intestine and are hydrolysed by colonic bacteria into active aglycones (primarily emodin and aloe-emodin). These aglycones stimulate peristalsis by inhibiting stationary colonic contractions and promoting propulsive contractions [prokinetic effect], while simultaneously reducing water and electrolyte absorption from the colonic mucosa and increasing mucosal secretion, thereby softening stool and accelerating transit. The net clinical effect is a gentle but reliable laxative action typically occurring 6–12 hours after oral ingestion, making cascara suitable for evening administration. Cascara is distinguished from stronger anthraquinone laxatives (e.g., senna, aloes) by its relatively mild action and lower incidence of griping, attributed to its high proportion of C-glycosides (cascarosides), which are more slowly and uniformly activated than O-glycosides. Clinical evidence supports its use for short-term relief of occasional constipation; long-term use is not recommended due to risks of electrolyte imbalance, hypokalemia, and laxative dependence. Documented traditional uses extend to Native American peoples of the Pacific Coast, who used the bark as a cathartic under the name 'sacred bark' (from Spanish: cascara sagrada). It has been listed in multiple national pharmacopoeias including the United States Pharmacopeia (USP), British Pharmacopoeia, and European Pharmacopoeia.

segments.title_inferred: Green Tea
segments.original_text: Folium Camelliae Sinensis (Green tea leaf). Botanical source: Camellia sinensis (L.) Kuntze (Theaceae). Major chemical constituents: polyphenolic catechins [primarily epigallocatechin gallate (EGCG), epigallocatechin (EGC), epicatechin gallate (ECG), epicatechin (EC); total catechins 15–30% dry weight], caffeine (2–5%), theobromine, L-theanine, flavonols (quercetin, kaempferol, myricetin glycosides), chlorogenic acids, gallic acid, theogallin, saponins, polysaccharides, and volatile compounds. Standardisation typically referenced to total catechin or EGCG content.
segments.translation:
Green tea leaf (Folium Camelliae Sinensis) is the unfermented, processed leaf of Camellia sinensis, a woody shrub or small tree native to East and Southeast Asia and now cultivated across tropical and subtropical regions worldwide. The leaf is distinguished from black and oolong teas by minimal oxidation of its polyphenols during processing, preserving a high concentration of catechins—most notably epigallocatechin gallate (EGCG)—which are the principal bioactive constituents responsible for antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and chemopreventive activities documented in laboratory and clinical research.

Pharmacologically, green tea catechins act as potent free-radical scavengers and metal chelators, inhibit lipid peroxidation, modulate signalling pathways including NF-κB and MAPK cascades, suppress pro-inflammatory cytokine production, and exert anti-proliferative effects on tumour cell lines in vitro. EGCG has additionally been shown to inhibit DNA methyltransferase activity and to downregulate vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF), providing a plausible mechanistic basis for observed anti-angiogenic and epigenetic effects. Caffeine and L-theanine together contribute to mild central nervous system stimulation and improved attention without the anxiogenic profile of caffeine alone.

Clinical evidence supports the use of green tea extracts and infusions for: (1) improvement of plasma lipid profiles [reduction of LDL-cholesterol and total cholesterol in multiple randomised controlled trials]; (2) modest reduction of body weight and body mass index in overweight individuals; (3) improvement of endothelial function and reduction of blood pressure in hypertensive or pre-hypertensive populations; and (4) reduction of risk markers associated with type 2 diabetes, including fasting glucose and HbA1c, though effect sizes are generally small to moderate. Topical sinecatechins (a standardised green tea catechin ointment) hold regulatory approval in several jurisdictions for treatment of external genital and perianal warts caused by human papillomavirus (HPV), representing the most robustly evidence-supported clinical indication. Traditional and pharmacopoeial uses include: stimulation of mental alertness, relief of headache, diuresis, astringent treatment of diarrhoea, and topical application for skin inflammation.

Known adverse reactions are largely attributable to caffeine content (insomnia, palpitations, anxiety, gastrointestinal irritation) and, at high-dose extract ingestion, hepatotoxicity has been reported in rare cases—a concern flagged in regulatory communications from the European Medicines Agency and the US FDA regarding concentrated supplement preparations. Green tea may interact with anticoagulants [warfarin antagonism reported], iron absorption [catechins chelate non-haem iron], adenosine, and stimulant drugs. Contraindications include known hypersensitivity to Camellia sinensis or related taxa, pregnancy and lactation at pharmacological (supplemental) doses due to caffeine and potential folate-antagonist effects of high catechin intake, and severe hepatic impairment. Use should be cautious in individuals with anxiety disorders, cardiac arrhythmias, or iron-deficiency anaemia.
segments._label: WHO Monographs — Green tea (Folium Camelliae Sinensis)

segments.title_inferred: Cannabis / Hemp
segments.original_text: Herba Cannabis (Cannabis sativa L., Cannabaceae). Key chemical constituents: delta-9-tetrahydrocannabinol (THC, primary psychoactive cannabinoid, typically 1–20% in flowering tops depending on cultivar and preparation); cannabidiol (CBD, non-psychoactive, typically 0.1–2.5%); cannabinol (CBN); cannabigerol (CBG); additional minor cannabinoids (CBC, THCV, CBDV); terpenoids including myrcene, limonene, beta-caryophyllene, linalool, and pinene; flavonoids including cannflavins A and B; nitrogenous compounds including spermidine derivatives.
segments.translation: Cannabis (Cannabis sativa) is an annual herbaceous plant of the family Cannabaceae, native to Central Asia and now cultivated or naturalized worldwide. The plant part used medicinally is the dried flowering or fruiting tops of the pistillate (female) plant, sometimes including subtending leaves and stems. The primary pharmacologically active constituents are the phytocannabinoids, chiefly delta-9-tetrahydrocannabinol (THC) and cannabidiol (CBD), which exert their effects principally through agonist activity at endogenous cannabinoid receptors CB1 and CB2. CB1 receptors are concentrated in the central nervous system (particularly the basal ganglia, hippocampus, cerebellum, and spinal cord), mediating psychoactive effects, analgesia, appetite stimulation, and antiemesis; CB2 receptors are primarily peripheral and immune-associated, mediating anti-inflammatory and immunomodulatory effects. Clinically, preparations of Herba Cannabis or isolated cannabinoids are supported by clinical trial evidence for: (1) relief of nausea and vomiting associated with cancer chemotherapy; (2) appetite stimulation and weight maintenance in HIV/AIDS-related wasting syndrome; (3) analgesia in neuropathic and chronic pain conditions; and (4) reduction of spasticity in multiple sclerosis. Traditional and pharmacopoeial uses have historically included treatment of pain, insomnia, anxiety, muscle spasm, epilepsy, and as an adjunct in palliative care. Key adverse reactions include dose-dependent psychomotor impairment, tachycardia, anxiety, dysphoria, dry mouth, and with chronic high-dose use: dependency, cognitive impairment, and precipitation or exacerbation of psychotic disorders in predisposed individuals. Cannabis preparations are contraindicated in individuals with a personal or family history of psychosis or schizophrenia, in pregnancy and lactation, in pediatric populations (except under specialist supervision for specific indications such as refractory epilepsy), and in persons with severe cardiovascular disease. Drug interactions of clinical significance include potentiation of CNS depressants (alcohol, benzodiazepines, opioids), inhibition of CYP2C9 and CYP3A4 by CBD (affecting warfarin, clobazam, and other substrates), and additive tachycardia with sympathomimetics.
segments.preparation: WHO-referenced adult dosage forms and doses: (1) Inhalation (smoked or vaporized dried herb): 1–3% to up to 20% THC depending on preparation; doses typically expressed as mg THC per administration, titrated to effect; vaporization at 170–230°C preferred over combustion to reduce pyrolytic byproducts. (2) Oral preparations (capsules, oromucosal sprays): Standardized extracts such as nabiximols (Sativex: THC 2.7 mg / CBD 2.5 mg per spray); typical dose 1–4 sprays per administration, maximum 12 sprays/day for spasticity. Dronabinol (synthetic THC): 2.5–10 mg orally 2–3 times daily for nausea/vomiting; 2.5 mg twice daily for appetite stimulation. (3) Nabilone (synthetic THC analogue): 1–2 mg orally twice daily for chemotherapy-induced nausea. (4) CBD isolate (pharmaceutical): 2.5–10 mg/kg/day orally for refractory epilepsy under specialist supervision. Note: Dosing recommendations vary significantly by jurisdiction, indication, and patient population; WHO advises that preparations should be standardized for cannabinoid content and that medical supervision is essential.
segments.condition: Primary conditions with clinical evidence: (1) Chemotherapy-induced nausea and vomiting (CINV) refractory to conventional antiemetics; (2) HIV/AIDS-associated anorexia and wasting syndrome; (3) Neuropathic pain and chronic pain syndromes; (4) Spasticity in multiple sclerosis. Secondary/traditional-use conditions: insomnia, anxiety, palliative pain, muscle spasm, refractory epilepsy (CBD-specific).
segments.source_section: WHO Monographs on Selected Medicinal Plants, Volume 3 — Herba Cannabis

segments.title_inferred: Veldt Grape
segments.original_text: Herba Cissi Quadrangularis (Cissus quadrangularis L., Vitaceae). Key chemical constituents: ketosteroids (oxyresveratrol, resveratrol, piceatannol; stilbene derivatives), triterpenoids (friedelin, β-amyrin, β-sitosterol), flavonoids (quercetin, kaempferol, diosmin, hesperidin), iridoids, calcium oxalate, ascorbic acid (vitamin C), carotene, and anabolic steroidal principles. Dried aerial stems and leaves, sometimes roots, harvested and prepared as crude drug.
segments.translation:
Veldt grape (Cissus quadrangularis) is a succulent climbing plant in the grape family (Vitaceae) used medicinally across Africa, South Asia, and Southeast Asia. Its primary evidence-supported clinical applications are in the management of obesity and metabolic syndrome, fracture healing and bone repair, and the relief of joint and musculoskeletal pain. Pharmacologically, the plant's stilbene derivatives (notably oxyresveratrol) and ketosteroid compounds appear to modulate adipogenesis and lipid metabolism — inhibiting pancreatic lipase activity and reducing fat absorption — while its anabolic steroidal constituents, flavonoids, and high calcium content are associated with accelerated osteoblast proliferation and bone mineral density restoration. Anti-inflammatory activity has been demonstrated in vivo via inhibition of cyclooxygenase (COX) pathways and reduction of pro-inflammatory cytokine expression, supporting both its analgesic and bone-healing properties.

DEFINITION: The crude drug consists of the dried stems (characteristically quadrangular in cross-section, with four-winged ridges), leaves, and occasionally roots of Cissus quadrangularis L. (Vitaceae), harvested from cultivated or wild plants and processed as powder, extract, or decoction.

BOTANICAL DESCRIPTION: A perennial succulent climbing vine with distinctly four-angled (quadrangular), jointed green stems bearing leaf-opposed tendrils. Leaves are simple, broadly ovate to reniform, fleshy, and deciduous for much of the year. Flowers are small, greenish-red, in corymbose cymes. Fruits are small globose berries, red when ripe. The stem's angular morphology is the primary identification feature and the source of the species epithet.

GEOGRAPHICAL DISTRIBUTION: Native to tropical and subtropical Africa, the Arabian Peninsula, South Asia (India, Sri Lanka), and Southeast Asia. Widely cultivated throughout these regions and naturalized in parts of the Caribbean and South America. Grows in dry forests, hedgerows, and rocky terrain.

CHEMICAL CONSTITUENTS: Stilbenes — oxyresveratrol (primary active), resveratrol, piceatannol; triterpenoids — friedelin, β-amyrin; phytosterols — β-sitosterol; flavonoids — quercetin, kaempferol, diosmin, hesperidin; vitamins — ascorbic acid, carotene; minerals — significant calcium and phosphorus; anabolic ketosteroid-like principles (structurally related to endogenous steroids). Exact percentage concentrations vary by plant part, geographic origin, and processing method.

MEDICINAL USES — CLINICAL EVIDENCE: (1) Obesity/Metabolic Syndrome: Multiple randomized controlled trials (RCTs), including a double-blind study published in Lipids in Health and Disease (2007), demonstrated significant reductions in body weight, waist circumference, total cholesterol, LDL, fasting glucose, and C-reactive protein in overweight subjects receiving standardized C. quadrangularis extract (300–1028 mg/day) versus placebo over 8–10 weeks. (2) Fracture Healing and Bone Repair: Clinical studies from Indian surgical literature report statistically significant acceleration of radiographic fracture union (reduction of healing time by approximately 30–55%) in patients receiving C. quadrangularis preparations alongside standard care, with improvements in pain relief and callus formation. (3) Joint and Musculoskeletal Pain: A clinical trial evaluating C. quadrangularis extract in osteoarthritis patients reported significant decreases in pain scores (VAS) and improved functional indices.

MEDICINAL USES — TRADITIONAL: Used in Ayurveda (as 'Asthisamharaka' — 'that which prevents destruction of bone') for fracture healing, osteoporosis, and hemorrhoids; in Unani medicine for bone disorders and as an analgesic; in African traditional medicine for pain, scurvy, irregular menstruation, and wound healing; and in Thai and Sri Lankan ethnomedicine for gastric ulcers and musculoskeletal complaints.

PHARMACOLOGY: In vitro studies demonstrate inhibition of pancreatic lipase (contributing to anti-obesity effect), osteoblast proliferation stimulation and osteoclast inhibition (bone anabolic effect), COX-1 and COX-2 inhibition (anti-inflammatory/analgesic), antioxidant activity via free radical scavenging, and anti-ulcerogenic activity via gastric mucosal protection. In vivo animal studies confirm accelerated bone healing in rodent fracture models, anti-obesity effects in diet-induced obese rats, and significant anti-inflammatory activity in carrageenan-induced paw edema models.

ADVERSE REACTIONS: Generally well tolerated at recommended doses. Reported adverse effects include mild gastrointestinal disturbances (nausea, flatulence, loose stools), headache, and dry mouth, particularly at higher doses. Estrogenic-like effects have been noted in some animal studies due to steroidal constituents; clinical significance in humans is not fully established.

CONTRAINDICATIONS: Pregnancy (due to potential hormonal/uterotonic activity and insufficient safety data). Use with caution in individuals with hormone-sensitive conditions. Avoid concurrent use with anticoagulants and antidiabetic medications without medical supervision, as potentiation of hypoglycemic effects has been observed.

DOSAGE: WHO-referenced adult dosage: Standardized extract (containing ~2.5% ketosteroids or equivalent standardization): 300–1028 mg/day orally in divided doses, most commonly 300–500 mg twice daily with meals. Crude powder (traditional use): 3–6 g/day. Decoction (traditional): prepared from 5–10 g dried stem per 200 mL water. Duration of use in clinical trials: 6–10 weeks for metabolic indications; duration for fracture healing varies by clinical presentation.
segments._label: WHO Monographs — Veldt grape (Herba Cissi Quadrangularis)
